Decontamination Procedures (164) -

Cut and Remove Overgarment Jacket
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Before cutting, dip scissors in a 5 percent chlorine solution to prevent contamination.
2. Make two cuts: one up each sleeve from the wrist to the shoulder and then across the shoulder through the collar (see Figure 8-4 on...
Procedure:

1. Before cutting, dip scissors in a 5 percent chlorine solution to prevent contamination.
2. Make two cuts: one up each sleeve from the wrist to the shoulder and then across the shoulder through the collar (see Figure 8-4 on page 8-7).
3. Cut around bandages, tourniquets, and splints, leaving them in place.
4. Do not allow gloves to touch the patient along the cut line.
5. Keep cuts close to the inside of the arms so most of the sleeve material can be folded outward.
6. Unzip the jacket and roll the chest sections to the respective sides, with the inner surface outward.
7. Continue by tucking the clothing between the arm and chest.
8. Roll the cut sleeves away from the arms, exposing the black liner.
overgarment,jacket,cutting,decontamination,nuclear_cbrn
Cut and Remove Overgarment Trousers
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Cut both trousers legs starting at the ankle as shown in Figure 8-5 on page 8-8.
2. Keep cuts near the inside of the legs, along the inseam, to the crotch.
3. Cut the left leg all the way to the waist, avoiding pockets.
4. Cut the...
Procedure:

1. Cut both trousers legs starting at the ankle as shown in Figure 8-5 on page 8-8.
2. Keep cuts near the inside of the legs, along the inseam, to the crotch.
3. Cut the left leg all the way to the waist, avoiding pockets.
4. Cut the right leg across at the crotch to meet the left leg cut.
5. Cut around bandages, tourniquets, and splints, leaving them in place.
6. Place scissors in a 5 percent chlorine solution after cutting.
7. Fold the cut trouser halves away from the patient and allow them to drop to the litter with the contaminated (green) side down.
8. Roll the inner leg portion under and between the legs.
overgarment,trousers,cutting,decontamination,nuclear_cbrn
Decontaminate Aircraft Interior for Radiological Contamination
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
For radiological contamination, use a radiac meter to determine the extent and location of contamination inside the aircraft. If there is contamination, determine the intensity of the contamination. If the contamination has an intensity greater...
For radiological contamination, use a radiac meter to determine the extent and location of contamination inside the aircraft. If there is contamination, determine the intensity of the contamination. If the contamination has an intensity greater than 0.33 cGy, the interior of the aircraft must be decontaminated.

Use hot, soapy water to wash the contaminated areas and a sponge to mop up the water and the contamination.
radiological decon, radiac meter, hot soapy water, aircraft interior decon
Decontaminate Aircraft Interior with Hot Soapy Water and IEDKs
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Decon the aircraft’s interior with hot, soapy water as long as maintenance personnel approve the use of it on certain pieces of equipment. Use IEDKs or damp, soapy washcloths to decon optical and electrical equipment and...
Decon the aircraft’s interior with hot, soapy water as long as maintenance personnel approve the use of it on certain pieces of equipment. Use IEDKs or damp, soapy washcloths to decon optical and electrical equipment and instruments.

NOTE: The IEDK leaves a charcoal residue on surfaces.
hot soapy water, IEDKs, aircraft interior decon, optical equipment decon, electrical equipment decon
Decontamination of Casualties in NBC Environments
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure for Decontaminating Casualties in NBC Environments

Overview:
Decontamination is a critical step in managing casualties exposed to chemical, biological, or radiological agents. It reduces the risk of recontamination and protects...
Procedure for Decontaminating Casualties in NBC Environments

Overview:
Decontamination is a critical step in managing casualties exposed to chemical, biological, or radiological agents. It reduces the risk of recontamination and protects medical personnel and facilities.

Steps:
1. Coordinate with the supported unit to establish and train a decontamination team. Medical units must be prepared to supervise these teams. Co-locate the decontamination site near the MTF (Medical Treatment Facility).
2. Personnel performing decontamination should wear appropriate PPE: mask, gloves, and protective overgarments.
3. Use monitoring equipment such as chemical agent monitors (CAMs) or automatic chemical agent alarms (M8A1) to assess contamination levels.
4. Follow these steps for patient decontamination:
- Step A: Decontaminate the patient’s mask and hood using an M291/M258A1 kit or a 0.5% chlorine solution. Roll the hood or cut it off after decontaminating.
- Step B: Remove gross contamination from the patient’s overgarment.
- Step C: Remove the patient’s protective overgarment and personal effects.
- Step D: Remove the patient’s battledress uniform (BDU).
- Step E: Transfer the patient to a decon litter.
- Step F: If environment permits, remove the mask and decontaminate the skin using an M291/M258A1 kit or 0.5% chlorine solution.
- Step G: Transfer the patient across the shuffle pit after ensuring all contamination has been removed and monitoring to confirm that the casualty is no longer in MOPP (Mission Oriented Protective Posture) protection.
5. Properly dispose of contaminated clothing and equipment using impermeable bags or containers, or bleaching powders. Disposal sites must be marked according to unit policy.
6. Use appropriate decontamination kits such as the M291/M258A1 for skin and mask decontamination.
7. Ensure that all personnel involved in decontamination are trained on the use of equipment, including immersion heaters, chemical agent monitors (CAMs), and M8 detector paper.

Equipment Required:
• 3 containers (2-gallon capacity) with one holding an immersion heater.
• One 50-pound drum of general-purpose detergent.
• Three containers (3-gallon capacity) and four additional containers for radiological decon.
• Two M258A1 or M291 decon kits per person.
• Four M1 chemical agent monitors (CAMs).
• Two boxes of plastic bags.
• Four M8A1 automatic chemical agent alarms.
• Ten 50-pound drums of STB (Stabilized Trichloroisocyanuric Acid).
• Two immersion heaters with fuel.
• Two books of M8 paper per squad.
• One role of M9 paper per squad.
• Four long-handled brushes.
• Four large sponges.
• Four bundles or rags.
• Four cutting tools (scissors, knives).
• One filter-air or filter canister per mask.
• Protective mask PLL parts and one hood per mask.
• Three AN/PDR77 radiacmeters or AN/VDR.
• Overdress garments and one set of BDUs per soldier if necessary.

Layout for a Patient Decontamination Station:
• Dirty Dump: Area where contaminated items are discarded.
• Contaminated Area: Zone near the casualty decon station.
• Triage Point (downwind): Located 75 yards from the decon station.
• Decon Station: Where decontamination takes place.
• Shuffle Pit: Used to transfer patients after decontamination.
• Clean Area: Zone upwind, where clean treatment occurs.
• Disposition Point: Final location for cleaned casualties.

Personnel Decontamination Lane (Stations):
|Station|Personnel|Equipment|
|---|---|---|
|1: Individual Gear Decon|2 attendants, 1 monitor (CAM operator)|3 30-gallon canisters, 2 long-handled brushes, 2 ponchos or plastic sheets, 1 CAM, 8 M8 detector paper, 4 M256A1 kits, 100 trash bags|
|2: Overboot and Hood Decon|1 attendant|2 cutting tools, 60 M258A1 or M295 (or one per person), 2 ponchos or plastic tarps, 100 trash bags|
|3: Overgarment Removal|1 attendant|10 M258A1/M295, 2 30-gallon containers, 100 trash bags|
|4: Overboot and Glove Removal|1 attendant|2 30-gallon containers, 100 trash bags, engineer tape, cutting tool|
|5: Monitor|1 CAM operator, 1 aidman or combat lifesaver|1 or more CAM or PDR-77, 5 M8 detector paper, 24 M258A1/M295|
|6: Mask Removal|2 attendants|1 M8A1 chemical alarm|
|7: Mask Decon Point|2 attendants, 1 monitor|4 3-gallon containers, 1 or more CAM or PDR-77, 2 sponges, 1 case paper towels, 1 immersion heater w/container, mask sanitizing solution|
|8: Reissue Point|Unit supply NCO, Unit NBC NCO|Mask PLL, overdress garments and BDUs if appropriate|

Notes:
• If only one radiacmeter is available, use it at Station 5 to monitor personnel. Pile together decontaminated equipment from Station 1 and decontaminated masks from Station 7.
• After a squad has been monitored through Station 5, an attendant should monitor the equipment pile.
NBC decontamination, chemical warfare decon, radiological decontamination, M291/M258A1 kit, patient decon, casualty decon, PPE for decon
Handle Contaminated Remains Following a Nuclear Detonation
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Develop procedures for handling contaminated remains following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Recognize that in the immediate aftermath of a nuclear detonation, only a small minority of fatalities will be contaminated.
2....
Objective: Develop procedures for handling contaminated remains following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Recognize that in the immediate aftermath of a nuclear detonation, only a small minority of fatalities will be contaminated.
2. Most contaminated fatalities are victims who evacuated and died due to traumatic or thermal injuries, many of whom may have been decontaminated during evacuation or medical treatment.
3. As remains are recovered from damage zones over time, the probability that remains are contaminated increases.
4. Complete external decontamination may not be possible for all decedents, and internal decontamination is not necessary or possible.
5. Decontamination should occur after victim identification, forensic work, or medicolegal procedures.
6. Coordinate with radiation experts to understand the risks of handling contaminated remains and develop effective protective measures for responders.

Considerations:
• Fatality management plans must include protocols for decontamination of remains.
• Protective equipment and training are essential for all personnel involved in handling contaminated remains.
contaminated remains, decontamination, nuclear detonation, radiation safety, fatality management
Perform Contact Time/Interior Decontamination for Aircraft
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
At this station, the decon solution is allowed to completely neutralize the chemical agent; also, the interior of the aircraft is decontaminated, if necessary. The aircraft will remain in station 3 for no less than 30 minutes. The decon solution...
At this station, the decon solution is allowed to completely neutralize the chemical agent; also, the interior of the aircraft is decontaminated, if necessary. The aircraft will remain in station 3 for no less than 30 minutes. The decon solution reacts with most chemical agents within 5 minutes. However, allowing the decon solution to remain on the contaminated surface for 30 minutes ensures a complete neutralization.

While the aircraft is in this station, an attendant inspects the interior of the aircraft for liquid contamination using the CAM and M8 detector paper. If liquid-chemical contamination is identified, he will decon the interior of the aircraft.

WARNING: Personnel should not be in the aircraft during this operation.
contact time, interior decon, chemical agent neutralization, aircraft decontamination, CAM, M8 detector paper
Remove Outer Gloves
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Before touching the patient, decon gloves with a 5 percent chlorine solution.
2. Lift the patient’s arms up and out of the cutaway sleeves unless detrimental to his condition.
3. Grasp the fingers of the gloves, roll the cuffs...
Procedure:

1. Before touching the patient, decon gloves with a 5 percent chlorine solution.
2. Lift the patient’s arms up and out of the cutaway sleeves unless detrimental to his condition.
3. Grasp the fingers of the gloves, roll the cuffs over the fingers, and turn the gloves inside out.
4. Do not remove the inner cotton gloves at this time.
5. Carefully lower the patient’s arms across the chest after the outer gloves have been removed (see Figure 8-6 on page 8-8).
6. Do not allow the patient's arms to come into contact with the exterior of his overgarment.
7. Drop the gloves into the contaminated-waste bag.
8. Decon team members decontaminate their gloves with a 5 percent chlorine solution.
gloves,removal,decontamination,nuclear_cbrn
Use Herman-Nelson Portable Duct Heaters for Hot-Air Decontamination
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Field studies show the Herman-Nelson portable duct heaters to be effective in decontaminating chemically contaminated vehicle and aircraft interiors.

However, using a hot-air decon has the following drawbacks: time required, **heat...
Field studies show the Herman-Nelson portable duct heaters to be effective in decontaminating chemically contaminated vehicle and aircraft interiors.

However, using a hot-air decon has the following drawbacks: time required, heat damage, and downwind vapor hazards. A hot-air decon for a helicopter or light aircraft requires about 30 to 120 minutes to remove all of the contamination. Times are based on the amount of contamination and interior surface materials. Enough time must be provided to get the contaminated surface hot enough to vaporize the agent.

The Herman-Nelson heaters discharge air at a temperature between 150°F and 280°F. Pressurized containers, such as fire extinguishers, must be removed before starting a hot-air decon. These items will fail catastrophically and could cause severe damage. The heat will also damage sensitive electronic equipment.

Chemical agents irreversibly bind to many materials, particularly gaskets, vinyl coatings, seat belts, and seat cushions. These items should be removed since they cannot be decontaminated. A hot-air decon does not destroy chemical agents. The air exiting the aircraft may contain hazardous levels of the agent.

A downwind exclusion area of about 164 yards must be maintained to prevent unwanted casualties.
Herman-Nelson heaters, hot-air decon, aircraft interior decon, heat damage, downwind exclusion area
Use of DS2 for Chemical Contamination Decontamination
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:
1. Scrub the DS2 into the exterior surface with brushes.
2. Wait 30 minutes, then wash off.
3. If a decon apparatus is not available, use field-expedient resources to apply DS2 or STB from bulk containers.
4. Use nonstandard...
Procedure:
1. Scrub the DS2 into the exterior surface with brushes.
2. Wait 30 minutes, then wash off.
3. If a decon apparatus is not available, use field-expedient resources to apply DS2 or STB from bulk containers.
4. Use nonstandard decontaminants as discussed in Appendix B if necessary.
Warning:
• Use extreme caution when handling DS2.
• Do not mix DS2 and STB because it will cause a fire.
• Do not spray DS2 on personnel or protective clothing.
• DS2 is a combustible solution. Do not spray DS2 on hot engine blocks.
• Severe chemical burns can result if safety precautions are not observed.
• DS2 can severely injure eyes and skin, and if inhaled, can cause illness.
• DS2 can damage the NBC protective overgarment.
• Long-term contact with DS2 can damage the NBC protective gloves, hood, and overboots.
ds2,chemical decon,warnings,safety precautions
Airfield Area Decontamination Procedure
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: Decon the area required to sustain the mission (see Figure 5-2).

Steps:
1. Apply STB slurry on the entrance/exit of airfield hangers.
2. Ensure that the STB application includes **10 feet on each side of the runway between the...
Objective: Decon the area required to sustain the mission (see Figure 5-2).

Steps:
1. Apply STB slurry on the entrance/exit of airfield hangers.
2. Ensure that the STB application includes 10 feet on each side of the runway between the entrance and the exit.
3. Decontaminate buildings’ or hangars’ entrance/exit and doors accordingly.
4. Use a field-expedient method to cover the major entry/exit toward the site if necessary.

Note: A transfer hazard will remain a problem until all parts of the airfield have weathered.
airfield,decon,nuclear,cbrn,military,procedure
Apply DS2 Decontaminant at Station 2
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Applying DS2 Decontaminant:

1. Divide the vehicle into four parts, and assign each part to a member of the scrubbing team to ensure even workload distribution and avoid duplication.

2. **Apply DS2 starting at the top of the...
Procedure for Applying DS2 Decontaminant:

1. Divide the vehicle into four parts, and assign each part to a member of the scrubbing team to ensure even workload distribution and avoid duplication.

2. Apply DS2 starting at the top of the vehicle and work downward toward the undercarriage, ensuring full coverage.

3. Pay special attention to the undercarriage, especially if the vehicle has crossed a contaminated area.

4. Use a mop for general application as it is less tiring, although this method results in more spillage.

5. Use the M13 decontaminating apparatus, portable (DAP) to apply DS2 to hard-to-reach areas if needed, even though it requires more physical effort from the scrubbing team.

Notes:
• Planning factors for DS2 application are detailed in Table 4-4 on page 4-20.
• Ensure that each member of the scrubbing team wears a TAP apron or wetweather gear to protect against exposure to DS2.
DS2 application, decontamination, vehicle decon, scrubbing team, M13 DAP, mop usage, undercarriage cleaning
Check Patient for Contamination After Overgarment Removal
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure to Check Patient for Contamination

1. Inspect the BDU:
- Use M8 detector paper or the CAM (Chemical Agent Monitor) to check the patient's BDU for contamination.
- Carefully survey the patient, paying particular...
Procedure to Check Patient for Contamination

1. Inspect the BDU:
- Use M8 detector paper or the CAM (Chemical Agent Monitor) to check the patient's BDU for contamination.
- Carefully survey the patient, paying particular attention to:
- Discolored areas,
- Damp spots,
- Tears on the uniform,
- Areas around the neck, wrists, and ears,
- Bandages, tourniquets, and splints.

2. Remove contaminated spots:
- Wash any contaminated spots with soap and water.
- Always dip the scissors in a 5 percent chlorine solution after each cut.

3. Recheck contamination:
- Recheck the area with detection equipment (M8 paper or CAM).

4. Handle significant contamination on BDU:
- If significant contamination is found on the BDU:
- Remove the BDU.
- Spot decontaminate the skin.
- To remove the BDU, follow the procedures for removing the protective overgarment as described in paragraphs 8-145 through 8-147.
- Do not remove the patient's identification tags.
check for contamination, M8 paper, CAM, spot decon, BDU removal
Check at Station 5
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
### Procedure: Check at Station 5

Overview:
At station 5, a final check is performed to ensure that all vehicles and equipment have been properly decontaminated and are free of contamination.

### Steps for Final Check
1. Use...
### Procedure: Check at Station 5

Overview:
At station 5, a final check is performed to ensure that all vehicles and equipment have been properly decontaminated and are free of contamination.

### Steps for Final Check
1. Use appropriate detection tools (e.g., M8 detector paper, M256A1 detector kits) to inspect all vehicles and equipment for residual contamination.
2. If contamination is detected:
- Re-decontaminate the item as necessary.
3. Ensure that all decontamination records are properly documented and maintained.

### Equipment Required
• 2 CAMs (Chemical Agent Monitors)
• 10 M256A1 detector kits
• 20 books of M8 detector paper
• 2 AN/VDR-2
• 2 M8A1 or M22 ACAAs (Agent Concentration and Chemical Analysis Assemblies)

### Additional Notes
• Ensure that all personnel at station 5 are trained in the use of detection tools and proper documentation procedures.
• Follow safety protocols when handling contaminated materials and equipment.
check, station 5, decontamination procedure, nuclear cbrn
Close Thorough-Decon Site After Processing Contaminated Units
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
### Procedure
1. Confirm completion of decontamination for all vehicles and soldiers from the contaminated unit.
2. Coordinate with the supported unit to ensure that no contaminated elements remain unprocessed.
3. Close the DED first, as...
### Procedure
1. Confirm completion of decontamination for all vehicles and soldiers from the contaminated unit.
2. Coordinate with the supported unit to ensure that no contaminated elements remain unprocessed.
3. Close the DED first, as directed by the chemical unit (decon or smoke/decon platoons).
4. Process the chemical unit through the DTD after closing the DED.
5. Close the DTD once the chemical unit has processed through it.
6. Mark the area as contaminated using an NBC-5 report, and report its exact location to the supported unit.

### Notes:
• Ensure that all personnel involved in site closure are trained on the procedures for closing the DED and DTD.
• Use proper marking kits (e.g., NBC marking kits) to clearly identify contaminated areas.
site closure, thorough decon, dtd processing, nbc report
Close the DED Area Sequentially
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
### Procedure: Close the DED Area Sequentially

Overview:
The Decontamination (DED) area is closed in sequence, starting at station 1. All vehicles, equipment, and nonexpendable supplies are inspected for contamination. If contamination is...
### Procedure: Close the DED Area Sequentially

Overview:
The Decontamination (DED) area is closed in sequence, starting at station 1. All vehicles, equipment, and nonexpendable supplies are inspected for contamination. If contamination is found on an item, it is decontaminated.

### Steps to Close the DED Area
1. Begin closing the DED area sequentially, starting at station 1.
2. Inspect all vehicles, equipment, and nonexpendable supplies for contamination using appropriate detection equipment.
3. If contamination is found on an item:
- Decontaminate the item immediately.
4. Continue this process through each station until the DED area is fully closed.

### Additional Notes
• Ensure that all decontamination procedures are followed as outlined in the FM 3-5/MCWP 3-37.3 guidelines.
• Maintain proper documentation of contamination checks and decontamination actions taken.
DED area closure, decontamination procedure, nuclear cbrn, FM 3-5/MCWP 3-37.3
Combat Medic Care During Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Combat medic care during decontamination:
1. The combat medic gently cuts away the bandage.
2. Decontaminate the area around the wound and irrigate it with a 0.5 percent chlorine solution.
3. If bleeding begins, replace the bandage with a...
Combat medic care during decontamination:
1. The combat medic gently cuts away the bandage.
2. Decontaminate the area around the wound and irrigate it with a 0.5 percent chlorine solution.
3. If bleeding begins, replace the bandage with a clean one.
4. Replace the old tourniquet by placing a new one ½ to 1 inch above the old one.
5. Remove the old tourniquet and decontaminate the patient's skin with a 0.5 percent chlorine solution.
6. Do not remove splints.
7. Decontaminate the splint by thoroughly rinsing it, to include the padding and cravats, with a 0.5 percent chlorine solution.

WARNING:
• Do not use an SDK around any wounds.
• Do not remove splints. The splint will not be removed until the patient has been evacuated to a forward MTF or hospital.
combat medic, decon procedure, tourniquet replacement, splint decontamination, chlorine solution
Complete Air Lock Decontamination Process
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Step 10. Enter air lock and ensure door is closed. When the low pressure indicator light in the PE module goes out, rotate the purge time clockwise to its full extent. Do not set the purge time until after the low pressure light goes...
Step 10. Enter air lock and ensure door is closed. When the low pressure indicator light in the PE module goes out, rotate the purge time clockwise to its full extent. Do not set the purge time until after the low pressure light goes out.

Step 11. Decontaminate your gloves again. Then decontaminate the bottom (rolled portion) of your hood. Wait for completion of the purge cycle. When the timer bell sounds, loosen your gloves but do not remove them yet.
air lock decontamination, nuclear cbrn
Conduct Thorough Decontamination Operations
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: Reduce or eliminate contamination from unit and individual equipment to allow safe operation at reduced MOPP levels.

Procedure Steps:
1. Perform thorough-decon operations using appropriate decontamination methods (DTD, DED, or...
Objective: Reduce or eliminate contamination from unit and individual equipment to allow safe operation at reduced MOPP levels.

Procedure Steps:
1. Perform thorough-decon operations using appropriate decontamination methods (DTD, DED, or DAD) as required by the situation.
2. Ensure periodic checks for residual contamination after each operation using:
- CAM (Chemical Agent Monitor)
- M8/M9 detector paper
- M256A1 detector kit
3. After completing thorough decon, move the unit to a tactical assembly area (AA) for further operations.
4. During the tactical assembly phase, the unit may undergo reconstitution or prepare for future operations.
5. Combat-service-support (CSS) elements replenish stocks, refit equipment, and replace personnel as needed.
6. Contaminated units should be assisted by a chemical unit to perform DTD (Decontamination of Troops and Equipment).
7. A supporting chemical unit performs either DED (Dispersed Decontamination) or DAD (Detachment Decontamination), depending on the situation.

Planning Considerations:
• The layout of a thorough-decon site is determined by METT-TC (Mission, Enemy, Terrain and Weather, Troops and Equipment, Time Available, and Civil Considerations).
• Thorough decon is resource-intensive and should be conducted beyond the range of enemy direct-fire systems.
• Decon sites are typically established near reconstitution areas or rear areas depending on unit size:
- Company-sized units – Brigade’s rear area
- Battalion-level units – Division’s rear area
- Units larger than battalion – Corps’s rear area
thorough decon, DTD, DED, DAD, decontamination procedures, nuclear cbrn
Contact Time/Interior Decon at Station 3
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
### Procedure: Contact Time/Interior Decon at Station 3

Overview:
At station 3, the contact time and interior decontamination process is performed to ensure that all surfaces are properly treated with DS2 and inspected for...
### Procedure: Contact Time/Interior Decon at Station 3

Overview:
At station 3, the contact time and interior decontamination process is performed to ensure that all surfaces are properly treated with DS2 and inspected for contamination.

### Steps for Contact Time/Interior Decon
1. Allow sufficient contact time for DS2 to take effect on contaminated surfaces.
2. Inspect any unused supplies and equipment for contamination using appropriate detection tools (e.g., M8 detector paper, M256A1 detector kits).
3. If no contamination is detected:
- Load the equipment and supplies onto a vehicle for transport or storage.
4. If contamination is found on an item:
- Throw all contaminated supplies into the nearest sump.

### Sump Requirements
• 35 cubic feet of space is required for 250 gallons of liquid drain off.
• Once a sump is 2/3 full, prepare a new sump to ensure proper containment and disposal.

### Equipment Required
• 2 AN/VDR-2
• 3 TAP aprons
• 6 30-gallon containers
• 10 books of M8 detector paper
• 30 sponges
• 8 M256A1 detector kits
• 50 trash bags
• 1 clipboard with a pen
• 1 stopwatch

### Additional Notes
• Ensure that all personnel at station 3 are trained in the use of detection equipment and proper decontamination procedures.
• Follow safety protocols when handling contaminated materials and sump disposal.
contact time, interior decon, station 3, decontamination procedure, nuclear cbrn
Cross the Hot Line to Clean Treatment Area
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Have the decontaminated patient cross the hot line to the clean treatment area.
2. Ensure the patient's boots are decontaminated at the hot line before entering the clean area.
3. At the entrance of the **CPS or clean...
Procedure:

1. Have the decontaminated patient cross the hot line to the clean treatment area.
2. Ensure the patient's boots are decontaminated at the hot line before entering the clean area.
3. At the entrance of the CPS or clean treatment area, remove the patient’s combat boots and protective mask.
hot line,clean treatment area,boot decon,mask removal
DS2 Application at Station 2
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
### Procedure: DS2 Application at Station 2

Overview:
At station 2, the DS2 (Decontamination Solution 2) is applied to vehicles and equipment as part of the decontamination process.

### Steps for DS2 Application
1. Apply DS2 using mops...
### Procedure: DS2 Application at Station 2

Overview:
At station 2, the DS2 (Decontamination Solution 2) is applied to vehicles and equipment as part of the decontamination process.

### Steps for DS2 Application
1. Apply DS2 using mops and brushes to all contaminated surfaces.
2. After application, throw the mops and brushes that were used in applying the DS2 into a sump or bury them to prevent further contamination.
3. Load the unused cans of DS2 and M13 DAPs (Decontamination Application Packs) onto a vehicle for transport or storage.

### Equipment Required
• 18 long-handled brushes
• 9 mops with extra mop heads
• 3 30-gallon containers
• 9 M13 DAPs
• Sufficient DS2

### Additional Notes
• Ensure that all personnel at station 2 are trained in the proper use of DS2 and associated equipment.
• Follow safety protocols when handling chemical decontamination solutions.
DS2 application, station 2, decontamination procedure, nuclear cbrn
Decon the Patient's Mask and Hood
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decon of the Patient's Mask and Hood

1. Move the patient to the clothing removal station. After the patient has been triaged and stabilized (if necessary) by the senior medic in the patient-decon area, move him to the litter...
Procedure for Decon of the Patient's Mask and Hood

1. Move the patient to the clothing removal station. After the patient has been triaged and stabilized (if necessary) by the senior medic in the patient-decon area, move him to the litter stands at the clothing removal station.

2. Decon the hood. Brush contamination off or use soap and water to wipe down the front, sides, and top of the hood.

3. Remove the hood. Remove the hood by cutting it with scissors or by loosening it from the mask attachment points for the quick-doff hood or other similar hoods (see Figure 8-3, page 8-6). Before cutting the hood, dip the scissors in a 5 percent chlorine solution. Cut the neck cord and the small string under the voicemitter. Release or cut the hood shoulder straps and unzip the hood zipper. Cut the hood upward to the top of the eye-lens outsert, staying close to the filter-inlet cover and eye-lens outsert, then across the forehead to the outer edge of the other eye-lens outsert. Proceed downward toward the patient's shoulder, staying close to the eye-lens outsert and filter-inlet cover, then across the lower part of the voicemitter to the zipper. After dipping the scissors in the 5 percent chlorine solution, cut the hood from the center of the forehead over the top of the head. Fold the left and right sides of the hood to the side of the patient's head, laying the sides of the hood on the litter.
decon, mask, hood, decontamination, nuclear cbrn
Decontaminate Gloves and Mask During Purge Cycle
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. During the entrance purge, the soldier decontaminates:
- Gloves,
- Mask, and
- Hood including outer portion of the rolled hood.
2. After completion of air purge, the soldier removes his mask and hood.
glove decon, mask removal, purge cycle
Decontaminate Hood During NBC Operations
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Effective decon may require two soldiers.
2. The soldier to be decontaminated first (soldier 1) turns and faces the shelter guard or buddy (soldier 2).
3. Soldier 2 loosens drawstrings over the voicemitter.
4. Soldier 1 turns,...
Procedure:

1. Effective decon may require two soldiers.
2. The soldier to be decontaminated first (soldier 1) turns and faces the shelter guard or buddy (soldier 2).
3. Soldier 2 loosens drawstrings over the voicemitter.
4. Soldier 1 turns, and soldier 2 wipes top, back, and sides of the hood with single passes of decon wipes.
5. Soldier 2 then decontaminates his own gloves.
6. He tightly rolls the hood up and off soldier 1's overgarment jacket, tucking straps into the roll. This prepares the hood for removal.
7. After soldier 1's hood is decontaminated, he performs the same procedures for soldier 2.
hood decontamination, NBC operations, two-person procedure
Decontaminate Hood and Mask for CB Contamination with Buddy Assistance
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Steps:

Buddy 1—

• (1) Loosens Buddy 2's draw cord.

• (2) Removes Buddy 2's underarm straps from under his/her arms.

• (3) Moves Buddy 2's straps over his/her shoulders.
CB contamination, decontaminate hood and mask, buddy assistance, draw cord, underarm straps

Steps:

1. Use the decontamination kit to clean individual equipment as necessary.
2. Follow proper procedures for decontamination to ensure all surfaces are free from C/B contaminants.

Details:
• Ensure that all gear and equipment used...
Steps:

1. Use the decontamination kit to clean individual equipment as necessary.
2. Follow proper procedures for decontamination to ensure all surfaces are free from C/B contaminants.

Details:
• Ensure that all gear and equipment used during exposure is thoroughly cleaned.
• Decontamination should be performed in accordance with established protocols.

High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Steps:

1. Decontaminate individual equipment as necessary during the C/B response.
2. Use appropriate decontamination procedures to ensure that all gear is clean and safe for use.

Details:
• This step is evaluated separately in task...
Steps:

1. Decontaminate individual equipment as necessary during the C/B response.
2. Use appropriate decontamination procedures to ensure that all gear is clean and safe for use.

Details:
• This step is evaluated separately in task 031-503-1013.
• Ensure that decontamination is performed according to established protocols.

Decontaminate Individual Gear (Weapons, Helmet, Load-Bearing Equipment, Mask Carrier) Using STB Mix
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decontaminating Individual Gear with STB Mix:

1. Mix STB Solution: The soldier mixes three parts earth to two parts STB.
2. Apply STB to Contaminated Gear:
- For chemical/biological contamination: Brush or rub STB onto...
Procedure for Decontaminating Individual Gear with STB Mix:

1. Mix STB Solution: The soldier mixes three parts earth to two parts STB.
2. Apply STB to Contaminated Gear:
- For chemical/biological contamination: Brush or rub STB onto individual gear (helmet and mask carrier) and the hose of the M43 mask if worn.
- For radiological contamination: Use hot, soapy water to brush or wipe radiological contamination from individual gear. Wash it with hot, soapy water (if available) and set it aside to dry on an uncontaminated surface (plastic, poncho, or similar material).
3. Remove Excess STB: Gently shake off any excess STB.
4. Set Gear Aside: Place the gear on an uncontaminated surface.
STB mix, decontaminate gear, chemical contamination, biological contamination, radiological contamination
Decontaminate Individual Gear for CB Contamination Without Assistance
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Steps:

1. Remove and discard your chemical protective helmet cover.

2. Cover the gear with STB dry mix.

3. Brush or rub STB dry mix into the material, and shake off any excess STB.

4. **Set your gear aside on an uncontaminated...
Steps:

1. Remove and discard your chemical protective helmet cover.

2. Cover the gear with STB dry mix.

3. Brush or rub STB dry mix into the material, and shake off any excess STB.

4. Set your gear aside on an uncontaminated surface (plastic, poncho, or similar material).

Note: If at any time during the technique you suspect you have spread contamination onto your skin or undergarments, decontaminate immediately with the individual decontaminating kit available and then proceed with the MOPP gear exchange.
CB contamination, decontaminate gear, STB dry mix, chemical protective helmet cover, MOPP gear exchange
Decontaminate Litter After Overboot Removal
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. While holding the patient’s heels off the litter, have a team member wipe the end of the litter with a 0.5 percent chlorine solution to neutralize any contamination that was transferred to the litter from the overboots.
2....
Procedure:

1. While holding the patient’s heels off the litter, have a team member wipe the end of the litter with a 0.5 percent chlorine solution to neutralize any contamination that was transferred to the litter from the overboots.
2. Lower the patient's heels onto the decontaminated litter.
3. Place the overboots in the contaminated-waste bag.
4. Decontaminate your gloves with the 5 percent chlorine solution after removal.

Notes:
• This step is part of the decontamination process for NBC exposure.
• Ensure that all steps are performed carefully to avoid causing additional harm or discomfort to the patient.
litter decontamination, contaminated litter handling, NBC decon, protective gear removal
Decontaminate Mask and Hood Using the Triple-Buddy Method for Chemical/Biological Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decontaminating Mask and Hood with the Triple-Buddy Method (Chemical/Biological):

1. Use IEDKs: If wearing the M42 or M43 mask, use the buddy-team method (three buddies). Buddy #1 uses an IEDK to wipe Buddy #2's hood, mask,...
Procedure for Decontaminating Mask and Hood with the Triple-Buddy Method (Chemical/Biological):

1. Use IEDKs: If wearing the M42 or M43 mask, use the buddy-team method (three buddies). Buddy #1 uses an IEDK to wipe Buddy #2's hood, mask, canister, and hose of the M42 or M43 mask.
- NOTE: Do not press so hard that you break Buddy #2's face-mask seal. Use as many wipes as necessary.
2. Wipe Gloves and Hold Equipment Away: Buddy #3 wipes off his gloves and holds Buddy #1's hose and canister away from his contaminated overgarment.
3. Roll Hood: After Buddy #1 wipes Buddy #2’s mask and then his own gloves, he rolls Buddy #2’s hood starting from the rear and using 2-inch tucks until it reaches the center of his head (see Figure 3-6, page 3-9).
4. Tighten Hood Roll: Roll the front of Buddy #2’s hood tightly under the outlet valve and filter.
5. Ensure Hood is Off Garment: Ensure that the hood is off of Buddy #2's garment (see Figure 3-7, page 3-10).
mask decontamination, hood rolling, triple-buddy method, IEDKs
Decontaminate Patient's Skin Using Spot Decon
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Use soap and water or a 0.5 percent chlorine solution to spot decontaminate the patient’s skin and areas of potential contamination, including:
- Areas around the neck
- Wrists
- Lower parts of the face
2....
Procedure:

1. Use soap and water or a 0.5 percent chlorine solution to spot decontaminate the patient’s skin and areas of potential contamination, including:
- Areas around the neck
- Wrists
- Lower parts of the face
2. Decontaminate the patient's identification tags and chain if necessary.

Additional Steps:
• Have the patient hold his breath, close his eyes, and lift or assist him with lifting his mask at the chin.
• Wash the patient’s face and exposed areas of skin with soap and water or wipe them with a 0.5 percent chlorine solution.
• Start wiping from the top of the ear and quickly wipe downward, covering:
- All folds in the skin
- Ear lobes
- Upper lip
- Chin
- Dimples
- Nose
• Continue up the other side of the face to the top of the other ear.
• Wipe the inside of the mask where it touches the face.
• Have the patient reseal and check his mask.

CAUTION: Keep the decon solution out of the patient’s eyes.
decontamination,skin,radioactive,0.5% chlorine solution,spot decon
Decontaminate and Maintain NBC Protection Gloves
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure for Decontaminating and Maintaining NBC Protection Gloves:

1. **Contamination Handling:
- If the 14 or 25 mil glove set becomes contaminated with liquid chemical agents, decontaminate or replace them within 24 hours after...
Procedure for Decontaminating and Maintaining NBC Protection Gloves:

1. **Contamination Handling:
- If the 14 or 25 mil glove set becomes contaminated with liquid chemical agents, decontaminate or replace them within 24 hours after exposure.
- If the 7 mil glove set becomes contaminated, decontaminate or replace it within 6 hours after exposure.
2. Decontamination Solution:
- Use a 5% bleach and water solution or a 5% HTH (household hypochlorite) and water solution for decontamination.
3. **Reusability:
- After decontamination, the gloves may be reused indefinitely as long as they remain serviceable.
4. Avoid Contaminants:
- Avoid contact with DS2, break-free, antiseize compound, or any other petroleum-based products, which can attack the rubber polymers and make them sticky.
5. **Removal of Contaminants:
- If the gloves come into contact with these materials, use a bleach and water solution to remove them as soon as possible.
6. Serviceability Test:
- To determine if a glove set is damaged or serviceable, perform the following test:
1. Fill the gloves with air and submerge in water.
2. Prior to submerging, hold the base of the glove with both hands (using thumb and index fingers), with the glove fingers hanging downward.
3. Rotate the glove several times to ensure an air pocket is formed below the palm.
4. Squeeze the inflated glove and examine for escaping air.
5. Submerge in water. If bubbles escape, the glove is unserviceable.
NBC gloves decontamination, bleach solution, serviceability test, contamination handling, glove maintenance
Decontaminate the Patient's Hood
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decontaminating the Patient's Hood:

1. Brush off contamination or use soap and water to wipe down the front, sides, and top of the hood.
2. Remove the hood by cutting it with scissors or by loosening it from the mask...
Procedure for Decontaminating the Patient's Hood:

1. Brush off contamination or use soap and water to wipe down the front, sides, and top of the hood.
2. Remove the hood by cutting it with scissors or by loosening it from the mask attachment points for the quick-doff hood or other similar hoods (see Figure 8-3, page 8-6).
- Before cutting the hood, dip the scissors in a 5 percent chlorine solution.
- Cut the neck cord and the small string under the voicemitter.
- Release or cut the hood shoulder straps and unzip the hood zipper.
- Cut the hood upward to the top of the eye-lens outsert, staying close to the filter-inlet cover and eye-lens outsert, then across the forehead to the outer edge of the other eye-lens outsert.
- Proceed downward toward the patient's shoulder, staying close to the eye-lens outsert and filter-inlet cover, then across the lower part of the voicemitter to the zipper.
- After dipping the scissors in the 5 percent chlorine solution, cut the hood from the center of the forehead over the top of the head.
- Fold the left and right sides of the hood away from the patient's head and remove the hood.
decontamination,hood,mask,cut,scissors,chlorine solution
Decontaminate the Patient's Protective Mask and Exposed Skin
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decontaminating the Patient's Protective Mask and Exposed Skin:

1. Prepare the decon solution: Use a 0.5 percent chlorine solution.
2. Decon the mask: Wipe down the external parts of the protective mask with the...
Procedure for Decontaminating the Patient's Protective Mask and Exposed Skin:

1. Prepare the decon solution: Use a 0.5 percent chlorine solution.
2. Decon the mask: Wipe down the external parts of the protective mask with the solution.
3. Protect the mask filters: Cover the mask’s air inlets with gauze or your hands to keep the mask filters dry.
4. Wash exposed skin: Wash the patient's face, including the neck and behind the ears, using soap and warm water or wipe them with a 0.5 percent chlorine solution.
5. Do not remove the protective mask during this process.
protective mask,exposed skin,washing,nuclear,cbrn,chemical,biological
Decontamination Procedures for the M203 Grenade Launcher Using the IEDK
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
NOTE: The M203 is not a crew-served weapon; however, this weapon is considered critical for accomplishing the infantry squad’s mission. Decon the M16A2 according to STP 21-24-SMCT. Before decontaminating the M203 grenade launcher, ensure...
NOTE: The M203 is not a crew-served weapon; however, this weapon is considered critical for accomplishing the infantry squad’s mission. Decon the M16A2 according to STP 21-24-SMCT. Before decontaminating the M203 grenade launcher, ensure that the dust cover is closed and the magazine is stored in the well. Ensure that the barrel assembly is closed.

To decon the M203 grenade launcher, follow these steps:

1. Step 1: Decon your gloves using the IEDK.
2. Step 2: Use the IEDK to decon the barrel, hand guard, and receiver.
3. Step 3: Use the IEDK to decon the lower receiver group and butt stock.
4. Step 4: Repeat step 1.
M203 grenade launcher, decon procedures, IEDK, nuclear cbrn
Decontamination Procedures for the M249 Squad Automatic Weapon (SAW) Using the IEDK
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
NOTE: Ensure that the feed tray of the weapon is closed. This prevents the powder from the IEDK from falling inside the feed tray and jamming the weapon. Do not touch the barrel of the weapon with chemical protective gloves when it is hot. The...
NOTE: Ensure that the feed tray of the weapon is closed. This prevents the powder from the IEDK from falling inside the feed tray and jamming the weapon. Do not touch the barrel of the weapon with chemical protective gloves when it is hot. The heat of the barrel could melt the gloves.

To decon the M249 SAW, follow these steps:

1. Step 1: Decon your gloves using the IEDK.
2. Step 2: Use the IEDK to remove all liquid contamination from the bipod, barrel assembly, gas regulator, and carrying handle.
3. Step 3: Use the IEDK to decon the feed-tray cover, magazine, trigger assembly, and shoulder stock.
4. Step 4: Repeat step 1.

NOTE: Ensure that the weapon is upright when decontaminating the feed-tray cover with the IEDK. This prevents powder from the IEDK from falling into the ammunition feeder. Place the magazine in the well before the decon.
M249 SAW, decon procedures, IEDK, nuclear cbrn
Decontamination Procedures for the M60 Machine Gun Using the IEDK
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
NOTE: Ensure that the feed tray of the weapon is closed. This prevents the powder from the IEDK from falling inside the feed tray and jamming the weapon. Do not touch the barrel of the weapon with the chemical protective gloves when it is hot....
NOTE: Ensure that the feed tray of the weapon is closed. This prevents the powder from the IEDK from falling inside the feed tray and jamming the weapon. Do not touch the barrel of the weapon with the chemical protective gloves when it is hot. The heat of the barrel could melt the gloves.

Decon Procedures for Individual and Crew-Served Weapons 10-1

To decon the M60 machine gun, follow these steps:

1. Step 1: Decon your gloves using the IEDK.
2. Step 2: Use the IEDK to remove all liquid contamination from the bipod, barrel assembly, forearm assembly, and carrying handle. Use a second IEDK if necessary.
3. Step 3: Use the IEDK to decon the feed-tray cover, shoulder stock, and trigger assembly.
4. Step 4: Repeat step 1.

NOTE: Do not decon the flash suppressor with the IEDK. Powder debris may fall into the barrel and cause the weapon to malfunction.
M60 machine gun, decon procedures, IEDK, nuclear cbrn
Decontamination and Undressing of Ambulatory Patients
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. The ambulatory patient is decontaminated and undressed as follows.
2. Some procedures can be done with one soldier, while others require more than one.
3. **Remove the LCE by unfastening/unbuttoning all connectors or tie...
Procedure:

1. The ambulatory patient is decontaminated and undressed as follows.
2. Some procedures can be done with one soldier, while others require more than one.
3. Remove the LCE by unfastening/unbuttoning all connectors or tie straps.
4. Place the equipment in a plastic bag.
5. Put the plastic bag in the designated storage area for later decon.
LCE removal, decontamination, protective equipment, storage procedures
Decontamination of 60-Millimeter Mortar Using IEDK
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decontaminating a 60-Millimeter Mortar:

1. Decon Your Gloves: Use the IEDK to decontaminate your gloves.
2. Decon the Mortar Tube and Handgrip: Apply the IEDK to the mortar tube and handgrip. If heavily contaminated, use...
Procedure for Decontaminating a 60-Millimeter Mortar:

1. Decon Your Gloves: Use the IEDK to decontaminate your gloves.
2. Decon the Mortar Tube and Handgrip: Apply the IEDK to the mortar tube and handgrip. If heavily contaminated, use an additional IEDK as needed.
3. Decon Bipod and Base Plate: Use the IEDK to decontaminate the bipod and base plate.
4. Decon Exterior of Telescope: Apply the IEDK to the exterior of the telescope. Do not apply the IEDK to the lens. If contamination is present on the lens, flush it with water.
5. Repeat Step 1: Re-decontaminate your gloves again after completing all steps.

Important Notes:
• Ensure that mechanical parts of the mortar are dried after decontamination.
• Lubricate the mortar when the mission permits.
• Do not touch the mortar tube with chemical protective gloves when it is hot, as heat could melt the gloves.
• The IEDK may only remove liquid hazards; vapor hazards may still be present on decontaminated items. Do not unmask until it has been determined safe to do so.
60-millimeter mortar, IEDK decon, decontamination procedure, chemical protective gloves, lens flushing
Decontamination of Ambulatory Patients in Clean Treatment Area
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. All ambulatory patients requiring EMT or AMT in the clean treatment area of the BAS must be decontaminated.
2. Stable patients not requiring treatment at the BAS but needing evacuation to a medical clearing station or corps...
Procedure:

1. All ambulatory patients requiring EMT or AMT in the clean treatment area of the BAS must be decontaminated.
2. Stable patients not requiring treatment at the BAS but needing evacuation to a medical clearing station or corps hospital should be evacuated in their protective overgarments and masks using any available transportation.
3. Before evacuation, spot remove all thickened agents from their protective clothing.
4. Most ambulatory patients will be treated in the contaminated treatment area and returned to duty.
5. Upon removal of an ambulatory patient's clothing, they become a litter patient.
6. The BAS and clearing station do not have replacement clothing for those cut off during decon.
7. Place the patient in a patient protective wrap (PPW) for protection during evacuation.

Note: Place the bandage scissors used in this procedure in a container of 5 percent chlorine solution when not in use.
ambulatory patient, decontamination, protective overgarments, patient protective wrap, chlorine solution
Decontamination of Biological Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:
1. Wash with soap and water.
2. If water is not available, use SDKs in the same manner as described for chemical-agent decon.
biological contamination,washing,soap and water
Decontamination of Biological Contamination Using Bleach and Alternatives
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:
1. Use a bleach solution for biological contamination.
2. If bleach is not available, use hot, soapy water.
3. Apply with brushes and scrub the surface well.
4. Rinse the surface after scrubbing (no wait is required).
5. DS2 and STB...
Procedure:
1. Use a bleach solution for biological contamination.
2. If bleach is not available, use hot, soapy water.
3. Apply with brushes and scrub the surface well.
4. Rinse the surface after scrubbing (no wait is required).
5. DS2 and STB are also effective against most known biological contamination, but they are not preferred due to their caustic nature.
6. Use other nonstandard biological decontaminants as described in Appendix B if necessary.
biological contamination,bleach,hot soapy water
Decontamination of Equipment Surfaces Using On-Board Portable Decon Apparatuses
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:
1. Use the on-board portable decon apparatus (such as the M11 or M13) to decon surfaces that you must touch on the exterior of the vehicle or equipment you must use to do your job.
2. The IEDK may be used on equipment that DS2 may...
Procedure:
1. Use the on-board portable decon apparatus (such as the M11 or M13) to decon surfaces that you must touch on the exterior of the vehicle or equipment you must use to do your job.
2. The IEDK may be used on equipment that DS2 may cause damage to by corrosive action.
m11,m13,iedk,portable decon apparatus
Decontamination of Individual Equipment Using IEDKs
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:
1. Use the IEDKs to decon individual equipment.
2. Wear your Kevlar® helmet protective cover to prevent or reduce the adsorption of any liquid chemical agent.
3. Wash with soap and water and bleach solutions (see Appendix B for...
Procedure:
1. Use the IEDKs to decon individual equipment.
2. Wear your Kevlar® helmet protective cover to prevent or reduce the adsorption of any liquid chemical agent.
3. Wash with soap and water and bleach solutions (see Appendix B for details).

Decontamination of Javelin Weapon System Using IEDK
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decontaminating the Javelin and Its Components:

• Refer to Table 10-1, page 10-6, for specific decon procedures for the Javelin and its components.

Important Notes and Warnings:
• The IEDK only removes a liquid hazard....
Procedure for Decontaminating the Javelin and Its Components:

• Refer to Table 10-1, page 10-6, for specific decon procedures for the Javelin and its components.

Important Notes and Warnings:
• The IEDK only removes a liquid hazard. Decontaminated items may still present a vapor hazard. Do not unmask until it has been determined safe to do so.
• Close the lens covers on the Javelin before decontamination, as decon powders on the lens can obscure your vision.
• Do not use the IEDK on the eyecup because decon powder entering the eyecup can obscure your vision.
Javelin decon, IEDK, lens cover closure, vapor hazard warning
Decontamination of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) and Essential Gear
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:
1. Wipe down your mask, hood, gloves, and other essential gear.
2. Do not attempt to remove chemical contamination from your protective overgarment unless there are obvious clumps of the agent.
3. If there are clumps, scrape off the...
Procedure:
1. Wipe down your mask, hood, gloves, and other essential gear.
2. Do not attempt to remove chemical contamination from your protective overgarment unless there are obvious clumps of the agent.
3. If there are clumps, scrape off the material.
4. Brush off radiological or frozen chemical-agent contamination.
Note: See FM 3-4 for detailed information on your overgarment’s protective qualities.
decontamination,ppe,chemical contamination,protective gear
Decontamination of Radiological Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:
1. Locate radiological contamination with monitoring equipment.
2. Remove by brushing and shaking it off.
3. Wash exposed areas of your skin, paying particular attention to your hair and fingernails.
4. Avoid breathing the dust...
Procedure:
1. Locate radiological contamination with monitoring equipment.
2. Remove by brushing and shaking it off.
3. Wash exposed areas of your skin, paying particular attention to your hair and fingernails.
4. Avoid breathing the dust particles you shake off by wearing your protective mask or a piece of cloth over your nose and mouth.
5. If wet, conduct a MOPP-gear exchange as soon as possible because brushing or shaking will not remove the contamination.
6. Wipe off your equipment with warm, soapy water using rags or damp paper towels.
radiological contamination,washing,brushing,shaking
Decontamination of Radiological Contamination Using Field Techniques
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:
1. If contaminated by fallout, rain out, neutron-induced contamination, or any type of radiological agent, use your monitoring equipment to locate the contamination and decon as required.
2. If detection equipment is not available and...
Procedure:
1. If contaminated by fallout, rain out, neutron-induced contamination, or any type of radiological agent, use your monitoring equipment to locate the contamination and decon as required.
2. If detection equipment is not available and you suspect contamination, decon.
3. Radiological contamination can usually be removed by brushing or scraping.
4. Use water to flush away radiological contamination; however, control runoff using drainage ditches that flow into a sump.
5. Remember, you have not destroyed the contamination—it has just been moved. The runoff will still be hazardous.
6. If time permits, brush or scoop away the top inch of soil from your fighting position to lower the amount of radiological contamination affecting you.
radiological decon,brushing,scraping,runoff control
Decontamination of Tritium Gas Contamination Areas
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decontaminating Areas Exposed to Tritium Gas:

1. Flush the Area with Air: The best method for decontaminating an area exposed to tritium gas is to flush it with air.

2. Protect Personnel from Inhalation Hazards: Personnel...
Procedure for Decontaminating Areas Exposed to Tritium Gas:

1. Flush the Area with Air: The best method for decontaminating an area exposed to tritium gas is to flush it with air.

2. Protect Personnel from Inhalation Hazards: Personnel entering areas containing tritium gas should wear a self-contained type of breathing apparatus due to the internal hazard posed by inhalation.

3. Dispose or Degass Contaminated Objects:
- Objects exposed to tritium for an extended period may absorb the gas and should be disposed of if possible.
- If disposal is not feasible, objects can be degassed under a vacuum by flushing with helium or hydrogen.

4. Monitor Surface Cleanliness: Surfaces that appear clean may become recontaminated within hours due to percolation.

5. Tritium Oxide (T2O) in Water Cannot Be Removed: There is no practical way to remove tritium oxide from water because of its similarity to natural water.
tritium, decontamination, gas, breathing apparatus, degassing, percolation, T2O, water
Decontamination of Uranium Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decontaminating Areas Exposed to Uranium:

1. Identify Source of Contamination: The most likely source is a nuclear weapon accident where fissionable uranium (uranium-235 or uranium-238) is spread as metal or oxide dust.

2....
Procedure for Decontaminating Areas Exposed to Uranium:

1. Identify Source of Contamination: The most likely source is a nuclear weapon accident where fissionable uranium (uranium-235 or uranium-238) is spread as metal or oxide dust.

2. Remove Insoluble Uranium Deposits:
- Brush or vacuum the contaminated surface to remove the insoluble uranium deposits.
- Treat the area with mineral acids or oxidizing acids after initial removal.
- Seal the treated surface to prevent recontamination.

3. Large-Area Decontamination Methods:
- For large areas, remove the top layer of the contaminated surface.
- Alternatively, seal the entire surface if removal is not feasible.
uranium, decontamination, mineral acids, oxidizing acids, sealing, nuclear weapon accident
Designation and Training of Fixed-Site Decon Teams
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: The commander should designate and train teams that can perform decon for fixed-site operations.

Techniques:
• The following are fixed-site decon techniques that the teams could apply:
- Airfield Decon: See Figure 5-2 and...
Objective: The commander should designate and train teams that can perform decon for fixed-site operations.

Techniques:
• The following are fixed-site decon techniques that the teams could apply:
- Airfield Decon: See Figure 5-2 and Figure 5-3.
- Helipad Decon: See Figure 5-4.
- POMCUS/Motor Park Decon: See Figure 5-5.

Determine Correct DS2-to-Agent Ratios for Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Calculating DS2-to-Agent Ratios:

• For H agents, the required DS2-to-agent ratio is 55 to 1.
• For G agents, the required DS2-to-agent ratio is 25 to 1.

Example Application for an M1A1 Tank:
• For H agents:...
Procedure for Calculating DS2-to-Agent Ratios:

• For H agents, the required DS2-to-agent ratio is 55 to 1.
• For G agents, the required DS2-to-agent ratio is 25 to 1.

Example Application for an M1A1 Tank:
• For H agents: 15 gallons of DS2 are needed.
• For G agents: 7 gallons of DS2 are needed.

Note: It is essential that there be sufficient DS2 on the item being decontaminated to ensure complete neutralization of chemical agents.
DS2 ratio, H agent decon, G agent decon, M1A1 tank decon
Enter Shelter After Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Step 13. Enter the shelter and resume breathing.

## WARNING
When entries are performed in a contaminated environment, monitor every 30 minutes. If detector/monitoring shows positive, all personnel should mask until the source of the...
Step 13. Enter the shelter and resume breathing.

## WARNING
When entries are performed in a contaminated environment, monitor every 30 minutes. If detector/monitoring shows positive, all personnel should mask until the source of the contamination is located and removed and/or further tests indicate the contamination is no
