Entry Instructions for Combat Vehicle and Aircrew IPE
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Step 1: If you wear your vehicle helmet underneath your hood, the first step is to remove the hood (from back to front) from your helmet. Then remove it from around the eye lenses and then from the filter hose. If you wear your hood underneath...
Step 1: If you wear your vehicle helmet underneath your hood, the first step is to remove the hood (from back to front) from your helmet. Then remove it from around the eye lenses and then from the filter hose. If you wear your hood underneath your helmet, remove your helmet first. Then remove the hood from your mask in the manner described.

Step 2: With mask and helmet (if applicable) still on, remove your overgarment jacket and trousers. Use the same basic procedures outlined for troops in the ground-troop ensemble, with one exception. When performing the doffing procedure, bend at your waist to prevent the filter canister and hose from touching you when your overgarment is being removed.

Step 3: Proceed to the air lock or hot line. Remove boots as you step into the air lock.

Step 4: Just before entering the protective enclosure, remove your mask, helmet, add gloves. Seal your mask inside your M1/M1A1 bag and enter the enclosure.

Note: For systems without an air lock, remove your mask, helmet, and gloves only after tests indicate the absence of vapor. Place your mask inside your M1/M1A1 bag and seal the bag.
entry,combat vehicle,aircrew IPE,CVC mask,M25-series,M42,aircraft crewman helmet,M24,M43,undressing area,ventilation
Exit Instructions for Combat Vehicles and Aircrew IPE
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Note: For systems without an air lock, all soldiers don MOPP gear before anyone exits the protective enclosure. After the exit, those remaining reseal and purge the enclosure. When vapor contamination drops below detection levels, the remaining...
Note: For systems without an air lock, all soldiers don MOPP gear before anyone exits the protective enclosure. After the exit, those remaining reseal and purge the enclosure. When vapor contamination drops below detection levels, the remaining soldiers can follow unmasking procedures.

Step 1: Put on clean overgarment, overboots or GVOs, and gloves inside the shelter.

Step 2: Check to ensure the air lock is unoccupied. Stop breathing, and step into the entrance, taking your M1/M1A1 bag with you.

Step 3: Open the M1/M1A1 bag, don mask, and put on gloves. (Note: A bib section is available that will fit between the protective entrance and the frame of any door, and when taped down, seals the entrance from outside contamination. Entry and exit restrictions remain the same.) For guidance on maintenance and parts of the SCPE see TM 3-4240-288-12&P.
exit,combat vehicle,aircrew IPE,MOPP gear,air lock,M1/M1A1 bag,mask,bib section,SCPE,TM 3-4240-288-12&P
Exit Instructions for Ground-Troop Ensemble
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Step 1: Put on clean overgarments, overboots, and gloves inside the shelter.

Step 2: Check the compartment control module (CCM) to ensure the air lock (M14 PE) is unoccupied. Stop breathing, and step into the entrance taking your M1/M1A1...
Step 1: Put on clean overgarments, overboots, and gloves inside the shelter.

Step 2: Check the compartment control module (CCM) to ensure the air lock (M14 PE) is unoccupied. Stop breathing, and step into the entrance taking your M1/M1A1 bag with you.

Step 3: Open the M1/M1A1 bag, remove your mask by the straps with one hand, and make sure the hood is inside out over the front. Place your mask to your chin and face, and pull the head harness over your head. Tighten cheek straps, clear and seal your mask, and resume breathing. Unroll the hood. Pull the hood over your head, attach the straps, and tighten the neck cord.

Step 4: Exit the air lock and ensure the PE door is fully closed after exiting.
exit,ground-troop ensemble,overgarments,air lock,M1/M1A1 bag,mask,doffing procedures
Exit from a Tank During NBC Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Step 1. Traverse the turret until the main gun is centered over the front slope.

Step 2. Put on mask and protective gloves.

Step 3. If you are the loader, perform exit before any crew member begins.

Put on the SCALP or either a...
Step 1. Traverse the turret until the main gun is centered over the front slope.

Step 2. Put on mask and protective gloves.

Step 3. If you are the loader, perform exit before any crew member begins.

Put on the SCALP or either a field-expedient contamination avoidance item or rain gear and boot covers. Carrying your personal decon kit, exit through the loader's hatch.

Step 4. If you are not the loader, but are required to exit, move to the loader's station. Put on the SCALP or a field-expedient contamination avoidance item or rain gear and boot covers. Carry your personal decon kit and exit through the loader's hatch. If you are the last to exit, carry the decon apparatus and close the hatch.

Step 5. If you are the loader, determine if the tank and surrounding area are contaminated.

Note: Follow procedures for detecting the presence of chemical agents. For hasty identification, the loader should use M8/M9 chemical agent detector paper for suspected liquid agents. The tank commander can use detector/monitoring equipment to detect any vapor agents. If the need to exit the tank is urgent, skip this time-consuming step and assume this area is contaminated.

Step 6. If no contamination is present, crew members may reduce their MOPP level and perform step 7. If contamination is present, decontaminate the loader's hatch and an area approximately 4 feet in diameter around it.

Step 7. Perform the task(s) that dictated the exit.
NBC, decontamination, tank exit, SCALP, MOPP level, chemical agents, loader procedure
Fixed-Site Decontamination Overview
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Purpose: Personnel and equipment need to be decontaminated to reduce the hazard and the spread of contamination, limit casualties, and minimize operational degradation. The goal is to restore the unit to an acceptable level of operational...
Purpose: Personnel and equipment need to be decontaminated to reduce the hazard and the spread of contamination, limit casualties, and minimize operational degradation. The goal is to restore the unit to an acceptable level of operational effectiveness.

Criticality: The inability of a fixed site to move away from contamination makes decon more critical. Decon is necessary when protective items cannot be worn in performing the mission or when long-term occupancy is anticipated.

Decon Capabilities:
• Decon capabilities may be designed and built into a facility and used if the facility becomes contaminated.
• Mobile decon equipment may also be available at a fixed site to decon equipment, roads, and buildings.
• Interior decon may be accomplished with large commercial heaters.

Conventional Methods:
• Loading docks, entries and exits, and building exteriors can be decontaminated with more conventional methods such as:
- STB dry mix or STB slurry
- Soap and water
- M13 DAP

Equipment Utilization: Commanders should identify all systems that are capable of contributing to the decon effort, including fire trucks, steam cleaners, and water pumps.
fixed-site,decontamination,nuclear,cbrn,military,procedure
Helipad Area Decontamination Procedure
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: To decon a helipad site.

Steps:
1. Spray the STB slurry on the entrance/exit approach or path (see Figure 5-4).
2. Since airborne STB particles can damage sensitive aircraft components, **the decontaminated area should be washed...
Objective: To decon a helipad site.

Steps:
1. Spray the STB slurry on the entrance/exit approach or path (see Figure 5-4).
2. Since airborne STB particles can damage sensitive aircraft components, the decontaminated area should be washed with water before landing helicopters.

Note: Ensure that all areas are thoroughly cleaned to prevent contamination of aircraft.
helipad,decon,nuclear,cbrn,military,procedure
Immediate Decontamination for Chemical Agents on the Skin
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Perform immediate decontamination (ID) as soon as possible if exposed to a chemical agent on the skin.
2. Use the following techniques:
- Skin decon: Wash contaminated skin with water and soap or use a decontaminating...
Procedure:

1. Perform immediate decontamination (ID) as soon as possible if exposed to a chemical agent on the skin.
2. Use the following techniques:
- Skin decon: Wash contaminated skin with water and soap or use a decontaminating solution.
- Personal wipe down: Wipe contaminated areas with wipes designed for chemical agent removal.
- Operator’s spray down: Spray contaminated areas with an appropriate decontamination agent.

3. Begin this process within 1 minute of exposure to prevent the agent from penetrating further into the skin.
4. This level of decon is performed by the individual or a crew and stops the agent from penetrating.

Note: Immediate decon should be conducted as soon as practical, ideally before 1 minute after contamination occurs.
immediate decon, chemical agent, skin decon, operator spray down
Implement Work/Rest Cycle for DED Operations
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
### Procedure
1. Assess workload and personnel capacity to determine the need for a work/rest cycle.
2. Schedule rest periods based on the duration of decontamination operations and the physical demands of the tasks.
3. Rotate personnel...
### Procedure
1. Assess workload and personnel capacity to determine the need for a work/rest cycle.
2. Schedule rest periods based on the duration of decontamination operations and the physical demands of the tasks.
3. Rotate personnel through different stations (e.g., primary wash, DS2 application, rinse) to prevent fatigue.
4. Monitor heat exposure using temperature sensors or visual signs of heat stress (e.g., sweating, dizziness).
5. Provide hydration and rest areas for personnel during breaks.
6. Adjust the work/rest cycle as needed based on operational tempo and environmental conditions.

### Notes:
• Failure to implement a work/rest cycle could result in heat casualties and mission failure.
• Ensure that all personnel are trained on the importance of rest cycles and how to recognize signs of heat stress.
work rest cycle, ded operations, heat casualties, mission failure
Include Self-Decontamination Instructions in Public Education Campaigns
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Action Item: Include self-decontamination instructions in public education campaigns.

### Individuals Directed to Evacuate by Emergency Response Officials:
• These are individuals leaving the immediate impact zone (e.g., MDZ or LDZ zones) who...
Action Item: Include self-decontamination instructions in public education campaigns.

### Individuals Directed to Evacuate by Emergency Response Officials:
• These are individuals leaving the immediate impact zone (e.g., MDZ or LDZ zones) who may require assistance from responders to evacuate (e.g., search and rescue, emergency medical service).
• Some may be able to evacuate without responder assistance but as part of an organized immediate evacuation.
• These individuals may undergo preliminary screening at ad hoc screening locations.
• Emergency response officials must consult with radiation protection professionals regarding appropriate screening criteria and decontamination recommendations for these individuals that reflect the priorities and resources available.

### Individuals Who Self-Evacuate Without Official Direction:
• This includes individuals who self-evacuate before emergency responders arrive.
• Once responders arrive, there may be insufficient responders to direct everyone, and people may continue to self-evacuate.
• Responders will be unable to provide onscene screening and decontamination assistance before these individuals evacuate and at best may direct them to an ad hoc screening location.
• Ideally, public education campaigns provide self-decontamination instructions to the public prior to emergency incidents.
• Even then, self-decontamination instructions must be provided to the public at ad hoc screening locations or through post-incident public outreach mechanisms.
• Planners should anticipate some of these individuals will be going directly to hospitals or seeking care in public shelters prior to being screened for contamination.
• Appendix 5.2: Strategies for Screening and Decontaminating People discusses special considerations for screening individuals arriving at shelters.
self-decontamination,public education,evacuation,nuclear detonation,screening,decontamination
Individual-Gear Decon at Station 1
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Individual-Gear Decon at Station 1:

1. At this station, contamination is removed from individual gear (LCE, mask carrier, helmet, and weapon) to a negligible risk level.
2. Follow the procedures outlined in Chapter 1 of this...
Procedure for Individual-Gear Decon at Station 1:

1. At this station, contamination is removed from individual gear (LCE, mask carrier, helmet, and weapon) to a negligible risk level.
2. Follow the procedures outlined in Chapter 1 of this manual for thorough decontamination of personal equipment.
individual gear decon,LCE,mask carrier,helmet,weapon,negligible risk level
Monitor for Contamination During Entry
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. When entering a contaminated environment, monitor every 15 minutes.
2. If the detector changes color, or the CAM indicates more than one bar, all soldiers should mask immediately until:
- The **source of...
Procedure:

1. When entering a contaminated environment, monitor every 15 minutes.
2. If the detector changes color, or the CAM indicates more than one bar, all soldiers should mask immediately until:
- The source of contamination is located and removed, or
- Further tests indicate that contamination is no longer present.
3. Note the location of any liquid agent on the entering soldier.
4. Use decontaminants only on areas known to be contaminated.
5. The soldier removes the LCE (Liquid Chemical Protection Ensemble) and unfastens:
- Velcro tabs,
- Tie cords, and
- Trouser-leg zippers.
monitoring, contamination detection, decontamination, LCE removal, NBC protection
NBC Decontamination Overview
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Overview of NBC Decontamination Procedures:

• NBC (Nuclear, Biological, Chemical) decontamination is a critical process to remove or neutralize contaminants from personnel, equipment, and environments.

Purpose:
• To reduce the risk of...
Overview of NBC Decontamination Procedures:

• NBC (Nuclear, Biological, Chemical) decontamination is a critical process to remove or neutralize contaminants from personnel, equipment, and environments.

Purpose:
• To reduce the risk of contamination spread and protect personnel from harm.

Scope:
• Applies to all military personnel and units during training, exercises, and contingency operations.

Key Principles:
• Decontamination must be conducted promptly after exposure.
• Use appropriate decontamination agents based on the type of contaminant (chemical, biological, or radiological).
• Follow established procedures to ensure effectiveness and safety.
• Ensure that all personnel are trained in NBC decontamination techniques.

Responsibilities:
• Commanders must ensure that decontamination is integrated into operational planning.
• Medical personnel must be prepared to treat contaminated individuals.
• Decontamination teams must be trained, equipped, and ready for rapid response.
NBC decontamination, nuclear biological chemical, military procedures, decontamination agents, contamination spread, personnel safety
Operate a Single Air Lock Protective Entrance
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Single air lock protective entrances are commonly used in vans and shelters modified for collective protection (e.g., the M12 protective entrance). This system consists of a clean shelter area and an air lock.

Before entering the air lock from...
Single air lock protective entrances are commonly used in vans and shelters modified for collective protection (e.g., the M12 protective entrance). This system consists of a clean shelter area and an air lock.

Before entering the air lock from a contaminated area, personnel must remove their MOPP gear except gloves and mask. Minor exposure to chemical agent vapor is possible during this process.

Clothing may absorb any chemical agent vapor in the atmosphere during this brief exposure. The amount of agent absorbed depends on:
• Agent concentration in the atmosphere
• Length of exposure
• Type of agent
• Type of clothing exposed
• Climatic conditions

The air purge in the air lock flushes out contaminated air brought in and reduces the amount of absorbed agent on clothing before the soldier enters the protective shelter. After entry, monitoring ensures hazardous levels of agent were not carried inside.
single air lock, M12 entrance, decontamination, chemical vapor exposure
Operate a Two-Stage Air Lock Protective Entrance with Contamination Control Area (CCA)
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
A two-stage air lock is created by adding a contamination control area (CCA) to a single air-lock system. This configuration provides better control of liquid and vapor hazards during entry and exit.

Entering soldiers remove MOPP gear in the...
A two-stage air lock is created by adding a contamination control area (CCA) to a single air-lock system. This configuration provides better control of liquid and vapor hazards during entry and exit.

Entering soldiers remove MOPP gear in the CCA before proceeding into the air lock. This additional step enhances protection against chemical agents and improves overall contamination control.
two-stage air lock, CCA, contamination control area, MOPP gear removal
Operational Decontamination Procedures Using the IEDK
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Operational decon is conducted to sustain combat operations, remove gross contamination, and reduce the transfer/spread of contamination. Before decon, soldiers should visually inspect the weapon system for contamination. If liquid contamination...
Operational decon is conducted to sustain combat operations, remove gross contamination, and reduce the transfer/spread of contamination. Before decon, soldiers should visually inspect the weapon system for contamination. If liquid contamination is present, follow the procedures prescribed for each weapon system. The protection level required for operational-decon operations is MOPP4.

WARNING: Do not apply an IEDK to any optic lens. The abrasive effect of the charcoal inside the IEDK will damage the lens.

NOTE: The United States Marine Corp (USMC) does not possess the IEDK; therefore, it will have to use additional SDKs to decon individual and crew-served weapons.
operational decon, IEDK, MOPP4, decontamination procedures, nuclear cbrn
POMCUS/Motor Park Site Decontamination Procedure
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: At a prepositioning of material configured to unit sets (POMCUS)/motor park site, decon entrance/exit approaches to limit contamination.

Steps:
1. Exposed vehicles in a POMCUS/motor park will become contaminated but most likely...
Objective: At a prepositioning of material configured to unit sets (POMCUS)/motor park site, decon entrance/exit approaches to limit contamination.

Steps:
1. Exposed vehicles in a POMCUS/motor park will become contaminated but most likely there will not be enough personnel to drive the vehicles to a decon station.
2. Decon parked equipment by spraying it with an STB slurry or hot, soapy water (see Figure 5-5).
3. The M12A1 PDDA crew drives around the parked vehicles and performs vehicle washdown to accelerate the weathering process.
pomcus,motor park,decon,nuclear,cbrn,military,procedure
Perform Aircrew Spot Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. After personal wipe down, aviators may conduct a spot decon on their aircraft.
2. The goal is to limit the transfer and spread of contamination while sustaining flight operations.
3. Focus on **essential operating...
Procedure:

1. After personal wipe down, aviators may conduct a spot decon on their aircraft.
2. The goal is to limit the transfer and spread of contamination while sustaining flight operations.
3. Focus on essential operating surfaces on the aircraft that must be touched during operations.
4. Use IEDK or JP81 as decontamination agents.
5. Do not use JP8 inside the aircraft.
6. This procedure applies to the first six functional activities.
7. Do not spray water inside the aircraft during this process.

Notes:
• The aircrew spot decon provides the same benefits as the operator’s spray down.
• Exact procedures and areas to decon are identified in the aircraft’s maintenance manual.
• See Table 7-2, pages 7-4 through 7-6, for additional guidance.
aircrew spot decon, decontamination agents, IEDK, JP81, aircraft decon
Perform Primary Wash at Station 1
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
### Procedure: Perform Primary Wash at Station 1

Overview:
At station 1, the primary wash involves spraying all vehicles and equipment with hot, soapy water to remove any contamination that could have been transferred during the primary...
### Procedure: Perform Primary Wash at Station 1

Overview:
At station 1, the primary wash involves spraying all vehicles and equipment with hot, soapy water to remove any contamination that could have been transferred during the primary wash operations.

### Steps for Primary Wash
1. Spray all vehicles and equipment with hot, soapy water to remove any contamination that may have occurred during the primary wash operations.
2. Drain the water billets or fabric tanks of water after washing.
3. Inspect all equipment and vehicles for contamination using appropriate detection equipment (e.g., M8 detector paper, M256A1 detector kits).

### Equipment Required
• 3 M12A1 PDDAs
• 3 1,500-gallon tanks
• 3 65-GPM pumps
• 6 long-handled brushes
• 8 TAP aprons
• Liquid detergent

### Additional Notes
• Ensure that all personnel at station 1 are trained in the use of detection equipment and decontamination procedures.
• Follow proper safety protocols when handling hot water and soapy solutions.
primary wash, station 1, decontamination procedure, nuclear cbrn
Post-Attack Decontamination Actions
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
After an attack, soldiers must take the following actions:

1. Ensure contaminated items are not stowed in CPE (Collective Protection Equipment).
2. Acquire decon support if required.
3. Resupply expendable materials such as IPE...
After an attack, soldiers must take the following actions:

1. Ensure contaminated items are not stowed in CPE (Collective Protection Equipment).
2. Acquire decon support if required.
3. Resupply expendable materials such as IPE (Individual Protective Equipment), mask and shelter filters, and individual decon kits.
4. Continue entry and exit procedures until one hour after detectors indicate the absence of agent vapors outside the shelter.
5. Resume before-attack actions, but continue periodic monitoring of the shelter interior with detector/monitoring equipment.

The shelter attendant will:
• Pass the all clear signal to shelter occupants when safe to do so.
• Service the filter system if needed.
• Assist entry and exit procedures.
• Continue attendant duties.
post-attack actions, decon procedures, CPE, IPE resupply, entry exit procedures, shelter attendant duties
Prepare Decontamination Station Equipment and Supplies
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
### Preparation of Decontamination Station

To prepare the decontamination station, ensure the following equipment and supplies are available:

1. Containers: Four 30-gallon containers for each two decon lanes used.
2. Brushes: Two...
### Preparation of Decontamination Station

To prepare the decontamination station, ensure the following equipment and supplies are available:

1. Containers: Four 30-gallon containers for each two decon lanes used.
2. Brushes: Two long-handled brushes for each two decon lanes used.
3. STB Slurry Mix: Sufficient STB slurry mix for chemical contamination.
4. Hot, Soapy Water and Sponge: For radiological contamination.
5. Rinse Water: Required for rinsing after decontamination.
6. Ponchos or Plastic Sheets: Two ponchos or plastic sheets.
7. Engineer Tape: Used to divide the poncho or sheet into halves as a contamination control line.

### Digging and Setting Up the Sump

1. Dig a sump that is 6 feet long, 6 feet wide, and 4 feet deep (minimum).
2. Place three 30-gallon containers near the sump for ease of changing.
3. Fill two containers with an STB slurry mix and place them at the sump.
4. Fill the other two containers with clean water for rinsing and position them about 3 feet forward of the STB cans.
5. Place two long-handled scrub brushes at each can of STB slurry.

### Preparing the Slurry Mix

1. Mix 100 pounds of STB with 20 gallons of hot water to prepare the slurry mix. The chemical unit provides the hot water.
2. Change the mixture after 20 soldiers have decontaminated their gear.
3. Change the rinse water after every 10 soldiers or when it appears dirty, placing the waste mixture in the sump.

### Setting Up the Checkpoint

1. Place a poncho or plastic sheet on the ground at the checkpoint and divide it in half using engineer tape to create a contamination control line.
2. The checkpoint should be a minimum of 10 feet from all other stations for accurate detection equipment readings.
3. Place the following chemical detection/identification equipment at the checkpoint:
- 1 CAM (Chemical Agent Monitor)
- 8 books of M8 detector paper
- 4 M256A1 detector kits
- 100 plastic trash bags
- 1 AN/VDR-2 for radiological contamination.

### Personnel Assignment

Three soldiers are required to operate this station:

1. One soldier supervises the decon of individual gear, takes the decontaminated equipment to the checkpoint, and prepares a new slurry mixture as necessary.
2. One soldier remains at the checkpoint and checks all the gear using detection equipment to ensure it is decontaminated.
3. One soldier transports the decontaminated gear to the reissue point.
decontamination, preparation, equipment, supplies, slurry mix, sump, checkpoint, personnel assignment
Prepare to Remove Overgarment Trousers and Overboots Using the Triple-Buddy Method
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Preparing to Remove Overgarment Trousers and Overboots with the Triple-Buddy Method:

1. Unfasten Hood Straps: Buddy #1 unfastens the shoulder straps on Buddy #2's hood, pulls them over his shoulder, and reattaches them to the...
Procedure for Preparing to Remove Overgarment Trousers and Overboots with the Triple-Buddy Method:

1. Unfasten Hood Straps: Buddy #1 unfastens the shoulder straps on Buddy #2's hood, pulls them over his shoulder, and reattaches them to the Velcro fastener.
2. Loosen Hood Drawcord: Loosen the drawcord on Buddy #2's hood and tie off the microphone cord to the hose of his mask.
3. Remove M9 Detector Paper: Remove the M9 detector paper from Buddy #2's overgarment.
4. Unzip Trousers and Roll Cuffs: Unzip Buddy #2's trouser legs and roll a cuff in each trouser leg, ensuring that the cuffs do not come above the top of his overboots.
5. Unfasten or Cut Overboot Fasteners: Unfasten or cut the fasteners on Buddy #2's overboots. NOTE: A soldier can do this step by himself or with the help of his buddy.
overgarment trousers, overboots, triple-buddy method, decontamination
Proceed Through the Shuffle Pit to Clean Treatment Area
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Step 7 - Proceed Through the Shuffle Pit to the Clean Treatment Area

Have the decontaminated patient proceed through the shuffle pit to the clean treatment area. To ensure that the patient's boots are well decontaminated, have him stir the...
Step 7 - Proceed Through the Shuffle Pit to the Clean Treatment Area

Have the decontaminated patient proceed through the shuffle pit to the clean treatment area. To ensure that the patient's boots are well decontaminated, have him stir the contents of the shuffle pit with his boots as he crosses it.

The patient's combat boots and protective mask will be removed at the entrance of the CPS or clean treatment area.
shuffle pit, decon procedures, boots cleaning, clean treatment area, NBC
Properly Remove Hood During Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Step 6. Decontaminate gloves before rolling hood. (Use assistance if possible.) Leave the hood zipper closed. Grasp the hood by the straps and lift the hood off your shoulders and partially off your head until most of the back of your head is...
Step 6. Decontaminate gloves before rolling hood. (Use assistance if possible.) Leave the hood zipper closed. Grasp the hood by the straps and lift the hood off your shoulders and partially off your head until most of the back of your head is exposed.

Roll the hood. Start at your chin, making sure the zipper and neck cord are tucked into the roll, and work around the entire mask until the rolled hood will stay up, off your shoulders. Roll the hood tightly against your mask without pulling the hood off the back of your head.

Note: If your assistant is also entering the shelter, steps 1 through 6 should be performed on him or her before proceeding to step 7.
hood removal, decontamination procedure, nuclear cbrn
Protect Yourself from CBRN Injury/Contamination When Changing MOPP (Using JSLIST)
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Objective: Protect yourself from chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear (CBRN) injury/contamination when changing mission-oriented protective posture (MOPP) gear using the joint-service lightweight integrated suit technology...
Objective: Protect yourself from chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear (CBRN) injury/contamination when changing mission-oriented protective posture (MOPP) gear using the joint-service lightweight integrated suit technology (JSLIST).

Conditions:
• You are in MOPP4 with loadbearing equipment (LBE).
• Your MOPP gear is contaminated.
• Your buddy is in MOPP4 with LBE and is available to assist you with MOPP gear exchange.
• You have an uncontaminated set of MOPP gear for yourself and your buddy, a personal decontamination kit, an M295 individual equipment decontamination kit (IEDK), long-handled scrub brushes, cutting tools, super tropical bleach (STB), a chemical-agent monitor (CAM), 3-gallon pails, sponges, paper towels, soap, and water.
• This task will be performed in MOPP4.

Standards:
• Protect yourself from CBRN injury/contamination when changing MOPP using JSLIST.
• Decontaminate individual gear and equipment without spreading contamination onto your skin or undergarments.
• Set uncontaminated gear aside on an uncontaminated surface.
• Change overgarments, overboots, and gloves without spreading contamination to the uncontaminated set of MOPP gear.
• Change MOPP gear without you or your buddy becoming a casualty.

Note:
• If the environment is chemically or biologically contaminated, omit steps 2 and 4b in the training information outline below.
• If the environment is radiologically contaminated, omit steps 1 and 4a.
• If the environment is radiologically and chemically and/or biologically contaminated, perform all steps.

Procedure:
1. Prepare for gear exchange: Ensure that you are in MOPP4 with LBE and that your buddy is also in MOPP4 with LBE and available to assist you.
2. Set up decontamination area: Use the uncontaminated set of MOPP gear, personal decontamination kit, IEDK, long-handled scrub brushes, cutting tools, STB, CAM, 3-gallon pails, sponges, paper towels, soap, and water to create a decontamination station on an uncontaminated surface.
3. Decontaminate contaminated gear:
- Use the IEDK, long-handled scrub brushes, cutting tools, STB, CAM, 3-gallon pails, sponges, paper towels, soap, and water to decontaminate your contaminated MOPP gear.
- Ensure that you do not spread contamination onto your skin or undergarments during the process.
4. Change overgarments, overboots, and gloves:
a. If the environment is radiologically contaminated, remove your contaminated overgarments, overboots, and gloves carefully to avoid spreading contamination.
b. If the environment is chemically or biologically contaminated, skip this step.
5. Set aside uncontaminated gear: Place the uncontaminated set of MOPP gear on an uncontaminated surface for later use.
6. Ensure safety: Ensure that you and your buddy do not become casualties during the process by following all CBRN safety protocols.
CBRN, decontamination, MOPP gear exchange, JSLIST, personal protective equipment, chemical contamination, biological contamination, radiological contamination, nuclear contamination
Remove All Contamination from Protective Masks at the Mask Decon Point
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Removing Contamination from Protective Masks:

• The mask is decontaminated at the mask decon point (station 7) following established procedures to ensure all contamination is removed.
• This includes using appropriate...
Procedure for Removing Contamination from Protective Masks:

• The mask is decontaminated at the mask decon point (station 7) following established procedures to ensure all contamination is removed.
• This includes using appropriate decontamination solutions and methods as specified in FM 3-5/MCWP 3-37.3.
• Ensure that no residual contamination remains on the mask after the process is complete.
• The mask should be inspected for damage or wear before reuse.
mask decon point,protective masks,decontamination solutions,M8A1/M22 ACAA
Remove CVCUS Overboots Using the Triple-Buddy Method to Limit Spread of Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Removing CVCUS Overboots with the Triple-Buddy Method:

1. Open Zippers on CVCUS: Buddy #1 opens all the zippers on Buddy #2’s CVCUS in the following order: wrist, ankles, and front.
2. Stand Behind Buddy #2: While standing...
Procedure for Removing CVCUS Overboots with the Triple-Buddy Method:

1. Open Zippers on CVCUS: Buddy #1 opens all the zippers on Buddy #2’s CVCUS in the following order: wrist, ankles, and front.
2. Stand Behind Buddy #2: While standing behind Buddy #2 and adjacent to the clean area, Buddy #1 grasps the shoulders of Buddy #2’s CVCUS and instructs him to make a fist to ensure that his gloves do not come off.
3. Pull Down CVCUS Below Knees: Pull Buddy #2's CVCUS down below his knees, turning it inside out.
4. Partially Remove Overboots: Instruct Buddy #2 to partially remove his overboots by alternately stepping on each heel and pulling up on his foot.
5. Kneel and Grasp Leg and Overboot: While kneeling, Buddy #1 grasps one leg of Buddy #2’s CVCUS and his overboot and instructs him to remove his leg and foot simultaneously.
6. Step Onto Clean Area: As Buddy #2 removes each leg and foot, he steps directly onto the clean area.
7. Decontaminate Gloves Again: Buddy #1 decontaminates his gloves again.
CVCUS overboots removal, triple-buddy method, contamination control
Remove Contaminated Overboots and Gloves to Limit Spread of Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Removing Contaminated Overboots and Gloves:

1. Engineer tape is used to mark the liquid-contamination control line.
2. The attendant unfastens or cuts the elastic closures on the soldier’s overboots.
3. The soldier stands next...
Procedure for Removing Contaminated Overboots and Gloves:

1. Engineer tape is used to mark the liquid-contamination control line.
2. The attendant unfastens or cuts the elastic closures on the soldier’s overboots.
3. The soldier stands next to and faces the liquid-contamination control line.
4. The soldier steps back about 12 inches from the liquid-contamination control line.
5. The attendant steps on the back of the soldier’s overboot and instructs him to lift his heel and work his foot out of the overboot and step across the liquid-contamination control line. Repeat the process on the other foot.
6. The attendant discards the overboots.
7. The soldier holds the fingertips of his gloves and partially slides his hands out.
8. The attendant removes the soldier’s gloves.
9. The attendants perform the following steps:
- Put the gloves and overboots into the containers on the "dirty" side of the control line.
- Submerge the gloves and overboots in the container of hot, soapy water, ensuring that no water remains inside when removed from the container.
- Submerge the gloves and overboots in the STB/HTH solution and thoroughly scrub them.
- Rinse the scrubbed items and check for holes, tears, or punctures. If found, discard the items.
- Place the usable items on a poncho or a plastic tarp to air dry.
- Place the air-dried usable items into plastic trash bags along with an M256A1 detector kit. If the kit shows contamination remaining, the attendants can recycle the items or discard them. However, if the kit shows no contamination, they can be reused.
overboots,gloves,decontamination,liquid-contamination control line,STB/HTH solution,M256A1 detector kit
Remove Gross Contamination From the Patient's Protective Overgarment
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Removing Gross Contamination from the Protective Overgarment:

1. Identify visible contamination spots: Locate all areas of the overgarment that are visibly contaminated.
2. Prepare decon solution: Use a 0.5 percent...
Procedure for Removing Gross Contamination from the Protective Overgarment:

1. Identify visible contamination spots: Locate all areas of the overgarment that are visibly contaminated.
2. Prepare decon solution: Use a 0.5 percent chlorine solution.
3. Remove contamination: Apply the solution to remove all visible contamination from the overgarment.
overgarment,decontaminate,nuclear,cbrn,chemical,biological
Remove Jacket During NBC Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Soldier 1 turns and faces soldier 2.
2. Soldier 2 unsnaps the jacket, top to bottom, and then unzips it.
3. Soldier 1 turns his back to soldier 2, and soldier 2 carefully removes the jacket.
jacket removal, NBC decon, two-person procedure
Remove Outer Gloves and Cotton Glove Liners
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Step 1: Remove outer gloves

• Grasp the fingers of the gloves, roll the cuffs over the fingers, and turn the gloves inside out.
• Do not remove the inner cotton gloves at this time.
• Drop the gloves into the contaminated-waste bag.
• Do not...
Step 1: Remove outer gloves

• Grasp the fingers of the gloves, roll the cuffs over the fingers, and turn the gloves inside out.
• Do not remove the inner cotton gloves at this time.
• Drop the gloves into the contaminated-waste bag.
• Do not allow the patient to touch his clothing or other contaminated objects with his hands.

Step 2: Remove cotton glove liners

• Instruct the patient to remove his cotton glove liners to reduce the possibility of spreading contamination.
• Have the patient grasp the heel of one glove liner with the other gloved hand, peeling it off of his hand.
• Hold the removed glove by the inside and grasp the heel of the other glove, peeling it off of his hand.
• Place both gloves in the contaminated-waste bag.
glove removal, cotton glove liners, decontamination procedure, contaminated-waste bag
Remove Overboots
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Cut the overboot laces and fold the lacing eyelets flat outward.
2. While standing at the foot of the litter, hold the patient’s heel with one hand.
3. Pull the overboot downward, then toward you to remove it.
4. Remove the two...
Procedure:

1. Cut the overboot laces and fold the lacing eyelets flat outward.
2. While standing at the foot of the litter, hold the patient’s heel with one hand.
3. Pull the overboot downward, then toward you to remove it.
4. Remove the two overboots simultaneously to reduce the likelihood of contaminating one of the combat boots.

Notes:
• This step is part of the decontamination process for NBC exposure.
• Ensure that all steps are performed carefully to avoid causing additional harm or discomfort to the patient.
overboot removal, contaminated footwear handling, NBC decon, protective gear removal
Remove Overboots and Start Purge Cycle During NBC Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Carrying an M258A1 or M291 decon kit and his M1/M1A1 waterproof bag, soldier 1 checks to be sure the air lock is unoccupied.
2. He opens the entrance door and prepares to remove overboots.
3. Soldier 1 stands before the...
Procedure:

1. Carrying an M258A1 or M291 decon kit and his M1/M1A1 waterproof bag, soldier 1 checks to be sure the air lock is unoccupied.
2. He opens the entrance door and prepares to remove overboots.
3. Soldier 1 stands before the entrance, removes the second overboot, and steps into the entrance with his exposed boot.
4. He carefully places the overboots away from the entry path, closes the door, and starts the purge cycle.
overboot removal, purge cycle, NBC decon
Remove Patient's BDU and Undergarments
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Step 4 - Remove the Patient's BDU and Undergarments

Cut and remove the BDU. To cut and remove the BDU jacket and trousers, follow the procedures (described in paragraphs 8-78 and 8-79) for removing the protective overgarment.

**Remove the...
Step 4 - Remove the Patient's BDU and Undergarments

Cut and remove the BDU. To cut and remove the BDU jacket and trousers, follow the procedures (described in paragraphs 8-78 and 8-79) for removing the protective overgarment.

Remove the combat boots. Cut the bootlaces along the tongue. Remove the boots by pulling them toward you. Place the boots in the contaminated-waste bag. Do not touch the patient's skin with your contaminated gloves when removing his boots.

Cut and remove the undergarments. Follow the procedures for cutting away the protective overgarment and rolling it away from the patient (see paragraphs 8-78 and 8-79). If the patient is wearing a brassiere, cut it between the cups. Cut both shoulder straps where they attach to the cups and lay them back off of the shoulders. Remove the socks and cotton gloves. Do not remove the identification tags.
BDU removal,undergarments removal,combat boots,cut clothing,contaminated-waste bag
Remove Patient’s Personal Effects and Protective Overgarment
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure to Remove Patient’s Personal Effects and Protective Overgarment

1. Remove personal effects:
- Remove the patient's personal effects from his protective overgarment and BDU pockets.
- Place the articles in a plastic bag,...
Procedure to Remove Patient’s Personal Effects and Protective Overgarment

1. Remove personal effects:
- Remove the patient's personal effects from his protective overgarment and BDU pockets.
- Place the articles in a plastic bag, label the bag with the patient's identification, and seal the bag.
- If the articles are not contaminated, return them to the patient.
- If the articles are contaminated, place them in the contaminated holding area until they can be decontaminated, then return them to the patient.

2. Remove overgarment jacket:
- Have the patient stand with his feet spread apart at shoulder width.
- Unsnap the front flap of the jacket and unzip the jacket.
- If the patient can extend his arms:
- Instruct him to clinch his fist and extend his arms backward at about a 30° angle.
- Move behind the patient, grasp his jacket collar at the sides of the neck, and peel the jacket off the shoulders at a 30° angle down and away from the patient.
- Gently pull the inside sleeves over the patient's wrists and hands.
- If the patient cannot extend his arms:
- Cut the jacket to aid in its removal.
- Before cutting, dip the scissors in a 5 percent chlorine solution to prevent contamination of the patient's BDU or undergarment.
- Make two cuts: one up each sleeve from the wrist up to the shoulder and then across the shoulder through the collar.
- Cut around bandages, tourniquets, and splints, leaving them in place.
- Do not allow your gloves to touch the patient along the cut line.
- Peel the jacket back and downward to avoid spreading contamination.
- Ensure that the outside of the jacket does not touch the patient or his inner clothing.

3. Cut and remove overgarment trousers:
- Unfasten or cut all ties, buttons, or zippers before grasping the trousers at the waist and peeling them down over the patient's combat boots.
- If necessary, cut both trouser legs starting at the ankle.
- Keep the cuts near the inside of the legs, along the inseam, to the crotch.
- Cut around all bandages, tourniquets, and splints.
- Continue to cut up both sides of the zipper to the waist and allow the narrow strip with the zipper to drop between the legs.
- Peel or allow the trouser halves to drop to the ground.
- Have the patient step out of the trouser legs one at a time.
- Place the trousers in the contaminated-waste bag.
- Place the scissors in a 5 percent chlorine solution.

4. Remove outer gloves:
- Grasp the fingers of the gloves, roll the cuffs over the fingers, and turn the gloves inside out.
- Do not remove the inner cotton gloves at this time.
- Drop the gloves into the contaminated-waste bag.
- Do not allow the patient to touch his clothing or other contaminated objects with his hands.

5. Remove overboots:
- Cut the overboot laces and fold the lacing eyelets flat on the ground.
- Step on the toe and heel eyelets to hold the overboot on the ground and have the patient step out of it.
- Repeat this procedure for the other overboot.
- If the overboots are in good condition, they can be decontaminated and reissued.

6. Remove cotton glove liners:
- Instruct the patient to remove his cotton glove liners to reduce the possibility of spreading contamination.
- Have the patient grasp the heel of one glove liner with the other gloved hand, peeling it off of his hand.
- Hold the removed glove by the inside and grasp the heel of the other glove, peeling it off of his hand.
- Place both gloves in the contaminated-waste bag.
remove personal effects, remove overgarment jacket, cut trousers, remove gloves, decon procedures, contaminated waste
Remove Trouser During NBC Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Soldier 2 unfastens the snap and tie cord on the waistband and opens the trouser front zipper on soldier 1.
2. He slowly rolls trousers down to the hips with one roll. (Soldier 1 can do these latter two steps if necessary).
3....
Procedure:

1. Soldier 2 unfastens the snap and tie cord on the waistband and opens the trouser front zipper on soldier 1.
2. He slowly rolls trousers down to the hips with one roll. (Soldier 1 can do these latter two steps if necessary).
3. Leaning forward, soldier 1 places both hands against the van or shelter while soldier 2 pulls trouser legs off one at a time.
4. Trouser legs turn inside out as they come off. Overboots remain on. This prevents transfer of contaminated items.
5. Soldier 1 then helps remove soldier 2's trousers.
6. Soldier 1 loosens his own overboots.
trouser removal, NBC decon, two-person procedure
Remove and Decontaminate the FMC (Filter Mask Cover)
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Removing and Decontaminating the FMC:

1. Cut the FMC tie wire: Use scissors that have been dipped in a 5 percent chlorine solution to cut the patient's FMC tie wire, allowing the FMC to fall into a plastic bag.
2. **Seal the...
Procedure for Removing and Decontaminating the FMC:

1. Cut the FMC tie wire: Use scissors that have been dipped in a 5 percent chlorine solution to cut the patient's FMC tie wire, allowing the FMC to fall into a plastic bag.
2. Seal the bag: Seal the plastic bag containing the FMC.
3. Rinse the outside of the bag: Rinse the outside of the sealed plastic bag with a 5 percent chlorine solution.
4. Place the bag under the mask: Place the plastic bag under the back of the protective mask’s head straps.
5. Keep the FMC with the patient: The FMC will remain with the patient in the contaminated area until a clean copy is made before moving to the clean area.
FMC,filter mask cover,plastic bag,nuclear,cbrn,chemical,biological
Remove and Store Mask and Hood Assembly
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Still wearing the loosened gloves, soldier 1 bends at the waist with legs apart.
2. He grasps the back of the rolled hood and lifts it forward over his head.
3. He grasps the mask at the outlet-valve covers and pulls it down.
4....
Procedure:

1. Still wearing the loosened gloves, soldier 1 bends at the waist with legs apart.
2. He grasps the back of the rolled hood and lifts it forward over his head.
3. He grasps the mask at the outlet-valve covers and pulls it down.
4. He bows his head to slip his face out of the mask.
5. Still holding the mask so filters face the floor, he lifts mask and hood off his head.
6. The soldier places the mask and hood assembly into his M1/M1A1 bag and seals the bag tightly.
7. He loosens and drops his gloves to the floor.
8. Then he enters the shelter, carrying his M1/M1A1 bag.
mask hood removal, storage, M1/M1A1 bag
Remove the FMC (Filtering Facepiece Mask)
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Removal of the FMC

1. Cut the patient's FMC tie wire, allowing the FMC to fall into a plastic bag.

2. Seal the plastic bag and rinse the outside of the bag with soap and water. This prevents further contamination...
Procedure for Removal of the FMC

1. Cut the patient's FMC tie wire, allowing the FMC to fall into a plastic bag.

2. Seal the plastic bag and rinse the outside of the bag with soap and water. This prevents further contamination spread.

3. Place the plastic bag under the back of the protective mask’s head straps. The FMC will remain with the patient in the contaminated area, and a clean copy will be made before the patient is moved to the clean area.
fmc removal, decon, nuclear cbrn
Remove the Mask Without Contaminating the Soldier
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Removing the Mask Safely:

1. The attendant pulls the hood over the front of the mask, grabs the mask by the voicemitter cover, and pulls the mask off the soldier while he holds his breath.
2. The attendant holds the mask open so...
Procedure for Removing the Mask Safely:

1. The attendant pulls the hood over the front of the mask, grabs the mask by the voicemitter cover, and pulls the mask off the soldier while he holds his breath.
2. The attendant holds the mask open so that the soldier can remove the inserts without touching the outside of the mask if the mask has optical inserts.
3. The soldier walks upwind 16 feet, crosses the vapor-contamination control line, and then resumes breathing.
4. The attendant brings the mask to station 7.
Note: No vapor hazard is expected beyond the vapor-hazard contamination control line.
5. The chemical unit positions the M8A1 or M22 ACAA upwind of the site.
6. The soldier moves straight ahead while his mask, which may still be emitting vapors, is held on the "dirty" side of the vapor-contamination control line and taken to the next station.
mask removal,vapor-contamination control line,M8A1/M22 ACAA,optical inserts
Rinse Aircraft After Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
At this station, the decon solution is removed from the aircraft. Spray the aircraft with water from top to bottom. Take care not to damage the aircraft’s skin. This station will use about 250 gallons of water.

Failure to remove all decon...
At this station, the decon solution is removed from the aircraft. Spray the aircraft with water from top to bottom. Take care not to damage the aircraft’s skin. This station will use about 250 gallons of water.

Failure to remove all decon solution from the aircraft’s skin may cause corrosion.
rinse station, decon solution removal, aircraft rinsing, corrosion prevention
Rinse at Station 4
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
### Procedure: Rinse at Station 4

Overview:
At station 4, the rinse process is performed to remove any residual DS2 and contaminants from vehicles and equipment after decontamination.

### Steps for Rinse
1. Use appropriate rinsing...
### Procedure: Rinse at Station 4

Overview:
At station 4, the rinse process is performed to remove any residual DS2 and contaminants from vehicles and equipment after decontamination.

### Steps for Rinse
1. Use appropriate rinsing equipment (e.g., M17 LDSs) to thoroughly rinse all vehicles and equipment that have undergone decontamination at previous stations.
2. Ensure that the rinse water is properly drained or disposed of in accordance with safety protocols.

### Equipment Required
• 3 M17 LDSs
• 3 3,000-gallon tanks
• 3 65-GPM pumps
• 2 TPUs (Tactical Personnel Units)
• 4 TAP aprons

### Additional Notes
• Ensure that all personnel at station 4 are trained in the use of rinsing equipment and proper disposal procedures.
• Follow safety protocols when handling rinse water and contaminated materials.
rinse, station 4, decontamination procedure, nuclear cbrn

High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Risk

• If this step is not done properly, the agent may be transferred to the undergarment or the skin.
contamination risk,nuclear biological chemical decon,MOPP gear exchange
Set Up Alternate DED Layout for M12A1 PDDA-Equipped Unit
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
### Personnel and Equipment Requirements

| Stations | Personnel | Equipment/Supplies |
|---|---|---|
| Station 1 - Primary Wash | 1 NCOIC*, 2 PDDE operators*, 4 sprayers, 2 scrubbers | 2 M12A1 PDDAs, 2 3,000-gallon tanks, 2 65-GPM pumps, 4...
### Personnel and Equipment Requirements

| Stations | Personnel | Equipment/Supplies |
|---|---|---|
| Station 1 - Primary Wash | 1 NCOIC*, 2 PDDE operators*, 4 sprayers, 2 scrubbers | 2 M12A1 PDDAs, 2 3,000-gallon tanks, 2 65-GPM pumps, 4 long-handled brushes, 6 TAP aprons, Liquid detergent |
| Station 2 - DS2 Application | 1 NCOIC*, 8 appliers | 14 long-handled brushes, 8 mops with extra mop heads, 3 30-gallon containers, 8 M13 DAPs, Sufficient DS2 |
| Station 3 - Contact Time/Interior Decon | 1 NCO*, 2 interior decon assistants | 2 AN/VDR-2, 3 TAP aprons, 6 30-gallon containers, 10 books of M8 detector paper, 30 sponges, 8 M256A1 detector kits, 50 trash bags, 1 clipboard with a pen, 1 stopwatch |
| Station 4 - Rinse | 1 NCOIC*, 1 PDDE operator*, 2 sprayers | 1 M12A1 PDDA, 1 3,000-gallon tank, 3 65-GPM pumps, 2 TPUs, 2 TAP aprons |
| Station 5 - Check | 2 NCO/CAM operators* | 2 CAMs, 10 M256A1 detector kits, 20 books of M8 detector paper, 2 AN/VDR-2, 2 M8A1 or M22 ACAAs |
| C2 | 1 PL*, 1 PSG* | 1 HMMWV with a radio, 3 NBC marking kits |

### Total Personnel
• Minimum of 11 soldiers from the decon platoon (Total: 29)

### Notes:
• Ensure all personnel and equipment are available as listed.
• Coordinate with the supported unit to ensure that all contaminated elements have been processed before closing the site.
alternate ded layout, m12a1 pdda, personnel requirements, equipment supplies, decon platoon

### DTD Personnel and Equipment Recapitulation

The following table outlines the personnel and equipment required for each station in a thorough decontamination process:

|Stations|Personnel|Equipment/Supplies|
|---|---|---|
|Station 1 - individual-gear decon|2 attendants<br>1 monitor (CAM operator)|4 30-gallon containers<br>2 long-handled brushes<br>2 ponchos or plastic sheets<br>1 CAM<br>8 books of M8 detector paper<br>4 M256A1 detector kits<br>100 plastic trash bags<br>Sufficient STB slurry mix<br>Rinse water<br>*Hot, soapy water<br>*AN/VDR-2<br>*Sponge|
|Station 2 - overboots and hood decon|1 attendant|Cutting tool<br>One SKD or IEDK per person<br>Large plastic sheet<br>Plastic trash bags (as required)<br>10 drums of STB<br>1 shovel|
|Station 3 - overgarment removal|1 attendant|2 30-gallon containers<br>100 plastic trash bags<br>10 boxes of SDKs|
|Station 4 - overboot and glove removal|3 attendants|8 30-gallon containers<br>2 long-handled brushes<br>M8 detector paper (as required)<br>1 immersion heater<br>100 plastic trash bags<br>1 CAM<br>Engineer tape<br>1 cutting tool<br>2 ponchos or large plastic sheets<br>10 percent STB/HTH solution<br>Hot, soapy water<br>Cold rinse water|
|Station 5 - monitor|1 attendant (CAM operator)<br>1 aidman (or combat lifesaver)|1 CAM<br>First aid supplies<br>5 books of M8 detector papers<br>1 case of SDKs|
|Station 6 - mask removal|2 attendants|1 M8A1 or M22 ACAA<br>Engineer tape|
|Station 7 - mask decon point|2 attendants<br>1 monitor|1 30-gallon container<br>4 3-gallon containers<br>1 CAM<br>2 sponges<br>One case of paper towels<br>1 immersion heater<br>Mask sanitizing solution<br>Hot, soapy water<br>Rinse water|
|Station 8 - reissue point|Unit NBC NCO/supply sergeant|Mask PLL|

• Denotes the equipment needed for radiological contamination.
NOTE: Assume that an operational decon was done before the thorough decon.

Setting Up DED and DTD Areas
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Setting Up DED and DTD Areas:

1. Coordinate with the chemical unit leader operating the decon site.
2. Begin sending contaminated vehicles by priority for processing.
3. Maintain communication between the predecon staging area and...
Procedure for Setting Up DED and DTD Areas:

1. Coordinate with the chemical unit leader operating the decon site.
2. Begin sending contaminated vehicles by priority for processing.
3. Maintain communication between the predecon staging area and the chemical unit leaders.
4. Ensure that all assistant vehicle drivers are the first individuals sent through the DTD to facilitate a driver exchange at station 3 (see paragraph 4-94).
DED,DTD,decontamination,chemical unit leader,vehicle processing,driver exchange

+ 64 more procedures. Get the full library in the app.

Radiation Protection (151) -

Constructing a Radiation Dosimeter Using KFM and Aluminum Foil Leaves
Critical Source: Nuclear War Survival Skills Cresson Kearny
To construct a radiation dosimeter using a Kodak Film Monitor (KFM) and aluminum foil leaves, follow these steps:

1. Prepare the Can:
- Use a cylindrical can with a diameter of approximately 2 inches.
- Ensure that the can has a lip at...
To construct a radiation dosimeter using a Kodak Film Monitor (KFM) and aluminum foil leaves, follow these steps:

1. Prepare the Can:
- Use a cylindrical can with a diameter of approximately 2 inches.
- Ensure that the can has a lip at the top and bottom for threading purposes.

2. Insert Aluminum Foil Leaves:
- Cut aluminum foil into thin, flat leaves (approximately 1/8 inch thick).
- Place these leaves inside the can, ensuring they are evenly spaced and not overlapping.

3. Secure the Threads:
- Fasten threads at the top of the can to hold the aluminum foil leaves in place.
- Ensure that the threads are tight enough to prevent any movement of the foil leaves during exposure.

4. Insert KFM Film:
- Insert a Kodak Film Monitor (KFM) into the can, ensuring it is positioned between the aluminum foil leaves and the bottom of the can.
- The KFM should be in direct contact with the aluminum foil leaves to capture radiation exposure accurately.

5. Seal the Can:
- Seal the top of the can using a tight-fitting lid or additional threads, ensuring that no light or air can enter the can.
- Make sure the can is completely sealed to protect the KFM and foil leaves from external contamination.

6. Mark the Can for Reference:
- Label the can with the date of construction and any relevant information (e.g., location, purpose).
- This will help in identifying the dosimeter later during readings.

7. Calibration and Use:
- Before use, calibrate the dosimeter by exposing it to a known radiation source or using a reference standard.
- After exposure, remove the can from its location and take readings using the KFM film.
- Compare the readings before and after exposure to determine the dose rate in R/HR (Rads per Hour).

8. Interpretation of Results:
- Use the provided table to interpret the KFM readings:
- For a 2 mm hole, the corresponding values are: 6.2, 1.6, 0.4, 0.1, and 0.03.
- For a 4 mm hole, the corresponding values are: 12., 3.1, 0.8, 0.2, and 0.06.
- For a 6 mm hole, the corresponding values are: 19., 4.6, 1.2, 0.3, and 0.08.
- For an 8 mm hole, the corresponding values are: 25., 6.2, 1.6, 0.4, and 0.10.
- For a 10 mm hole, the corresponding values are: 31., 7.7, 2.0, 0.5, and 0.13.
- For a 12 mm hole, the corresponding values are: 37., 9.2, 2.3, 0.6, and 0.15.
- For a 14 mm hole, the corresponding values are: 43., 11., 2.7, 0.7, and 0.18.
- These values correspond to different time intervals (15 seconds, 1 minute, 4 minutes, 16 minutes, and 1 hour) for exposure.

9. Safety Precautions:
- Handle the KFM film with care to avoid contamination or damage.
- Ensure that all components are properly sealed to prevent exposure of the dosimeter to external radiation sources.
- Store the dosimeter in a safe location when not in use to protect it from physical damage or environmental factors.
KFM, radiation dosimeter, aluminum foil leaves, nuclear war survival, radiation protection
Define and Respond to RDD (Radiation Dispersal Device)
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
An RDD is a Radiation Dispersal Device, commonly referred to as a 'dirty bomb,' which spreads radioactive material through conventional explosives.

### Response Steps:
1. Evacuate the area immediately if an RDD is suspected or confirmed.
2....
An RDD is a Radiation Dispersal Device, commonly referred to as a 'dirty bomb,' which spreads radioactive material through conventional explosives.

### Response Steps:
1. Evacuate the area immediately if an RDD is suspected or confirmed.
2. Alert authorities and emergency services (e.g., police, fire department, hazmat teams).
3. Avoid contact with any debris or materials that may be contaminated.
4. Follow decontamination procedures once it is safe to do so, as directed by radiation safety officers.
rdd,dirty bomb,radiation dispersal device,hazmat response
Emergency Worker Safety Strategies for Radiation Exposure
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Ensure emergency workers minimize radiation exposure while performing critical duties.

Procedure:
1. Use of Equipment and Knowledge: Responders must have appropriate knowledge and equipment to perform their duties safely.
2....
Objective: Ensure emergency workers minimize radiation exposure while performing critical duties.

Procedure:
1. Use of Equipment and Knowledge: Responders must have appropriate knowledge and equipment to perform their duties safely.
2. Monitor and Report Conditions: Use radiation detection instruments to monitor local radiological conditions and report findings promptly.
3. Follow ALARA Principle: Emergency workers should keep individual radiation exposures as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA) without hindering their ability to save and sustain life.
4. Refer to Additional Resources: For detailed strategies on emergency worker safety, including radiation detection and monitoring, refer to:
- Chapter 2, Section 2.2: Emergency Worker Safety
- Appendix 2.1: Alternative Techniques to Determine Dose
emergency worker safety, ALARA principle, radiation monitoring, nuclear detonation response
Follow Responder Dose Guidelines for Radiation Exposure
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Title: Follow Responder Dose Guidelines for Radiation Exposure

Procedure:
1. Adhere to the following responder dose guidelines:
- 50 mSv (5 rem): All occupational exposures.
- Apply when all reasonably achievable actions have...
Title: Follow Responder Dose Guidelines for Radiation Exposure

Procedure:
1. Adhere to the following responder dose guidelines:
- 50 mSv (5 rem): All occupational exposures.
- Apply when all reasonably achievable actions have been taken to minimize dose.
- 100 mSv (10 rem): Protecting valuable property necessary for public welfare.
- Exceeding 50 mSv is unavoidable, but all appropriate actions are taken to reduce dose.
- Monitoring must be available to project or measure dose.
- 250 mSv (25 rem): Lifesaving or protection of large populations.
- Exceeding 50 mSv is unavoidable, but all appropriate actions are taken to reduce dose.
- Monitoring must be available to project or measure dose.
- >250 mSv (>25 rem): Lifesaving or protection of large populations.
- Apply only when all conditions above are met and for people fully aware of the risks involved.
- 500 mGy (50 rad): Lifesaving or protection of large populations.
- NCRP recommends that a decision be made to withdraw an emergency responder from the Hazard Zone (HZ) when cumulative absorbed dose reaches 0.5 Gy (50 rad).
- This is considered a decision dose, not a dose limit.
2. For potential doses >50 mSv (>5 rem), consider medical monitoring programs.
3. In the case of very large incidents, such as an improvised nuclear device (IND), incident commanders may need to raise property and lifesaving response worker guidelines to prevent further loss of life and massive spread of destruction.
responder dose guidelines,radiation exposure,nuclear detonation,decision dose
Identify Fallout and Its Characteristics
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Fallout is dust, dirt, or particles from an explosion that have radiation molecules on or in them.
fallout,radiation,nuclear disaster
Implement an Emergency Worker Safety Program
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Integrate emergency worker safety into overall operational planning.
- Review all operational tasks.
- Analyze potential hazards to workers.
- Establish necessary protections for emergency responders.
2. **Apply the...
Procedure:

1. Integrate emergency worker safety into overall operational planning.
- Review all operational tasks.
- Analyze potential hazards to workers.
- Establish necessary protections for emergency responders.
2. Apply the ALARA principle (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).
- Make reasonable and practical efforts to keep radiation exposure below levels that cause acute health effects.
- Reduce the risk of stochastic effects, such as cancer later in life.
- Balance radiation protection with maximizing lifesaving operations and protecting critical infrastructure.
3. Adopt dose guidelines for emergency exposure situations:
- These are not limits but conditions where higher doses may be justified for lifesaving actions or preventing catastrophic events.
4. Use the following responder dose guidelines (Table 4):
- 50 mSv (5 rem): All occupational exposures; all reasonably achievable actions have been taken to minimize dose.
- 100 mSv (10 rem): Protecting valuable property necessary for public welfare; exceeding 50 mSv is unavoidable, but monitoring is available.
- 250 mSv (25 rem): Lifesaving or protection of large populations; monitoring is available to project or measure dose.
- >250 mSv (>25 rem): Lifesaving or protection of large populations; only for people fully aware of the risks involved.
- 500 mGy (50 rad): NCRP recommends a decision be made to withdraw an emergency responder from the hazardous zone when cumulative absorbed dose reaches 0.5 Gy (50 rad). This is considered a *decision dose*, not a limit.
5. Medical monitoring programs should be considered for potential doses >50 mSv (>5 rem).
6. In very large incidents, such as an improvised nuclear device (IND), incident commanders may need to raise property and lifesaving response worker guidelines.
emergency worker safety, ALARA principle, radiation exposure limits, responder dose guidelines, nuclear detonation
Initial Radiation Zone Response Protocol
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Minimize radiation exposure during the initial phase of a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Issue Immediate Instructions: Provide the following instruction to everyone within 50 miles of the detonation site, unless specific...
Objective: Minimize radiation exposure during the initial phase of a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Issue Immediate Instructions: Provide the following instruction to everyone within 50 miles of the detonation site, unless specific fallout hazard areas have been identified:
- Get Inside, Stay Inside, Stay Tuned.
2. Monitor Radiation Levels: Understand that radiation levels change rapidly over time and that fallout accumulates downwind before decaying.
3. Assess Fallout Hazards: Recognize that fallout emits over half of its energy in the first hour, after which radiation levels drop significantly.
4. Adjust Response Based on Timeframe:
- For the first few hours, responders should remain near adequate shelters and closely monitor radiation levels.
- If radiation levels increase rapidly during this period, shelter immediately.
5. Stay Informed: Continuously monitor updates from emergency authorities to adjust response actions as needed.
radiation zones, nuclear detonation, fallout hazard areas, get inside stay inside, radiation exposure
Initial Response to a Nuclear Detonation within 50 Miles
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Minimize exposure to radiation from a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Immediately follow the instruction: 'Get Inside, Stay Inside, Stay Tuned.'
2. Do not leave shelter unless advised by official sources.
3. **Monitor...
Objective: Minimize exposure to radiation from a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Immediately follow the instruction: 'Get Inside, Stay Inside, Stay Tuned.'
2. Do not leave shelter unless advised by official sources.
3. Monitor emergency broadcasts for updates on fallout zones and safety instructions.
4. If you are within 50 miles of a detonation site and no specific fallout areas have been identified, remain indoors until further notice.
nuclear detonation, radiation zones, fallout, sheltering, emergency response
Minimize Radiation Exposure in Fallout Areas
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
To reduce the risk of acute and long-term health effects from radiation exposure, follow these steps:

### Key Actions:
1. Seek shelter immediately after a nuclear detonation to protect against initial blast effects.
2. **Remain safely...
To reduce the risk of acute and long-term health effects from radiation exposure, follow these steps:

### Key Actions:
1. Seek shelter immediately after a nuclear detonation to protect against initial blast effects.
2. Remain safely sheltered for at least 24 hours following fallout deposition to minimize exposure.
3. Find suitable shelter if caught in an area during fallout deposition.
4. Perform dry decontamination:
- Brush off any fallout particles from clothing and skin.
- Remove or change outer layers of clothing and footwear.
- Wipe exposed skin to remove contamination.
5. Avoid leaving contaminated areas without proper decontamination, as particles in contact with the skin can cause localized beta burns.

### Additional Information:
• For more details on radiation dose management and protective actions, refer to Chapters 2 and 3.
• For information on external decontamination procedures, see Chapter 5.
radiation exposure, fallout areas, decontamination, sheltering, beta burns
Responder Actions Based on Radiation Detection Equipment Availability
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Ensure responders minimize radiation exposure based on the availability of detection equipment.

Procedure:
1. Responders Without Radiation Detection Instruments:
- Shelter until informed it is safe to respond.
2....
Objective: Ensure responders minimize radiation exposure based on the availability of detection equipment.

Procedure:
1. Responders Without Radiation Detection Instruments:
- Shelter until informed it is safe to respond.
2. Responders With Radiation Detection Instruments:
- Shelter and use radiation detection equipment to monitor and report local radiological conditions.
3. Outdoor Radiation Levels Above 10 R/hr:
- Continue sheltering unless there is a time-critical, life-safety issue (e.g., fire, building collapse, or medical emergency).
4. Outdoor Radiation Levels Below 10 R/hr:
- Assess immediate area for hazards.
- Remain near adequate shelters and closely monitor radiation levels during the first few hours.
- If radiation levels increase rapidly, shelter immediately.
5. Emergency Workers:
- Keep individual radiation exposures as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA) without hindering their ability to save and sustain life.
radiation detection equipment, responder actions, ALARA principle, radiation exposure limits
Responder Actions in a Dangerous Radiation Zone (DRZ)
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Action: For responders and emergency workers:

1. Shelter in place or avoid this area unless undertaking critical, planned protection activities for large populations.
2. Use radiation monitoring equipment to alert you of excess...
Action: For responders and emergency workers:

1. Shelter in place or avoid this area unless undertaking critical, planned protection activities for large populations.
2. Use radiation monitoring equipment to alert you of excess exposure.
3. Wear personal protective equipment (PPE) appropriate for all hazards present, including non-radiological hazards.
4. Emergency workers should only enter this area after being fully informed of the risks.

Important Notes:
• This zone is highly hazardous due to high radiation levels that can cause injury or death with prolonged exposure.
• Responders must be prepared for multiple types of hazards, not just radiological ones.
• All actions should be coordinated and based on full risk assessment.
responder actions, drz, radiation monitoring, ppe, emergency response, nuclear detonation
Responders' Actions Based on Radiation Levels
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Ensure responder safety while performing duties during a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. If responders do not have radiation detection instruments, they should shelter until informed it is safe to respond.
2. **Responders...
Objective: Ensure responder safety while performing duties during a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. If responders do not have radiation detection instruments, they should shelter until informed it is safe to respond.
2. **Responders with radiation detection instruments should:
- Shelter initially.
- Use their equipment to monitor and report local radiological conditions.**
3. If outdoor radiation levels exceed 10 R/hr:
- Responders should continue to shelter, unless there is a time-critical life-safety issue (e.g., fire, building collapse, or medical emergency).
4. If outdoor radiation levels are below 10 R/hr:
- Assess the immediate area for hazards.
- Stay near adequate shelters and closely monitor radiation levels during the first few hours.
- If radiation levels increase rapidly, shelter immediately.
radiation detection, responder safety, fallout monitoring, sheltering, radiation levels
Stay Indoors in Fallout Shelter for 2 Weeks (Within 350 Mile Radius)
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Action: Stay indoors in a fallout shelter or basement for at least two weeks.

When: If you are within the 350-mile “downwind” radius of an explosion. This is likely to cover most of the U.S.A. under typical scenarios.
fallout shelter, radiation protection, nuclear disaster, downwind radius
Wear Protective Fallout Clothing Outdoors
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Within the first two weeks after the last explosion, protective fallout clothing should be worn outside at all times and shed before entering a fallout shelter. Protective fallout clothing includes:

• Headgear or a full brimmed hat with hair...
Within the first two weeks after the last explosion, protective fallout clothing should be worn outside at all times and shed before entering a fallout shelter. Protective fallout clothing includes:

• Headgear or a full brimmed hat with hair completely covered.
• Boots or sturdy shoes.
• Heavy or thick clothing, including gloves (long sleeved shirts and long pants).
• A dust or gas mask or some other form of respiratory protection to ensure that fallout particles are not inhaled.
protective clothing,nuclear disaster
Administer Thyro-Block or Potassium Iodide/Iodate
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Action: Begin taking Thyro-Block tablets or another form of potassium iodide/iodate according to the instructions on the bottle.

When: Start this before or as soon as you get an official warning about a nuclear disaster.
thyro-block, potassium iodide, radiation protection, nuclear disaster
Avoid Neutron-Induced Radiation Areas and Implement Protection Techniques
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
To avoid neutron-induced radiation areas and protect personnel from exposure:

General Avoidance Guidelines:
1. Avoid neutron-induced radiation areas whenever possible.
2. If avoidance is not feasible, follow the protection techniques used...
To avoid neutron-induced radiation areas and protect personnel from exposure:

General Avoidance Guidelines:
1. Avoid neutron-induced radiation areas whenever possible.
2. If avoidance is not feasible, follow the protection techniques used for fallout.
3. All enemy airbursts are assumed to create hazard areas.
4. Friendly bursts employed in packages may create induced areas very near one another.

Specific Protection Measures:
• Do not cross the GZ (Ground Zero) area for 24 to 96 hours after the burst.
• Routine occupancy of an area of induced radiation is possible from two to five days after the burst, but only in low-dose-rate regions.
• During occupancy, the area should be checked with a dosimeter once per hour to monitor dose rates.
• Seek the least contaminated region available for long-term occupation.

Important Considerations:
• The actual area of contamination is usually substantially less than the plotted area, depending on the actual yield and height of burst.
• Accumulated doses over time can increase risk significantly; therefore, limit exposure duration and monitor dose rates frequently.
avoid neutron-induced radiation,protection techniques,dosimeter use,ground zero,exposure limits
Calculate Total Dose Received in a Fallout Area Crossing
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
To calculate the total dose received when crossing a fallout area:

1. Determine the average dose rate (R1 average) using one-half of the maximum dose rate encountered.
2. Estimate the time of stay (Ts) by dividing the distance across the...
To calculate the total dose received when crossing a fallout area:

1. Determine the average dose rate (R1 average) using one-half of the maximum dose rate encountered.
2. Estimate the time of stay (Ts) by dividing the distance across the contamination by the constant speed of crossing.
