• This process helps commanders understand the extent and movement of nuclear contamination to make tactical decisions.
nuclear reconnaissance,ANBACIS,hazard prediction,NBC reports
Long-Term Effects of Radiation Exposure
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Understand that a total accumulated dose of 100 R received over a few weeks has minimal long-term effects.
2. Note that studies on Japanese A-bomb survivors indicate no more than one in 100 people receiving 100 R would likely...
Procedure:

1. Understand that a total accumulated dose of 100 R received over a few weeks has minimal long-term effects.
2. Note that studies on Japanese A-bomb survivors indicate no more than one in 100 people receiving 100 R would likely die within 30 years from cancer or leukemia.
3. Recognize that this relatively small risk should not deter recovery efforts after a nuclear attack, especially when survival depends on access to food and other essentials.
4. Prioritize immediate life-saving actions over long-term health risks in the aftermath of a major nuclear event.
long term effects, radiation exposure, cancer risk, survival, nuclear attack
Maintain Dose Records for Emergency Workers
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Develop and implement a system to maintain accurate dose records for all emergency workers.
2. Ensure that each emergency worker records their DRD readings at designated intervals as outlined in the REP Program Plan.
3. Maintain...
Procedure:

1. Develop and implement a system to maintain accurate dose records for all emergency workers.
2. Ensure that each emergency worker records their DRD readings at designated intervals as outlined in the REP Program Plan.
3. Maintain these records in a secure, accessible format (e.g., digital or physical logbooks).
4. Include details such as date, time, location, and any exposure notes for each recorded dose reading.
5. Ensure that all dose records are reviewed regularly by supervisors or radiological health officers to monitor compliance with administrative limits.
6. Store dose records in accordance with regulatory requirements and ensure they are available for audits or inspections.
dose records, emergency workers, radiation protection, record keeping, administrative limits
Manage Radiological Exposure for Emergency Workers
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Follow established plans/procedures to manage radiological exposure and dose.
2. Use equipment such as dosimetry devices and radio protective drugs as required.
3. Monitor exposure and dose through regular checks and...
Procedure:

1. Follow established plans/procedures to manage radiological exposure and dose.
2. Use equipment such as dosimetry devices and radio protective drugs as required.
3. Monitor exposure and dose through regular checks and record-keeping.
4. Identify procedures for obtaining authorization to receive emergency exposures in excess of the PAGs (Protective Action Guidelines).
5. Ensure compliance with NUREG-0654/FEMA-REP-1, Rev. 2 (specific sections: C.2.c, H.11, H.11.b, K.2.b, K.3, K.3.a, M.1.b, and O.1).

Important Notes:
• Emergency workers must be trained in the use of dosimetry devices and protective drugs.
• Authorization for exposure above PAGs should only be obtained through formal procedures and under strict supervision.
• Regular monitoring is essential to ensure safety and compliance with health and safety standards.
radiological exposure, dosimetry, radio protective drugs, PAGs, NUREG-0654/FEMA-REP-1
Managing Radiation Exposure in Shelters
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Use a KFM (Kevlar Fallout Meter) to determine the safest locations inside the shelter for minimizing radiation exposure.
2. During the first several hours after fallout begins, move to areas with lower dose rates (e.g., middle...
Procedure:

1. Use a KFM (Kevlar Fallout Meter) to determine the safest locations inside the shelter for minimizing radiation exposure.
2. During the first several hours after fallout begins, move to areas with lower dose rates (e.g., middle of a trench or corner of a basement).
3. Improve shielding by:
- Digging deeper into the ground if practical.
- Reducing the size of openings and partially blocking them with earth, water containers, etc., while maintaining adequate ventilation.
4. Cover nose and mouth with a towel or cloth to prevent inhaling fallout particles.
5. Monitor humidity levels inside the shelter:
- If relative humidity is 90% or higher, a KFM cannot be charged even by quickly unrolling tape.
- Use a ventilating pump (e.g., KAP) to maintain airflow and reduce humidity if necessary.
shelter, radiation shielding, fallout meter, ventilation, nuclear attack
Minimize Radiation Dose from Fallout by Taking Shelter
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Identify a protective structure such as a building or shelter to take cover in during fallout exposure.
2. Enter the structure immediately upon detecting fallout, even if it has broken windows, as this provides adequate...
Procedure:

1. Identify a protective structure such as a building or shelter to take cover in during fallout exposure.
2. Enter the structure immediately upon detecting fallout, even if it has broken windows, as this provides adequate protection from ground shine dose.
3. Stay inside the structure until radiation levels have significantly decreased and it is safe to exit.
4. Avoid prolonged outdoor exposure in areas contaminated with fallout, as external exposure is the primary hazard during initial response phases.
5. Remove contaminated clothing or skin if exposed to fallout on the surface, as this reduces further external exposure.
6. Monitor radiation levels using available detection equipment and follow official guidance for when it is safe to leave shelter.

Notes:
• The outdoor radiation hazard from fallout (ground shine dose) is typically orders of magnitude more hazardous than internal exposure concerns resulting from inhalation or ingestion.
• Even buildings with broken windows can provide adequate protection from ground shine dose.
fallout,radiation,dose,shelter,protection,nuclear,emergency
Mitigate Effects of Meteorological Conditions on Fallout Spread
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Understand Meteorological Influence:
- Wind and precipitation significantly influence the spread of local fallout.
- Strong winds can distribute radioactive particles over large areas, increasing contamination risk.
-...
Procedure:

1. Understand Meteorological Influence:
- Wind and precipitation significantly influence the spread of local fallout.
- Strong winds can distribute radioactive particles over large areas, increasing contamination risk.
- Snow and rain accelerate the deposition of fallout onto the ground, potentially creating localized hotspots of high contamination.

2. Monitor Weather Conditions:
- Track wind patterns, precipitation, and temperature changes in radiologically contaminated areas.
- Be alert to unusual weather events such as local rain showers originating above the radioactive cloud, which may lead to heavy fallout in limited areas.

3. Take Protective Actions Based on Weather:
- In high-wind conditions, shelter indoors or use protective coverings to minimize exposure to airborne particles.
- During snowfall or rainfall, avoid contact with contaminated surfaces and decontaminate exposed skin and clothing promptly.

4. Evacuate if Necessary:
- If meteorological conditions lead to the formation of localized areas of heavy contamination, evacuate immediately and seek shelter in a secure location.
meteorological effects, fallout spread, radiation protection, decontamination
Monitor and Control Radiation Exposure for Emergency Workers
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure: Monitor and control radiation exposure levels for emergency workers during the initial response to a nuclear detonation.

Steps to Implement:
1. Establish protocols for monitoring cumulative absorbed doses, especially in the first...
Procedure: Monitor and control radiation exposure levels for emergency workers during the initial response to a nuclear detonation.

Steps to Implement:
1. Establish protocols for monitoring cumulative absorbed doses, especially in the first 72 hours post-detonation when conditions are austere and full equipment may not be available.
2. Use dosimeters or other radiation detection instruments to measure exposure levels of emergency workers.
3. Ensure that all emergency workers have access to protective gear and equipment designed for high-radiation environments.
4. Implement a system for recording and tracking individual exposure levels in real-time.
5. Review exposure data regularly and make decisions on whether to withdraw workers if cumulative doses reach the decision point (e.g., 50 rad/0.5 Gy).
6. Adapt routine practices as necessary during the initial response phase while maintaining strict control over worker exposures.

Note: This procedure is critical for protecting emergency responders from excessive radiation exposure and ensuring public health and safety.
radiation exposure monitoring,cumulative dose tracking,emergency worker protection,dosimetry protocols
Obtain or Develop Additional Dosimetry Methods as Needed
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Ensure that emergency workers have access to accurate dosimetry methods during a nuclear incident.

Procedure:
1. Identify any additional or improved dosimetry methods required for the specific scenario (e.g., personal radiation...
Objective: Ensure that emergency workers have access to accurate dosimetry methods during a nuclear incident.

Procedure:
1. Identify any additional or improved dosimetry methods required for the specific scenario (e.g., personal radiation detectors, group dosimetry).
2. Obtain or develop these methods in advance of potential incidents.
3. Distribute dosimeters to emergency workers as soon as they become available.
dosimetry methods, radiation detection, emergency worker equipment
Perform Bioassay for Detection of Internal Radiation Contamination
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Bioassay:
• Bioassay should be performed in conjunction with other survey data when:
- Personnel are in or near a vehicle hit by a DU round.
- Personnel climb on vehicles before complete decontamination.
- Personnel are in a room where a...
Bioassay:
• Bioassay should be performed in conjunction with other survey data when:
- Personnel are in or near a vehicle hit by a DU round.
- Personnel climb on vehicles before complete decontamination.
- Personnel are in a room where a firing control device containing tritium breaks.
• Assume all tanks and vehicles killed by US forces are contaminated with DU.
• Foreign vehicles may contain radium or other radioactive materials (e.g., dials, gauges).
• Bioassay samples from personnel who have contact with damaged foreign vehicles should be analyzed for these commodities.
• Refer to USACHPPM’s TG 211 for procedures on collecting, labeling, and shipping bioassay samples.
bioassay,internal contamination,radiation detection,DU,tritium
Prepare an NBC 3 Report
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Use NBC 2 Reports: Division NBCC uses the NBC 2 reports and current wind information to predict fallout areas.
2. Send to Affected Units: The NBC 3 report is sent to all units that could be affected by the hazard.
3....
Procedure:

1. Use NBC 2 Reports: Division NBCC uses the NBC 2 reports and current wind information to predict fallout areas.
2. Send to Affected Units: The NBC 3 report is sent to all units that could be affected by the hazard.
3. Plot Fallout Area: Each unit plots the NBC 3 report and determines which of its subordinate units are affected, then warns those units accordingly.
4. Define Zones:
- Zone I: Areas where the dose rate will exceed 150 centigray per hour (cGyph) within 4 hours.
- Zone II: No more than 50 cGyph in 4 hours and less than 150 cGyph in 24 hours.
5. Use as Battlefield Intelligence: Commanders should use the report to plan missions based on the predicted fallout area.
6. Decision-Making for Units in Fallout Area:
- The commander must decide whether to stay or move, based on mission requirements, exposure status of the unit, and higher headquarters guidance.
7. Use ANBACIS: As the ANBACIS system improves, commanders can view modeled hazard areas on a computer screen instead of relying solely on safe-sided STANAG plots for decision-making.
8. Include Required Line Items: The following line items are used in the NBC 3 nuclear report:
- Alfa (strike serial number)
- Delta (date/time group)
- Foxtrot (location of attack)
- Yankee (left and right radial lines)
- Zulu (effective wind speed, downwind distance of Zone I, and cloud radius)
NBC 3 report, fallout prediction, radiation protection, military procedures
Prepare and Transmit NBC 2 Reports for Nuclear Bursts
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Collect Raw Data:
- Designated observer units automatically submit raw data each time the enemy attacks with nuclear weapons.
- This data includes details on all types of bursts (air, surface, or unknown).

2. **Evaluate...
Procedure:

1. Collect Raw Data:
- Designated observer units automatically submit raw data each time the enemy attacks with nuclear weapons.
- This data includes details on all types of bursts (air, surface, or unknown).

2. Evaluate and Format Data:
- The NBC 2 report reflects evaluated nuclear burst data and is created for all bursts: air, surface, and unknown.
- If the type of burst is surface or unknown, fallout predictions are made as part of the report.
- Precedence for the report is established in FSOP/OPLAN/OPORD or other written instructions based on urgency.

3. Communicate NBC 2 Reports:
- Communication means for the NBC 2 report must be established by FSOP/OPLAN/OPORD or other written instructions.
- Send the report to all affected subordinate units, higher and adjacent headquarters.

4. Update as Needed:
- If subsequent data changes the yield or ground zero (GZ) location after an NBC 2 report is sent, send a new NBC 2 report with the same strike serial number and date-time of attack.

5. NBC 2 Report Generation Levels:
- In theory, the NBC 2 report is formulated only at division NBCC level.
- However, in practice, it may be generated at battalion or brigade levels if they have access to two or more NBC 1 reports that allow for an accurate yield and exact GZ location determination.
- If higher headquarters provides NBC 2 reports, they must be used as they generally contain more accurate data.
NBC 2 report,nuclear burst,fallout prediction,observer units,command levels
Respond to Water Surface Burst Fallout
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Understand Fallout Characteristics:
- In water surface bursts, particles are lighter and smaller compared to land-based detonations.
- These particles extend over a greater area but produce less local fallout.
-...
Procedure:

1. Understand Fallout Characteristics:
- In water surface bursts, particles are lighter and smaller compared to land-based detonations.
- These particles extend over a greater area but produce less local fallout.
- Particles contain mostly sea salts with some water, which may cause cloud seeding effects leading to rainout and areas of high local fallout.

2. Take Protective Actions:
- Avoid exposure to contaminated water or airborne particles from the fallout.
- Use protective gear such as masks and gloves when handling potentially contaminated materials.
- Decontaminate skin, clothing, and equipment after potential exposure.

3. Monitor for Contamination:
- Watch for signs of contamination on skin, clothing, or equipment.
- If rain occurs above the radioactive cloud, be alert to localized areas of heavy fallout that may form.

4. Evacuate if Necessary:
- In cases of high local fallout from water surface bursts, evacuate the area immediately and seek shelter in a secure location.
water surface burst, fallout, radiation protection, decontamination
Selecting Appropriate PPE for Emergency Workers in Nuclear Incidents
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Assess the primary hazard as external exposure from penetrating radiation (not inhalation or ingestion).
2. Determine if respiratory protection is needed, based on non-radiological hazards such as smoke, dust, or...
Procedure:

1. Assess the primary hazard as external exposure from penetrating radiation (not inhalation or ingestion).
2. Determine if respiratory protection is needed, based on non-radiological hazards such as smoke, dust, or vapors.
3. Avoid using typical HAZMAT PPE for mitigating penetrating radiation, as it does not reduce exposure and may increase responder dose due to increased time spent in hazardous areas.
4. Select PPE based on the specific non-radiological hazards present in damage zones (e.g., fires, toxic industrial chemicals, sharp debris).
5. Ensure that emergency workers have access to appropriate PPE for their assigned activities during the response.
6. Use a radiation detector as the most important equipment to alert workers to radiation levels of concern.

> Note: This procedure is based on guidance from the National Institute of Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) regarding PPE selection in CBRN incidents.
PPE selection, radiation protection, emergency workers, CBRN incidents
Selecting the Optimal Route Through an Induced-Radiation Area
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Assess mission requirements and environmental factors:
- Identify the lowest dose rate area that is consistent with the mission.
- Consider terrain obstacles such as mountains, swamps, or other unpassable features that...
Procedure:

1. Assess mission requirements and environmental factors:
- Identify the lowest dose rate area that is consistent with the mission.
- Consider terrain obstacles such as mountains, swamps, or other unpassable features that may limit route options.
- Evaluate temporary obstacles like tree blowdowns, fires, or rubble that could affect movement.

2. Prioritize crossing methods:
- If possible, use the following priority order for crossing an induced radiation area:
1. Aircraft
2. Armored vehicles and personnel carriers
3. Wheeled vehicles
4. On foot

3. Minimize exposure time:
- Calculate the time of stay (stay time) during the crossing using the relationship provided in the text.
- Ensure that the route selected minimizes the duration spent within the induced radiation area.
induced-radiation,route selection,nuclear contamination,exposure time
Short Outdoor Trips for Waste Disposal During Fallout Period
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Action: When leaving your shelter, do so only for short periods of time to get rid of sewage and trash.

When: After the initial fallout period has passed.
fallout management, waste disposal, nuclear disaster
Understand Public Fallout Shelter Capabilities
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
A public fallout shelter is a building or structure designated by the government that is said to provide ample protection from radiation and also supposedly has enough supplies in it to house, feed, and provide sanitary needs for the amount of...
A public fallout shelter is a building or structure designated by the government that is said to provide ample protection from radiation and also supposedly has enough supplies in it to house, feed, and provide sanitary needs for the amount of persons allowed into the shelter. However, most public fallout shelters are not stocked.
fallout shelter,nuclear disaster
Understand the Meaning of RES (Radiation Exposure Status)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
RES stands for Radiation Exposure Status, which is used to classify individuals based on their level of radiation exposure and determine appropriate medical care or monitoring protocols.
res,radiation exposure status,medical classification,monitoring protocols
Understand the Role of RAMT (Radiological Advisory Medical Team)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
A RAMT is a Radiological Advisory Medical Team that provides medical advice and support in radiological emergencies, including assessing radiation exposure, managing health effects, and advising on treatment protocols.
ramt,radiological advisory medical team,radiation exposure,treatment protocols
Understand the Role of RPO (Radiation Protection Officer)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
An RPO is a Radiation Protection Officer responsible for ensuring that radiation safety standards are followed in environments where radioactive materials or sources are used, stored, or transported.
rpo,radiation protection officer,safety standards,radioactive materials
Understand the Role of a RADCON (Radiological Control Team)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
A RADCON is a Radiological Control Team responsible for managing and mitigating radiological hazards, including monitoring radiation levels, decontaminating affected areas, and ensuring safety protocols are followed in radiological incidents.
radcon,radiological control team,radiation hazards,safety protocols

+ 51 more procedures. Get the full library in the app.

Decontamination (117) -

Assessing Critical Infrastructure Decontamination Needs
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Consider the following factors when assessing decontamination needs for critical infrastructure:

1. The DRZ may involve lethal, non-uniform fallout deposition, or hot spots.
2. Responders working in areas with **significant fallout...
Consider the following factors when assessing decontamination needs for critical infrastructure:

1. The DRZ may involve lethal, non-uniform fallout deposition, or hot spots.
2. Responders working in areas with significant fallout contamination require:
- Real-time radiation measurements
- A robust, actively managed personal dose-monitoring system

3. Fallout decays rapidly (see Chapter 1), so it is generally preferable to delay infrastructure decontamination activities, if possible.
4. Consider temporary solutions to reduce exposure to workers at critical infrastructure facilities:
- Burying radioactive contamination by tilling contaminated soil in the surrounding area. Leaving the tilled soil rough reduces radiation exposure.
- Adding or enhancing shielding (heavy materials) around key locations of interest. Consider use of concrete highway barriers and/or earthen and rubble berms.
- Washing/spraying down vegetation (e.g., trees) and other elevated surfaces.

5. Where possible, infrastructure outside the HZ and DRZ should be used. These facilities and locations could be available immediately and can be expected to be free of contamination.
6. Use FEMA’s Continuity of Operations Program (COOP) guidance and planning resources as a template for local emergency preparedness planners to choose appropriate COOP locations that will not be affected by fallout or require decontamination.
7. If decontamination is required in the early hours after a nuclear explosion, local responders may perform these duties, despite little or no radiological decontamination training.
8. Consider effective, fast, and easy-to-implement decontamination methods, such as:
- Vacuuming
- Showering
- Hosing

9. Decontamination targets are key for identifying when infrastructure is “clean.” While background radiation times two is widely used as a general estimate, the EPA will ultimately set decontamination target guidelines for access.

Note: Critical infrastructure decontamination should only be initiated when basic information becomes available regarding fallout distribution, current and projected radiation dose rates, and structural integrity of the elements to be decontaminated.
critical infrastructure, decontamination, nuclear detonation, fallout, radiation, shielding, COOP
Immediate Skin Decontamination Using SDKs
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Immediate Skin Decontamination Using SDKs:

1. Recognize contamination: As soon as a soldier becomes aware of chemical or biological contamination on his bare skin, he initiates immediate decon techniques without waiting for...
Procedure for Immediate Skin Decontamination Using SDKs:

1. Recognize contamination: As soon as a soldier becomes aware of chemical or biological contamination on his bare skin, he initiates immediate decon techniques without waiting for command.
2. Use the M291 (SDK) kit:
- Apply the M291 SDK within 1 minute of becoming contaminated.
- Follow instructions provided on the outside of the kit, on the individual packet within the kit, and in STP 21-1-SMCT.
3. Decontaminate affected areas: Use the M291 to decontaminate the hood, mask, gloves, and weapon.
4. Use IEDK if needed: If an SDK is not available, use the IEDK (M295 kit) for decontamination.

Alternate Method if No SDK Available:
• Pinch-blott the chemical contamination from the skin with a cloth and flush it with water from a canteen.
• Use soap and water (or hot water) to wash the agent from the skin. This method is less effective than using decon kits but still recommended when no SDKs are available.

Biological Contamination:
• Wash with soap and water to remove nearly all biological agents from the skin.
• A 0.5 percent chlorine solution (calcium hypochlorite [HTH] or household bleach) is an effective biological decontaminant. See Appendix B for details on how to make this solution.

Radiological Contamination:
• Brush, wash, or wipe off radiological dust particles from the skin and equipment.
SDK,M291,IEDK,M295,chemical decon,biological decon,radiological decon,immediate decon
Chemical, Biological, and Radiological (CBR) Decontamination (TM 3-220)
Critical Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Procedure for CBR Decontamination

1. Identify the type of contamination using appropriate detection equipment.
2. Isolate the contaminated area to prevent further spread of contaminants.
3. Wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE),...
Procedure for CBR Decontamination

1. Identify the type of contamination using appropriate detection equipment.
2. Isolate the contaminated area to prevent further spread of contaminants.
3. Wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE), including chemical-resistant suits, gloves, and respirators.
4. Apply decontaminating agents according to the type of contaminant:
- Chemical: Use neutralizing agents such as water or specific chemical decontamination solutions.
- Biological: Use disinfectants such as bleach or alcohol-based solutions.
- Radiological: Use absorbent materials and specialized decontamination solutions to remove radioactive particles.
5. Follow a systematic approach, starting from the least contaminated areas and moving toward more heavily contaminated zones.
6. Monitor the effectiveness of decontamination using detection equipment and ensure that all levels of contamination are reduced to safe limits.
7. Dispose of contaminated waste in accordance with established protocols to prevent secondary contamination.
8. Conduct post-decontamination inspections to verify that the area is safe for reoccupation.
CBR decontamination, TM 3-220, chemical biological radiological, PPE use
Decontaminate Radiation-Contaminated Objects and Materials
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Proper decontamination is essential to reduce the risk of radiation exposure after a nuclear event.

### Decontamination Procedures:
• Washing everything thoroughly is the primary method for decontaminating objects.
• **Never burn, boil, or...
Proper decontamination is essential to reduce the risk of radiation exposure after a nuclear event.

### Decontamination Procedures:
• Washing everything thoroughly is the primary method for decontaminating objects.
• Never burn, boil, or dust off contaminated materials, as this can release radiation back into the air and increase the danger to others.
• Burning does not eliminate radiation. It only redistributes it in a more hazardous form.

### Disposal of Contaminated Materials:
• Any object that cannot be properly washed (e.g., sponges, cakes, breads, berries, and other porous materials) should be disposed of immediately to prevent further contamination.
• These items are difficult to clean effectively and may retain radiation even after washing.
radiation decontamination, nuclear cleanup, contaminated materials, safe disposal, radiation safety
Decontamination and Disposal of Chemical Munitions
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decontaminating and Disposing of Chemical Munitions:

1. Assess chemical munition status:
- In combat, chemical munitions may be encountered or captured from the enemy.
- Damaged or deteriorated munitions may leak and...
Procedure for Decontaminating and Disposing of Chemical Munitions:

1. Assess chemical munition status:
- In combat, chemical munitions may be encountered or captured from the enemy.
- Damaged or deteriorated munitions may leak and must be decontaminated, evacuated, and disposed of safely by specialized units.
2. Protective measures:
- All soldiers must use protective clothing and masks when there is a possibility of exposure to toxic chemical agents.
3. MOPP level: The MOPP (Mission-Oriented Protective Posture) level will be specified by the unit commander.
4. Detection equipment:
- Use detection equipment such as CAM, M256A1 detector kit, M8A1 or M22 ACAA, and M8/M9 detector paper to monitor chemical munitions.
5. Unexploded leaking munitions:
- Treat unexploded leaking munitions the same as conventional ordnance.
- Submit a spot report to an explosive ordnance disposal (EOD) unit for handling.
6. Refer to regulations: When disposing of and decontaminating chemical munitions, refer to Army Regulation (AR) 385-10 and TM 10-277 for additional safety guidance.
chemical munition decon, EOD unit, MOPP level, chemical detection equipment
Handling Chemical Munitions
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Handling Chemical Munitions:

1. Do not handle chemical projectiles:
- Treat them as you would a fused high-explosive (HE) round.
2. Protective measures: Security and protective measures must match those given to...
Procedure for Handling Chemical Munitions:

1. Do not handle chemical projectiles:
- Treat them as you would a fused high-explosive (HE) round.
2. Protective measures: Security and protective measures must match those given to conventional munitions.
3. Notify EOD unit: Notify an explosive ordnance disposal (EOD) unit for the disposal and decontamination of chemical munitions.
4. Responsibility of EOD units:
- EOD units are responsible for sealing, packaging, and disposing of chemical munitions safely.
chemical munition handling, EOD unit, high-explosive round
Implement Decontamination Action Levels and Procedures
Critical Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Define action levels for determining when decontamination is required for emergency workers, the general public, equipment, vehicles, and personal possessions.
2. Establish clear procedures for radiological decontamination that...
Procedure:

1. Define action levels for determining when decontamination is required for emergency workers, the general public, equipment, vehicles, and personal possessions.
2. Establish clear procedures for radiological decontamination that are appropriate for different exposure scenarios.
3. Ensure that all personnel involved in decontamination are trained on proper techniques and safety protocols.
4. Provide access to decontamination facilities and supplies at the incident site or nearby locations as needed.
5. Develop a plan for the safe disposal of contaminated waste generated during decontamination efforts, including methods such as incineration, burial, or other approved disposal practices.
6. Document all decontamination activities, including who was decontaminated, when it occurred, and the results of contamination checks before and after decontamination.
decontamination, action levels, radiological incident, waste disposal, emergency workers
Operational Decontamination During Fire Mission
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Operational Decontamination During an Ongoing Fire Mission:

1. Commander’s decision: The commander initiates the decon operation after assessing the situation and ensuring that fire support can continue with minimal...
Procedure for Operational Decontamination During an Ongoing Fire Mission:

1. Commander’s decision: The commander initiates the decon operation after assessing the situation and ensuring that fire support can continue with minimal disruption.
2. Request support: Request support from the battalion for operational decon, especially if time is limited (e.g., 2 ½ hours to complete movement and be in a firing position).
3. Coordinate with supporting units:
- Send an advance party to meet the supporting power-driven decon-equipment (PDDE) crew.
- Organize the site for vehicle washdown and MOPP-gear exchange.
4. Set up PDDE: The PDDE crew sets up the equipment and runs the vehicle washdown while the advance party prepares the MOPP-gear exchange with battery supply personnel.
5. Decon process:
- Rotate platoons through the decon site one at a time, as directed by the battalion chemical NCO.
- Firing platoons move quickly to ensure artillery firing tables are available for continued operations.
6. Maintain MOPP4: Soldiers remain in MOPP4 due to ongoing vapor hazards from residual contamination until decon is complete and a new area is confirmed clear of contamination.
7. Resupply and reposition:
- After decon, move to the alternate position and begin ammo resupply.
- Monitor wind direction to ensure soldiers remain upwind of any desorbing vapors from equipment.
8. Relief procedures: Rotate soldiers into clean areas for rest and relief; those returning to the guns assume MOPP4 again.
operational decon,PDDE,MOPP4,vehicle washdown,fire mission
Perform Self-Decontamination Actions
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Follow these steps to perform self-decontamination:

Steps:
1. Remove contaminated clothes and shoes and place them in a bag.
2. Wash your body with warm water.
3. Use cloth, sponge, soft brush, etc., to clean skin or clothing.
4. Begin...
Follow these steps to perform self-decontamination:

Steps:
1. Remove contaminated clothes and shoes and place them in a bag.
2. Wash your body with warm water.
3. Use cloth, sponge, soft brush, etc., to clean skin or clothing.
4. Begin with mild agents like soap and water.
5. When showering, try to direct rinse water away from face and body. If washing your hair, do not use conditioner.
6. Keep materials out of eyes, nose, mouth, and wounds.
7. Avoid scratching, burning, or causing breaks in the skin.

Notes:
• These actions can be performed at any location or at ad hoc screening locations set up by emergency response organizations to facilitate washing.
• Prior to opening these facilities, planners should ensure that an ample supply of replacement clothing, shoes, plastic bags, and wet wipes are available.
self-decontamination,nuclear,clothing removal,washing
Aircraft MOPP-Gear Exchange and Washdown Procedure
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Shut down the aircraft if the aircrew must perform a MOPP-gear exchange.
2. After the MOPP-gear exchange, use a shuffle pit before reboarding the aircraft.
3. **Use the M12A1 PDDE or M17 LDS to spray down the aircraft with...
Procedure:

1. Shut down the aircraft if the aircrew must perform a MOPP-gear exchange.
2. After the MOPP-gear exchange, use a shuffle pit before reboarding the aircraft.
3. Use the M12A1 PDDE or M17 LDS to spray down the aircraft with hot, soapy water and then rinse it.
4. Spray the aircraft from the top to the bottom, working from the front to the rear.
5. Avoid spraying near the tail rotor when the aircraft is running.
6. Follow guidelines in the aircraft’s maintenance manual during washdown operations.

Remarks:
• Aviation personnel must ensure proper use of equipment and follow safety protocols.
• Ensure that all decontamination procedures are conducted safely to avoid damage to the aircraft.
MOPP-gear exchange,aircraft washdown,M12A1 PDDE,M17 LDS,hot soapy water
Aircraft Spot Decontamination Overhaul at AVIM Facilities
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Identify areas and equipment to be worked on during overhaul.
2. Assign maintenance personnel or a chemical decon platoon from division or corps as responsible personnel.
3. **Select appropriate decontaminants based on the...
Procedure:

1. Identify areas and equipment to be worked on during overhaul.
2. Assign maintenance personnel or a chemical decon platoon from division or corps as responsible personnel.
3. Select appropriate decontaminants based on the surface type:
- DS2
- Diesel fuel
- 5 percent chlorine solution
- Hot, soapy water
- *For surfaces that are easily destroyed by liquid or corrosion, use hot air instead.*
4. Wash with diesel fuel and then hot, soapy water.
5. Rinse the equipment thoroughly after washing.
6. Check for contamination using M8 detector paper or CAM (Chemical Agent Monitor).
7. If time permits, allow the equipment to weather to reduce chemical contamination to negligible risk levels.
8. Use caustic decontaminants only on assemblies that have been removed from the aircraft.
9. Rinse assemblies thoroughly before replacing them.

Remarks:
• This process has the same results as a deliberate decon.
• Caustic decontaminants should be used with caution and only on disassembled parts.
aircraft,decontamination,overhaul,AVIM,diesel fuel,hot soapy water,M8 detector paper,CAM
Aircraft Washdown and Chemical Agent Absorption Prevention
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Conduct an aircraft washdown when operating an aircraft contaminated by gross amounts of chemical agent.
2. This limits the absorption of chemical agents into paint, plastic, and rubber portions of the aircraft.
3....
Procedure:

1. Conduct an aircraft washdown when operating an aircraft contaminated by gross amounts of chemical agent.
2. This limits the absorption of chemical agents into paint, plastic, and rubber portions of the aircraft.
3. Chemical agents absorbed into these materials can pose a vapor hazard and be difficult to decon later.

Remarks:
• Aircraft washdown is essential for reducing long-term contamination risks.
• CARC (Chemical Agent Resistant Coating) paint helps keep contamination on the surface, making it easier to neutralize or remove.
chemical agent absorption,CARC paint,vapor hazard
Basic Skills Decontamination Procedure
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Objective: Immediate neutralization or removal of contamination from exposed portions of the skin.

Procedure:
1. Assess the situation and identify the type of contamination (chemical, biological, etc.).
2. **Remove contaminated...
Objective: Immediate neutralization or removal of contamination from exposed portions of the skin.

Procedure:
1. Assess the situation and identify the type of contamination (chemical, biological, etc.).
2. Remove contaminated clothing immediately if it is not part of the MOPP gear.
3. Wash the affected area thoroughly with water for at least 5 minutes to remove any visible contaminants.
4. Use a decontamination kit or solution as specified by unit protocols, applying it directly to the skin in a systematic manner (from clean to contaminated areas).
5. Rinse again with water after using the decontamination solution to ensure all residue is removed.
6. Dry the area and apply any necessary protective cream or ointment as directed by medical personnel.
7. Report the incident to a superior or medical officer for further evaluation and possible follow-up treatment.

Notes:
• This procedure must be performed without supervision by each soldier.
• Ensure that decontamination solutions are used according to manufacturer instructions and unit guidelines.
• If symptoms persist after decontamination, seek immediate medical attention.
basic skills decontamination,nuclear,cbrn,chemical,biological,decontamination procedure
Chemical-Agent Patient Decontamination Procedures
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
General Decontamination Procedures for Chemical-Agent Patients:

1. Decon Team Setup:
- A 8-man patient-decon team is responsible for decontaminating patients before they receive medical treatment in the clean treatment area.

2. **Use of...
General Decontamination Procedures for Chemical-Agent Patients:

1. Decon Team Setup:
- A 8-man patient-decon team is responsible for decontaminating patients before they receive medical treatment in the clean treatment area.

2. Use of Chlorine Solutions:
- Decontaminate the following items using a 5 percent chlorine solution:
- Gloves
- Aprons
- Litters
- Scissors
- Patient’s hood
- Other nonskin contact areas
- Use a 0.5 percent chlorine solution to decontaminate:
- The patient’s mask
- Skin
- Splints
- Wounds (irrigate wounds with this solution)

3. Decon Process:
- Decontaminate the patient's skin, bandages, wounds, mask, identification tags with chain, and splints by wiping them with a 0.5 percent chlorine solution.

4. Additional Considerations:
- Some procedures can be done with one soldier, while others require more than one soldier.
- For treatment procedures, refer to FMs 8-9, 8-33, and 8-285.
chemical agent decon, patient decon procedures, chlorine solution use
Conduct Initial External Contamination Screening
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure for Conducting Initial External Contamination Screening:

1. Check for radioactive contamination on individuals’ bodies, clothing, and shoes.
2. Use proper radiation detection instruments operated by trained professionals.
3....
Procedure for Conducting Initial External Contamination Screening:

1. Check for radioactive contamination on individuals’ bodies, clothing, and shoes.
2. Use proper radiation detection instruments operated by trained professionals.
3. Perform the initial screening in a matter of seconds, as detailed radiological surveys are not required at this stage.
4. Adjust the screening process based on:
- The situation (e.g., level of contamination, number of people to be screened)
- Available staff and resources
- Decontamination resources available on-site
5. If necessary, use more restrictive radiological screening criteria depending on the situation and resource availability.
6. Ensure that screening does not delay prompt evacuation or other critical response operations.
external contamination, radiation detection, decontamination, screening criteria, nuclear emergency
Conduct Operational Decontamination (Operational Decon)
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: Limit the spread of contamination, allow temporary relief from MOPP4, and facilitate additional decon requirements.

Requirements:
• A 120-square-yard area
• No nearby water source may be required depending on the number of...
Objective: Limit the spread of contamination, allow temporary relief from MOPP4, and facilitate additional decon requirements.

Requirements:
• A 120-square-yard area
• No nearby water source may be required depending on the number of contaminated vehicles

Process:
1. Conduct a vehicle washdown using hot, soapy water for 2 to 3 minutes per vehicle.
2. Perform a MOPP-gear exchange, where soldiers remove contaminated MOPP gear and replace it with clean gear from the MOPP-gear-exchange point.

Techniques:
• Decentralized company control: Unit commander selects decon site, links up with PDDE crew, and executes operational decon.
• Centralized battalion control: Battalion chemical NCO controls decon; site is selected by the battalion commander.
• Centralized brigade control: Brigade chemical NCO performs functions similar to battalion-level control when decon assets are consolidated within the brigade.

Note: This process may eliminate the need for a thorough decon by speeding up the weathering process.
operational decon, vehicle washdown, MOPP gear exchange, decentralized control, centralized control, nuclear biological chemical decontamination
Conducting Detailed Aircraft Decontamination (DAD)
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: Reduce contamination hazards to a negligible risk level before maintenance operations or reconstitution efforts.

### Procedure:
1. Perform DAD as part of a reconstitution effort or before maintenance operations (after every 25 hours...
Objective: Reduce contamination hazards to a negligible risk level before maintenance operations or reconstitution efforts.

### Procedure:
1. Perform DAD as part of a reconstitution effort or before maintenance operations (after every 25 hours of flight time).
2. When the aircraft is overhauled or grounded for scheduled maintenance, reduce contamination hazards to negligible risk levels by going through a DAD.
3. When components are removed from the aircraft for repair, care must be taken because of residual contamination.
4. Decon these components either before cannibalization or overhaul.
5. The DAD area is composed of the following five stations:
- Primary wash
- Decon-solution application
- Contact time/interior decon
- Rinse
- Check (see Figure 7-2).
DAD procedure, aircraft decon steps, component decon, maintenance decon
Contamination Check Station Procedure for Vehicle Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Check contamination level: At this station, the vehicle is checked to determine if it has a negligible or significant contamination level.
2. Chemical contamination check: Use the CAM (Chemical Agent Monitor) to detect...
Procedure:

1. Check contamination level: At this station, the vehicle is checked to determine if it has a negligible or significant contamination level.
2. Chemical contamination check: Use the CAM (Chemical Agent Monitor) to detect vapor from residual liquid contamination. A one bar or lower reading on the CAM indicates a negligible contamination level.
3. Verify with M8 detector paper: If the CAM detects vapor contamination, use M8 detector paper to verify the presence of liquid contamination.
4. Confirm with M256A1 detector kit: If suspected false positives occur from both the CAM and M8 detector paper, use an M256A1 detector kit for confirmation.
5. Refer to Table 4-6: Consult Table 4-6 on page 4-22 for common interferences that can cause false-positive readings on the CAM (e.g., insect repellent, brake fluid, gasoline vapors, etc.).
6. Recycle vehicle if needed: If significant contamination is found, recycle the vehicle to station 2 for chemical contamination or station 1 for radiological contamination.
7. Consider desorption effects: After decon, chemical agents may desorb from surfaces painted with CARC (Chemical Agent Resistant Coating) more slowly than those painted with alkyd. This should be considered when checking the effectiveness of decontamination.
contamination check, CAM, M8 detector paper, M256A1 kit, desorption effects
Coordinate with Radiation Protection Professionals for Decontamination Recommendations
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Emergency response officials must coordinate with radiation protection professionals at the state, local, and federal levels.
2. This includes collaboration with:
- State/local Radiation Control Program staff
- The Advisory...
Procedure:

1. Emergency response officials must coordinate with radiation protection professionals at the state, local, and federal levels.
2. This includes collaboration with:
- State/local Radiation Control Program staff
- The Advisory Team for Environment, Food and Health
- ROSS (Radiation Operations Support Service)
3. Develop screening criteria and decontamination recommendations in partnership with these professionals.
4. Share these recommendations with CRCs (Contamination Reduction Centers) and shelters.
5. Ensure that all communication is clear and accessible to the public, including:
- Instructions in languages appropriate for the affected community
- Accessibility for people with disabilities or access and functional needs
radiation protection professionals, decontamination recommendations, coordination, CRCs, shelters
Decon Station 1 - Primary Wash Procedures
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
At this station, the gross contamination and dirt are removed from the vehicle. The vehicle is sprayed for 2 to 3 minutes with hot, soapy water. The vehicle is then scrubbed to help remove caked-on dirt. The mechanical action of scrubbing also...
At this station, the gross contamination and dirt are removed from the vehicle. The vehicle is sprayed for 2 to 3 minutes with hot, soapy water. The vehicle is then scrubbed to help remove caked-on dirt. The mechanical action of scrubbing also helps remove thickened chemical agents. Although the undersurfaces are difficult to reach, try to remove as much dirt as possible. After scrubbing the vehicle, spray it again for 2 to 3 minutes to remove loosened dirt and contamination. This station uses about 250 gallons of water per vehicle. Larger vehicles with large quantities of dirt use more water. The runoff from this station is contaminated and must be treated as hazardous. The contaminated runoff must be controlled; if available, engineer support may be used. This station requires high water-pressure systems (M12A1 power-driven decon apparatus [PDDA] and M17 Lightweight Decontaminating System [LDS]) rather than low water-volume systems (65GPM pumps).
primary wash, decon station 1, hot soapy water, runoff control, PDDA, LDS
Decon-Solution Application Station for DAD
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: Apply a decon solution to the aircraft.

### Procedure:
1. Use the M12A1 PDDE or the M17 LDS (in the siphinjector mode) to apply a decon solution at this station.
2. After applying the solution, a member of the scrubbing team scrubs...
Objective: Apply a decon solution to the aircraft.

### Procedure:
1. Use the M12A1 PDDE or the M17 LDS (in the siphinjector mode) to apply a decon solution at this station.
2. After applying the solution, a member of the scrubbing team scrubs the aircraft’s skin.
3. This allows chemical agents to mix with the decon solution, especially when thickened chemical agents are present.
4. For decontaminants, refer to Appendix B.
5. Do not use STB or HTH because of their corrosive nature.
decon solution application, M12A1 PDDE, M17 LDS, chemical agent mixing
Decontaminate Animals in a Nuclear Emergency
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Action: Decontaminate animals to reduce the risk of spreading radioactive contamination.

Steps:
1. Dust off and partially remove contamination: Any action to dust off and partially remove contamination is helpful, similar to human...
Action: Decontaminate animals to reduce the risk of spreading radioactive contamination.

Steps:
1. Dust off and partially remove contamination: Any action to dust off and partially remove contamination is helpful, similar to human decontamination procedures.
2. Brushing animals: Care should be taken to avoid inhaling any particulates. Use a dust mask and brush the animals outside and upwind if possible.
3. Bathing and grooming: When possible, bathing and grooming thoroughly can remove additional contamination.
4. Designate areas for pet owners: At CRCs, areas can be designated for pet owners to clean their own animals, which reduces anxiety for the animals and speeds up the process.
5. Provide assistance: Assistance should be provided to those unable to clean their animals themselves.
6. Instructions for self-decontamination: For those unable to report to a CRC, instructions for cleaning pets should be provided with instructions for self-decontamination.
animal decontamination, nuclear emergency, pet grooming, radiation exposure
Decontaminate Buildings Contaminated with Persistent Chemicals
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. When buildings are contaminated with persistent chemicals, their value for cover, concealment, and shelter is reduced.
2. Wood and concrete may absorb liquid agents and emit toxic vapors for days or weeks.
3. Building decon is...
Procedure:

1. When buildings are contaminated with persistent chemicals, their value for cover, concealment, and shelter is reduced.
2. Wood and concrete may absorb liquid agents and emit toxic vapors for days or weeks.
3. Building decon is very difficult and requires large quantities of decontaminants.
4. To reduce the hazard:
- Cover contamination with plastic sheets, STB slurry, sodium silicate, or other substances that cover or absorb the agent.
5. Even parts of a building not intended for occupation may need to be decontaminated to prevent contamination from spreading.
building decon, persistent chemicals, toxic vapors, cover contamination
Decontaminate Contaminated Snow Immediately
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Vehicles and personnel covered with contaminated snow must be decontaminated immediately, before the snow has a chance to melt and freeze.
2. Contaminated snow forms layers of ice that make contamination difficult to...
Procedure:

1. Vehicles and personnel covered with contaminated snow must be decontaminated immediately, before the snow has a chance to melt and freeze.
2. Contaminated snow forms layers of ice that make contamination difficult to remove.
3. Use tree branches (if available) to remove contaminated snow.
4. Radioactive fallout mixed with snowfall must be removed as soon as possible.
contaminated snow, decontaminate immediately, tree branches, radioactive fallout
Decontaminate Litter Patients Using Chlorine Solutions
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decontaminating Litter Patients:

1. Prepare the Decon Station:
- Set up a decon station as shown in Figure 8-2, ensuring that the clean treatment area is located upwind and at least 75 yards from the contaminated area.
-...
Procedure for Decontaminating Litter Patients:

1. Prepare the Decon Station:
- Set up a decon station as shown in Figure 8-2, ensuring that the clean treatment area is located upwind and at least 75 yards from the contaminated area.
- Establish a dirty dump (downwind) for disposal of contaminated materials.

2. Use Appropriate Chlorine Solutions:
- Use a 5 percent chlorine solution to decontaminate:
- Gloves
- Aprons
- Litters
- Scissors
- Patient’s hood
- Other nonskin contact areas
- Place the bandage scissors in a container of 5 percent chlorine solution between each use.

3. Use a 0.5 Percent Chlorine Solution for Skin and Wounds:
- Use a 0.5 percent chlorine solution to decontaminate:
- The patient’s mask
- Skin
- Splints
- Wounds (irrigate wounds with this solution)

4. Avoid Vigorous Scrubbing:
- When applying the 0.5 percent solution, avoid vigorous scrubbing as it may force chemical agents into the skin.

Note:
• Litter patients requiring emergency medical treatment (EMT) or ambulatory medical treatment (AMT) in the clean area of the MTF will be completely decontaminated.
• If a patient requires immediate evacuation, only spot-decontaminate the wound area and MOPP gear to remove gross contamination. The patient should be evacuated in their MOPP gear.
litter patient decon, chlorine solution, skin decon, wound irrigation
Decontamination of Streets and Sidewalks in Civilian Areas
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Decontaminating Streets and Sidewalks:

1. Identify areas where liquid agents may have been absorbed by streets, sidewalks, or other porous surfaces.
2. Recognize that these surfaces can release toxic vapors when heated by the...
Procedure for Decontaminating Streets and Sidewalks:

1. Identify areas where liquid agents may have been absorbed by streets, sidewalks, or other porous surfaces.
2. Recognize that these surfaces can release toxic vapors when heated by the sun.
3. Determine if decontamination is necessary based on the level of contamination and potential risk to civilians.
4. If possible, allow natural weathering processes to reduce hazards over time, provided the situation permits.
5. Repeat decontamination operations as needed to ensure safety levels are maintained.

Important Considerations:
• Decontamination operations should be conducted away from civilian populations to prevent injury or interference with the work.
• Civilians must not be forced to participate in preparing decon sites or assisting with decon operations.
• Volunteers who have been cleared by the Assistant Chief of Staff, G5 (Civil Affairs) may assist.
decontamination, streets, sidewalks, civilians, toxic vapors, weathering
Execute Operational Decontamination (Execution Phase)
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: Process contaminated vehicles and personnel through the decon site.

Steps:
1. Establish positive control: Ensure security and control over the decon site before processing begins.
2. Vehicle movement: Vehicles move from...
Objective: Process contaminated vehicles and personnel through the decon site.

Steps:
1. Establish positive control: Ensure security and control over the decon site before processing begins.
2. Vehicle movement: Vehicles move from the predecon area to the decon site either on order or by following the vehicle ahead.
3. Contaminated personnel removal: Soldiers exit contaminated vehicles and proceed to the MOPP-gear-exchange point.
4. Provide security: The contaminated unit is responsible for providing security during the execution phase.
5. Vehicle washdown: Each vehicle receives a 2- to 3-minute washdown with hot, soapy water.
6. MOPP-gear exchange:
- Soldiers remove contaminated MOPP gear and replace it with clean gear from the MOPP-gear-exchange point.
7. Remount vehicles: After completing decon, soldiers remount their vehicles and move to an after-decon assembly area (AA) to await further instructions or proceed to their next battle position.
execution phase, vehicle washdown, MOPP gear exchange, after-decon assembly area
Handle Vehicles After Decontamination
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
After decontamination:

1. Vehicles that exhibit acceptable levels of radiation should be returned to their owners if possible.
2. Depending on the owner’s status (evacuated, injured, deceased, etc.), vehicles may require **long-term...
After decontamination:

1. Vehicles that exhibit acceptable levels of radiation should be returned to their owners if possible.
2. Depending on the owner’s status (evacuated, injured, deceased, etc.), vehicles may require long-term storage.
3. Vehicles that remain contaminated must be segregated from successfully decontaminated vehicles to prevent cross-contamination.
4. If resources allow, perform additional rounds of decontamination on vehicles that continue to exhibit unacceptable levels of contamination.

> Note: Planners must anticipate the need for storing potentially large numbers of decontaminated vehicles.
vehicle storage, radiation levels, segregation, nuclear decontamination
Individual Gear Decontamination Procedure
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Task: Remove contamination to a negligible risk from individual gear (LCE, mask carrier, helmet, and weapon)

### Soldier's Actions:
1. Decontaminates his own gloves with hot, soapy water or an STB slurry.
2. **Decontaminates his hose and...
Task: Remove contamination to a negligible risk from individual gear (LCE, mask carrier, helmet, and weapon)

### Soldier's Actions:
1. Decontaminates his own gloves with hot, soapy water or an STB slurry.
2. Decontaminates his hose and canister (if wearing the M42 mask) with either hot, soapy water and a sponge or an STB slurry mix.
3. Decontaminates his own gear by washing and scrubbing it for 4 minutes in a decontaminant container with hot, soapy water or an STB slurry mix.

### Attendant's Actions:
1. Takes the gear to the equipment checkpoint and places it on the "dirty" side of the contamination control line (engineer tape).
2. Waits as the monitor holds the detection device 1 inch from the gear and checks it for residual contamination.
3. Recycles any gear that is above one bar of contamination.
4. Places the gear on the clean side of the contamination control line if it passes the check.
5. Carries the gear to the reissue point.

### Notes:
• Change the slurry mix after 20 soldiers have decontaminated their gear and the rinse water after 10 soldiers or when it appears dirty (place the waste in the sump).
individual gear decon, STB slurry, decontaminant container, contamination control line, equipment checkpoint
Operate Decontaminating Apparatus ABC-M11
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Inspect the apparatus for any damage or wear before use.
2. Ensure the decontamination solution is properly filled in the reservoir according to manufacturer specifications.
3. Turn on the apparatus and allow it to...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the apparatus for any damage or wear before use.
2. Ensure the decontamination solution is properly filled in the reservoir according to manufacturer specifications.
3. Turn on the apparatus and allow it to warm up as per the operator's manual.
4. Apply the decontaminating solution evenly over the contaminated surface using the spray nozzle or applicator.
5. Rinse the area thoroughly with clean water after decontamination is complete.
6. Dispose of used decontamination solution in accordance with hazardous waste disposal guidelines.
7. Clean and store the apparatus properly after use to maintain its functionality.

Notes:
• Always wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) when handling chemical agents or decontaminating solutions.
• Follow all safety protocols outlined in TM 3-4230-204-12&P.
decontaminating apparatus, ABC-M11, decontamination procedure, chemical agent removal
Operate Individual Equipment Decontamination Kit M280
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Inspect the kit for any damage or missing components before use.
2. Wear appropriate PPE, including gloves, goggles, and a face mask.
3. Open the decontamination solution container and pour the contents into the...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the kit for any damage or missing components before use.
2. Wear appropriate PPE, including gloves, goggles, and a face mask.
3. Open the decontamination solution container and pour the contents into the applicator device as instructed in the manual.
4. Apply the decontaminating solution to the contaminated equipment using the applicator, ensuring full coverage of the affected area.
5. Rinse the treated area thoroughly with clean water after the recommended contact time has elapsed.
6. Dispose of used materials in accordance with hazardous waste disposal guidelines.
7. Clean and store the kit properly after use to maintain its functionality.

Notes:
• Avoid direct contact with the decontamination solution unless absolutely necessary.
• Follow all safety protocols outlined in TM 3-4230-224-10.
individual equipment decontamination, M280, chemical agent removal

High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Inspect the kit for any damage or missing components before use.
2. Wear appropriate PPE, including gloves, goggles, and a face mask.
3. Open the decontamination solution container and pour the contents into the...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the kit for any damage or missing components before use.
2. Wear appropriate PPE, including gloves, goggles, and a face mask.
3. Open the decontamination solution container and pour the contents into the applicator device as instructed in the manual.
4. Apply the decontaminating solution to the contaminated skin using the applicator, ensuring full coverage of the affected area.
5. Rinse the treated area thoroughly with clean water after the recommended contact time has elapsed.
6. Dispose of used materials in accordance with hazardous waste disposal guidelines.
7. Clean and store the kit properly after use to maintain its functionality.

Notes:
• Avoid direct contact with the decontamination solution unless absolutely necessary.
• Follow all safety protocols outlined in TM 3-4230-216-10.

Operate the M17 Lightweight Decontamination System
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure for Operating the M17 Lightweight Decontamination System:

1. Transport and Setup: Transport the M17 system using a 3/4-ton trailer, 5/4-ton cargo truck, cargo aircraft, or helicopter (sling load). Set up in a secure location near...
Procedure for Operating the M17 Lightweight Decontamination System:

1. Transport and Setup: Transport the M17 system using a 3/4-ton trailer, 5/4-ton cargo truck, cargo aircraft, or helicopter (sling load). Set up in a secure location near the decontamination site.

2. Water Source Connection: Connect to a natural water source up to 30 feet (9.15 meters) away and 9 feet (2.75 meters) below pump level. If no natural source is available, use the built-in 3000-gallon (113.55-hectoliter) water storage tank.

3. Power Supply: Ensure that the small generator is operational to supply electricity for ignition and safety control functions.

4. Engine Start: Start the 7.3 horsepower engine and allow it to warm up.

5. Pump Operation: Use the self-priming pump to draw water into the system, then pressurize it using the pump assembly.

6. Water Heating: The fan assembly delivers combustion air to the heater, which heats the water through a 90-foot (27.45 meters) long coil of tubing.

7. Decontamination Process:
- For hasty decontamination: Use the system to provide pressurized water at temperatures up to 248 degrees Fahrenheit (119.88 degrees Centigrade) at a rate of up to 9 gallons (34.06 liters) per minute.
- For personnel showers: Adjust the system to deliver hot, clean water for decontamination of personnel.

8. Storage and Maintenance: After use, ensure that all components are cleaned and stored properly. The M17 is salt-water resistant and can be used in various environments.

Notes:
• This system replaces the M12A1 Decontamination Apparatus in chemical companies, improving logistics and mobility.
• It provides first-time hasty decontamination capability for battalions.
M17, Lightweight Decontamination System, decontamination, water heating, personnel shower, chemical agents
Operate the M21/M22 Modular Decontamination System (MDS)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure for Operating the M21/M22 Modular Decontamination System (MDS):

1. Transport and Setup: Transport the M21 Decontaminant Pumper and M22 High Pressure/HOT Water Module using a 3/4-ton trailer towed by an M1037 High Mobility...
Procedure for Operating the M21/M22 Modular Decontamination System (MDS):

1. Transport and Setup: Transport the M21 Decontaminant Pumper and M22 High Pressure/HOT Water Module using a 3/4-ton trailer towed by an M1037 High Mobility Multipurpose Wheeled Vehicle.

2. Water Source Connection:
- The M21 draws decontaminant from a container on the ground or from natural sources if necessary.
- The M22 module can draw water from natural sources and dispense it at variable pressures, temperatures, and flow rates.

3. Power Supply: Ensure that the system has access to power for operation of pumps and modules.

4. Dispensing Decontaminant:
- Use the M21 to dispense decontamination solution-2 (DS2) or liquid field expedient decontaminants through spray wands.
- The M22 High Pressure Washer delivers hot pressurized water up to 3000 psi at a rate of 5 gpm through two spray wands.

5. Accessories: Use the included hoses, wands, nozzles, hydrant adapters, and injector for optimal performance.

6. Hydrant Adapters: Utilize hydrant adapters to access urban water supplies if available.

7. Water Storage: Use two 3000-gallon self-supporting collapsible water tanks in conjunction with a 125-gpm-water pump as needed.

8. Decontamination Process:
- The MDS is used for detailed equipment decontamination at three stations described in FM 3-5.
- It replaces the M17 Lightweight Decontamination System and the M12A1 Skid Mounted Decon Apparatus.

9. Operational Use: Chemical companies will use the MDS to fulfill the decontamination requirements of the initial wash, decontaminant application, and rinse steps of detailed equipment decontamination.

Notes:
• The MDS is fielded to dual-purpose smoke/chemical companies for operational decontamination.
• Non-chemical units may be provided components of the MDS for operational use.
M21, M22, Modular Decontamination System, decontaminant pumper, high pressure washer, water storage
Overboot and Glove Removal Procedure
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Task: Remove overboots and gloves

### Attendant's Actions:
• This procedure is not fully detailed in the provided text but would typically involve assisting the soldier to remove contaminated overboots and gloves safely, ensuring that no...
Task: Remove overboots and gloves

### Attendant's Actions:
• This procedure is not fully detailed in the provided text but would typically involve assisting the soldier to remove contaminated overboots and gloves safely, ensuring that no further contamination spreads. Specific steps may include:
- Assisting the soldier in removing the overboot by grasping the cuff and pulling it off carefully.
- Removing the glove by peeling it off from the wrist, taking care not to touch contaminated surfaces.
• The exact process would be determined based on standard operating procedures for decontamination stations.
overboot removal, glove removal, contamination control, decon station
Overboot and Hood Decontamination Procedure
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Task: Neutralize gross contamination on overboots, lower trouser legs, protective mask, and hood

### Soldier's Actions:
1. Walks into the shuffle pit and spreads his legs apart (double-shoulder width), bends at the waist, and uses his hands...
Task: Neutralize gross contamination on overboots, lower trouser legs, protective mask, and hood

### Soldier's Actions:
1. Walks into the shuffle pit and spreads his legs apart (double-shoulder width), bends at the waist, and uses his hands to thoroughly rub the STB dry mix or the STB slurry on his overboots and lower trouser legs.

2. Buddy #1 cuts the shoulder straps and drawcord on Buddy #2’s hood. He pulls Buddy #2’s hood inside out over the front of the mask, being careful not to touch the exposed neck or head. Buddy #1 gathers Buddy #2’s hood in one hand and using a cutting tool, cuts away the hood as close as possible to the eye-lens outsert, voicemitter, and inlet-valve cover. Make sure nothing is left dangling below the bottom of the mask. If a replacement hood is not available, decon Buddy #2’s hood in the same manner as for a MOPP-gear exchange.

### Attendant's Actions:
1. Instructs Buddy #1 on how to roll Buddy #2’s hood to ensure that it is accomplished to standard.
2. Monitors the shuffle pit and adds more STB after 10 soldiers have processed through it.
overboot decon, hood removal, STB dry mix, shuffle pit, MOPP-gear exchange
Overgarment Removal Procedure
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Task: Remove contaminated overgarment before the agent penetrates material and touches the undergarments or the skin

### Attendant's Actions:
1. Assists the soldier in removing his overgarment.
2. **Cuts or removes the M9 detector paper...
Task: Remove contaminated overgarment before the agent penetrates material and touches the undergarments or the skin

### Attendant's Actions:
1. Assists the soldier in removing his overgarment.
2. Cuts or removes the M9 detector paper from around the soldier’s wrist.
3. Unfastens the Velcro closure over the jacket zipper.
4. Unties the waist cord and unfastens the Velcro straps on the soldier’s jacket.
5. Unfastens the back snaps.
6. Cuts or removes the M9 detector paper from the soldier’s trousers, if binding.
7. Unfastens the Velcro straps and zipper on the cuffs of the soldier’s trousers.
8. Unfastens the front waist snaps and unzips the front zipper. He has the soldier lift one leg and point that foot down and bend slightly at the knees for stability.
9. Grasps the cuff of the elevated boot with a hand on each side and pulls the cuff in an alternating, jerking motion until the soldier can step out of the trouser leg. The process is repeated on the other leg.
overgarment removal, M9 detector paper, jacket zipper, trousers removal, stability position
Perform Dry Decontamination on Vehicle Interiors Using HEPA Vacuums
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Dry decontamination is effective for non-porous surfaces such as fabric and seats in vehicle interiors. The procedure involves:

1. Use HEPA vacuum systems to remove contaminants from the interior of vehicles.
2. **Spray-and-vacuum...
Dry decontamination is effective for non-porous surfaces such as fabric and seats in vehicle interiors. The procedure involves:

1. Use HEPA vacuum systems to remove contaminants from the interior of vehicles.
2. Spray-and-vacuum technologies can be used, which involve:
- Spraying a powdery substance throughout the vehicle, especially in delicate or inaccessible components.
- Allowing the powder to absorb for approximately 30 minutes.
- Vacuuming the substance after absorption.

> Note: These tools may not be widely available. Planners must include access methods in their response plans if they intend to use them.
dry decontamination, HEPA vacuum, vehicle interiors, nuclear detonation
Perform Wet Decontamination on Vehicle Exteriors
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Wet decontamination is more effective for non-porous surfaces and involves the following steps:

1. Apply detergent using long-handled brushes to remove contaminated dust, mud, and debris.
2. Use water pressure of 60–120 psi and spray it...
Wet decontamination is more effective for non-porous surfaces and involves the following steps:

1. Apply detergent using long-handled brushes to remove contaminated dust, mud, and debris.
2. Use water pressure of 60–120 psi and spray it onto vehicle exteriors for 2–3 minutes to effectively decontaminate surfaces.

> Note: Some advanced wet decontamination tools may be more effective than water alone but are not always readily available. Planners must include access methods in their response plans if they intend to use them.

> Water Resource Consideration: Wet decontamination sometimes involves large volumes of water, so planners must consider the availability of water resources when choosing appropriate decontamination methods.
wet decontamination, vehicle exteriors, detergent, high-pressure water
Personal Wipe Down Techniques
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Personal Wipe Down:

1. Timing: Perform the personal wipe-down within 15 minutes of being contaminated.
2. Use detection tools: Use the CAM and/or M8/M9 detector paper to detect and monitor contamination on equipment and...
Procedure for Personal Wipe Down:

1. Timing: Perform the personal wipe-down within 15 minutes of being contaminated.
2. Use detection tools: Use the CAM and/or M8/M9 detector paper to detect and monitor contamination on equipment and personnel.
3. Wipe down process:
- Wipe all exposed skin areas thoroughly, paying special attention to any areas that may have come into contact with contaminants.
- Ensure that all contaminated gear is wiped down as well.
4. Monitor for residual contamination: After wipe-down, use the CAM or M8/M9 detector paper again to check for any remaining contamination.
5. Repeat if necessary: If contamination is detected after the initial wipe-down, repeat the process until no further contamination is found.
personal wipe down,CAM,M8/M9 detector paper,decon monitoring
Plan for Decontamination of Critical Infrastructure and Waste Management Operations
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Coordinate with waste management personnel in your district during planning and response activities.
2. Develop plans to decontaminate critical infrastructure and support extended response operations related to waste...
Procedure:

1. Coordinate with waste management personnel in your district during planning and response activities.
2. Develop plans to decontaminate critical infrastructure and support extended response operations related to waste management.
3. Ensure that all waste management procedures are aligned with nuclear detonation response protocols.
4. Include decontamination of waste management facilities as part of the overall emergency response plan.
5. Train personnel on proper decontamination techniques for both hazardous materials and radioactive fallout.
6. Maintain communication channels between planners, emergency response officials, and waste management teams to ensure a unified response strategy.
decontamination,waste management,nuclear detonation,response planning
Prepare Chlorine Solution for Patient Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Preparation of Chlorine Solutions:

1. 5 Percent Chlorine Solution:
- Use calcium hypochlorite granules (HTH) or sodium hypochlorite (household bleach).
- For 5 gallons of water:
- HTH: 48 ounces or 40 heaping spoonfuls.
...
Preparation of Chlorine Solutions:

1. 5 Percent Chlorine Solution:
- Use calcium hypochlorite granules (HTH) or sodium hypochlorite (household bleach).
- For 5 gallons of water:
- HTH: 48 ounces or 40 heaping spoonfuls.
- Household Bleach: 2 quarts.

2. 0.5 Percent Chlorine Solution:
- Use calcium hypochlorite granules (HTH) or sodium hypochlorite (household bleach).
- For 5 gallons of water:
- HTH: 6 ounces or 5 heaping spoonfuls.
- Household Bleach: Not recommended for this concentration, as household bleach is approximately a 5 percent solution.

Measurement Notes:
• When using HTH in bulk, use the plastic spoon supplied with your meal, ready-to-eat (MRE).
• Use a heaping spoonful of chlorine granules (all that the spoon will hold). Do not shake any granules off the spoon before adding to the water.

Important:
• Do not dilute household bleach in water, as it is already approximately a 5 percent solution.
chlorine solution, decontamination, HTH, sodium hypochlorite, patient decon
Prepare for Operational Decontamination (Preparation Phase)
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: Set up the site and prepare for operational decon.

Steps:
1. Decision to conduct operational decon: This is determined based on contamination levels, as described in Chapter 1 of the source material.
2. **Identify vehicles...
Objective: Set up the site and prepare for operational decon.

Steps:
1. Decision to conduct operational decon: This is determined based on contamination levels, as described in Chapter 1 of the source material.
2. Identify vehicles needing decontamination: Use the CAM (Contaminated Area Marker) system to identify contaminated vehicles.
3. Request PDDE support: Coordinate with higher headquarters (HQ) and request support from the Personnel Decontamination Detachment (PDDE).
4. Select a decon site:
- The site may be designated in the operation order (OPORD) or selected based on current METT-TC conditions.
