jungle,chemical agents,MOPP gear,vapor hazards,persistent agents
Proper Use and Maintenance of Saratoga Suit
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Wear the Saratoga suit in NBC contaminated environments to protect against chemical agent vapors, liquid droplets, biological agents, toxins, and radioactive particles.
2. The suit is a reusable, two-piece ensemble consisting...
Procedure:

1. Wear the Saratoga suit in NBC contaminated environments to protect against chemical agent vapors, liquid droplets, biological agents, toxins, and radioactive particles.
2. The suit is a reusable, two-piece ensemble consisting of a coat with an integrated hood and trousers made of a cotton ripstop outer layer and filter layer containing carbon spheres that absorb chemical agents before they reach the inner layer.
3. In non-NBC contaminated environments, the Saratoga may be laundered up to four times during its service life.
4. The suit provides protective capabilities for 30 days with active protection of 24 hours during that period.
5. Do not attempt to decontaminate or reimpregnate the suit after exposure to chemical agents.
6. After exposure, discard the Saratoga suit within 24 hours to ensure safety.
7. The suit protects against chemical agent vapors, aerosols, and droplets, as well as all known biological agents.

Key Specifications:
• Protection Period: 24 hours
• Durability: 30 days continuous wear
• Concentration Resistance: 10 mg/m² challenge for chemical agents and any battlefield challenge for biological agents
• Max Effective Temperature: 120°F (48.9°C)
• Storage Life: 13 years
• Weight: Approximately 4.7 pounds (2.13 kilograms)
Saratoga suit, chemical protection, NBC contamination, decontamination, protective gear
Use of J-FIRE Ensemble for Firefighters
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Utilize the Joint Firefighter Integrated Response Ensemble (J-FIRE) during fire fighting, life saving, and rescue operations in toxic chemical/biological environments.
2. The ensemble includes a chemical/biological protective...
Procedure:

1. Utilize the Joint Firefighter Integrated Response Ensemble (J-FIRE) during fire fighting, life saving, and rescue operations in toxic chemical/biological environments.
2. The ensemble includes a chemical/biological protective mask with positive pressure air, self-contained breathing capabilities, and non-pressure breathing protection.
3. J-FIRE reduces thermal burden on firefighters while providing 24 hours of continuous liquid/vapor protection after six launderings.
4. It is intended for use by Air Force and Army firefighters in hazardous environments where chemical/biological threats are present.
5. The mask combines multiple protective features to ensure safety during operations in contaminated areas.
J-FIRE, firefighter gear, NBC protection, chemical/biological ensemble
Using M9 Chemical Agent Detector Paper
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Use the M9 chemical agent detector paper to detect the presence of liquid nerve agents (G and V) or blister agents (H and L).
2. The paper changes color to red or reddish when exposed to a chemical agent.
3. **Read the...
Procedure:

1. Use the M9 chemical agent detector paper to detect the presence of liquid nerve agents (G and V) or blister agents (H and L).
2. The paper changes color to red or reddish when exposed to a chemical agent.
3. Read the paper only under white-light sources, as other lighting may produce false readings.
4. Self-adhesive M9 paper can be attached to most surfaces, including clothing and equipment.
5. When attaching to clothing, place it on the upper portion of your right arm, left wrist, and either your left or right ankle for adequate representation of contamination.
6. When placing on equipment, ensure the location is clean (free from dirt, oil, and grease) and not likely to be stepped on.
7. Remove M9 paper before spraying DS2 over it; otherwise, removal becomes nearly impossible.
8. M9 paper is usable in temperatures above 32°F (0°C) but may produce false readings at extremely high temperatures or due to scuffs, organic liquids, and DS2 (which turns the paper blue).

Notes:
• If the paper shows spots or streaks of pink, red-brown, red-purple, or any shade of red, assume it has been exposed to a chemical agent.
• See TM 3-6665-311-10 for further information.
M9 detector paper, chemical agent detection, DS2 solvent, false readings, nerve agents, blister agents
Apply Flexibility in MOPP System Usage
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
MOPP (Mission Oriented Protective Posture) is not a fixed or rigid system. Flexibility allows subordinate commanders to adjust the amount of MOPP protection required based on their situation while maintaining combat effectiveness.

###...
MOPP (Mission Oriented Protective Posture) is not a fixed or rigid system. Flexibility allows subordinate commanders to adjust the amount of MOPP protection required based on their situation while maintaining combat effectiveness.

### Flexibility Examples
• Commanders can place all or part of units in different MOPP levels or authorize variations within a given level.
• Example: At high probability of chemical attack, two soldiers man an LP/OP; one wears a mask and the other does not. This ensures that if an attack occurs, one soldier is already masked and can continue the mission.

### Benefits of Flexibility
• Reduces heat stress and performance degradation while still allowing mission accomplishment.
• Commanders make on-the-spot decisions to modify MOPP based on threat, temperature, and unit work intensity.
MOPP flexibility, combat effectiveness, mission accomplishment, heat stress reduction
Conduct CPOG Risk Assessment
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
CPOG Risk Assessment:

• Extending the wear of the CPOG beyond 14 days is an option for the commander, but there is insufficient information available from testing to provide an estimated risk of injury or incapacitation associated with...
CPOG Risk Assessment:

• Extending the wear of the CPOG beyond 14 days is an option for the commander, but there is insufficient information available from testing to provide an estimated risk of injury or incapacitation associated with extending CPOG wear beyond 14 days.

• The heavy impregnation of charcoal in the lining of the CPOG serves to continue providing protection against exposure to liquid chemical agents.

• The chemical protection provided by the CPOG is superior to that provided by any other type of field expedient protection such as wet weather gear.

• In summary, the BDO and CPOG are both well-designed effective garments, but the preferred chemical overgarment for use is the BDO. The BDO's improved design and capabilities enhance the protection offered to the soldier.
CPOG, risk assessment, chemical overgarment, protection, charcoal lining, BDO, NBC defense
JSLIST Overgarment Features and Benefits
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Use the JSLIST overgarment as part of the next generation chemical/biological protective clothing ensemble.
2. The design includes raglan sleeves for increased maneuverability, an integrated hood that eliminates the need for a...
Procedure:

1. Use the JSLIST overgarment as part of the next generation chemical/biological protective clothing ensemble.
2. The design includes raglan sleeves for increased maneuverability, an integrated hood that eliminates the need for a heavy butyl rubber hood, and is lighter and more supportable than previous models.
3. The JSLIST overgarment can be laundered up to six times, making it more durable and reusable compared to older models like the Battle Dress Overgarment.
4. It provides durability for 45 days of wear, extending beyond the 30-day durability of the Battle Dress Overgarment.
5. The JSLIST system reduces heat stress associated with protective gear while maintaining full chemical/biological protection capabilities.
JSLIST, overgarment, chemical protection, NBC gear, durability
Proper Use of Patient Protective Wrap (PPW)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Use the Patient Protective Wrap (PPW) to protect patients from all known chemical agents for up to six continuous hours.
2. The PPW is a single-use item and should be discarded after use.
3. It is a sturdy, lightweight item...
Procedure:

1. Use the Patient Protective Wrap (PPW) to protect patients from all known chemical agents for up to six continuous hours.
2. The PPW is a single-use item and should be discarded after use.
3. It is a sturdy, lightweight item weighing approximately 2.7 kg.
4. Although permeable to both oxygen and carbon dioxide, the rate at which carbon dioxide is produced by a typical patient exceeds the rate at which gas passes through the wrap.
5. Do not leave a patient in the PPW for longer than six hours to avoid potential health risks due to CO₂ buildup.
6. The wrap provides protection against chemical agents but should be used only as directed.
PPW, patient protection, chemical agent, single-use item, NBC gear
Replace BDO Due to Damage or Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:
1. Inspect the BDO (Biological, Chemical, and Radiological Overgarment) for any of the following:
- Rips or tears in the fabric
- Broken or missing fasteners
- Exposure to petroleum, oils, or lubricants (POL)
2. If any of the...
Procedure:
1. Inspect the BDO (Biological, Chemical, and Radiological Overgarment) for any of the following:
- Rips or tears in the fabric
- Broken or missing fasteners
- Exposure to petroleum, oils, or lubricants (POL)
2. If any of the above conditions are present, replace the BDO immediately.
3. For example, if a POL spill on a BDO sleeve or trouser leg soaks through the material, replace the BDO.
4. The BDO remains serviceable if the vapor-barrier bag is damaged (e.g., pinholes, rips, tears), provided:
- The overgarment has not been physically damaged
- It has not been exposed to water, POL spills, or chemical agents
5. If packaging leaks are discovered, seal/repair them as soon as possible.
6. Use common duct tape as an appropriate and expedient method for repairing the vapor-barrier bag.
7. Sealing the bag protects the BDO from direct exposure to moisture, smoke, and fuel solvent vapors that can jeopardize its protective qualities.
8. If the original vapor-barrier bag is no longer available, use a replacement storage bag that is at minimum water resistant or water repellent.
BDO, chemical protection, overgarment, replacement, damage, contamination
Use Protective Gloves for Decontaminated Equipment Handling
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Protective gloves must be worn at MOPP1 through MOPP3 when handling equipment that has been decontaminated.

### Purpose
• Prevents contact with chemical agents that may have been absorbed by equipment surfaces.
• Reduces risk of...
Protective gloves must be worn at MOPP1 through MOPP3 when handling equipment that has been decontaminated.

### Purpose
• Prevents contact with chemical agents that may have been absorbed by equipment surfaces.
• Reduces risk of contamination and exposure to soldiers.
protective gloves, decontaminated equipment, chemical agent prevention
Conduct Chemical Overgarment Risk Assessment
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Leaders conduct MOPP analysis to determine what protection level (e.g., MOPP 0, MOPP1, etc.) should be used. Decisions are made based on an analysis of mission, threat, and the risk a commander is willing to accept.

For example, some commanders...
Leaders conduct MOPP analysis to determine what protection level (e.g., MOPP 0, MOPP1, etc.) should be used. Decisions are made based on an analysis of mission, threat, and the risk a commander is willing to accept.

For example, some commanders in Southwest Asia during Operation Desert Shield made an initial decision that their units should go to MOPP1 and don their initial set of contingency overgarments. Subsequently, they had to decide when to replace these materials or vehicle parts.

Chemical contamination may be detected initially when using chemical detectors. Remember, going to MOPP open or unmasking is a command decision.
MOPP analysis, overgarment risk assessment, chemical detection, NBC protection
Decontaminating Chemical Protective Footwear Covers (CPFCs)
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Purpose: Decontaminate CPFCs to maintain their protective capabilities against chemical agents.

Procedure:
1. Use a 5% bleach and water solution or a 5% HTH and water solution for decontamination.
2. Ensure the overboot is fully submerged...
Purpose: Decontaminate CPFCs to maintain their protective capabilities against chemical agents.

Procedure:
1. Use a 5% bleach and water solution or a 5% HTH and water solution for decontamination.
2. Ensure the overboot is fully submerged in the solution, if possible, or wipe it thoroughly with a cloth soaked in the solution.
3. Allow the overboot to air dry completely after decontamination.
4. Inspect the overboot for signs of deterioration (e.g., rips, tears, torn laces, sticky rubber) following decontamination.
5. Replace the overboot if any signs of deterioration are found.

Notes:
• Refer to FM 3-5 for detailed information on deliberate decontamination processes.
• Decontaminated overboots must be stored in a clean, dry location until reuse.
decontamination,cpfc,overboots,chemical protection,nuclear cbrn
Donning Chemical Protective Footwear Covers (CPFCs)
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Purpose: Properly donning chemical protective footwear covers to ensure protection from chemical agents.

Procedure:
1. Check the type of CPFC being worn (single heal flap or newer fishtail double heel flap).
2. Refer to specific donning...
Purpose: Properly donning chemical protective footwear covers to ensure protection from chemical agents.

Procedure:
1. Check the type of CPFC being worn (single heal flap or newer fishtail double heel flap).
2. Refer to specific donning instructions provided for each variation before wearing.
3. Ensure the overboots are placed over combat boots and not worn directly on the skin.
4. Secure the laces properly to avoid snagging on protrusions (e.g., tanks) that could cause tearing or puncturing.
5. Avoid traversing rough terrain while wearing the overboot to prevent tears or punctures.

Notes:
• Replace the overboot if it is punctured, torn, or shows signs of deterioration (e.g., rips, torn laces, sticky rubber).
• The CPFCs are being replaced by GVO/BVOs as stocks become available.
donning,cpfc,overboots,chemical protection,footwear covers,nuclear cbrn
Inspecting and Replacing Chemical Protective Footwear Covers (CPFCs)
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Purpose: Ensure the continued serviceability of CPFCs to maintain protection against chemical agents.

Procedure:
1. Inspect CPFCs regularly for signs of wear or damage, such as rips, tears, torn laces, or sticky rubber.
2. Replace any...
Purpose: Ensure the continued serviceability of CPFCs to maintain protection against chemical agents.

Procedure:
1. Inspect CPFCs regularly for signs of wear or damage, such as rips, tears, torn laces, or sticky rubber.
2. Replace any overboot that shows signs of deterioration following decontamination or normal use.
3. Avoid traversing rough terrain while wearing the overboot to prevent punctures and tears.
4. Ensure laces are properly secured to avoid snagging on protrusions (e.g., tanks) that could cause damage.
5. Replace CPFCs with GVO/BVOs as they become available in the field.

Notes:
• Damaged or deteriorated overboots must be discarded immediately and not reused.
• Regular inspection is critical to maintaining protective capabilities.
replacement,cpfc,overboots,inspection,nuclear cbrn
Modify MOPP Gear for Ventilation and Comfort
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
MOPP Open Configurations allow soldiers to adjust gear for ventilation and comfort at certain levels:

• At MOPP1, MOPP2, or MOPP3, soldiers may leave the overgarment jacket open.
• At MOPP3, soldiers may leave the hood open or rolled...
MOPP Open Configurations allow soldiers to adjust gear for ventilation and comfort at certain levels:

• At MOPP1, MOPP2, or MOPP3, soldiers may leave the overgarment jacket open.
• At MOPP3, soldiers may leave the hood open or rolled (referred to as "MOPP open").

### Commander's Decision
Commanders decide which variations to use based on:
• Threat level
• Temperature conditions
• Unit work intensity
MOPP open, ventilation, gear modification, commander's discretion
Proper Use and Storage of Chemical Protective Overgarment (CPOG)
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:
1. The CPOG is a plain OD green expendable two-piece overgarment consisting of:
- One coat
- A pair of trousers
2. The jacket has a full-length zipper opening covered by a protective flap.
3. The trousers have a fly front and...
Procedure:
1. The CPOG is a plain OD green expendable two-piece overgarment consisting of:
- One coat
- A pair of trousers
2. The jacket has a full-length zipper opening covered by a protective flap.
3. The trousers have a fly front and zipper closure on the lower outside section of each leg.
4. The CPOG is made of material with an outer layer of nylon cotton and an inner layer of charcoal impregnated polyurethane foam.
5. Some charcoal may be deposited on the skin and clothing under the overgarment, but this does not detract from its chemical protective characteristics or harm the wearer.
6. The CPOG comes sealed in a vapor-barrier bag, which protects against rain, moisture, and sunlight.
7. To protect the CPOG’s protective qualities:
- Wear wet weather gear over the overgarment when exposed to rain
8. Normally wear the CPOG over the duty uniform; however:
- In high temperatures, it may be worn over underwear
- In extreme cold weather, it is sized to wear over arctic extreme cold weather environmental clothing
- Mission requirements may dictate wearing the CPOG under arctic clothing (e.g., white arctic outergarment for concealment)
CPOG, chemical protection, storage, overgarment use
Using NBC Protective Covers (NBC-PC)
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Purpose: Protect supplies and equipment from contamination by liquid agents, biological agents, and radioactive dust.

Procedure:
1. Select the appropriate NBC protective cover (NBC-PC) for use in the field.
2. Place the cover over the...
Purpose: Protect supplies and equipment from contamination by liquid agents, biological agents, and radioactive dust.

Procedure:
1. Select the appropriate NBC protective cover (NBC-PC) for use in the field.
2. Place the cover over the supplies or equipment to be protected, ensuring full coverage of all surfaces.
3. Secure the cover tightly around the edges to prevent liquid agents from seeping through.
4. Ensure the cover is used as a barrier between covered items and potential contamination sources (e.g., liquid agents, biological agents, radioactive dust).
5. Replace the cover if it becomes damaged or begins to break down due to environmental conditions.

Notes:
• The NBC-PC provides protection for up to 48 hours against liquid agents.
• It can provide protection for up to six weeks without agent exposure before beginning to break down.
nbc protective cover,nuclear cbrn,chemical protection
Using Protective Masks (M17-Series and M40-Series)
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Purpose: Protect wearers from breathing air contaminated with chemical and/or biological agents.

Procedure:
1. Select the appropriate protective mask based on the situation: field masks (e.g., M17-series) or specialized masks (e.g.,...
Purpose: Protect wearers from breathing air contaminated with chemical and/or biological agents.

Procedure:
1. Select the appropriate protective mask based on the situation: field masks (e.g., M17-series) or specialized masks (e.g., M40-series, M24/M25-series, M42, M43, special purpose masks).
2. Ensure the mask is properly fitted to the face and secured with the appropriate straps.
3. Check for any signs of damage or wear on the mask before use.
4. Replace the mask if it becomes damaged or compromised during use.
5. Use the mask in accordance with its designated purpose (e.g., field, tank, aircraft, combat vehicle, aviator).

Notes:
• The M17-series is currently issued to every soldier and will be replaced by the M40-series.
• Masks must be used as directed for their specific applications.
protective masks,nuclear cbrn,chemical protection
Using the Chemical Protective Helmet Cover (PASGT)
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Purpose: Protect the PASGT helmet from chemical and biological contamination.

Procedure:
1. Ensure the helmet cover is made of butyl-coated nylon cloth with an elastic web enclosed in the hem.
2. Place the cover over the PASGT helmet,...
Purpose: Protect the PASGT helmet from chemical and biological contamination.

Procedure:
1. Ensure the helmet cover is made of butyl-coated nylon cloth with an elastic web enclosed in the hem.
2. Place the cover over the PASGT helmet, ensuring it fits snugly around the edges.
3. Secure the cover by pulling the elastic web tight to prevent gaps that could allow agents to penetrate.
4. Ensure the cover is olive green and one size fits all for compatibility with standard PASGT helmets.
5. Replace the cover if it becomes damaged or compromised during use.

Notes:
• The cover is designed to keep chemical and biological agents from penetrating the kevlar helmet.
• Regular inspection of the cover is necessary to ensure continued protection.
helmet cover,pasgt,nuclear cbrn,chemical protection
Inspect and Maintain NBC Equipment Bag
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:
1. The NBC equipment bag is used to consolidate and transport the CPOG (Chemical Protective Overgarment), chemical protective gloves, and chemical protective boots.
2. The bag is constructed of abrasion-resistant nylon and...
Procedure:
1. The NBC equipment bag is used to consolidate and transport the CPOG (Chemical Protective Overgarment), chemical protective gloves, and chemical protective boots.
2. The bag is constructed of abrasion-resistant nylon and includes:
- A unique closure system
- Velcro fastening
- Two compression straps
- Quick release buckles for protection from cargo tie-down straps
3. Attach the NBC equipment bag to the current developmental load-carrying equipment (LCE) using the quick release buckles.
4. Ensure that the closure system is secure and that all components are properly stored within the bag.
NBC equipment bag, storage, transportation, chemical protection

Fallout Prediction (26) -

Determine Relative Hazard in Overlapping Fallout Zones
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure for Determining the Relative Hazard When Two or More Fallout Predictions Overlap:

1. Identify areas where predicted fallout hazard zones overlap.
2. Assess the classification of each overlapping zone (e.g., Zone I, Zone II).
3. **The...
Procedure for Determining the Relative Hazard When Two or More Fallout Predictions Overlap:

1. Identify areas where predicted fallout hazard zones overlap.
2. Assess the classification of each overlapping zone (e.g., Zone I, Zone II).
3. The hazard classification of an area with overlapping fallout predictions should be designated as the higher classification involved. For example:
- If a Zone I and Zone II overlap, designate the area as Zone I.
- If only Zone II overlaps, designate the area as Zone II.
4. Use Figures 6-6 and 6-7 for visual reference to confirm classifications.

Note: This rule applies under nuclear warfare conditions where overlapping fallout patterns are likely due to multiple bursts occurring in close proximity.
fallout prediction,overlap zones,hazard classification,nuclear warfare,multiple burst procedures
Calculate Fallout Area at a Specific Time After Detonation
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure to determine the area of fallout deposition at a specific time after detonation:

1. Multiply effective downwind speed by time: Calculate the distance traveled by the fallout cloud using the formula: Effective Downwind Speed...
Procedure to determine the area of fallout deposition at a specific time after detonation:

1. Multiply effective downwind speed by time: Calculate the distance traveled by the fallout cloud using the formula: Effective Downwind Speed (knots) × Time (hours). For example, 16 knots × 1.5 hours = 24 nautical miles.

2. Draw a dotted arc for middle of fallout area: Using GZ as the center and the calculated distance (e.g., 24 nautical miles), draw a dotted arc across the fallout plot to represent the middle of the expected fallout area at that time.

3. Determine safety distance: From the table on the template or Figure 3-34, find the safety distance for the yield group (e.g., DELTA group has a safety distance of 5 nautical miles).

4. Adjust arc radii by safety distance: Add and subtract the safety distance from the calculated distance to create two new arcs: 24 + 5 = 29 nautical miles, and 24 - 5 = 19 nautical miles.

5. Define fallout area boundaries: The area confined between these two arcs and the crosswind boundaries of the fallout area defines the approximate region where fallout will be deposited at that specific time (e.g., H+1.5 hours).

6. Label the plot: Indicate on the fallout template: NAV EDM used, yield (estimated or actual), GZ location, and geographic chart number/scale.
fallout area calculation, safety distance, effective downwind speed, time after detonation, fallout plot labeling
Construct a Simplified Fallout Predictor
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Step 1. Select an appropriate map scale. On a piece of pliable, transparent material or overlay paper, draw a thin dotted line (reference line) to a scaled length of 50 kilometers from a point selected to represent GZ (Figure 3-27).

**Step...
Step 1. Select an appropriate map scale. On a piece of pliable, transparent material or overlay paper, draw a thin dotted line (reference line) to a scaled length of 50 kilometers from a point selected to represent GZ (Figure 3-27).

Step 2. Draw and graduate in kilometers two radial lines from GZ at angles of 20 degrees to the left and to the right of the dotted reference line (Figure 3-28).

Step 3. On the side of GZ opposite the reference line, draw a series of concentric semicircles (using the selected map scale) having radii of:

• 1.2 kilometers
• 1.9 kilometers
• 4.2 kilometers
• 6.8 kilometers
• 11.2 kilometers
• 18.0 kilometers
• 28 kilometers

These figures correspond to stabilized cloud radii from nuclear bursts with yields of:

• 2 kilotons
• 5 kilotons
• 30 kilotons
• 100 kilotons
• 3 megatons, respectively.

Step 4. Label the semicircles as follows:

• Starting with the semicircle closest to GZ and moving up from GZ, label the semicircles A, B, C, D, E, F, and G.
• Moving down from GZ, label the semicircles: 2 kilotons, 5 kilotons, 30 kilotons, 100 kilotons, 300 kilotons, 1 megaton, and 3 megatons.
nuclear fallout predictor, map scale, pliable material, transparent overlay, GZ reference line, radial lines, semicircles, cloud radii, yield labels
Determine Fallout Direction Based on Nuclear Detonation Height and Atmospheric Conditions
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Assess the height of the nuclear detonation (HOB): The height of burst determines how fallout is dispersed.
2. Monitor atmospheric conditions: Wind speed and direction at different altitudes influence fallout...
Procedure:

1. Assess the height of the nuclear detonation (HOB): The height of burst determines how fallout is dispersed.
2. Monitor atmospheric conditions: Wind speed and direction at different altitudes influence fallout trajectory.
3. Compare with ground-level wind observations: Fallout may not follow the same path as surface winds due to altitude differences.
4. Use radiation monitoring tools: To determine safety of an area after a nuclear detonation.
5. Account for precipitation effects: Radioactive particles can be deposited by rain or snow, creating local radiation hot spots.

Notes:
• Fallout direction is not always predictable based on ground-level wind alone.
• Precipitation can significantly alter the distribution of radioactive material.
fallout prediction,nuclear detonation,atmospheric conditions,radiation monitoring
Determine Fallout Prediction Procedures for Land and Sea Surface Bursts
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Significance of Fallout Ashore Versus at Sea

• The detailed and simplified procedures for fallout prediction are intended for use by all three services.
• Predictions are based on assumed land surface bursts. It is recognized that the fallout...
Significance of Fallout Ashore Versus at Sea

• The detailed and simplified procedures for fallout prediction are intended for use by all three services.
• Predictions are based on assumed land surface bursts. It is recognized that the fallout from a sea surface burst may be different, but very little direct information is available on fallout from bursts on the surface of deep ocean water.
• The sea acts as an absorbent of and shields against radioactive products. However, these products may remain a hazard on land until they have decayed.
• Recipients of warnings ashore do not have the mobility of ships at sea and must deal with the hazard in most cases.
• Ships will be particularly interested in determining the approximate area where fallout will reach the surface at a given time after burst.
• Ships with meteorological capability may obtain required meteorological data for computation of effective downwind using standard pressure level winds.
• Basic wind data for this purpose are generally available from meteorological sources (airbases, meteorological ships, or mobile weather stations).
• Ships without meteorological capability will normally predict fallout areas by using the simplified procedure.
• Fallout prediction and plotting of fallout areas on board naval ships is discussed in this chapter.
fallout, nuclear burst, sea surface, land surface, prediction procedures
Determine Fallout Zone Boundaries Using NAV EDM and Yield Group
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Step-by-step procedure for plotting fallout zones based on nuclear detonation data:

1. Estimate the yield group: If the estimated yield is uncertain, use the highest yield in the group it belongs to for safety. For example, if the estimated...
Step-by-step procedure for plotting fallout zones based on nuclear detonation data:

1. Estimate the yield group: If the estimated yield is uncertain, use the highest yield in the group it belongs to for safety. For example, if the estimated yield is 70 KT and falls into the DELTA group (which includes yields up to 100 KT), use 100 KT as the effective yield.

2. Select data from NAV EDM: Based on the selected yield group, retrieve the corresponding effective downwind direction and speed from the NAV EDM. For example, for the DELTA group, this might be an effective downwind direction of 122 degrees and a speed of 16 knots.

3. Draw the GN line: On the fallout template, draw a line (GN line) from the ground zero (GZ) through the effective downwind direction on the compass rose.

4. Determine Zone I and II distances: Using the nomogram in Figure 3-37 or similar reference, calculate the downwind distance for Zone I and double it to get Zone II. For example, if Zone I is 30 nautical miles, Zone II would be 60 nautical miles.

5. Draw arcs for zones: Using GZ as the center, draw two arcs with radii equal to the distances of Zone I and Zone II on the appropriate chart scale.

6. Determine cloud radius: From the template or Figure 3-38, read the cloud radius (e.g., 3.7 nautical miles) and draw a semicircle upwind of GZ using this as the radius.

7. Connect arcs to semicircle: Draw lines from the intersections of the Zone I arc with radial lines to connect with the ends of the semicircle, forming the fallout area boundaries.
fallout prediction, nuclear detonation, NAV EDM, Zone I and II, cloud radius, effective downwind direction
Determine Time of Completion Using Dose-Rate Readings
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
The actual time of completion may be determined by taking a series of dose-rate readings at the same location over a period of time or by looking at an NBC 4 Peak Report. The peak reading indicates that fallout is complete.

Procedure:
1. Plot...
The actual time of completion may be determined by taking a series of dose-rate readings at the same location over a period of time or by looking at an NBC 4 Peak Report. The peak reading indicates that fallout is complete.

Procedure:
1. Plot these dose rates against time on log paper.
2. The dose rate will increase, reach a maximum, and then start to decrease with time.
3. Plot a series of readings until the reading falls on the same straight line. This will indicate a constant rate of decay.
4. The time of completion can be determined from the graph by reading the dose rate on the straight line showing constant decay.
dose-rate readings,NBC4,peak report,time of completion
Determining Safety Distance for Fallout Prediction
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure to Determine Safety Distance for Fallout Prediction:

Step 1: Multiply the effective downwind speed by the time (in hours) after detonation.

Step 2: To the distance obtained in Step 1, add and subtract the **safety...
Procedure to Determine Safety Distance for Fallout Prediction:

Step 1: Multiply the effective downwind speed by the time (in hours) after detonation.

Step 2: To the distance obtained in Step 1, add and subtract the safety distance from either:
- The template for standard yield groups, or
- The graph in Figure 3-34 for any yield.
This accounts for finite cloud size, diffusion, and wind fluctuations.

Step 3: On the fallout plot (template), use GZ as the center, and draw two arcs across the fallout pattern using the distances obtained from Step 2 as radii.

• The zone enclosed between these two arcs will typically contain the area of surface deposition at a specific time after detonation.
safety distance, fallout prediction, effective downwind speed, time after detonation
Estimate Time of Arrival for Fallout Using Effective Wind Speed
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

To estimate the time of arrival of fallout at a specific distance from GZ:
1. Use the formula: _Time of arrival (hr) = distance from GZ (km) / effective wind speed (kmph)_.
2. For operational purposes, apply these rules of thumb:
...
Procedure:

To estimate the time of arrival of fallout at a specific distance from GZ:
1. Use the formula: _Time of arrival (hr) = distance from GZ (km) / effective wind speed (kmph)_.
2. For operational purposes, apply these rules of thumb:
- The actual arrival may occur as early as one-half of the estimated time of arrival. For example, if the estimated time is H+4 hours, actual arrival may be as early as H+2 hours.
- If fallout has not arrived by twice the estimated time (or 12 hours, whichever comes first), assume it will not arrive at all. Example: If estimated arrival is H+5 and no fallout has occurred by H+10, assume no fallout will reach that area.
time of arrival, effective wind speed, fallout prediction, nuclear contamination, H-hour
Generating an NBC 3 Nuclear Report for Detailed Fallout Prediction
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Collect data: The NBC collection/subcollection center must gather sufficient data from multiple sources, including:
- Numerous NAV NBC 1 and NAV NBC 2 nuclear reports from designated units.
- A visual description of the...
Procedure:

1. Collect data: The NBC collection/subcollection center must gather sufficient data from multiple sources, including:
- Numerous NAV NBC 1 and NAV NBC 2 nuclear reports from designated units.
- A visual description of the crater.
- Exact location of ground zero (GZ).

2. Analyze data: Use the collected information to determine weapon yield, GZ, and type of burst.

3. Generate NBC 3 report: Once sufficient data is available, the NBC center will generate an NBC 3 nuclear report for a detailed fallout prediction.

4. Include detailed information in the report: The NBC 3 report should provide tactical units with precise data on:
- Extent of fallout.
- Arrival time of fallout.
- Potential operational impact.

Notes: This report is intended to give tactical commanders more accurate and detailed information than the simplified NAV NBC 2 report. It allows for better planning, especially when fallout may affect operations or personnel.
nbcc, nbc 3 report, detailed fallout prediction, nuclear contamination, tactical commander, ground zero location, crater description
Plotting Fallout Using NAV NBC 2 and NAV EDM Reports
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Obtain necessary reports: Retrieve the NAV NBC 2 nuclear report and NAV EDM data.

2. Determine downwind distance of Zone I: Use the actual yield (item N in the NAV NBC 2 report) as an entrance figure in Figure 3-36 or...
Procedure:

1. Obtain necessary reports: Retrieve the NAV NBC 2 nuclear report and NAV EDM data.

2. Determine downwind distance of Zone I: Use the actual yield (item N in the NAV NBC 2 report) as an entrance figure in Figure 3-36 or Figure 3-37 to determine the downwind distance for Zone I.

3. Calculate cloud radius: Use the actual yield from the NAV NBC 2 report as your entrance figure in Figure 3-38 to determine the cloud radius.

4. Determine safety distance: Use the actual yield (from NAV NBC 2) as an entrance figure in Figure 3-34 to calculate the safety distance.

5. Prepare fallout plot: Using the calculated data, create a fallout plot on the appropriate template.

6. Indicate required information on the plot: Include the following details on the template:
- NAV NBC 2 nuclear report used
- Yield (item N)
- Date-time of burst (item D)
- GZ (ground zero) location (item F)
- Geographic chart number (scaling)
- NAV EDM used

Notes: This procedure is based on the principles described in preceding sections and includes adjustments specific to plotting fallout using NAV NBC 2 and NAV EDM data. The plot provides a quick reference for tactical commanders to understand potential fallout patterns.

Additional Actions After Plotting: If the unit is within the potential fallout pattern, the commander should:
- Start continuous monitoring of radiation levels.
- Cover supplies and equipment (including food and water).
- Warn adjacent and subordinate units about the potential threat.
- Ensure dosimeters (IM93s) are zeroed and issued to appropriate individuals.
nav nbc 2, fallout plotting, nuclear contamination, nav edm, zone i downwind distance, cloud radius, safety distance, tactical commander, fallout prediction
Plotting Fallout Using NAV NBC 3 Nuclear Report
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure for Plotting Fallout from a NAV NBC 3 Nuclear Report:

1. Obtain the NAV NBC 3 nuclear report, which contains critical information such as:
- Strike serial number (A)
- Date-time of burst (D)
- Location of burst (F)
-...
Procedure for Plotting Fallout from a NAV NBC 3 Nuclear Report:

1. Obtain the NAV NBC 3 nuclear report, which contains critical information such as:
- Strike serial number (A)
- Date-time of burst (D)
- Location of burst (F)
- Weapon yield (N)
- Direction measured clockwise from GN to left and right radial lines (Y)
- Effective downwind speed, Zone I distance, and cloud radius (Z)

2. Extract the following data from the report:
- From letter Y, determine the angle between the GN line and both the left and right radial lines.
- From letter Z, extract:
- Effective downwind speed (first three digits, in knots)
- Zone I distance (next three digits, in nautical miles)
- Cloud radius (last two digits, in nautical miles)

3. Determine the effective downwind direction:
- The effective downwind direction is the bisector of the angle formed by the left and right radial lines.
- For example, if the angles are 147° and 187°, the effective downwind direction is at 167° (the midpoint between the two angles).

4. Plot the GN line:
- On the fallout template, draw a line through 167° on the compass rose and label it as GN.

5. Calculate Zone II distance:
- Multiply the Zone I downwind distance by 2 to determine the Zone II distance (e.g., if Zone I is 8 nautical miles, Zone II is 16 nautical miles).

6. Draw arcs for Zones I and II:
- Using GZ as the center:
- Draw an arc with a radius equal to the Zone I downwind distance (e.g., 8 nautical miles) between the radial lines.
- Draw another arc with a radius equal to the Zone II downwind distance (e.g., 16 nautical miles) between the same radial lines.

7. Draw cloud radius semi-circle:
- Using GZ as the center and the cloud radius from letter Z (e.g., 2 nautical miles), draw a semi-circle upwind of GZ to scale.

8. Connect arcs with semicircle ends:
- From the intersections of the Zone I arc with the radial lines, draw lines connecting them to the ends of the cloud radius semi-circle.

9. Determine safety distance for fallout:
- Refer to Figure 3-36 in Chapter 3 to find the safety distance based on the weapon yield (e.g., for a 10 KT weapon, it is 2.5 nautical miles).

10. Calculate effective downwind distance at specific time after detonation:
- Multiply the effective downwind speed by the number of hours after detonation.
Example: If wind speed is 10 knots and the time is 1.25 hours after detonation:
_10 knots × 1.25 hours = 12.5 nautical miles._

11. Add/subtract safety distance from calculated downwind distance:
- Add: 12.5 + 2.5 = 15 nautical miles
- Subtract: 12.5 – 2.5 = 10 nautical miles

12. Draw arcs for the fallout area at specific time:
- Using GZ as the center, draw two arcs with radii of 15 and 10 nautical miles.
- The area confined by these arcs and the crosswind boundaries defines the approximate fallout zone at H + 1.25 hours after detonation.

13. Label the fallout template with the following information:
- (1) NAV NBC 3 nuclear report used
- (2) Yield and date-time of burst
- (3) GZ (ground zero)
- (4) Geographic chart number and scaling

Special Cases:
• If effective downwind speed is less than 5 knots, the fallout area will be circular.
• In such cases, the NAV NBC 3 report contains no letter Y.
• Letter Z will only contain three digits indicating Zone I distance (e.g., 010 for 10 nautical miles).
• Plot two concentric circles with GZ as the center and radii equal to Zone I and Zone II distances (Zone II is twice Zone I).
NAV NBC 3 nuclear report,fallout plotting,effective downwind direction,GZ,Zone I and Zone II distances,safety distance,cloud radius,fallout prediction,map scale
Prepare Fallout Prediction Using M5A2
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Step 1: Identify the prediction. Record the location of GZ (Ground Zero) and the date-time of burst on the predictor.

Step 2: Determine effective wind speed and downwind direction from the appropriate line of the effective downwind...
Step 1: Identify the prediction. Record the location of GZ (Ground Zero) and the date-time of burst on the predictor.

Step 2: Determine effective wind speed and downwind direction from the appropriate line of the effective downwind message.

Step 3: Determine downwind distances of Zone I and II:
1. Use the nomogram (Figure E-6) on the M5A2 to find the downwind distance of Zone I by connecting the effective wind speed with the yield point using a straight edge or hairline.
2. Read the downwind distance in kilometers at the intersection point.
3. The downwind distance of Zone II is twice that of Zone I.
4. Draw arcs between the two radial lines, using GZ as the center, with radii equal to the distances determined for Zones I and II.

Step 4: Draw left and right tangents from the cloud radius line (based on yield group) to the points where the radial lines intersect Zone I arcs. This area represents the primary hazard zone.

Step 5: Label Zones I and II clearly. Darken the perimeter of the prediction with a grease pencil to emphasize the hazardous area.

Step 6: Draw time-of-arrival arcs using effective wind speed:
1. Use the effective wind speed to draw arcs between radial lines or tangent lines within Zones I and II.
2. Label each arc as hours after H-hour (e.g., H+1, H+2).
3. Estimate times of arrival by dividing the distance from GZ in kilometers by the effective wind speed in km/h:
- _Time of arrival (hr) = distance from GZ (km) / effective wind speed (kmph)_.
4. If a time-of-arrival arc coincides with a zone boundary, extend it with a dashed line and label with the appropriate time of arrival.
5. Do not draw arcs beyond Zone II.

Orientation: Ensure that the scale of the M5A2 matches the map scale. Place the GZ point on the predictor over the actual or assumed GZ on the map. Rotate the entire predictor until the effective downwind direction in degrees aligns with GN (Grid North).
M5A2, fallout prediction, nuclear contamination, ground zero, effective wind speed, downwind direction, Zone I, Zone II, time of arrival arcs
Prepare Fallout Wind Vector Plot
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Step 1. Prepare the fallout wind vector plot as described in Appendix D to this field manual. The fallout wind vector plot is prepared each time new upper air wind data is received.
fallout prediction,wind vector plot,NBC report,nuclear contamination
Understand Naval Effective Downwind Message (NAV EDM) Format and Yield Groups
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
The Naval Effective Downwind Message (NAV EDM) contains the following information:

1. Yield Groups:
- ALFA: 2 KT and less
- BRAVO: More than 2 KT to 5 KT
- CHARLIE: More than 5 KT to 30 KT
- DELTA: More than 30 KT to 100 KT
-...
The Naval Effective Downwind Message (NAV EDM) contains the following information:

1. Yield Groups:
- ALFA: 2 KT and less
- BRAVO: More than 2 KT to 5 KT
- CHARLIE: More than 5 KT to 30 KT
- DELTA: More than 30 KT to 100 KT
- ECHO: More than 100 KT to 300 KT
- FOXTROT: More than 300 KT to 1 MT
- GOLF: More than 1 MT to 3 MT

2. Message Format:
- NAV Effective Downwind Message ZULU DDttttZ ALFA dddFFF BRAVO dddFFF CHARLIE dddFFF DELTA dddFFF ECHO dddFFF FOXTROT dddFFF GOLF dddFFF.
- ZULU DDttttZ: Date (DD) and time (ttttZ) in GMT at which the actual wind conditions were measured (e.g., 250600Z is the 25th day of the month at 0600 GMT).
- ddd: Effective downwind direction in degrees.
- FFF: Effective downwind speed in knots (e.g., ALFA 080025 means a downwind direction of 080 degrees and an effective downwind speed of 25 knots for yields of 2 KT or less).

3. Validity:
- A NAV EDM is valid for six hours from the time the winds were measured (ZULU in the message).
- If wind conditions change significantly within this period, a new NAV EDM will be transmitted.

Important: Naval ships receiving NBC reports from non-naval sources may have to convert metric units into maritime units of measurements.
nav edm,yield groups,message format,downwind direction,downwind speed,gmt
Understanding the Need for Fallout Prediction Systems
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Recognize the hazard: Understand that fallout-producing nuclear bursts create large-area radiological hazards that can cause mass casualties if not detected and mitigated.

2. Plan accordingly: Commanders at all levels...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the hazard: Understand that fallout-producing nuclear bursts create large-area radiological hazards that can cause mass casualties if not detected and mitigated.

2. Plan accordingly: Commanders at all levels must be aware of the effects of fallout and take actions to minimize its impact on military operations and personnel.

3. Identify when a fallout prediction is required: A fallout prediction may be necessary in the following situations:
- When planning to use a nuclear weapon that lacks 99-percent probability of being fallout safe or when using a contact backup fuze (as part of target analysis).
- If information indicates that fallout is occurring or will occur from a nuclear burst (friendly or enemy), enabling commanders to warn higher, adjacent, and subordinate units.

4. Use fallout prediction during time lag: During the period between the nuclear burst and availability of measured data (from radiological monitoring/surveys), use the fallout prediction as the primary source of information for estimating radiation hazards on tactical operations or plans.

5. Take action based on predictions: Use the fallout prediction to avoid contamination if possible, ensuring the safety of personnel and minimizing operational disruption.
fallout prediction system, radiological hazard, nuclear burst, commander planning, fallout safe probability, contact backup fuze, target analysis, time lag, measured data, tactical operations
Use a Simplified Fallout Predictor for Nuclear Contamination Prediction
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Step 1. Determine the downwind distance of Zone I from Figure E-6, Appendix E, using the procedures described earlier.

Step 2. Place the protractor over an actual or assumed GZ on the map and draw a line to represent the effective downwind...
Step 1. Determine the downwind distance of Zone I from Figure E-6, Appendix E, using the procedures described earlier.

Step 2. Place the protractor over an actual or assumed GZ on the map and draw a line to represent the effective downwind direction for the desired yield group.

Step 3. Place GZ of the predictor over GZ on the map, and rotate the predictor until reference lines coincide with the effective downwind direction.

Step 4. Verify the simplified fallout prediction by entering the following information on the predictor:

• Strike serial number (line Alfa of NBC 2)
• Coordinates of GZ
• Date-time group of detonation

Step 5. Identify the simplified fallout prediction using the appropriate yield group and effective wind speed from line Charlie of the EDM.

Step 6. Use the corresponding semicircle on the predictor (e.g., semicircle C for a yield of 16 kilotons).

Step 7. Read the downwind distance of Zone I (18 kilometers) from the nomogram on the predictor.

Step 8. Draw an arc between the radial lines of the predictor at a distance of 18 kilometers downwind from GZ (Figure 3-30).

Step 9. Double this distance and draw a second arc between the radial lines of the predictor at a distance of 36 kilometers downwind from ground zero.

Step 10. Draw two straight lines tangent to the appropriate cloud radius semicircle (e.g., 30-kiloton cloud radius) and extend them to where the Zone I arc intersects the radial lines.

Step 11. The area enclosed by the two lines, the cloud radius semicircle (40° angle), and the Zone I arc is Zone I.

Step 12. The area enclosed by the 18-kilometer and 36-kilometer arcs and the radial lines is Zone II.

Step 13. Draw a series of dashed arcs at distances equal to the product of the effective wind speed (16 kilometers per hour) and the hours of interest after the burst (e.g., 16 kilometers at H +1, 32 kilometers at H+2) to represent the estimated times of arrival of fallout.

Step 14. Arcs that fall outside Zone II need not be drawn.

Step 15. Draw a straight line from the center of the azimuth dial through the effective downwind direction (90 degrees) on the azimuth dial, and label the line “GN.”

Step 16. Place the center of the azimuth dial on the predictor over the estimated GZ (e.g., MN553298) on the map (the scales of the map and predictor must correspond).

Step 17. Rotate the predictor around the GZ point until the GN line is pointing toward GN.

Step 18. The predictor is now oriented so that fallout is going toward 90 degrees.

Step 19. Evaluate the area predicted to be covered by fallout.
simplified fallout prediction, Zone I, Zone II, effective downwind direction, yield group, wind speed, nomogram, azimuth dial, GZ coordinates
Adjust Fallout Predictor for Expanded Warning Area Angles Greater Than 40 Degrees
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

If the fallout wind vector plot from the NBCC (Nuclear, Biological, and Chemical Center) has a warning area angle greater than 40 degrees:
1. Update the effective downwind message to reflect this expanded angle for the affected yield...
Procedure:

If the fallout wind vector plot from the NBCC (Nuclear, Biological, and Chemical Center) has a warning area angle greater than 40 degrees:
1. Update the effective downwind message to reflect this expanded angle for the affected yield group.
2. Expand the warning area on the M5A2 predictor equally on both sides of the central line, matching the angle provided in the effective downwind message.
3. This adjustment ensures that the fallout hazard is accurately represented beyond the standard 40-degree angle of the simplified predictor.
expanded warning area, NBCC, effective downwind message, M5A2 adjustment, nuclear fallout
Interpret NAV EDM for Expanded Angles in Fallout Prediction
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
When the angle of the sector must be expanded in the NAV EDM:

1. A bracket containing a figure is added to the normal 6-digit figure.
2. The number inside the bracket indicates the angle formed by the two radial lines that must be expanded (e.g.,...
When the angle of the sector must be expanded in the NAV EDM:

1. A bracket containing a figure is added to the normal 6-digit figure.
2. The number inside the bracket indicates the angle formed by the two radial lines that must be expanded (e.g., 60 degrees).
3. This expansion means the angle is increased by the indicated number of degrees, split evenly on both sides of the downwind axis (e.g., 30 degrees to each side for a total of 60 degrees).
4. The angle expansion can also be given by adding a seventh digit to any of the yield groups.

Example: In Figure 3-23, for yield group GOLF, the increased angle is indicated as 60 degrees (30 degrees to each side of the downwind axis).
angle expansion,nav edm,radial lines,figure 3-23
Interpret NAV EDM for Low Effective Downwind Speeds
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
When the effective downwind speed is less than 5 knots for a given yield group:

1. The applicable line of the NAV EDM contains only three digits.
2. These three digits represent the downwind distance of Zone I in nautical miles.
3. An effective...
When the effective downwind speed is less than 5 knots for a given yield group:

1. The applicable line of the NAV EDM contains only three digits.
2. These three digits represent the downwind distance of Zone I in nautical miles.
3. An effective downwind direction is not transmitted in this case, as the fallout pattern becomes a circle around GZ (ground zero).
4. Zone II will be another circle around GZ with a radius double that of Zone I.
5. Use 5 knots when estimating arrival time for fallout.

Important: This special case applies only when the effective downwind speed is less than 5 knots.
low downwind speed,nav edm,zone i,zone ii,nautical miles
Interpret Special Case Fallout Patterns for Expanded Angles
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
When the fallout is not expected to fall within the normal 40-degree angle of the prediction:

1. The appropriate line on the effective downwind message will have nine digits.
2. The first six digits represent wind direction and wind speed.
3. The...
When the fallout is not expected to fall within the normal 40-degree angle of the prediction:

1. The appropriate line on the effective downwind message will have nine digits.
2. The first six digits represent wind direction and wind speed.
3. The last three digits show the angle in degrees between the left and right radial lines (see Figure 3-22).
4. This expanded angle is used to adjust the fallout prediction boundaries beyond the standard 40-degree sector.

Important: This special case applies when the fallout pattern extends outside the normal 40-degree angle of the prediction.
expanded angles,fallout pattern,radial lines,figure 3-22
Interpret Special Case Fallout Patterns for Low Wind Speeds
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
When the effective wind speed is less than 8 kmph for a given yield group:

1. The applicable line will contain only three digits.
2. These three digits represent the radial line distance (obtained by entering the nomogram in Figure E-6 with the...
When the effective wind speed is less than 8 kmph for a given yield group:

1. The applicable line will contain only three digits.
2. These three digits represent the radial line distance (obtained by entering the nomogram in Figure E-6 with the estimated yield and 8 kmph) of Zone I.
3. In this case, no wind speed is given.
4. The fallout pattern will be two concentric circles: one for Zone I and another for Zone II, which has a radius double that of Zone I.

Important: This special case applies only when the effective wind speed is less than 8 kmph.
low wind speed,fallout pattern,zone i,zone ii,nomogram,figure e-6
Ship’s Fallout Template Usage
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure for Using the Ship’s Fallout Template:

1. Obtain the ship’s fallout template (Figure 3-33), which is similar to the M5A2 predictor used by land forces.
2. Note that unlike the M5A2, the semicircles upwind of GZ on the ship’s fallout...
Procedure for Using the Ship’s Fallout Template:

1. Obtain the ship’s fallout template (Figure 3-33), which is similar to the M5A2 predictor used by land forces.
2. Note that unlike the M5A2, the semicircles upwind of GZ on the ship’s fallout template do not refer to preselected weapon-yield cloud radii.
3. Use the template for predicting fallout patterns specific to ships.
4. Follow standard fallout prediction procedures using the ship-specific template (see Figure 3-26 for comparison with M5A2).
ship fallout template, nuclear contamination, m5a2 predictor, ground zero
Simplified Fallout Prediction (Circular Case)
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure for Simplified Fallout Prediction (Circular Case):

1. Obtain the effective downwind message and identify line Bravo.
2. Determine the yield group based on the estimated yield of the nuclear burst (e.g., 4 kilotons falls into yield...
Procedure for Simplified Fallout Prediction (Circular Case):

1. Obtain the effective downwind message and identify line Bravo.
2. Determine the yield group based on the estimated yield of the nuclear burst (e.g., 4 kilotons falls into yield group Bravo).
3. Note the three digits in line Bravo (e.g., 007), which indicate a wind speed of less than 8 kmph.
4. On overlay paper, clear plastic, or an M5A2 predictor drawn to scale:
- Draw a circle with a 7-kilometer radius and label it Zone I.
- For Zone II, double the distance of Zone I (i.e., 14 kilometers) and draw another circle using the same center as Zone I.
- Label this second circle Zone II.
5. Label the prediction with GZ (ground zero) and the date-time of detonation.
6. Place the completed fallout prediction on the map for reference.
circular case, fallout prediction, nuclear contamination, zone i, zone ii

High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:
1. The outer perimeter of militarily significant contamination is the key information for units.
2. Unit procedures are:
- Simplified
- Less accurate than NBCC procedures
- Emphasize speed over accuracy.
3. Fallout...
Procedure:
1. The outer perimeter of militarily significant contamination is the key information for units.
2. Unit procedures are:
- Simplified
- Less accurate than NBCC procedures
- Emphasize speed over accuracy.
3. Fallout predictions are estimated quickly using simplified methods.
4. NBC 4 reports are plotted, but minimal effort is spent analyzing the degree of contamination.

Use Nomograms and Figures for Fallout Prediction in Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure to use nomograms and figures from FM 3-3-1 for fallout prediction:

1. Refer to Figure 3-36 (page 3-29): Use this graph or similar reference to determine the downwind distance of Zone I.

2. **Use Figure 3-37 (page 3-30) for...
Procedure to use nomograms and figures from FM 3-3-1 for fallout prediction:

1. Refer to Figure 3-36 (page 3-29): Use this graph or similar reference to determine the downwind distance of Zone I.

2. Use Figure 3-37 (page 3-30) for nomogram: Determine the downwind distance of Zone I and calculate Zone II by doubling it.

3. Consult Figure 3-38 (page 3-31): Read the cloud radius from this figure or template to draw a semicircle upwind of GZ.

4. Use Figure 3-34: Find the safety distance for the yield group (e.g., DELTA) and apply it in calculations for fallout area boundaries.

5. Cross-reference with preprinted templates: Use preprinted semicircles or other markings on the template to aid in drawing accurate arcs and lines.
nomogram use, fallout prediction figures, cloud radius determination, safety distance table

Radiation Detection (22) -

Use Radiation Detection Equipment to Identify Dangerous Areas
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Locate and avoid areas with high radiation levels.

Procedure:
1. Use portable radiation detection equipment (e.g., Geiger counters) to measure radiation levels in the environment.
2. Identify areas with irregular patches of...
Objective: Locate and avoid areas with high radiation levels.

Procedure:
1. Use portable radiation detection equipment (e.g., Geiger counters) to measure radiation levels in the environment.
2. Identify areas with irregular patches of dangerous radiation, which may be outside the main fallout zones.
3. Avoid entering or staying in areas where radiation levels exceed safe thresholds.
4. Mark high-radiation areas for future reference and avoid them unless absolutely necessary.

Notes:
• Precipitation and weather can create unexpected hotspots of radiation.
• Detection equipment is essential for verifying conditions and identifying these areas.
radiation detection,equipment use,nuclear zones,safe areas
Calculate H-Hour Using Fallout Dose Readings
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Calculate the estimated time of the nuclear detonation (H-hour) using fallout dose readings.

Procedure:
1. Collect two radiation dose readings: Ra and Rb, along with their respective times (Ta and Tb) after the...
Objective: Calculate the estimated time of the nuclear detonation (H-hour) using fallout dose readings.

Procedure:
1. Collect two radiation dose readings: Ra and Rb, along with their respective times (Ta and Tb) after the event.
2. Determine the interval between readings: Tb - Ta = time difference (in hours or minutes) between Ra and Rb.
3. Use a family of slopes chart (Figure 6-2 in FM 3-3-1) to determine the decay exponent (n) based on the measured dose rates. Alternatively, use an assumed or previously determined value for n.
4. Calculate the time difference between H-hour and Ra using the formula:
T1 = (ln(Ra/Rb)) / n
5. Subtract T1 from Ta to find H-hour:
H-hour = Ta - T1

Example:
• Ra = 112 cGyph at 0500, 15 January (Ta)
• Rb = 24 cGyph at 2200, 15 January (Tb)
• Interval between readings: Tb - Ta = 17 hours
• Assume decay exponent (n) is 1.2 from Figure 6-2.
• Calculate T1:
T1 = ln(112/24) / 1.2 ≈ 6.5 hours
• H-hour: 0500, 15 January - 6.5 hours = 2230, 14 January

Notes:
• This calculation is only valid after fallout has completed.
• Use the ABC-Ml radiac calculator for more accurate results in field operations.
H-hour calculation, fallout dose readings, decay exponent, nuclear detonation time, radiation monitoring
Determine H-hour Using ABC-M1 Radiac Calculator
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Choose two readings made at a particular location. For example:
- Time: 1600, Dose Rate: 50 cGyph
- Time: 1830, Dose Rate: 40 cGyph

2. Locate the two dose rates on the outer disk of the ABC-M1 radiac calculator.

3....
Procedure:

1. Choose two readings made at a particular location. For example:
- Time: 1600, Dose Rate: 50 cGyph
- Time: 1830, Dose Rate: 40 cGyph

2. Locate the two dose rates on the outer disk of the ABC-M1 radiac calculator.

3. Determine the time interval between the two readings (in this example, from 1600 to 1830 is a 2½-hour interval).

4. Move the intermediate disk until the time interval of 2½ hours coincides with the corresponding dose rates on the outer disk.

5. Read the time under the higher dose rate (e.g., 50 cGyph) as the total time from H-hour to the first reading. In this example, the time read is 12 hours.

6. Calculate H-hour by subtracting the calculated time from the time of the first reading:
- First reading: 1600
- Time from H-hour: 12 hours
- H-hour = 1600 - 12 hours = 0400.

Notes:
• This method assumes a decay constant (n) of 1.2, which is standard for fallout prediction.
• The ABC-M1 calculator should be used in conjunction with the NBC 3 nuclear report for accurate predictions.
• H-hour represents the time when the most intense radiation dose occurs at a given location.
ABC-M1 radiac calculator,H-hour,dose rate,nuclear fallout,prediction,radiation detection
Monitor Water Sources for Radiological Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Use the AN/PDR27 radiation detection device to monitor water sources.
2. Set the AN/PDR27 to the higher scale setting.
3. Insert the probe into a plastic bag to protect it from direct contact with the water.
4. Submerge...
Procedure:

1. Use the AN/PDR27 radiation detection device to monitor water sources.
2. Set the AN/PDR27 to the higher scale setting.
3. Insert the probe into a plastic bag to protect it from direct contact with the water.
4. Submerge the probe in the water source and take readings.
5. If high-intensity radiological contamination is detected, do not consume or enter the water.
6. Report findings to appropriate personnel for further action.

Important Notes:
• This procedure should be conducted before consuming or entering any water source in an area that may have been affected by nuclear fallout.
• Contaminated runoff can flow into lakes, rivers, and streams, creating concentrated energy levels that pose a serious health risk.
water contamination,radiation detection,AN/PDR27,nuclear fallout,monitoring
Operate the AN/UDR-13 Radiac Set
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure for Operating the AN/UDR-13 Radiac Set:

1. Turn on the device: Locate and press the power button to activate the AN/UDR-13.
2. Select mode: Use the push-button pad to select between prompt gamma/neutron dose measurement or...
Procedure for Operating the AN/UDR-13 Radiac Set:

1. Turn on the device: Locate and press the power button to activate the AN/UDR-13.
2. Select mode: Use the push-button pad to select between prompt gamma/neutron dose measurement or fallout gamma dose-rate measurement.
3. Set alarm thresholds: Adjust audio and visual alarm thresholds for both dose-rate and mission dose using the push-button pad.
4. Enable sleep mode: If not in active use, activate the 'sleep' mode with automatic wake-up to conserve battery life.
5. Monitor data readout: Observe the liquid crystal display (LCD) for real-time data on radiation levels, warnings, and mode messages.
6. Interpret results: Use the dosimeter capability of 1-999 cGy (neutrons/gamma-prompt initial and fallout) or the ratemeter capability of 0.1-999 cGy/hr (gamma fallout) to assess radiation exposure levels.
7. Document findings: Record all readings and alarms for further analysis or reporting purposes.
AN/UDR-13,Radiac Set,radiation detection,nuclear event,fallout environment
Proper Use of the Alpha Probe
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Ensure the alpha probe is in working condition and not damaged.
2. Position the probe surface less than an inch from the potential radiation source.
3. Take a reading. If the probe detects alpha radiation:
- Repeat the same...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the alpha probe is in working condition and not damaged.
2. Position the probe surface less than an inch from the potential radiation source.
3. Take a reading. If the probe detects alpha radiation:
- Repeat the same reading with a single sheet of paper between the probe and the potential source.
- The reading should return to near zero if the paper stops the alpha particles.
4. If the reading remains high after using the paper, check the mylar window on the probe for damage.
5. Replace the damaged mylar window and repeat the readings.

Notes:
• The alpha probe is a 100-cm² Zinc Sulfide scintillation detector.
• It detects alpha particles and is usually read in CPM (counts per minute).
• Accuracy ranges: ±10% above 1000 CPM, ±30% from 200 to 1000 CPM. No data available below 200 CPM.
• The PDR-77 is capable of detecting alpha activity at 56.6 DPM per 100 cm², but it is not accurate at this level.
alpha probe, radiation detection, nuclear safety, PDR-77, alpha particles, Zinc Sulfide detector
Proper Use of the Beta/Gamma Probe (AN/VDR-2)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Ensure the beta/gamma probe is properly attached to the Radiacmeter.
2. The probe contains two G-M tubes and measures external dose rate from gamma radiation, which presents the greatest external hazard.
3. If the beta window is...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the beta/gamma probe is properly attached to the Radiacmeter.
2. The probe contains two G-M tubes and measures external dose rate from gamma radiation, which presents the greatest external hazard.
3. If the beta window is opened, the probe will also measure beta radiation.
4. When operating in this mode (beta detection), use the probe only for detecting radiation, not for quantification.
5. The beta/gamma probe should be used in most situations to detect and quantify gamma radiation.
6. Accuracy: ±20% at 0.0005 cGy/hr (0.5 mR/hr). Below this level, the probe is only suitable for detection, not quantification.

Notes:
• The beta/gamma probe is used in vehicles or by individual soldiers as a hand-held instrument.
• It provides quantitative measures of radiation to decontaminate personnel, equipment, and supplies.
• It includes an audible and/or visual alarm compatible with vehicular NBC protective systems.
beta/gamma probe, AN/VDR-2, radiation detection, nuclear safety, gamma radiation
Proper Use of the X-Ray Probe (AN/PDR-77)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Ensure the X-ray probe is properly attached to the Radiacmeter.
2. The X-ray probe contains a 5-inch by 0.25-inch NaI crystal with a PMT for detecting X-rays below 95 keV.
3. Select one of the three energy ranges on the...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the X-ray probe is properly attached to the Radiacmeter.
2. The X-ray probe contains a 5-inch by 0.25-inch NaI crystal with a PMT for detecting X-rays below 95 keV.
3. Select one of the three energy ranges on the probe:
- 17 keV: Detects photons between 12.5 keV to 21.5 keV.
- 60 keV: Detects photons between 50 keV to 70 keV.
- Peak Align: Detects radiation from 70 keV to 95 keV.
4. Adjust the carrying handle to maintain an optimal height of 12 inches above the ground for measurement accuracy.
5. The probe is designed for use at nuclear weapons accidents (Broken Arrows) and optimized for detecting X-rays associated with Plutonium-239 and Americium-241.
6. It can detect 0.374 mg/m² of Plutonium-239 when the probe is one inch from the source.

Notes:
• The X-ray probe is not suitable for detecting low energy X-rays due to poor response below 80 keV.
• It should be used primarily in high-energy scenarios such as nuclear accidents.
X-ray probe, AN/PDR-77, radiation detection, nuclear safety, Plutonium-239, Americium-241
Selecting Appropriate Radiation Detection Equipment for Civil Defense
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Objective: Choose the right radiation detection equipment based on your needs and budget.

Steps:
1. Assess Needs: Determine whether you need a high-range civil defense survey meter (for measuring large radiation levels) or an...
Objective: Choose the right radiation detection equipment based on your needs and budget.

Steps:
1. Assess Needs: Determine whether you need a high-range civil defense survey meter (for measuring large radiation levels) or an ultra-sensitive health physics survey meter (for precise measurements of low-level fallout).
2. Consider Cost and Availability:
- High-range meters are essential for nuclear war scenarios, but may be harder to find and more expensive.
- Ultra-sensitive meters are useful but not essential for most civil defense applications.
3. Purchase Options:
- Buy surplus civil defense CD V-710 survey meters from companies like Survival Inc. (as of the time this book was written).
- Consider building a fallout meter using instructions from Cresson Kearny, available in appendices of this book.
4. Budget for Accessories:
- Ensure you have extra batteries and store them in the refrigerator to prolong their life.
5. Alternative Suppliers: Check yellow pages or contact companies like:
- Edmund Scientific Company
- Nuclear Associates, Inc.
- Applied Health Physics
- Eberline Instrument Company
- Dosimeter Corporation of America
radiation detection equipment, civil defense survey meter, high-range meter, ultra-sensitive meter, surplus meters, radiation dosimeters
Use the ADM-300 Multi-Function Radiac
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure for Using the ADM-300 Multi-Function Radiac:

1. Turn on the device: Locate and press the power button to activate the ADM-300.
2. Select detection mode: Use the interface to select between high or low intensity radiation...
Procedure for Using the ADM-300 Multi-Function Radiac:

1. Turn on the device: Locate and press the power button to activate the ADM-300.
2. Select detection mode: Use the interface to select between high or low intensity radiation detection, including alpha, beta, and gamma radiation.
3. Monitor readings: Observe the display for real-time data on radiation levels from radiological accidents or wartime scenarios.
4. Interpret results: The ADM-300 is capable of detecting a wide range of radiation intensities and can be used in various environments, including battlefield conditions.
5. Document findings: Record all readings for further analysis or reporting purposes.
6. Maintain the device: Ensure proper storage and maintenance to ensure continued functionality at U.S. Air Force installations and other locations.
ADM-300,MFR,Radiac,radiation detection,nuclear event,warfare radiation
Using and Maintaining a Civil Defense Survey Meter
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Objective: Properly use and maintain a high-range civil defense survey meter for accurate radiation measurement.

Steps:
1. Battery Installation: Use two D-cells and a Mallory M-215 22.5v battery (available at Radio Shack) to power the...
Objective: Properly use and maintain a high-range civil defense survey meter for accurate radiation measurement.

Steps:
1. Battery Installation: Use two D-cells and a Mallory M-215 22.5v battery (available at Radio Shack) to power the meter.
2. Calibration Check: Ensure the meter is calibrated correctly before use, especially if it has been stored for long periods.
3. **Measurement Procedure:
- Turn on the meter and allow it to stabilize.
- Place the sensor near the area being measured (e.g., outside surfaces, clothing, or equipment).
- Read the display in the 0 to 500 roentgen per hour range.
4. Recording Data: Keep a log of all measurements taken, including date, time, and location.
5. Battery Replacement: Replace batteries regularly and store spare batteries in the refrigerator to extend their shelf life.
6. Storage: Store meters in a dry, cool place when not in use.
survey meter usage, battery maintenance, radiation measurement, calibration, data logging
Building a Fallout Meter from Household Materials (Kearny's Method)
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Objective: Assemble an accurate fallout meter using materials typically found around the home.

Steps:
1. Obtain Instructions: Access detailed step-by-step instructions from Cresson Kearny, available in appendices of this book or through...
Objective: Assemble an accurate fallout meter using materials typically found around the home.

Steps:
1. Obtain Instructions: Access detailed step-by-step instructions from Cresson Kearny, available in appendices of this book or through libraries and files (limited availability).
2. **Gather Materials:
- Use common household items such as a Geiger counter tube, power supply components, and housing materials.
3. **Assembly Process:
- Follow the instructions to assemble the meter, ensuring all electrical connections are secure.
4. Testing: Test the assembled meter using a cobalt-60 gamma source or other known radiation source to ensure accuracy.
5. Calibration: Calibrate the meter for use in fallout scenarios by comparing readings with known standards.
Kearny fallout meter, DIY radiation meter, building a dosimeter, home-made survey meter
Calibration of the PDR-77 Radiac Set
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. The PDR-77 must be calibrated every six months to ensure accurate readings.
2. Calibration is performed as a single instrument, meaning that using a probe from one unit with the control unit of another set may result in...
Procedure:

1. The PDR-77 must be calibrated every six months to ensure accurate readings.
2. Calibration is performed as a single instrument, meaning that using a probe from one unit with the control unit of another set may result in erroneous readings.
3. Ensure that both the probe and the Radiacmeter are from the same PDR-77 set during calibration.
4. Follow the procedures outlined in TM 11-6665-365-12&P for proper calibration.

Notes:
• Calibration is essential to maintain the accuracy of radiation measurements.
• Using mismatched components can lead to unreliable data and compromised safety.
PDR-77 calibration, radiation detection, nuclear safety, Radiac Set
Conduct Aerial Radiological Reconnaissance (ARR)
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Conduct an aerial radiological reconnaissance (ARR) to identify and map nuclear contamination in a tactical area.

Procedure:
1. Preparation and Coordination:
- The Nuclear, Biological, and Chemical Center (NBCC) is...
Objective: Conduct an aerial radiological reconnaissance (ARR) to identify and map nuclear contamination in a tactical area.

Procedure:
1. Preparation and Coordination:
- The Nuclear, Biological, and Chemical Center (NBCC) is responsible for directing all ARR operations and radiological surveys.
- Only the NBCC has dedicated personnel to evaluate and analyze ARR data and convert it into usable form for command-wide use.

2. ARR Execution:
- The ARR team selects checkpoints, routes, and course legs upon arrival over the contaminated area.
- The NBCC preplans only the general area over which the ARR is conducted.
