- Under hostile air defense artillery (ADA) conditions, route and course leg techniques are not recommended due to their unique signatures. In such cases, the point technique with nap-of-the-earth flight at appropriate speed is recommended.

3. Flight Operations:
- The ARR team locates the edge of the contaminated area upon arrival.
- Checkpoints that can be identified from the air and on the map are determined by the team.
- Often, the team flies on an azimuth from a known point as a modification of the course leg technique.
- Altitude and airspeed are determined by the ARR team based on aerial survey guidelines.

4. Data Collection:
- The team uses selected checkpoints for route, course leg, and point techniques.
- An additional technique involves flying from a checkpoint along a compass heading while maintaining constant airspeed.
- Airspeed and flight duration are recorded on DA Form 1971-1-R.

5. Shielded Readings:
- If accurate AGCF (Aircraft Ground Contamination Factor) dose rates are determined for the aircraft, shielded readings may be taken from the same height as the aerial dose rate of the AGCF data.

6. Post-ARR Debriefing:
- A debriefing is held by the NBCC’s centralized defense team after the ARR is complete to review findings and refine future operations.

Notes:
• The product of ARR is a simplified contamination plot, which reflects results from unit monitoring and Ground Radiological Reconnaissance (GRR) reports.
• This plot has a short lifespan and is constantly updated as additional intelligence data becomes available.
• A tactical situation must be stable for a full aerial survey to occur.
aerial reconnaissance, nuclear contamination, ARR techniques, radiological survey, NBCC coordination, point technique, nap-of-the-earth flight, AGCF dose rates, DA Form 1971-1-R
Operating the IM174/PD Radiacmeter
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure for Using the IM174/PD Radiacmeter

The IM174 series is a portable tactical survey instrument used to measure gamma radiation dose rates from 0 to 500 cGyph. It is primarily used by NBC personnel during survey and monitoring...
Procedure for Using the IM174/PD Radiacmeter

The IM174 series is a portable tactical survey instrument used to measure gamma radiation dose rates from 0 to 500 cGyph. It is primarily used by NBC personnel during survey and monitoring tasks.

Steps:
1. Ensure the device is powered on and calibrated according to manufacturer specifications.
2. Hold the radiacmeter at a consistent distance (as recommended in TM 11-6665-213-12) from the area being surveyed.
3. Allow the instrument to stabilize for accurate readings.
4. Record gamma radiation dose rates displayed on the device.
5. Use the data collected to determine contamination levels and take appropriate action.

Notes:
• Refer to FM 3-3 for detailed procedures.
• Additional information is available in TM 11-6665-213-12.
IM174/PD Radiacmeter, gamma radiation measurement, NBC personnel, radiation survey
Selection of PDR-77 Probes Based on Radionuclide Type
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Identify the type of radionuclide or source being detected (e.g., Tritium, Americium-241, Cesium-137, etc.).
2. Refer to Table 7-C: PDR-77 Probe Selection Chart for guidance on which probe is most appropriate.
3. Based on the...
Procedure:

1. Identify the type of radionuclide or source being detected (e.g., Tritium, Americium-241, Cesium-137, etc.).
2. Refer to Table 7-C: PDR-77 Probe Selection Chart for guidance on which probe is most appropriate.
3. Based on the chart:
- For Tritium (3H) or Fire Control Devices: Use the µR Probe and perform a wipe test.
- For Americium-241 or Beryllium Mix: Use the Beta/Gamma Probe, Alpha Probe, or Wipe Test depending on the task (detect, measure, locate).
- For Cesium-137 or Radiation Dispersal Devices: Use the Beta/Gamma Probe, X-ray Probe, or Wipe Test.
- For Radium: Use the Beta/Gamma Probe for detection and measurement.
- For Thorium (Optical Glass): Use the Beta/Gamma Probe or Alpha Probe for detection and measurement.
- For Depleted Uranium (DU), Armor, or Penetrators: Use the Beta/Gamma Probe, Alpha Probe, X-ray Probe, or Wipe Test depending on the task.
- For Cobalt-60 or Medical Devices: Use the Beta/Gamma Probe, X-ray Probe, or Wipe Test.
4. Always use the most appropriate probe for accurate detection and measurement based on the specific radionuclide and scenario.

Notes:
• The chart provides a quick reference for selecting probes based on the type of radiation source and task (detect, measure, locate).
• Proper probe selection is critical to ensure accurate readings and effective decontamination.
PDR-77 probe selection, radionuclide detection, nuclear safety, radiation measurement
Understanding Radiological Detection Equipment
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Overview:

Radiation cannot be detected by human senses, so specialized instrumentation is required for detection and measurement. Radiation detectors must provide both qualitative and quantitative measurements to assess the hazard level based...
Overview:

Radiation cannot be detected by human senses, so specialized instrumentation is required for detection and measurement. Radiation detectors must provide both qualitative and quantitative measurements to assess the hazard level based on radiation type, energy spectrum, and exposure quantity.

Key Points:
• No single instrument can fulfill all requirements; different types of instruments are used depending on the nature of the radiation hazard.
• Commonly used detectors include ionization chambers, Geiger-Mueller counters, proportional counters, and scintillation counters.
• Each type has specific capabilities and limitations that must be understood for effective use in field operations.
radiation detection, ionization chamber, geiger-mueller counter, proportional counter, scintillation counter
Using a Geiger-Mueller Counter for Radiation Detection
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Overview:

Geiger-Mueller counters are used to detect single ionizing events and are sensitive to low levels of radiation. They produce audible clicks as an indication of radiation detection.

How It Works:
1. Detects gamma photons or beta...
Overview:

Geiger-Mueller counters are used to detect single ionizing events and are sensitive to low levels of radiation. They produce audible clicks as an indication of radiation detection.

How It Works:
1. Detects gamma photons or beta particles, with less efficiency for gamma rays compared to beta particles.
2. A discriminating shield is typically provided:
- When open: Measures both beta and gamma radiation.
- When closed: Measures only gamma radiation.
3. The sensitivity to alpha radiation depends on the window thickness of the counter.

Characteristics:
• Very rugged and reliable in field conditions.
• Energy dependent, meaning performance varies based on radiation energy levels.
geiger-mueller counter, radiation detection, beta particles, gamma photons, discriminating shield
Using an Ionization Chamber for Radiation Detection
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Overview:

Ionization chambers are used to measure dose and dose rate from gamma and x-radiation. A typical example is the pocket dosimeter, which is small enough to be worn comfortably.

How It Works:
1. The ionization chamber contains a...
Overview:

Ionization chambers are used to measure dose and dose rate from gamma and x-radiation. A typical example is the pocket dosimeter, which is small enough to be worn comfortably.

How It Works:
1. The ionization chamber contains a quartz fiber loop that moves in response to radiation exposure.
2. Radiation entering the chamber causes ionization within the sensitive volume.
3. The distance the fiber moves is proportional to the dose received.

Characteristics:
• Sensitive to shock and humidity.
• Can be read at any time without a supplementary charger-reader by simply holding it up to a light source and looking into it.
ionization chamber, pocket dosimeter, radiation measurement, quartz fiber loop
Using and Maintaining a Radiation Dosimeter for Personal Exposure Monitoring
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Objective: Monitor personal radiation exposure using a dosimeter.

Steps:
1. Wear the Dosimeter: Clip the pen-like device to your belt and wear it at all times while in potentially exposed areas.
2. **Reading Exposure Levels:
- Unclip...
Objective: Monitor personal radiation exposure using a dosimeter.

Steps:
1. Wear the Dosimeter: Clip the pen-like device to your belt and wear it at all times while in potentially exposed areas.
2. **Reading Exposure Levels:
- Unclip the dosimeter and look into the end of the device (telescope fashion).
- Read the internal scale to determine total radiation exposure received.
3. Recharging:
- Recharge the dosimeter once a week using a special recharger, which costs approximately $60.
4. Replacement: Replace the dosimeter if it becomes damaged or if readings become inconsistent.
5. **Selection Criteria:
- Choose direct-reading dosimeters in the 0 to 200 R or 0 to 600 R range for gamma radiation monitoring.
dosimeter usage, personal exposure monitoring, recharging dosimeters, gamma radiation
Using the AN/PDR27 Radiac Set
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure for Using the AN/PDR27 Radiac Set

The AN/PDR27 is a portable instrument used to detect beta radiation and measure gamma radiation. It is primarily used as a point source instrument to monitor low levels of contamination on personnel,...
Procedure for Using the AN/PDR27 Radiac Set

The AN/PDR27 is a portable instrument used to detect beta radiation and measure gamma radiation. It is primarily used as a point source instrument to monitor low levels of contamination on personnel, supplies, and equipment.

Steps:
1. Ensure the device is powered on and functioning properly.
2. Hold the AN/PDR27 close to the surface or object being tested (e.g., personnel, equipment, or supplies).
3. Observe the readings displayed on the instrument for beta and gamma radiation levels.
4. Record any detected contamination levels.
5. Take appropriate decontamination actions if necessary.

Notes:
• The device is issued to one per divisional company-size combat and support unit as needed.
• Additional information is available in TM 11-6665-209-15 and TM 11-6665-230-15.
AN/PDR27 Radiac Set, beta radiation detection, gamma radiation measurement, contamination monitoring
Storing and Maintaining Radiation Detection Equipment for Long-Term Use
Standard Source: Life After Doomsday
Objective: Ensure radiation detection equipment remains functional over long periods of storage.

Steps:
1. **Battery Storage:
- Store spare batteries in the refrigerator to extend their shelf life.
2. **Meter Storage:
- Keep meters in...
Objective: Ensure radiation detection equipment remains functional over long periods of storage.

Steps:
1. **Battery Storage:
- Store spare batteries in the refrigerator to extend their shelf life.
2. **Meter Storage:
- Keep meters in a dry, cool place away from direct sunlight and moisture.
3. **Regular Testing:
- Test all equipment periodically (every few months) using known radiation sources or calibration tools.
4. Inspection: Inspect meters and dosimeters for signs of damage, corrosion, or wear that may affect performance.
5. **Documentation:
- Maintain a log of all testing, maintenance, and storage activities to track equipment condition over time.
equipment storage, battery preservation, long-term maintenance, calibration log

Chemical Detection (20) -

Detect Chemical Agents Using M8 or M9 Detector Paper
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

Conditions: You are in mission oriented protective posture 2 (MOPP2) in a tactical environment or an area where there is a chemical threat. You are given a protective mask, a booklet of M8 detector paper, a dispenser of M9...
Procedure:

Conditions: You are in mission oriented protective posture 2 (MOPP2) in a tactical environment or an area where there is a chemical threat. You are given a protective mask, a booklet of M8 detector paper, a dispenser of M9 detector paper, an M256A1 chemical-agent detector kit, the assigned decontaminating kit (M291 or M295), DA Form 1594 (_Daily Staff Journal or Duty Officer's Log_), FM 3-11.4, FM 3-3, TM 3- 6665-307-10, TM 3-6665-311-10, and a complete set of MOPP gear or a chemical protective ensemble.

Special Conditions:
• Do NOT wear contact lenses when performing this task.
• Do NOT use masks with damaged filters because certain models contain hazardous materials.
• Do NOT change the filter element in a contaminated environment.

Standards: Detect chemical agents using M8 and M9 detector paper. Ensure that the M9 detector paper is attached to places likely to come into contact with liquid chemical agents. Detect and identify all liquid chemical agents in the area that are within the capabilities of the M8 or M9 detector paper without becoming a casualty.

Performance Steps:
1. Detect chemical agents using M9 detector paper.
2. Attach the M9 detector paper to your MOPP gear and equipment while wearing chemical-protective gloves.
- Place the M9 detector paper on the MOPP gear on opposite sides of your body.
- If you are right-handed, place a strip of M9 detector paper around your right upper arm, left wrist, and right ankle.
- Note: These are the places where a moving Soldier will most likely brush against a surface (such as undergrowth) that is contaminated with a liquid chemical agent.
- If you are left-handed, place a strip of M9 detector paper around your left upper arm, right wrist, and left ankle.
- Note: Do not attach M9 detector paper to hot, dirty, oily, or greasy surfaces because it may give a false positive reading.
- Place M9 detector paper on equipment where it will come in contact with contaminated objects, and it is visible to the operator.

Warnings:
• Always wear protective gloves when touching M9 detector paper.
• Do not get M9 detector paper in or near your mouth or on your skin.
• The M9 detector paper dye may cause cancer, but the risk is small because very little dye is used.
• M8 and M9 detector paper will NOT detect chemical-agent vapors.
M8,M9,detector paper,chemical agents,detection,protective gear,MOPP2
Operate Chemical Agent Detector Kit M256 and M256A1
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Inspect the kit for any damage or missing components before use.
2. Wear appropriate PPE, including gloves, goggles, and a face mask.
3. Turn on the detector and allow it to warm up as per the operator's manual.
4....
Procedure:

1. Inspect the kit for any damage or missing components before use.
2. Wear appropriate PPE, including gloves, goggles, and a face mask.
3. Turn on the detector and allow it to warm up as per the operator's manual.
4. Place the detector in the area of interest and wait for the device to complete its scan.
5. Monitor the display for any indications of chemical agent presence.
6. If a chemical agent is detected, follow the appropriate decontamination protocols immediately.
7. Clean and store the kit properly after use to maintain its functionality.

Notes:
• Avoid direct contact with suspected chemical agents unless absolutely necessary.
• Follow all safety protocols outlined in TM 3-6665-307-10.
chemical agent detector, M256, M256A1, chemical detection
Operate Chemical Agent Monitor System (CAM)
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Inspect the system for any damage or missing components before use.
2. Ensure all components are properly assembled and secured in place according to the operator's manual.
3. Turn on the monitor system and allow it to...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the system for any damage or missing components before use.
2. Ensure all components are properly assembled and secured in place according to the operator's manual.
3. Turn on the monitor system and allow it to initialize as per the instructions provided in TM 3-6665-327-13&P.
4. Monitor the system continuously for any signs of chemical agent detection.
5. If a chemical agent is detected, activate the decontamination protocols immediately and evacuate personnel if necessary.
6. After use, clean and store the equipment properly to maintain its functionality.

Notes:
• Always wear appropriate PPE when operating chemical agent monitor systems.
• Follow all safety protocols outlined in TM 3-6665-327-13&P.
chemical agent monitor system, CAM, chemical detection
Operate Water Testing Kit for Chemical Agents M272
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Inspect the kit for any damage or missing components before use.
2. Wear appropriate PPE, including gloves, goggles, and a face mask.
3. Collect a water sample from the area of interest using the provided collection...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the kit for any damage or missing components before use.
2. Wear appropriate PPE, including gloves, goggles, and a face mask.
3. Collect a water sample from the area of interest using the provided collection container.
4. Add the reagent to the water sample as instructed in the operator's manual.
5. Wait for the recommended time period as specified in the operator's manual.
6. Check for any color change or other indicators that may suggest the presence of chemical agents.
7. If a chemical agent is detected, follow the appropriate decontamination protocols immediately.
8. Dispose of used materials in accordance with hazardous waste disposal guidelines.

Notes:
• Avoid direct contact with suspected chemical agents unless absolutely necessary.
• Follow all safety protocols outlined in TM 3-6665-319-10.
water testing kit, M272, chemical agent detection
Conduct Contaminated Area Survey Using CAM
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Designated personnel must survey the suspected contaminated area with the CAM (Chemical Agent Monitor). Since the CAM is designed as a point detector, multiple readings must be taken in and around the unit area.

Select areas for sampling that...
Designated personnel must survey the suspected contaminated area with the CAM (Chemical Agent Monitor). Since the CAM is designed as a point detector, multiple readings must be taken in and around the unit area.

Select areas for sampling that were most heavily contaminated during the attack or low-lying areas where agent vapor may linger.

Decision criteria based on CAM readings:
• If CAM readings are three bars or less, go to MOPP open.
• If personnel show any signs of chemical agent poisoning, they should go to MOPP4.
• If meteorological conditions change, use the CAM to recheck the unit area as described above.

See Table 3-5 for allowable MOPP open times based on CAM readings.
CAM survey, chemical agent monitor, contaminated area assessment, MOPP open criteria
Simulate Chemical Agent Detection Using M8 Detector Paper
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Setup:
• Provide the items listed in the task condition statement.
• Simulate an unknown liquid chemical agent using expedient training aids (such as brake fluid, cleaning compound, gasoline, insect repellent, or antifreeze).
• Place the...
Setup:
• Provide the items listed in the task condition statement.
• Simulate an unknown liquid chemical agent using expedient training aids (such as brake fluid, cleaning compound, gasoline, insect repellent, or antifreeze).
• Place the simulated agent on nonporous material (such as an entrenching tool) for M8 detector paper.

Brief Soldier:
• Inform the Soldier that he/she will be entering an area where chemical agents have been used.
• Instruct him/her to attach M9 detector paper to his/her MOPP gear and equipment.
• Warn him/her that unsafe acts or actions that could produce a false reading will result in a NO GO score.
M8 detector paper, chemical agent simulation, detection procedure, nonporous material
Use M8 Detector Paper to Detect Chemical Agents
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Setup: Provide the items listed in the task condition statement.
2. Simulate an unknown liquid chemical agent by using expedient training aids (such as brake fluid, cleaning compound, gasoline, insect repellent, or...
Procedure:

1. Setup: Provide the items listed in the task condition statement.
2. Simulate an unknown liquid chemical agent by using expedient training aids (such as brake fluid, cleaning compound, gasoline, insect repellent, or antifreeze).
3. Place the simulated agent on nonporous material (such as an entrenching tool) for M8 detector paper.
4. Brief Soldier: Tell the Soldier that he/she will be entering an area where chemical agents have been used.
5. Instruct him to attach M9 detector paper to his/her MOPP gear and equipment.
6. Warn him that if you observe any acts that are unsafe or that could produce a false reading, you will stop the test and he/she will be scored a NO GO.
M8 detector paper, chemical agents, detection, CBRN
Using a Chemical Agent Monitor (CAM) to Detect Chemical Agents
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Prepare the CAM: Ensure the device is powered on and functioning properly.
2. Position the CAM: Place the monitor in an area where air sampling is required, such as near personnel or vehicles prior to or after...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the CAM: Ensure the device is powered on and functioning properly.
2. Position the CAM: Place the monitor in an area where air sampling is required, such as near personnel or vehicles prior to or after decontamination, inside collective protection shelters, or for monitoring relative concentrations of agents.
3. Allow Air Sampling: The CAM draws in air and samples it for contamination. It will automatically adjust its sampling time based on the concentration level detected:
- For very light concentrations, the CAM samples for a longer period to reduce false indications.
4. Monitor Readings: Observe the bar graph indicator on the device. This provides an approximation of the relative concentration of chemical agent vapor present in the air.
5. Interpret Results:
- If no vapor is detected, the instrument will not provide any indication.
- For transient vapors (due to weather, exposure time, or challenges), the CAM may give intermittent readings.
6. Use for Decision-Making: Use the relative concentration data provided by the CAM to determine the appropriate level of protective posture or to assess the completeness of decontamination.
7. Maintain the Device: Follow instructions in TM 3-6665-327-13&P for care and maintenance.
chemical agent monitor, CAM, chemical detection, vapor monitoring, decontamination, protective posture
Using the M256-Series Chemical Agent Detector Kit
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Purpose: Detect and identify field concentrations of nerve, blister, or blood agent vapors.

Components:
• 12 individually packaged samplers/detectors
• Instruction cards
• ABC-M8 VGH chemical agent detector paper
• Plastic case for...
Purpose: Detect and identify field concentrations of nerve, blister, or blood agent vapors.

Components:
• 12 individually packaged samplers/detectors
• Instruction cards
• ABC-M8 VGH chemical agent detector paper
• Plastic case for storage

Procedure:
1. Retrieve the M256-series kit from its compact plastic case.
2. Use the samplers/detectors to detect harmful vapor concentrations of nerve, blister, and blood agents.
3. Observe color changes in the samplers/detectors upon contact with chemical agents at hazardous concentrations.
4. Use ABC-M8 VGH paper for additional detection or verification if needed.
5. Follow instruction cards for proper interpretation of results and actions to take based on detected agent type and concentration.
6. Replace used samplers/detectors as necessary and store the kit securely in its case.

Notes:
• The M256-series is issued at squad, crew, or section level.
• See TM 3-6665-307-10 for detailed usage instructions.
M256-series chemical detector kit, nerve agent detection, blister agent detection, blood agent detection, chemical agent identification, ABC-M8 VGH paper
Using the M272 Water Testing Kit to Detect Chemical Warfare Agents in Water
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Prepare the Kit: Ensure all components of the M272 water testing kit are present and functioning properly.
2. Select Sampling Location: Choose a water source (e.g., well, lake, river, or city water system) that needs to be...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the Kit: Ensure all components of the M272 water testing kit are present and functioning properly.
2. Select Sampling Location: Choose a water source (e.g., well, lake, river, or city water system) that needs to be tested for chemical warfare agents.
3. Collect Water Sample:
- Use the appropriate tools in the kit to collect a representative sample of raw or treated water.
4. Test for Chemical Agents: Follow the instructions provided with the M272 kit to detect and identify dangerous levels of common chemical warfare (CW) agents in the collected water sample.
5. Interpret Results: Determine if harmful amounts of CW agents are present based on the test results.
6. Maintain Kit: Follow instructions in TM 3-6665-319-10 for care and maintenance.
M272 water testing kit, chemical warfare agents, water detection, CW agent identification
Deploying the M-21 Remote Sensing Chemical Agent Automatic Alarm (RSCAAL)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Use the M-21 RSCAAL as a two-man portable tripod-mounted system for detecting nerve and blister agent vapor clouds.
2. Detection method: Uses passive infrared sensors to detect changes in infrared energy emitted from...
Procedure:

1. Use the M-21 RSCAAL as a two-man portable tripod-mounted system for detecting nerve and blister agent vapor clouds.
2. Detection method: Uses passive infrared sensors to detect changes in infrared energy emitted from remote objects or agent clouds.
3. Line-of-sight dependency: Effective up to 3 miles with a field of view of 1.5 degrees vertical and 60 degrees horizontal.
4. Operational use: Employed for surveillance and reconnaissance missions between enemy and friendly forces.
5. Pair deployment: Where possible, deploy in pairs (two reconnaissance teams) so one RSCAAL can be used as overwatch while the other team moves.
6. Mounting options: Can also be mounted on the FOX vehicle.
7. Audio alarm range: The audio alarm is audible up to 400 meters.
8. Next-generation system: LSCAD is the next generation of RSCAAL, allowing detection on the move and from aerial platforms.
M-21 RSCAAL, chemical alarm system, infrared detection, FOX vehicle
Operate the AN/KAS-IA Chemical Warfare Directional Detector
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
The AN/KAS-IA provides standoff chemical agent detection capability for surface ships and has also been adapted to fixed-site shore facilities. It is a forward looking infrared (FLIR) based Electro-optic sensor that can remotely detect the...
The AN/KAS-IA provides standoff chemical agent detection capability for surface ships and has also been adapted to fixed-site shore facilities. It is a forward looking infrared (FLIR) based Electro-optic sensor that can remotely detect the presence of some chemical warfare agents. It also provides night vision capability for shipboard security. The standard shipboard installation consists of two AN/KAS-IA's (except for the smallest ships, which have one). Approximately 600 are deployed on surface ships.
AN/KAS-IA, chemical warfare detection, FLIR sensor, standoff detection
Operate the Shipboard Chemical Agent Point Detection System (CAPDS)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
The CAPDS is a fixed system capable of detecting nerve agents in vapor form using a baffle tube ionization spectrometer. CAPDS obtains a sample of external air, ionizes airborne vapor molecules, and collects them on a charged plate after...
The CAPDS is a fixed system capable of detecting nerve agents in vapor form using a baffle tube ionization spectrometer. CAPDS obtains a sample of external air, ionizes airborne vapor molecules, and collects them on a charged plate after eliminating lighter molecules via the baffle structure. When sufficient ion mass is collected, a pre-set potential is achieved, generating an alarm signal that is sent to damage control central and the bridge. The system is installed in an upper superstructure level and provides ships with the capability to detect nerve agents. The system will be activated when ships enter high threat areas and during operations in littoral waterways. The system is installed on most surface combatant’s ships.
CAPDS, nerve agent detection, baffle tube ionization spectrometer
Operating the M8A1 Automatic Chemical Agent Alarm System
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Deploy the M8A1 system as an automatic chemical agent detection and warning system for nerve agent vapors or inhalable aerosols.
2. Use in various configurations: Hand-carried, backpacked, or mounted on a tactical...
Procedure:

1. Deploy the M8A1 system as an automatic chemical agent detection and warning system for nerve agent vapors or inhalable aerosols.
2. Use in various configurations: Hand-carried, backpacked, or mounted on a tactical vehicle.
3. Detection method: Detects chemical agents by ionization of vapors.
4. System components: Composed of an M43A1 Chemical Agent Automatic Alarm Detector Unit and up to five M42 Chemical Alarm Units.
5. Primary use: Alert stationary units when a nerve agent vapor cloud is approaching or has arrived at their position.
6. Provide alerts: Offers both audible and visible warnings upon detecting nerve agents in the air.
7. Fielded units: Approximately 30,000 are currently in service.
8. Licensing requirement: Requires an NRC license for operation.
9. Interference note: Battlefield contaminants can interfere with the M256 and cause false readings.
M8A1, chemical alarm system, nerve agent, ionization detection
Use Collective-Protection Alarm Systems for Chemical Detection
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Collective-protection alarm systems are used in conjunction with unit TOE chemical detection equipment and warning assets to provide area warning to unit positions and occupants of collective-protection systems. Dedicated alarms are particularly...
Collective-protection alarm systems are used in conjunction with unit TOE chemical detection equipment and warning assets to provide area warning to unit positions and occupants of collective-protection systems. Dedicated alarms are particularly useful for systems that must operate alone and away from supporting units.

If an alarm is not provided, full entry and/or exit procedures must be carried out whenever anyone enters or exits the system. Even if an alarm is available, occupants must conduct internal monitoring. This can be less frequent than when there is a known external hazard.
collective-protection, chemical detection, alarm systems, entry/exit procedures
Use M8 Chemical Agent Detection Paper
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Inspect the M8 Paper: Ensure the sheet is undamaged and not expired (undamaged paper has an indefinite shelf life).
2. Apply to suspected liquid contamination: Gently touch the paper to a suspected liquid chemical...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the M8 Paper: Ensure the sheet is undamaged and not expired (undamaged paper has an indefinite shelf life).
2. Apply to suspected liquid contamination: Gently touch the paper to a suspected liquid chemical agent.
3. Observe color change:
- Yellow: Indicates presence of non-persistent G-type nerve agents.
- Black or dark green: Indicates presence of V-type nerve agents.
- Red: Indicates presence of blister (H) agents.
4. Do not rely solely on M8 Paper for identification: Use it only as an initial indicator, and confirm with another detection method such as the M256A1 Kit or other chemical monitoring equipment.

Important Notes:
• M8 paper is only effective for liquid agents, not vapor.
• It may produce false positives, so always cross-check results with additional testing methods.
• M8 Paper is commonly issued in a booklet of 25 sheets, carried by soldiers in their protective mask carriers or included in the M256A1 and M18A2 kits.
M8 paper, chemical detection, nerve agents, blister agents, false positives, liquid agent detection
Deploying the Individual Chemical Agent Detector (ICAD)
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Wear the ICAD as it is designed to be worn by an individual.
2. Ensure the detector is operational, as it does not require maintenance and has no radioactive source.
3. Monitor for alarms: The ICAD provides both audio...
Procedure:

1. Wear the ICAD as it is designed to be worn by an individual.
2. Ensure the detector is operational, as it does not require maintenance and has no radioactive source.
3. Monitor for alarms: The ICAD provides both audio and/or visual alerts when detecting nerve, blood, choking, or blister agents.
4. Use in various applications such as point detection, remote operations (connected to a radio), or mounted on vehicles.
5. Note the specifications: It weighs 8 oz and requires minimal training for use.
ICAD, chemical detector, nerve agent, blister agent, detection system
Using the M90 D1A Chemical Agent Detector (CAD)
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Deploy the M90 CAD as a man-portable instrument for detecting nerve or blister agent vapors in the air.
2. Indicate hazard levels: The device displays high, medium, and low concentrations of chemical agents.
3....
Procedure:

1. Deploy the M90 CAD as a man-portable instrument for detecting nerve or blister agent vapors in the air.
2. Indicate hazard levels: The device displays high, medium, and low concentrations of chemical agents.
3. Programmability: It can be updated to detect new agents as they are developed.
4. Transport options: Use via backpack carrier, vehicle, or fixed site configuration.
5. Power sources: Operable from batteries, vehicle power, or main power supply.
6. Remote control: Can be controlled remotely using the remote alarm unit.
7. Connectivity: Interface with a computer to pinpoint agent concentrations.
8. Operational versatility: Function in day or night conditions and used for verifying clean areas, area surveys, contamination identification, and decontamination verification.
9. Technology: Uses ion mobility spectroscopy and metal conductivity technology to monitor up to 30 chemicals simultaneously.
M90 CAD, chemical detector, nerve agent, blister agent, ion mobility spectroscopy
Using the MM-1 Mobile Mass Spectrometry Gas Chromatograph
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Deploy the MM-1 as the gas chromatograph used on the FOX Chemical Recon Vehicle.
2. Sample analysis: Heat a contaminated surface to vaporize a small sample and analyze it for chemical agents.
3. Detection capability:...
Procedure:

1. Deploy the MM-1 as the gas chromatograph used on the FOX Chemical Recon Vehicle.
2. Sample analysis: Heat a contaminated surface to vaporize a small sample and analyze it for chemical agents.
3. Detection capability: Detects not only chemical agents but also various industrial chemicals.
4. Operational use: Used primarily in reconnaissance missions for identifying contamination sources.
MM-1, gas chromatograph, chemical analysis, FOX vehicle
Using the SAW Mini-CAD for Chemical Detection
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Deploy the SAW Mini-CAD as a commercial pocket-sized instrument for detecting trace levels of sulfur mustard and G nerve agents.
2. High specificity: Designed to detect toxic vapors with high accuracy.
3. False alarms:...
Procedure:

1. Deploy the SAW Mini-CAD as a commercial pocket-sized instrument for detecting trace levels of sulfur mustard and G nerve agents.
2. High specificity: Designed to detect toxic vapors with high accuracy.
3. False alarms: Be aware that it may give false alarms from gasoline vapor or glass cleaner.
4. Portability: Ideal for use in field operations due to its compact size.
SAW Mini-CAD, chemical detector, sulfur mustard, G nerve agent

Emp Protection (20) -

Protect Against Electromagnetic Pulse (EMP) Effects
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Understand EMP Characteristics:
- Nuclear detonations produce an electromagnetic pulse that can damage or disrupt electronics such as computers and medical life-support systems.
- The strength and radius of the EMP...
Procedure:

1. Understand EMP Characteristics:
- Nuclear detonations produce an electromagnetic pulse that can damage or disrupt electronics such as computers and medical life-support systems.
- The strength and radius of the EMP depend on the type of burst and yield of the weapon.
- Significant EMP effects occur primarily with ground and high-altitude bursts.

2. Take Protective Measures:
- If warned of a nuclear detonation, immediately turn off all electronic equipment to prevent damage from an EMP.
- In some cases, burying sensitive electronics in the ground may provide additional protection against EMP effects.

3. Use Faraday Cages or Shielding:
- Store critical electronic devices inside Faraday cages or shielded enclosures to protect them from EMP exposure.

4. Maintain Emergency Systems:
- Ensure that backup systems and non-electronic medical life-support equipment are available in case of EMP disruption.

5. Stay Informed:
- Monitor for warnings about potential nuclear detonations or EMP threats, especially in areas prone to ground or high-altitude bursts.
EMP, electromagnetic pulse, radiation protection, electronic shielding
Apply EMP Mitigation Techniques in Three Environments
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Techniques - Mitigation

EMP mitigation techniques apply in the following three environments in which tactical units operate:

1. Administrative and training situations.
2. Preattack and during attack operations.
3. Postattack recovery and...
Techniques - Mitigation

EMP mitigation techniques apply in the following three environments in which tactical units operate:

1. Administrative and training situations.
2. Preattack and during attack operations.
3. Postattack recovery and continued operations.

• The distinctions made between these three environments are not hard and fast. For example, tactical operations will be conducted concurrent with the implementation of postattack recovery measures.
• The presentation of these techniques is cumulative, and the techniques suggested for administrative and training situations also should be carried forward to the preattack, during attack, and postattack environments.
EMP mitigation,environments,tactical units,postattack recovery
Avoid Adding Connectors to Hardened Equipment
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Rules of Thumb for EMP-Hardening Schemes

• Many EMP-hardening schemes create shields between the electronic equipment and external EMP environments.
• Any cable or other penetration that creates a hole or gap in the shield can seriously degrade...
Rules of Thumb for EMP-Hardening Schemes

• Many EMP-hardening schemes create shields between the electronic equipment and external EMP environments.
• Any cable or other penetration that creates a hole or gap in the shield can seriously degrade the system’s survivability.
• Field units should never add connectors to hardened equipment unless directed to do so.
• Even the small break in the shielding required “to allow a heater power cord or a ventilation hose to enter can be damaging.
EMP hardening,shielding,connectors,hardened equipment
Avoid Unapproved Additions to Protected Enclosures
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
- As a general rule, making an addition to stand-alone equipment that does not have wires or connectors running outside the protected enclosure does not interfere with the EMP hardening of the shelter.
• However, any addition of new connectors (such...
- As a general rule, making an addition to stand-alone equipment that does not have wires or connectors running outside the protected enclosure does not interfere with the EMP hardening of the shelter.
• However, any addition of new connectors (such as, running a telephone wire or extra air conditioning or ventilation hoses into a shelter) that do not go through an approved surge arrestor and penetration shield or connector, can seriously impact on the hardness of the protected area.
protected enclosure,surge arrestor,penetration shield,EMP hardening
Avoid the Single-Event Fallacy in EMP Planning
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Ensure that planning for EMP events accounts for multiple potential attacks rather than a single event.

Procedure:
1. Do not assume that an EMP attack will occur only once and then be over.
2. Anticipate the possibility of...
Objective: Ensure that planning for EMP events accounts for multiple potential attacks rather than a single event.

Procedure:
1. Do not assume that an EMP attack will occur only once and then be over.
2. Anticipate the possibility of follow-up attacks, such as additional high-altitude or surface-burst explosions after an initial EMP event.
3. Develop and maintain EMP mitigation postures consistent with tactical missions to prepare for multiple events.

Notes:
• An aggressor may use a precursor EMP attack followed by conventional attacks to exploit the situation.
• Planning must account for multiple potential EMP events.
EMP, multiple attacks, planning, single-event fallacy
Integrate EMP Mitigation into Battle Plans
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Integrate EMP mitigation into battle plans.

• AirLand Battle doctrine requires the synchronization of all elements of the force in integrated plans.
• Commanders must understand their superiors’ overall operational concepts and plans, and must...
Integrate EMP mitigation into battle plans.

• AirLand Battle doctrine requires the synchronization of all elements of the force in integrated plans.
• Commanders must understand their superiors’ overall operational concepts and plans, and must take the initiative to exploit opportunities to implement these plans as they occur.
• Effective EMP protection procedures are not confidential nor impractical. In most cases, effective EMP procedures are simply extensions of approved tactical doctrine.
• Place priority on EMP mitigation procedures that are consistent with the unit’s assigned missions and aligned with the basic operational concept—AirLand Battle doctrine.
• Place emphasis on mitigation techniques that are integrated into unit standing operating procedures, with particular emphasis on warning or short warning conditions.
EMP mitigation,battle plans,AirLand Battle doctrine,tactical doctrine
Integrate EMP Mitigation into Standard Operating Procedures
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Ensure that EMP mitigation is a standard part of unit operations and readiness.

Procedure:
1. Integrate effective EMP mitigation techniques into the unit’s standard operating procedures (SOPs).
2. Ensure units normally operate in...
Objective: Ensure that EMP mitigation is a standard part of unit operations and readiness.

Procedure:
1. Integrate effective EMP mitigation techniques into the unit’s standard operating procedures (SOPs).
2. Ensure units normally operate in a protective posture against potential EMP threats.
3. If warning of an attack is provided, implement additional protective measures within 15 minutes.
4. Train all personnel on these SOPs to ensure rapid and effective response during an emergency.

Notes:
• Standard operating procedures must be reviewed and updated regularly to reflect current threat assessments.
• Rapid implementation of protective measures can significantly reduce the impact of an EMP event.
EMP, SOPs, protective posture, emergency response
Obtain and Use Hardened Equipment for EMP Protection
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Ensure the use of equipment hardened against electromagnetic pulse (EMP) effects.

Procedure:
1. Through command channels, obtain information regarding the EMP hardness of all equipment in your unit.
2. Identify which equipment...
Objective: Ensure the use of equipment hardened against electromagnetic pulse (EMP) effects.

Procedure:
1. Through command channels, obtain information regarding the EMP hardness of all equipment in your unit.
2. Identify which equipment has been hardened through EMP shielding programs.
3. Prioritize and rely on hardened equipment for critical operations.
4. Ensure that hardened equipment remains properly maintained to preserve its EMP shielding capabilities.
5. Follow technical manuals and EMP mitigation procedures to maintain the hardness of shielding equipment.
6. Train all personnel in proper use and maintenance of hardened equipment.

Notes:
• Improper use or maintenance can reduce the effectiveness of EMP shielding.
• Ensure that all personnel understand the importance of maintaining hardened equipment.
EMP, hardened equipment, military, shielding, maintenance
Understand HEMP Components and Effects
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Understanding High-Altitude EMP (HEMP) Components and Their Effects:

1. First Component of HEMP:
- Generated by the interaction of initial gamma radiation with air molecules.
- Creates a rapidly rising, high-magnitude, short-duration...
Understanding High-Altitude EMP (HEMP) Components and Their Effects:

1. First Component of HEMP:
- Generated by the interaction of initial gamma radiation with air molecules.
- Creates a rapidly rising, high-magnitude, short-duration electric field (a few microseconds).
- Electric field strength can reach several tens of kilovolts per meter.
- Affects area as large as Nebraska or larger.
- Unprotected electronic devices may be damaged or disrupted, requiring reset or power cycling. Devices connected to long conductors are at greater risk.

2. Second Component of HEMP:
- Generated by scattered gamma rays and neutrons interacting with air molecules, creating a second pulse.
- Begins around a microsecond and lasts for many milliseconds.
- Similar in effect to a nearby lightning strike.
- Often not included in EMP assessments because lightning protection devices mitigate its effects.

3. Final Component of HEMP (Slow-Rising, Long-Duration):
- Composed of two effects:
- Blast Effect: The expanding plasma ball from the detonation disturbs Earth’s magnetic field, creating an electric field.
- Heave Effect: Two phenomena contribute to this effect:
- X-rays create a heated, conductive patch in the atmosphere that deflects Earth’s magnetic field lines, generating an electric field.
- Beta radiation works with bomb debris to create an electric dynamo in the upper atmosphere, generating an electric field.
- This component is analogous to solar activity and can result in Geomagnetic Disturbances (GMD).
- Creates a low-frequency electric field with relatively low magnitude (volts per kilometer).

4. Effects of HEMP:
- The slow-rising, long-duration portion of HEMP can have effects similar to those caused by GMDs.
- These effects are discussed in the next section on GMD.
HEMP, EMP, nuclear detonation, electric field, radiation, electronic devices, geomagnetic disturbance
Avoid Contact Between Equipment and Unintended Collectors or Antennas
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Where possible, avoid any physical contact between your equipment and unintended collectors or antennas.
2. Each point of contact provides an opportunity for EMP energy to enter and damage tactical equipment.
3. For example:
-...
Procedure:

1. Where possible, avoid any physical contact between your equipment and unintended collectors or antennas.
2. Each point of contact provides an opportunity for EMP energy to enter and damage tactical equipment.
3. For example:
- Signal and power equipment attached to commercial lines can pick up damaging surges of power.
4. Avoid unnecessary damage to civilian and military power lines, as they may be valuable for recovery and reconstitution.
EMP protection, equipment contact, unintended antennas, nuclear contamination avoidance
Avoid Creating Loops in Antennas or Collectors
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Avoid creating loops in wire and other antennas or collectors.
2. Loops act as magnetic dipole antennas, allowing magnetic EMP energy to affect connected systems.
3. Exercise caution with equipment that may have internal loops,...
Procedure:

1. Avoid creating loops in wire and other antennas or collectors.
2. Loops act as magnetic dipole antennas, allowing magnetic EMP energy to affect connected systems.
3. Exercise caution with equipment that may have internal loops, which can also function as unintended antennas.
4. Be aware of potential nonobvious loop configurations, such as:
- A phone line running from a van to a switchboard
- The switchboard linked by wire to a command post
- The command post connected to a generator via power lines
- The same generator providing power back to the van, creating an effective loop antenna or collector
EMP protection, avoid loops, magnetic dipole antennas, nuclear contamination avoidance
Avoid Over-Reliance on Expedient Shielding Methods
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Prevent the use of ineffective or impractical shielding methods for EMP protection.

Procedure:
1. Do not rely on wrapping equipment in aluminum foil or placing it in metal boxes as a general solution to EMP threats.
2. Use these...
Objective: Prevent the use of ineffective or impractical shielding methods for EMP protection.

Procedure:
1. Do not rely on wrapping equipment in aluminum foil or placing it in metal boxes as a general solution to EMP threats.
2. Use these methods only for small, redundant items such as extra calculators stored in sealed ammunition cans.
3. Ensure that critical equipment is not unnecessarily shielded if it would interfere with mission requirements.
4. Recognize that shielding all equipment in this manner may be impractical and may leave some equipment unprotected.

Notes:
• Expedient shielding methods are not a substitute for hardened or properly maintained equipment.
• Shielding should only be used where necessary and effective.
EMP, shielding methods, impractical solutions, military equipment
Avoid Reliance on Lightning Suppressors for EMP Protection
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Prevent reliance on lightning suppressors or arrestors as a method of EMP protection.

Procedure:
1. Educate personnel that military and commercial devices designed to protect against lightning strikes are generally not effective...
Objective: Prevent reliance on lightning suppressors or arrestors as a method of EMP protection.

Procedure:
1. Educate personnel that military and commercial devices designed to protect against lightning strikes are generally not effective against EMP effects.
2. Do not use lightning suppressors or arrestors as a substitute for proper EMP mitigation strategies.
3. Ensure all personnel understand the differences between lightning and EMP in terms of power and characteristics.

Notes:
• Lightning suppression devices may provide partial protection but are not sufficient to protect against high-intensity EMP events.
• Relying on these devices can lead to a false sense of security.
EMP, lightning suppressors, false security, protection methods
Avoid Use of Nonmilitary Standard Commercial Equipment for EMP Protection
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Prevent the use of nonmilitary standard commercial equipment that may not be hardened against EMP effects.

Procedure:
1. Avoid relying on nonstandard, commercially available equipment that has not been tested or certified for EMP...
Objective: Prevent the use of nonmilitary standard commercial equipment that may not be hardened against EMP effects.

Procedure:
1. Avoid relying on nonstandard, commercially available equipment that has not been tested or certified for EMP protection.
2. During peacetime, ensure that all critical equipment is sourced from military-standard supplies where possible.
3. Recognize that parts for standard-issue equipment are more likely to be available in the supply system than for nonstandard items.

Notes:
• Nonmilitary commercial equipment may not provide adequate protection against EMP effects.
• Relying on such equipment can increase vulnerability during an EMP event.
EMP, commercial equipment, military standard, supply system
Avoid the Most Vulnerable Antenna Types
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Where possible, avoid using the most vulnerable types of antennas.
2. The most vulnerable antenna types include:
- Long wires or rods
- Wide angle doublets
- Omnidirectional antennas
3. Less vulnerable antenna options...
Procedure:

1. Where possible, avoid using the most vulnerable types of antennas.
2. The most vulnerable antenna types include:
- Long wires or rods
- Wide angle doublets
- Omnidirectional antennas
3. Less vulnerable antenna options include:
- Antennas with smaller radiating elements (e.g., small directional antennas)
- Antennas designed to pick up frequencies above 100 megahertz
EMP protection, antenna types, vulnerable antennas, nuclear contamination avoidance
Disconnect and Collapse Collectors or Antennas When Possible
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. If the mission permits, disconnect and collapse all collectors or antennas.
2. Shut down electronic equipment and disconnect all antennas to reduce EMP vulnerability.
3. Simply turning off equipment is not sufficient; damaging...
Procedure:

1. If the mission permits, disconnect and collapse all collectors or antennas.
2. Shut down electronic equipment and disconnect all antennas to reduce EMP vulnerability.
3. Simply turning off equipment is not sufficient; damaging energy can still enter through connected antennas.
EMP protection, disconnect antennas, nuclear contamination avoidance
Ensure Proper Insulation of Antenna Guy Lines
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Ensure that antenna guy lines are properly insulated from the antenna.
2. Some large antennas use guy lines as braces for stability, and these lines can function as unintended antennas.
3. If not properly insulated, these guy...
Procedure:

1. Ensure that antenna guy lines are properly insulated from the antenna.
2. Some large antennas use guy lines as braces for stability, and these lines can function as unintended antennas.
3. If not properly insulated, these guy lines can couple EMP energy into the antenna and through it into electronic equipment.
4. Insulation of connections between the antenna and guy lines is critical to prevent this coupling.
EMP protection, insulation, guy lines, nuclear contamination avoidance
Identify and Survey Unintended Collectors or Antennas in Your Area
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Conduct a thorough survey of your area to identify all potential unintended collectors or antennas.
2. Look for objects that may act as unintended antennas, such as:
- Gun tubes
- Heating and ventilation ducts
- Water...
Procedure:

1. Conduct a thorough survey of your area to identify all potential unintended collectors or antennas.
2. Look for objects that may act as unintended antennas, such as:
- Gun tubes
- Heating and ventilation ducts
- Water pipes
- Fuel pipelines
- Conduits
- Grounding rods and wires
- Commercial phone and power lines
- Missiles
- Guy wires
- Fences
- Railroad tracks
- Power lines from generators
3. Remember the rule: *If you wouldn’t touch the object if it were standing upright in a lightning storm, assume it is an unintentional collector or antenna.*
EMP protection, unintended antennas, collectors, survey, nuclear contamination avoidance
Minimize Antenna Length to Reduce EMP Vulnerability
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Extend antennas to the minimum length necessary for their intended function.
2. The amount of potentially damaging EMP energy that enters a radio is largely dependent on the length of the antenna.
3. Shorter antennas reduce the...
Procedure:

1. Extend antennas to the minimum length necessary for their intended function.
2. The amount of potentially damaging EMP energy that enters a radio is largely dependent on the length of the antenna.
3. Shorter antennas reduce the risk of EMP damage.
EMP protection, antenna length, vulnerability reduction, nuclear contamination avoidance
Understand Equipment Vulnerability to EMP Effects
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Ensure personnel understand the factors that make equipment vulnerable or resistant to EMP effects.

Procedure:
1. Train all unit personnel on the characteristics of their equipment that influence vulnerability to EMP damage.
2....
Objective: Ensure personnel understand the factors that make equipment vulnerable or resistant to EMP effects.

Procedure:
1. Train all unit personnel on the characteristics of their equipment that influence vulnerability to EMP damage.
2. Identify key factors such as the use of microprocessor computer chips, presence of antennas (intended or unintended), and other design features that increase vulnerability.
3. Educate personnel on common myths and misconceptions about EMP effects and mitigation strategies.
4. Provide accurate information based on verified sources rather than popular science articles or rumors.

Notes:
• Misinformation can lead to ineffective or harmful mitigation practices.
• Understanding equipment vulnerabilities is critical for effective EMP protection.
EMP, equipment vulnerability, military training, myths, misconceptions

Chemical Decontamination (18) -

Perform Immediate Skin Decontamination
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Act quickly: Perform skin decontamination within 1 minute of being contaminated.
2. Use the correct kit: Use the skin decontaminating kit (SDK) to remove contamination from the skin.
3. Remove all contaminants: Ensure...
Procedure:

1. Act quickly: Perform skin decontamination within 1 minute of being contaminated.
2. Use the correct kit: Use the skin decontaminating kit (SDK) to remove contamination from the skin.
3. Remove all contaminants: Ensure that all visible chemical agents are removed from the skin using the SDK.

Notes:
• This is a basic survival skill for soldiers.
• Immediate action reduces the risk of chemical exposure and health effects.
skin decon, immediate decon, SDK, chemical agents
Decontaminate Yourself and Individual Equipment Using Chemical Decontaminating Kits
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Assume MOPP3 (don your protective mask in addition to MOPP2).

2. Decontaminate your skin using the M291 decontaminating kit.

CAUTION: The M291 decontaminating kit is for external use only. Keep decontaminating powder...
Procedure:

1. Assume MOPP3 (don your protective mask in addition to MOPP2).

2. Decontaminate your skin using the M291 decontaminating kit.

CAUTION: The M291 decontaminating kit is for external use only. Keep decontaminating powder out of your eyes and any cuts or wounds because the decontaminating powder may irritate your skin or eyes. Use water to wash the toxic agent out of your eyes, cuts, or wounds.

DANGER: Death or injury may result if you breathe toxic agents while decontaminating your face. If you need to breathe before you finish, reseal your mask, clear it, check it, get your breath, and then resume the decontaminating procedure.

_Note: After decontaminate with water, cover exposed cuts or wounds with appropriate first aid wrap or bandages before handling the decontaminating kit.

CAUTION: Do not handle or hold leaking packets above your head. Do not touch or rub your eyes, lips, or the inside of your mouth with anything that has been in contact with the decontaminating powder.

CAUTION: Do not attempt to decontaminate a loaded weapon. Always unload and clear the weapon and place the weapon on safe before starting decontaminating procedures. Immediate decontaminating techniques remove only the liquid hazard. Certain items may still present a vapor hazard. See your supervisor for unmasking procedures.

Steps:

• a. Decontaminate your hands and face and the inside of your mask.
- (1) Remove one skin decontamination packet from your carrying pouch.
- (2) Tear it open quickly at the notch.
- (3) Remove the applicator pad from the packet, and discard the empty packet.
- (4) Unfold the applicator pad, and slip your finger(s) into the handle.
- (5) Scrub the back of your hand, palm, and fingers until they are completely covered with black powder from the applicator pad.
- (6) Switch the applicator pad to the other hand, and repeat the procedure.

_Note: Do not discard the applicator pad at this time.

_Note: If you were masked with your hood zipped and the drawstring pulled tight when you were contaminated, stop. Discard the applicator pad, put on your protective gloves, and go to step 2b below. However, if you were masked, but the zipper and drawstring were not secure, go to step 2a(16) below.

_Note: The stars in the illustration on page 2-5 of TM 3-4230-229-10 show areas of the face that should be scrubbed with an extra stroke because they are hard to decontaminate.

_Note: The procedure is the same regardless of the type of protective mask used. If you are using the new style chemical-protective overgarment with a hood attached to the protective jacket, ignore the instructions for the hood.
chemical decontamination, MOPP3, M291 kit, skin decontamination, protective mask, decontaminating powder, first aid wrap, vapor hazard
Respond to Leaking Chemical Munition
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

When a leaking chemical munition is discovered, take the following steps:

1. Treat as a chemical attack: Assume all personnel in the area are exposed to the same hazards as an enemy chemical attack.
2. Evacuate the area:...
Procedure:

When a leaking chemical munition is discovered, take the following steps:

1. Treat as a chemical attack: Assume all personnel in the area are exposed to the same hazards as an enemy chemical attack.
2. Evacuate the area: Immediately move away from the munition and any contaminated zone.
3. Report the incident: Notify command and follow established NBC (Nuclear, Biological, Chemical) protocols for handling hazardous materials.
4. Avoid direct contact: Do not touch or attempt to contain the leaking munition without proper protective equipment.
5. Wait for decontamination teams: Allow trained personnel to handle the situation using appropriate decon procedures and equipment.

Warnings/Contraindications:
• Never attempt to repair or neutralize a leaking chemical munition without specialized training and equipment.
• Exposure to chemical agents can cause severe health effects, including respiratory failure, burns, and death.
leaking munition, chemical attack, decon procedure, NBC protocol, hazardous materials
Decontaminate Helmet and Equipment Using M295 Kit
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Decontaminate your helmet: Use the decontamination mitt to pat the helmet.
- Discard the decontamination mitt after use.

2. Use the second mitt to decontaminate the following items:
- Load-carrying equipment (LCE)...
Procedure:

1. Decontaminate your helmet: Use the decontamination mitt to pat the helmet.
- Discard the decontamination mitt after use.

2. Use the second mitt to decontaminate the following items:
- Load-carrying equipment (LCE) and accessories (e.g., canteen, ammunition pouch, first aid pouch).
- Mask carrier.
- Overboots.
- Gloves.

3. Repeat steps 3a(1) through 3a(7) if liquid contamination is still suspected or detected:
- Get another packet of decontamination material.
- Repeat the full process from step 3a(1) to 3a(7).
- Rub or blot areas where contamination is still suspected or detected.

4. Discard the decontamination mitt after each use.

5. Repeat the process as needed until all liquid contamination has been removed.

Important Warnings:
• The M295 kit only removes liquid hazards, not vapor hazards.
• Do not unmask until it has been determined that it is safe to do so.
decontaminate helmet, decontaminate equipment, M295 kit, chemical decontamination, CBRN, liquid contamination
Interior Vehicle Decontamination Using DS2 and HTH/STB Solution
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Interior Vehicle Decontamination at Station 3

1. Move the vehicle to a concealed position at Station 3.
2. Allow DS2 to remain on the contaminated surface for no less than 30 minutes:
- DS2 reacts with most chemical...
Procedure for Interior Vehicle Decontamination at Station 3

1. Move the vehicle to a concealed position at Station 3.
2. Allow DS2 to remain on the contaminated surface for no less than 30 minutes:
- DS2 reacts with most chemical agents within 5 minutes, but allowing it to stay for 30 minutes significantly reduces desorption (off-gassing) after decon operations.
- For distilled mustard (HD) vapors, even a 30-minute contact time may not fully prevent desorption.
3. Inspect the interior of the vehicle for liquid contamination using M8 detector paper:
- If chemical contamination is detected, proceed with interior decontamination.
4. Use a 5% solution of HTH or STB to wipe all reasonably accessible surfaces within the vehicle:
- Use a rag or sponge soaked in the solution.
- Avoid decon areas where contamination is unlikely (e.g., electrical assemblies, beneath the turret floor).
5. Do not mix HTH or STB with DS2, as this can cause a violent reaction.
6. Cover the seat and floor of the vehicle after completing interior decontamination.
7. Dismount carefully from the vehicle to avoid slipping on DS2-coated surfaces or tracking DS2 into the vehicle.
8. Proceed to the start of the DTD (Decontamination Traverse Device) for further processing.
DS2, HTH, STB, interior decon, vehicle decontamination, chemical agent neutralization
Perform MOPP-Gear Exchange
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Timing: Perform a MOPP-gear exchange within 6 hours of contamination if thorough decontamination is not possible.
2. Continue wearing MOPP gear: Soldiers should continue to wear their MOPP (Mission Oriented Protective...
Procedure:

1. Timing: Perform a MOPP-gear exchange within 6 hours of contamination if thorough decontamination is not possible.
2. Continue wearing MOPP gear: Soldiers should continue to wear their MOPP (Mission Oriented Protective Posture) gear during this time.
3. Reduce operating tempo: Adjust the pace of operations to account for the reduced combat effectiveness due to MOPP gear.

Notes:
• This is a temporary measure until thorough decontamination can be performed.
• It helps maintain protection while allowing limited operational activity.
MOPP gear exchange, MOPP levels, chemical decon
Perform Vehicle Washdown
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Timing: Perform a vehicle washdown within 6 hours of contamination if the mission does not allow for thorough decontamination.
2. Use appropriate methods: Use water and detergent to remove gross contamination from...
Procedure:

1. Timing: Perform a vehicle washdown within 6 hours of contamination if the mission does not allow for thorough decontamination.
2. Use appropriate methods: Use water and detergent to remove gross contamination from vehicles.
3. Limit spread: Ensure that the washdown process limits the spread of contamination to other areas or personnel.

Notes:
• This step is critical for reducing the risk of cross-contamination between units or equipment.
• It should be performed when full decontamination is not possible due to mission constraints.
vehicle washdown, gross contamination, MOPP gear
Effectiveness of Wash Types for Different Agents and Surfaces
Standard Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure to Determine Effective Decontamination Methods Based on Agent and Surface

1. Identify the chemical agent and surface type: For example, TGD on alkyd-painted metal or HD on canvas/webbing.
2. **Refer to Table 4-3 for effectiveness...
Procedure to Determine Effective Decontamination Methods Based on Agent and Surface

1. Identify the chemical agent and surface type: For example, TGD on alkyd-painted metal or HD on canvas/webbing.
2. Refer to Table 4-3 for effectiveness of wash types:
- The most effective decon method is listed first (e.g., steam; hot water and/or hot, soapy water).
- Less effective methods are listed in descending order.
3. Select the appropriate wash type based on agent/surface combination:
- For TGD on alkyd-painted metal: Use steam, hot water and/or hot, soapy water, or cold, soapy water.
- For HD on canvas/webbing: Use steam, hot water, hot, soapy water, or cold water.
4. Apply the selected wash type to contaminated surfaces in accordance with established decon procedures.
5. Document the effectiveness of the chosen method for future reference and training.
wash types, chemical agents, surface effectiveness, decon methods
Perform Detailed Equipment and Aircraft Decon (DED/DAD)
Standard Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Timing: Conduct DED (Detailed Equipment Decontamination) and DAD (Detailed Aircraft Decontamination) as part of reconstitution or during breaks in combat operations.
2. Use chemical decon units: These operations require...
Procedure:

1. Timing: Conduct DED (Detailed Equipment Decontamination) and DAD (Detailed Aircraft Decontamination) as part of reconstitution or during breaks in combat operations.
2. Use chemical decon units: These operations require support from a chemical decontamination unit to ensure thorough removal of contamination.
3. Restore functionality: Ensure that equipment is fully decontaminated so it can be used without MOPP gear.

Notes:
• This process restores full operational capability and reduces the need for protective gear.
• It should be conducted when the unit has access to specialized chemical decon resources.
DED, DAD, detailed equipment decon, aircraft decon
Perform Detailed Troop Decon (DTD)
Standard Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Conduct in conjunction with DED/DAD: Perform DTD (Detailed Troop Decontamination) alongside DED and DAD operations.
2. Contaminated unit responsibility: The contaminated unit is responsible for conducting this process and...
Procedure:

1. Conduct in conjunction with DED/DAD: Perform DTD (Detailed Troop Decontamination) alongside DED and DAD operations.
2. Contaminated unit responsibility: The contaminated unit is responsible for conducting this process and supporting the decon of equipment and aircraft.
3. Thorough removal: Ensure that all personnel are fully decontaminated to restore combat effectiveness.

Notes:
• This step ensures that soldiers can return to full operational status without MOPP gear.
• It is typically conducted in coordination with detailed equipment decon operations.
DTD, troop decon, DED/DAD support
Implement Decon in Combat Operations
Standard Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Combat effectiveness considerations: MOPP gear can degrade combat effectiveness; decon helps restore it.
2. Immediate decon: Saves lives and allows the use of individual equipment and key systems.
3. Operational decon:...
Procedure:

1. Combat effectiveness considerations: MOPP gear can degrade combat effectiveness; decon helps restore it.
2. Immediate decon: Saves lives and allows the use of individual equipment and key systems.
3. Operational decon: Reduces contamination spread and may allow for reduced MOPP levels.
4. Thorough decon: Removes units from combat but restores their effectiveness after full decontamination.

Notes:
• Decon in combat is a critical factor affecting unit readiness and performance.
• It requires coordination with detection and warning systems to ensure safety.
combat decon, MOPP gear, effectiveness
Use Chemical-Agent-Resistant Coating (CARC) for Equipment
Standard Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Apply CARC paint: Paint Army equipment with CARC to prevent the absorption of chemical agents.
2. Facilitate decon: This coating makes it easier to remove contamination from surfaces.
3. Use proper colors: Use the...
Procedure:

1. Apply CARC paint: Paint Army equipment with CARC to prevent the absorption of chemical agents.
2. Facilitate decon: This coating makes it easier to remove contamination from surfaces.
3. Use proper colors: Use the correct CARC paint colors for bumper numbers, spot painting, and other markings.

Notes:
• CARC is essential for reducing the persistence of chemical agents on equipment.
• Do not substitute CARC with other types of paint.
CARC, chemical-resistant coating, equipment painting
Use Humidity and Rain for Chemical Agent Breakdown
Reference Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Leverage moisture: Use humidity to slowly break down chemical agents on surfaces.
2. Use heavy rain: Heavy rainfall can physically remove contamination from surfaces but may lead to contaminated runoff in drainage...
Procedure:

1. Leverage moisture: Use humidity to slowly break down chemical agents on surfaces.
2. Use heavy rain: Heavy rainfall can physically remove contamination from surfaces but may lead to contaminated runoff in drainage areas.
3. Monitor runoff: Ensure that contaminated runoff is managed properly to prevent secondary contamination.

Notes:
• This method should be used as a supplementary step, not the primary means of decon.
• It helps reduce chemical persistence over time.
humidity, rain, agent breakdown
Use Sunlight for Agent Evaporation and Decomposition
Reference Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Expose to sunlight: Use sunlight to accelerate the evaporation and decomposition of chemical agents.
2. Kill biological agents: Ultraviolet and infrared radiation in sunlight rapidly kills most biological agents.
3....
Procedure:

1. Expose to sunlight: Use sunlight to accelerate the evaporation and decomposition of chemical agents.
2. Kill biological agents: Ultraviolet and infrared radiation in sunlight rapidly kills most biological agents.
3. Monitor effectiveness: Ensure that exposure is sufficient for effective decon.

Notes:
• This method should be used as an additional step to other decontamination procedures.
• It can help reduce the need for manual decon in certain conditions.
sunlight, evaporation, decomposition
Use Temperature for Chemical Agent Evaporation
Reference Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Leverage heat: Higher temperatures increase the rate of chemical agent evaporation.
2. Reduce persistence: As temperature rises, contamination becomes less persistent and easier to remove.
3. **No effect on radiological...
Procedure:

1. Leverage heat: Higher temperatures increase the rate of chemical agent evaporation.
2. Reduce persistence: As temperature rises, contamination becomes less persistent and easier to remove.
3. No effect on radiological agents: Temperature has no impact on radiological contamination.

Notes:
• This is a natural method of reducing chemical contamination over time.
• It should be used in conjunction with other decon methods for best results.
temperature, evaporation, chemical persistence
Use Weather Effects for Natural Decon
Reference Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Assess METT-TC: If the situation allows, leave contamination in place and rely on weather effects to naturally decontaminate it.
2. Mark and report: Mark contaminated areas and report them periodically for monitoring.
3....
Procedure:

1. Assess METT-TC: If the situation allows, leave contamination in place and rely on weather effects to naturally decontaminate it.
2. Mark and report: Mark contaminated areas and report them periodically for monitoring.
3. Recheck regularly: Periodically recheck the area to ensure that natural decon has occurred effectively.

Notes:
• This is a passive method of decon and should only be used when feasible.
• It reduces the need for active decontamination efforts but requires ongoing monitoring.
weather effects, natural decon, METT-TC
Use Wind for Aeration and Dispersal of Agents
Reference Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Leverage wind: Allow wind to disperse chemical agents by aeration.
2. Reduce concentration: Wind helps lower the concentration of contaminants in the air or on surfaces.
3. Monitor effectiveness: Ensure that the...
Procedure:

1. Leverage wind: Allow wind to disperse chemical agents by aeration.
2. Reduce concentration: Wind helps lower the concentration of contaminants in the air or on surfaces.
3. Monitor effectiveness: Ensure that the dispersal is effective and does not create new contamination hazards.

Notes:
• This method should be used as an additional step to other decon procedures.
• It can help reduce the need for manual decontamination in certain conditions.
wind, aeration, agent dispersal
Use Time for Radiological Contamination Neutralization
Reference Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Allow time to pass: Only time can neutralize and destroy radiological contamination.
2. Other methods as temporary solutions: Techniques like shielding or removal are only temporary measures.
3. Monitor decay: Ensure...
Procedure:

1. Allow time to pass: Only time can neutralize and destroy radiological contamination.
2. Other methods as temporary solutions: Techniques like shielding or removal are only temporary measures.
3. Monitor decay: Ensure that the decay of radioactive materials is being monitored for safety.

Notes:
• This method should be used in conjunction with other decon procedures for best results.
• It requires patience and monitoring over time.
time, radiological decay, neutralization

Radiation Measurement (17) -

Construct and Use a KFM Radiation Dosimeter
Critical Source: Nuclear War Survival Skills Cresson Kearny
Procedure for Constructing and Using a KFM (Kearny Fallout Monitor):

Materials Required:
• Two thin, flat leaves made of specified materials (as described in the source text)
• Clear plastic cover
• Millimeter scale cut out and attached to...
Procedure for Constructing and Using a KFM (Kearny Fallout Monitor):

Materials Required:
• Two thin, flat leaves made of specified materials (as described in the source text)
• Clear plastic cover
• Millimeter scale cut out and attached to the clear plastic cover

Construction Steps:
1. Ensure the KFM is constructed with the specified dimensions and materials, as outlined in the source text.
2. Attach the millimeter scale to the clear plastic cover of the KFM so that its zero mark aligns directly above the two leaves when they are in their discharged position on a horizontal surface.

Usage Steps:
1. Before exposure, charge and read the KFM, noting the initial separation of the leaves (the lower edge positions relative to the zero mark on the scale).
2. Expose the KFM to radiation for a specified time in the location where measurement is needed. Position it about 3 ft. above the ground outdoors.
