3. After exposure, read the KFM again, noting the new separation of the leaves (the lower edge positions relative to the zero mark on the scale).
4. Calculate the KFM reading by summing the apparent positions of the lower edges of both leaves from the zero mark on the scale.
- For example, if one leaf appears 9 mm to the right and the other appears 10 mm to the left of the zero mark, the KFM reading is 19 mm.

Dose Rate Determination:
Use the following table to determine radiation dose rates (R/hr) based on the KFM reading and time interval of exposure:

| KFM Reading | 1 Sec. | 1 Min. | 4 Min. | 15 Min. | 1 HR. |
|-------------|--------|--------|--------|---------|-------|
| 2 mm | 6.2 | 1.6 | 0.4 | 0.1 | 0.03 |
| 4 mm | 12.0 | 3.1 | 0.8 | 0.2 | 0.06 |
| 6 mm | 19.0 | 4.6 | 1.2 | 0.3 | 0.08 |
| 8 mm | 25.0 | 6.2 | 1.6 | 0.4 | 0.10 |
| 10 mm | 31.0 | 7.7 | 2.0 | 0.5 | 0.13 |
| 12 mm | 37.0 | 9.2 | 2.3 | 0.6 | 0.15 |
| 14 mm | 43.0 | 11.0 | 2.7 | 0.7 | 0.18 |

Important Notes:
• The KFM is never required to be calibrated or tested with a radiation source, as its accuracy is established by the unchanging laws of nature.
• This procedure assumes that the KFM has been constructed and maintained correctly with the specified dimensions and materials.
KFM,radiation dosimeter,fallout monitor,nuclear radiation measurement,dose rate calculation
Constructing a KFM (Kearny Fallout Monitor) for Radiation Measurement
Critical Source: Nuclear War Survival Skills Cresson Kearny
To construct a KFM (Kearny Fallout Monitor) for radiation measurement, follow these steps:

1. Materials Required:
- A metal can with a lid (approximately 3 inches in diameter)
- Aluminum foil leaves (cut into strips and folded to form a...
To construct a KFM (Kearny Fallout Monitor) for radiation measurement, follow these steps:

1. Materials Required:
- A metal can with a lid (approximately 3 inches in diameter)
- Aluminum foil leaves (cut into strips and folded to form a shield)
- Thread or string
- A ruler or measuring tape
- A pencil (for use as a ruler rest)
- A sheet of paper or cardboard for the base
- A marker or pen for labeling

2. Assembly Steps:
- Cut the metal can exactly on the side lines to create an open container.
- Fasten the threads holding the aluminum foil leaves at the top of the can (below the lip).
- Ensure that the aluminum foil leaves are securely attached and positioned inside the can.
- Insert a 1-inch pencil into the top of the can for use as a ruler rest.
- Create a seat by marking the bottom of the can (above the lip) with a marker or pen.
- Drill holes in the bottom of the can for the stop-thread. The hole size should be determined based on the desired sensitivity:
- 2 mm
- 4 mm
- 6 mm
- 8 mm
- 10 mm
- 12 mm
- 14 mm
- Ensure that the holes are drilled precisely and aligned with the stop-thread.

3. Usage Instructions:
- Place the KFM in a location where radiation exposure is to be measured (e.g., outside or near a suspected fallout area).
- Record the initial reading from the KFM before exposure.
- After the desired time interval of exposure, record the final reading from the KFM.
- Calculate the difference between the initial and final readings to determine the radiation dose rate.

4. Interpreting Results:
- Use the following table to interpret the KFM readings and calculate the radiation dose rates (in R/hr):

| Hole Size | 15 Sec. | 1 Min. | 4 Min. | 16 Min. | 1 Hour |
|-----------|---------|--------|--------|---------|--------|
| 2 mm | 6.2 | 1.6 | 0.4 | 0.1 | 0.03 |
| 4 mm | 12.0 | 3.1 | 0.8 | 0.2 | 0.06 |
| 6 mm | 19.0 | 4.6 | 1.2 | 0.3 | 0.08 |
| 8 mm | 25.0 | 6.2 | 1.6 | 0.4 | 0.10 |
| 10 mm | 31.0 | 7.7 | 2.0 | 0.5 | 0.13 |
| 12 mm | 37.0 | 9.2 | 2.3 | 0.6 | 0.15 |
| 14 mm | 43.0 | 11.0 | 2.7 | 0.7 | 0.18 |

- The difference between the initial and final readings should be multiplied by the corresponding value in the table to determine the radiation dose rate.

5. Safety Considerations:
- Handle the KFM with care, as it is used for measuring potentially hazardous levels of radiation.
- Avoid prolonged exposure to high radiation areas without proper protection.
- Ensure that all components are securely fastened and in good working condition before use.

6. Maintenance and Storage:
- Store the KFM in a dry, cool place when not in use.
- Inspect the aluminum foil leaves and threads regularly to ensure they remain intact and functional.
- Replace any damaged or worn components as needed.
KFM, radiation monitor, fallout measurement, nuclear emergency, radiation dose rate, aluminum foil leaves, metal can, hole size, radiation exposure
Build a Kearny Fallout Meter (KFM) for Radiation Measurement
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Purpose: To construct a reliable fallout meter to measure radiation dose rates in post-nuclear scenarios.

Materials Needed:
• See lists provided in Sections V, VI, and VII of the source text.

Steps:
1. Gather Materials: Ensure all...
Purpose: To construct a reliable fallout meter to measure radiation dose rates in post-nuclear scenarios.

Materials Needed:
• See lists provided in Sections V, VI, and VII of the source text.

Steps:
1. Gather Materials: Ensure all required components are available (see detailed lists in the original text for specifics).
2. Study Instructions: Spend approximately 1 1/2 hours studying the operating sections of these instructions to understand how to use the KFM effectively.
3. Assemble the Meter: Using low-cost materials and tools found in most homes, assemble the KFM by following the detailed construction steps provided in the source text.
4. Test the Meter: Once assembled, test the KFM to ensure it functions correctly and accurately measures radiation dose rates (R/hr).
5. Practice Use: Practice using the meter to measure dose rates, calculate radiation doses received, and determine permissible exposure times.

Notes:
• The KFM is as accurate as most civil defense fallout meters and can be built in 3–4 hours by untrained families.
• It is essential to build and test the KFM before a nuclear attack if possible. If not, it can still be constructed after fallout arrives, provided all materials are available.
KFM, Kearny Fallout Meter, radiation measurement, nuclear fallout, post-nuclear survival, radiation dose rate, R/hr, fallout meter construction
Using a KFM to Measure Radiation Dose Rates
Critical Source: Nuclear War Survival Skills Cresson Kearny
To use a KFM (Kearny Fallout Monitor) to measure radiation dose rates, follow these steps:

1. Prepare the KFM: Ensure that the KFM is made and maintained with the specified dimensions and materials. Its accuracy is automatically established by...
To use a KFM (Kearny Fallout Monitor) to measure radiation dose rates, follow these steps:

1. Prepare the KFM: Ensure that the KFM is made and maintained with the specified dimensions and materials. Its accuracy is automatically established by unchanging laws of nature and does not require calibration or testing with a radiation source.

2. Attach the Millimeter Scale: Cut out the millimeter scale and attach it to the clear plastic cover of the KFM so that its zero mark is directly above the two leaves in their discharged position when the KFM is resting on a horizontal surface.

3. Take Initial Reading: Before exposing the KFM to radiation, take an initial reading by noting the number of millimeters that the lower edge of one leaf appears to be on one side of the zero mark on the scale and almost at the same time noting the number of millimeters the lower edge of the other leaf appears to be on the other side of the zero mark. The sum of these two apparent positions is called a KFM reading.

4. Expose the KFM: Expose the KFM to radiation for a specified time in the location where measurement of the dose rate is needed. Position the KFM about 3 ft. above the ground outdoors.

5. Take Final Reading: After the exposure, read the KFM again by noting the number of millimeters that the lower edge of one leaf appears to be on one side of the zero mark and almost at the same time noting the number of millimeters the lower edge of the other leaf appears to be on the other side of the zero mark. The sum of these two apparent positions is called a KFM reading.

6. Calculate Dose Rate: Use the following table to determine the radiation dose rate (in R/hr) based on the difference between the initial and final readings, as well as the time interval of exposure:

| Difference in Reading | Time Interval of Exposure |
|----------------------|--------------------------|
| 2mm | 1 sec. |
| | 1 min. |
| | 4 min. |
| | 15 min. |
| | 1 hr. |
| 6.2 | |
| 1.6 | |
| 0.4 | |
| 0.1 | |
| 0.03 | |
| 4mm | 12 |
| | 3.1 |
| | 0.8 |
| | 0.2 |
| | 0.06 |
| 6mm | 19 |
| | 4.6 |
| | 1.2 |
| | 0.3 |
| | 0.08 |
| 8mm | 25 |
| | 6.2 |
| | 1.6 |
| | 0.4 |
| | 0.10 |
| 10mm | 31 |
| | 7.7 |
| | 2.0 |
| | 0.5 |
| | 0.13 |
| 12mm | 37 |
| | 9.2 |
| | 2.3 |
| | 0.6 |
| | 0.15 |
| 14mm | 43 |
| | 11 |
| | 2.7 |
| | 0.7 |
| | 0.18 |

Important Notes: The KFM is a reliable tool for measuring radiation dose rates in the event of nuclear fallout or exposure to ionizing radiation. It should be used with care and attention to detail, as accurate readings are critical for safety.

Warnings: Do not expose the KFM to extreme temperatures or moisture, as this may affect its performance. Always ensure that the scale is properly attached and aligned before taking a reading.
KFM,radiation dose rate,measurement,nuclear fallout,Natural Laws,calibration
Using the AN/VDR2 Radiac Set to Measure Radiation Levels
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Prepare the Device: Ensure the AN/VDR2 is powered on and functioning properly.
2. Position the Instrument: Place the AN/VDR2 in an area where radiation levels need to be measured (e.g., fallout zones, contaminated...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the Device: Ensure the AN/VDR2 is powered on and functioning properly.
2. Position the Instrument: Place the AN/VDR2 in an area where radiation levels need to be measured (e.g., fallout zones, contaminated areas).
3. Measure Radiation Levels:
- The device detects, measures, and displays gamma radiation dose rates from 0.01 uCyph to 100 Gyph.
- It also detects and displays beta particle dose rates from 0.01 uGy to 5 cGyph.
4. Measure Accumulated Dose: The AN/VDR2 measures, stores, and displays accumulated doses ranging from 0.01 uGy to 9.99 Gy.
5. Interpret Results: Use the displayed data to assess radiation exposure levels resulting from fallout or other sources of gamma rays and beta particles.
6. Maintain Device: Follow instructions in TM 11-6665-251-10 for care and maintenance.
AN/VDR2 radiac set, gamma radiation measurement, beta particle detection, fallout measurement, dose rate display
Using the IM93/UD Dosimeter to Measure Radiation Dose
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Prepare the Dosimeter: Ensure the IM93/UD dosimeter is powered on and functioning properly.
2. Charge the Device: Use the PP1578A/PD radiac-detector charger, which is an electrostatic-charge generator designed to serve all...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the Dosimeter: Ensure the IM93/UD dosimeter is powered on and functioning properly.
2. Charge the Device: Use the PP1578A/PD radiac-detector charger, which is an electrostatic-charge generator designed to serve all US and certain NATO combat dosimeters. Charge the device by turning the charging knob and placing it on the charging pedestal with the window exposed.
3. Read the Dosimeter: To read the accumulated gamma total dose:
- Point the instrument toward a bright light source (e.g., sunlight, vehicle headlights, or a flashlight).
- Look through the lens to view the dose reading.
4. Protect the Device: Use the dust cap on one end of the dosimeter to keep dirt from the charging contacts during storage and transport.
5. Maintain the Dosimeter: Follow instructions in TM 11-6665-214-10 for care and maintenance.
IM93/UD dosimeter, radiation dose measurement, gamma total dose, PP1578A/PD charger, NATO combat dosimeters
Define and Use the Roentgen (R) Unit of Radiation Measurement
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
The roentgen (R) is a unit used to measure the exposure of gamma or X-ray radiation in field dosimetry. One roentgen is essentially equal to one rad.

Key points:
• It measures the amount of radiation energy imparted to air.
• This unit can...
The roentgen (R) is a unit used to measure the exposure of gamma or X-ray radiation in field dosimetry. One roentgen is essentially equal to one rad.

Key points:
• It measures the amount of radiation energy imparted to air.
• This unit can only be applied to X-rays and gamma rays, not other types of ionizing radiation.
• Understanding this unit is essential for evaluating radiation exposure in emergency scenarios.
roentgen,radiation measurement,gamma ray,x-ray,field dosimetry
Define and Use the Roentgen Equivalent Man (rem) Unit of Radiation Dose
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
The roentgen equivalent man (rem) is a unit used to measure the biological effect of ionizing radiation on human tissue. It represents the dose of any ionizing radiation that produces the same biological effect as one rad of ordinary...
The roentgen equivalent man (rem) is a unit used to measure the biological effect of ionizing radiation on human tissue. It represents the dose of any ionizing radiation that produces the same biological effect as one rad of ordinary X-rays.

Key points:
• This unit measures the amount of energy absorbed in biological tissue.
• It is essential for assessing health risks from exposure to various types of radiation.
• The rem is used to compare the effects of different ionizing radiations on human health.
roentgen equivalent man,rem,radiation dose,biological effect
Determine Normalizing Factor Using Table of Values
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Step 1. Determine the time in hours and minutes after the burst that the reading was taken.

Step 2. Enter Table 6-5 with the time after burst. Read across to the appropriate decay exponent column and find the NF.

Step 3. Multiply the...
Step 1. Determine the time in hours and minutes after the burst that the reading was taken.

Step 2. Enter Table 6-5 with the time after burst. Read across to the appropriate decay exponent column and find the NF.

Step 3. Multiply the dose-rate reading by the normalization factor. The product is the H + 1 dose-rate reading.
normalizing factor, radiation measurement, fallout contamination, nuclear burst, decay exponent
Expose KFM to Fallout Radiation Using Dry-Bucket Method
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Prepare the KFM: Ensure the KFM is charged and placed inside the dry-bucket.
2. Position for Exposure: Leave the KFM inside the dry-bucket while exposing it to fallout radiation for one of the listed time intervals.
3....
Procedure:

1. Prepare the KFM: Ensure the KFM is charged and placed inside the dry-bucket.
2. Position for Exposure: Leave the KFM inside the dry-bucket while exposing it to fallout radiation for one of the listed time intervals.
3. Read Before Exposure: Read the KFM before exposure, ensuring the reading eye is a measured 12 inches above the SEAT of the KFM. Use a flashlight or other light source for visibility.
4. Expose to Radiation: Allow the KFM to remain inside the dry-bucket during the specified time interval of fallout radiation exposure.
5. Read After Exposure: Read the KFM again after the exposure, using the same method as before (12 inches above the SEAT with a light source).

Notes:
• This method is recommended for preserving the drying agent's effectiveness, especially in humid shelters.
• Repeated use of this method may require replacement of the drying agent if done frequently.
KFM,dry-bucket,fallout radiation,exposure,nuclear attack,radiation measurement
Understand and Use the Sievert (Sv) Unit of Radiation Dose Equivalent
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
The sievert (Sv) is the international system (SI) unit for dose equivalent, equal to 1 Joule/Kilogram. One sievert equals 100 rem.

Key points:
• This unit measures the biological effect of radiation on human tissue and is used globally in...
The sievert (Sv) is the international system (SI) unit for dose equivalent, equal to 1 Joule/Kilogram. One sievert equals 100 rem.

Key points:
• This unit measures the biological effect of radiation on human tissue and is used globally in radiological protection standards.
• It is essential for assessing health risks from exposure to ionizing radiation.
• The sievert allows for comparisons between different types of radiation based on their potential impact on human health.
sievert,radiation dose equivalent,health risk assessment
Use M4A1 Nuclear Yield Calculator for Unit-Level Estimation
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Obtain the M4A1 nuclear yield calculator, which is part of the M28A1 RADIAC calculator set (NSN 6665-01-130-3616).

2. Review instruction card included in the set for detailed instructions on how to use the calculator.

3....
Procedure:

1. Obtain the M4A1 nuclear yield calculator, which is part of the M28A1 RADIAC calculator set (NSN 6665-01-130-3616).

2. Review instruction card included in the set for detailed instructions on how to use the calculator.

3. Solve the example problem provided on the instruction card to verify that the calculator functions correctly within the specified tolerance. If it does not, destroy the calculator and obtain a new one.

4. Use the M4A1 calculator for rapid yield estimation based on any parameter except cloud-top or cloud-bottom height.

5. Be aware of potential issues: The calculator is a round nomogram with a fixed hairline, which may cause the yield pointer to go off scale on the high or low ends of the yield scale in certain situations.

6. Cross-check results with additional burst information if unexpected yield estimates are obtained that do not align with tactical nuclear weapon expectations.

7. Understand the relationship between cloud size and observer-GZ distance (flash-to-bang time) to interpret calculator results accurately.

8. Train all members of the unit NBC defense team in the use of the M4A1 calculator, as it is designated specifically for unit-level use due to its durability, size, and ease of operation.

9. Use GZ location and estimated yield calculated at the unit level to create a simplified fallout prediction.

10. Revise the simplified fallout prediction upon receipt of the NBC 2 report from the NBCC using new data.

11. Supersede the revised simplified fallout prediction with the NBC3 report when it is received.
M4A1 calculator, nuclear yield estimation, unit-level use, NBC defense team, fallout prediction
Using Slide Rule Scales for Radiation Decay Prediction
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Cut apart the two scales along the dotted line as shown in Figure 48.
2. Position the upper scale to represent the time after the nuclear explosion, ranging from one hour to forty days.
3. Position the lower scale to...
Procedure:

1. Cut apart the two scales along the dotted line as shown in Figure 48.
2. Position the upper scale to represent the time after the nuclear explosion, ranging from one hour to forty days.
3. Position the lower scale to represent the radiation dose rate, which is in arbitrary units (from 1 to 1000 R per hour).
4. Slide the scales back and forth to match a measured dose rate with the time when it was measured.
5. Use the scales to predict how rapidly the fallout radiation in your area will decay based on the alignment of the two scales.

Example:
• If a measurement of 1,000 R per hour is obtained a little less than three hours after the explosion, align the scales accordingly.
• By comparing the scales to the right of this point, you can see that:
- The dose rate will fall to 10 R per hour by the sixth day.
- The dose rate will fall to 1 R per hour by the fortieth day.

Adjusting the Scale:
• To cover a range from 10 to 10,000 R per hour, multiply all numbers on the scale by 10.
• To cover a range from 0.1 to 100 R per hour, divide all numbers on the scale by 10.
• To cover a range from 0.01 to 10 R per hour, divide all numbers on the scale by 100.

Important:
• Measure the radiation dose rate outside the shelter, exactly three feet above the ground for accurate readings.
slide rule,nuclear fallout,radiation decay,prediction,fallout radiation,dose rate scale
Adjust for Special Circumstances in Aerial Surveys Over Wooded or Urban Areas
Standard Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Identify Special Conditions: When conducting aerial surveys over wooded or urban areas, recognize that the AGCF may not accurately represent the air-to-ground ratio.

2. Apply a Correlation Factor: Use a specific...
Procedure:

1. Identify Special Conditions: When conducting aerial surveys over wooded or urban areas, recognize that the AGCF may not accurately represent the air-to-ground ratio.

2. Apply a Correlation Factor: Use a specific correlation factor (as shown in Figure 5-1) to adjust readings taken over trees or urban areas where fallout is closer to the aircraft and radiac instrument.

3. Document Adjustments: Ensure that all adjustments are clearly noted on DA Form 1971-1-R for accurate data interpretation.
wooded area adjustment, urban area correction, correlation factor
Calculate Overall Correction Factor (OCF) for Aerial Survey Data
Standard Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Multiply AGCF by NF: Multiply the Air-to-Ground Correction Factor (AGCF) by the normalization factor (NF) from Table 6-5 on page 6-26, corresponding to the start time of the aerial route or course leg.
- Example: _AGCF x NF...
Procedure:

1. Multiply AGCF by NF: Multiply the Air-to-Ground Correction Factor (AGCF) by the normalization factor (NF) from Table 6-5 on page 6-26, corresponding to the start time of the aerial route or course leg.
- Example: _AGCF x NF = OCF_

2. Use OCF for Each Course Leg: Calculate an OCF for each course leg or route of the survey.

3. Apply OCF to Dose Rates: Use the calculated OCF to multiply the dose rates on each leg of the survey to obtain normalized readings.

4. Normalize to H+1 Readings: The resulting normalized readings should be adjusted to H + 1 (reference time) as indicated in the control party column on DA Form 1971-1-R.

Notes:
• For ground surveys, use a vehicle correlation factor instead of AGCF.
• Round all dose rates to the nearest whole number.
• OCF is calculated by the NBCC (Nuclear Biological and Chemical Contamination Control) after the survey is completed.
OCF calculation, aerial survey data, AGCF, normalization factor, dose rate correction
Calculate Two OCFs for Recon or Survey Operations with Unknown Decay Rate
Standard Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Determine Initial OCF: Calculate the first OCF using n = 1.2 to provide a rapid means of developing an initial picture of contamination.

2. Calculate Final OCF: After determining the actual decay rate, calculate a second...
Procedure:

1. Determine Initial OCF: Calculate the first OCF using n = 1.2 to provide a rapid means of developing an initial picture of contamination.

2. Calculate Final OCF: After determining the actual decay rate, calculate a second OCF based on the real decay rate present in the area.

3. Use Appropriate OCFs: Apply both OCF values as needed for data interpretation and normalization to H + 1 readings.
two OCF calculation, decay rate unknown, n=1.2
Understand Radiation Measurement Units
Standard Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Understand the units used to measure radiation.

Radiation can be measured using different units depending on what is being measured:

• Radioactivity is measured in curie (Ci) or becquerel (Bq).
• Exposure is measured in...
Objective: Understand the units used to measure radiation.

Radiation can be measured using different units depending on what is being measured:

• Radioactivity is measured in curie (Ci) or becquerel (Bq).
• Exposure is measured in roentgen (R) or coulomb/kilogram (C/kg).
• Absorbed dose is measured in rad or gray (Gy).
• Dose equivalent is measured in rem or sievert (Sv).

These units are interrelated and can be converted as follows:

• 1 curie = 3.7x10¹⁰ becquerel (Bq) = 3.7x10¹⁰ disintegrations/second.
• 1 rad = 0.01 gray (Gy) or 1 centigray (cGy).
• 1 rem = 0.01 sievert (Sv).
• 1 roentgen (R) = 0.000258 coulomb/kilogram (C/kg).
• 1 gray (Gy) = 100 rad.
• 1 sievert (Sv) = 100 rem.

Understanding these units is essential for assessing radiation exposure and its potential health effects.
radiation units, curie, becquerel, roentgen, rad, gray, rem, sievert, measurement conversion

Radiation Safety (16) -

Assess Safety Distance for Nuclear Contamination
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
### Safety Distance Assessment Procedure

1. Consider the following factors:
- Blast radius: The distance from the explosion where blast overpressure can cause injury or death.
- Thermal radiation radius: The distance at which...
### Safety Distance Assessment Procedure

1. Consider the following factors:
- Blast radius: The distance from the explosion where blast overpressure can cause injury or death.
- Thermal radiation radius: The distance at which thermal radiation can cause burns or temporary vision loss.
- Nuclear radiation radius: The distance at which ionizing radiation exposure is lethal or harmful.

2. Determine the largest safety radius:
- For each type of radiation (blast, thermal, nuclear), calculate the respective radii using standard formulas and tables provided in FM 3-3-1.
- The largest radius among these three is considered the safety distance, as it represents the most hazardous exposure.

3. Account for personnel vulnerability categories:
- Personnel are assumed to be in one of three vulnerability categories: *unwarned, exposed*, *warned, exposed*, or *warned, protected*.
- Each category has different levels of protection and risk based on the environment (e.g., open ground vs. foxholes or vehicles).

4. Use appropriate transmission factors:
- For personnel in foxholes, use foxhole transmission factors to compute nuclear radiation radii.
- For personnel in tanks, use medium tank transmission factors with radiation liners for accurate calculations.

5. Adjust for vehicle damage criteria:
- Use the term *moderate damage to wheeled vehicles* as a reference point when calculating blast radii for casualties involving vehicles such as HMMWVs or 2-1/2-ton trucks.

6. Apply overpressure thresholds:
- For personnel in foxholes, use a threshold of 296 kilopascals (kPa) (43 psi) to determine when blast overpressures can cause lung hemorrhage and casualties.

7. Document the final safety distance:
- Record the calculated safety distance for future reference or operational planning.
safety distance,nuclear contamination,blast radiation,thermal radiation,nuclear radiation,vulnerability categories,foxhole transmission factors,tank transmission factors
Avoid Looking at the Fireball Stage of a Nuclear Explosion
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Do not look directly at the fireball stage of a nuclear explosion.
2. The brilliant light from the fireball can cause permanent eye damage.
3. Wait until the fireball transforms into the nuclear burst cloud stage,...
Procedure:

1. Do not look directly at the fireball stage of a nuclear explosion.
2. The brilliant light from the fireball can cause permanent eye damage.
3. Wait until the fireball transforms into the nuclear burst cloud stage, which is indicated by the light fading to a dull reddish glow.
4. At this point, the cloud may be observed safely and appears as either a spherical cloud (high airburst) or a mushroom-type cloud with or without a stem (low air or surface burst).
5. The fireball stage lasts until the light from the explosion ceases to radiate.
nuclear,fireball,eye damage,radiation safety
Responder Actions in the SDZ
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Minimize responder risks while managing response efforts in the Survivable Dose Zone (SDZ).

Procedure:
1. Do not prioritize response efforts within the SDZ due to likely hazardous outdoor radiation levels and near-complete...
Objective: Minimize responder risks while managing response efforts in the Survivable Dose Zone (SDZ).

Procedure:
1. Do not prioritize response efforts within the SDZ due to likely hazardous outdoor radiation levels and near-complete destruction.
2. If responders must enter the SDZ, they should:
- Wear PPE appropriate for non-radiological hazards, including protection from fire, sharps, hazardous dust, and smoke.
- Use high-range radiation monitoring instruments (see Appendix 2.1 for instrument information).
3. Avoid entering the SDZ until radiation dose rates have dropped substantially in the days following a nuclear detonation.
4. Reassess radiation dose rates within the SDZ when more resources become available later in the response.
5. Ensure that all response missions are justified to minimize responder risks.
SDZ, PPE, radiation monitoring, responder safety, nuclear detonation
Determine When It Is Safe to Leave a Fallout Shelter
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Purpose: To use the KFM or other radiation measuring devices to determine when it is safe to leave a fallout shelter.

Steps:
1. Measure Dose Rates: Use the KFM or another reliable instrument to measure the current radiation dose rate...
Purpose: To use the KFM or other radiation measuring devices to determine when it is safe to leave a fallout shelter.

Steps:
1. Measure Dose Rates: Use the KFM or another reliable instrument to measure the current radiation dose rate (R/hr) outside the shelter.
2. Compare with Safety Thresholds: Compare the measured dose rate against established safety thresholds for exposure. These thresholds depend on the duration of exposure and the type of activity being performed (e.g., short-term tasks vs. long-term habitation).
3. Calculate Exposure Risk: Use the KFM's built-in calculations or refer to radiation safety guidelines to determine if the measured dose rate is within safe limits for the intended activity.
4. Monitor Over Time: Continue monitoring radiation levels over time, as they may still be decreasing slowly even after the initial rapid decay phase.
5. Make a Decision: Based on all measurements and calculations, decide whether it is safe to leave the shelter permanently or only for short-term tasks.

Notes:
• The KFM can help calculate permissible exposure times based on dose rates.
• It is important not to rely solely on general broadcasts but to use local measurements.
fallout shelter safety, radiation exposure limits, KFM usage, post-nuclear evacuation, dose rate monitoring
Emergency Worker Dose Guidelines for Radiation Exposure
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Ensure emergency workers are protected from excessive radiation exposure.

Procedure:
1. Follow the EPA PAG Manual guidelines:
- Lifesaving activities: Maximum dose of 250 mSv (25 rem) over the entire response period.
-...
Objective: Ensure emergency workers are protected from excessive radiation exposure.

Procedure:
1. Follow the EPA PAG Manual guidelines:
- Lifesaving activities: Maximum dose of 250 mSv (25 rem) over the entire response period.
- Protection of property: Maximum dose of 100 mSv (10 rem) for the incident.
- Other work: Occupational limit of 50 mSv (5 rem) per year.
2. Health and safety authorities must establish control points for maximum responder doses and dose rates.
3. Operations beyond these limits require justification, based on protective action guidelines (PAGs).

Notes:
• These guidelines may be exceeded in lifesaving actions under specific conditions.
• Dose monitoring equipment should be used to track exposure levels in real time.
worker safety,dose limits,radiation guidelines,nuclear emergency
Identify Vulnerability Categories for Personnel in Nuclear Contamination Scenarios
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
### Vulnerability Category Identification Procedure

1. Unwarned, Exposed—Personnel:
- Applies to personnel standing in the open at time of burst.
- They drop to a prone position before the blast wave arrives.
- May have areas of...
### Vulnerability Category Identification Procedure

1. Unwarned, Exposed—Personnel:
- Applies to personnel standing in the open at time of burst.
- They drop to a prone position before the blast wave arrives.
- May have areas of bare skin exposed to direct thermal radiation and may suffer temporary loss of vision.
- Also applies to civilian personnel in open areas.

2. Warned, Exposed—Personnel:
- Applies to personnel who are prone on open ground with all skin areas covered.
- They have an overall thermal protection at least equal to that provided by a two-layer summer uniform.
- Troops have been warned but do not have time to dig foxholes.

3. Warned, Protected—Personnel:
- Applies to personnel who have some protection against heat, blast, and radiation.
- Includes the following protective environments:
- Tanks
- Armored personnel carriers
- Foxholes
- Weapons emplacements
- Command posts and shelters

4. Personnel in Foxholes:
- Refers to personnel in 1.8-meter-deep open foxholes with a 0.3-meter-firing step.
- Blast overpressures of 296 kilopascals (kPa) (43 psi) can cause lung hemorrhage, which is the blast injury mechanism for producing casualties.

5. Personnel in Tanks:
- Severe damage to tanks was used as a reference point to find blast radii for casualties involving personnel inside tanks.
- Nuclear radiation radii were computed using transmission factors for medium tanks with radiation liners.

6. Moderate Damage to Wheeled Vehicles:
- Applies to vehicles such as HMMWVs, 2-1/2-ton trucks, and other vehicles smaller than 2 tons.
- Originally referred to WWII-era vehicles but now includes modern equivalents.
vulnerability categories,nuclear contamination,foxholes,tanks,wheeled vehicles,thermal protection,blast overpressure
Public Actions in the SDZ
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Ensure public safety within the Survivable Dose Zone (SDZ) following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Stay indoors unless there is an imminent threat such as fire, building collapse, medical emergency, or other immediate...
Objective: Ensure public safety within the Survivable Dose Zone (SDZ) following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Stay indoors unless there is an imminent threat such as fire, building collapse, medical emergency, or other immediate danger.
2. Allow 12–24 hours for radiation levels to decay before attempting to move.
3. Use protected escape routes if possible, including:
- Connections between buildings
- Tunnels
- Core areas within buildings
- Sidewalk overhangs
- The shortest distances between adjacent structures.
SDZ, radiation decay, protected escape routes, public safety, nuclear detonation
Understand Radiation Dose Rates and Fallout Behavior
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Purpose: To understand how radiation dose rates change over time after a nuclear event.

Key Information:
• Radiation Dose Rate: Measured in roentgens per hour (R/hr). This is the primary metric used to assess fallout danger.
• **Decay...
Purpose: To understand how radiation dose rates change over time after a nuclear event.

Key Information:
• Radiation Dose Rate: Measured in roentgens per hour (R/hr). This is the primary metric used to assess fallout danger.
• Decay Pattern: Radiation levels decrease rapidly during the first few days after a nuclear attack, then decline more slowly over time. This makes it critical to monitor dose rates accurately.
• Importance of Measurement: Accurate measurement allows occupants of fallout shelters to determine when it is safe to leave the shelter and to control radiation exposure during emergency tasks (e.g., retrieving water).

Notes:
• Civil defense broadcasts may provide general information, but local measurements are essential for accurate assessment.
• The KFM can be used to measure dose rates and calculate permissible exposure times.
radiation dose rate, fallout decay, R/hr, nuclear safety, radiation monitoring, post-nuclear survival
Determine Radiation Safety Thresholds for Personnel
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Establish radiation safety thresholds based on risk levels.

Radiation Dose Limits (for previously unexposed troops):
1. Negligible Risk: 50 cGy
2. Moderate Risk: 70 cGy
3. Emergency Risk: 150 cGy

Use Case: These...
Objective: Establish radiation safety thresholds based on risk levels.

Radiation Dose Limits (for previously unexposed troops):
1. Negligible Risk: 50 cGy
2. Moderate Risk: 70 cGy
3. Emergency Risk: 150 cGy

Use Case: These thresholds are used to determine the safety of personnel in areas affected by nuclear radiation.
radiation dose limits,nuclear safety,military personnel
Determine Safe Time Outside During Radiation Exposure
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Assess the external radiation dose rate:
- If the dose rate is below 2 R per hour, you may begin to go outside on a regular basis.
- Limit your time outside to avoid receiving over 6 R of exposure each day.
- When the...
Procedure:

1. Assess the external radiation dose rate:
- If the dose rate is below 2 R per hour, you may begin to go outside on a regular basis.
- Limit your time outside to avoid receiving over 6 R of exposure each day.
- When the dose rate decays to 0.25 R per hour, you may leave the shelter permanently.

2. Use Table 20 for guidance:
- For a dose rate of 2.00 R/hr, the maximum safe stay time is 3 hours/day.
- For a dose rate of 1.00 R/hr, the maximum safe stay time is 6 hours/day.
- For a dose rate of 0.50 R/hr, the maximum safe stay time is 12 hours/day.
- For a dose rate of 0.25 R/hr, you may remain outside for 24 hours/day.

3. Monitor radiation levels regularly:
- Use tools such as the Kearny Fallout Meter (KFM) to measure and track external radiation dose rates accurately.
- Ensure that your exposure does not exceed safe limits, even when the dose rate is low.

4. Follow safety protocols:
- Always wear protective clothing and minimize skin exposure during excursions.
- Avoid prolonged stays in high-radiation areas unless absolutely necessary.
- If symptoms of radiation sickness appear (e.g., nausea, fatigue), seek shelter immediately and consult a medical professional if possible.
radiation exposure,safe time outside,external dose rate,Kearny Fallout Meter,radiation safety
Hazards in the MDZ
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Identify and manage hazards present in the Moderate Damage Zone (MDZ).

Procedure:
1. Expect elevated radiation levels, downed power lines, ruptured gas lines, unstable structures, sharp metal objects, broken glass, toxic dust...
Objective: Identify and manage hazards present in the Moderate Damage Zone (MDZ).

Procedure:
1. Expect elevated radiation levels, downed power lines, ruptured gas lines, unstable structures, sharp metal objects, broken glass, toxic dust from collapsed buildings, and ruptured fuel tanks.
2. Be aware that visibility may be limited in much of the MDZ due to:
- Dust raised by collapsed buildings
- Smoke from fires
3. Recognize that water infrastructure may be damaged, which can limit firefighting operations.
4. Use caution when navigating the area and ensure proper PPE is worn to mitigate risks.
MDZ hazards, radiation levels, water damage, visibility issues, PPE
Identify Stabilized Cloud Stage and Measurement Timing
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. The stabilized cloud stage begins when the nuclear cloud ceases to grow in height, typically between 4 to 14 minutes after detonation, depending on yield.
2. During this stage, measure:
- **Nuclear burst angular cloud...
Procedure:

1. The stabilized cloud stage begins when the nuclear cloud ceases to grow in height, typically between 4 to 14 minutes after detonation, depending on yield.
2. During this stage, measure:
- Nuclear burst angular cloud width at H+5 minutes (line item Lima)
- Stabilized cloud-top or cloud-bottom height measured at H+10 minutes (line item Mike)
- Stabilized cloud-top or cloud-bottom angle measured at H+10 minutes.
3. After H+10 minutes, no further measurements are taken, as the cloud's growth is due to wind, not nuclear energy.
4. Use these measurements in conjunction with nomograms and the ABC-M4A1 nuclear yield calculator for accurate yield estimation.
stabilized cloud,H+10 minutes,nomograms,ABC-M4A1
MDZ Damage and Casualty Management
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Address the challenges of responding to casualties in the Moderate Damage Zone (MDZ).

Procedure:
1. Recognize that the MDZ is an area with substantial damage to most structures, with only minor damage to heavily reinforced...
Objective: Address the challenges of responding to casualties in the Moderate Damage Zone (MDZ).

Procedure:
1. Recognize that the MDZ is an area with substantial damage to most structures, with only minor damage to heavily reinforced buildings.
2. Understand that people in this zone may experience injuries or death from:
- Blast over-pressure
- Building collapse
- Flying debris
- Fires
- Thermal burns
3. Note that radiation injuries and deaths may occur, even without significant fallout.
4. Prioritize urgent medical care for survivors in the MDZ as they are more likely to survive than those in other zones.
MDZ, moderate damage zone, medical care, blast injuries, radiation exposure
Managing Temporary Emergence from Shelter in Post-Radiation Scenario
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Monitor Radiation Exposure:
- Track each individual's daily radiation exposure to ensure it remains within safe limits.
- Prevent group members from leaving the shelter too soon, even if they feel anxious or eager to do...
Procedure:

1. Monitor Radiation Exposure:
- Track each individual's daily radiation exposure to ensure it remains within safe limits.
- Prevent group members from leaving the shelter too soon, even if they feel anxious or eager to do so.

2. Conduct Local Radiation Survey:
- Have the radiological defense team perform a detailed survey of local radiation levels on and around your property.
- Identify both 'hot spots' (areas with high radiation) and 'cold spots' (areas with lower radiation).

3. Adjust Dose Rate Measurements for Individual Heights:
- Measure dose rate at the midbody level of each individual, not at the standard 36-inch height.
- For adults: Use the standard measurement as a reference.
- For adult females: Multiply the standard dose by 1.2 to account for closer proximity to the ground.
- For toddlers (3 feet tall): Multiply the standard dose by 4.
- For crawling infants: Multiply the standard dose by 64.

4. Protect Children from High Radiation Exposure:
- If children must be taken across contaminated areas, carry them on an adult's shoulders to reduce their exposure (they receive only one-fourth of the standard adult dose).
- Avoid exposing children to high radiation levels even if adults are safe.

5. Avoid Lying Down in Contaminated Areas:
- Do not lie down or sleep on contaminated ground, even if the survey meter at 36 inches reads a safe level (e.g., 0.25 R per hour).
- Sleeping on contaminated ground can result in exposure of up to 128 R in one night.

6. Limit Security Team Exposure:
- Keep your primary security team inside the shelter as much as possible during this period.
- Send secondary or 'second-string' defenders to patrol the perimeter instead.
- Shield your best fighters from radiation exposure to ensure their long-term health and readiness for future emergencies.

7. Determine Safe Emergence Threshold:
- When the radiation dose rate falls below 6 R per day, it becomes safe to leave the shelter permanently or for extended periods.
- At this point, you may still choose to sleep in the shelter but can use outdoor areas during the day for more space and privacy.

8. Reorganize Teams Post-Permanent Emergence:
- Reassess team roles and responsibilities as your group transitions from shelter life to post-war living.
- Plan for long-term self-sufficiency through activities such as gardening, hunting, fishing, farming, or ranching.

9. Prepare for Social and Economic Challenges:
- Be prepared to manage the shift from a collective survival mindset (common in shelter life) to individual responsibility in self-sufficient living.
- Avoid conflicts by ensuring fair distribution of labor and resources among group members.

10. Engage with Other Groups and Local Government:
- Offer your knowledge and skills to local government or surviving groups as a gesture of community support.
- Share survival techniques without compromising your own food and supplies.
- Be prepared for the possibility that you may become the de facto local government in an area of chaos.

11. Assign New Roles Based on Expertise:
- Your security team may take on police duties.
- Your medical team may assume health department responsibilities.
- Your food-and-water team may act as county agents.
- The radiological defense team may lead decontamination efforts.
- Communications personnel may set up links with surrounding communities.

12. Train and Support the Group:
- Prepare your training staff to teach survival skills to others in the area.
- Ensure that all members of your group are prepared for their new roles and responsibilities.
radiation exposure, temporary emergence, dose rate measurement, hot spots, cold spots, child protection, security team management, post-radiation reorganization
Measure Angular Cloud Width at H+5 Minutes
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Measure the angular cloud width five minutes after detonation (H+5).
2. Use optical equipment as listed in Figure 3-3 for accurate measurement.
3. If optical equipment is unavailable, use a lensatic compass.
4. **Do...
Procedure:

1. Measure the angular cloud width five minutes after detonation (H+5).
2. Use optical equipment as listed in Figure 3-3 for accurate measurement.
3. If optical equipment is unavailable, use a lensatic compass.
4. Do not use binoculars, as they provide extremely inaccurate measurements.
5. The operator sights an azimuth to the left side of the cloud and one to the right side of the cloud.
6. Calculate the angular width by taking the difference between the two azimuth readings (in degrees or mils).
7. Report this measurement on line item Lima in the NBC 1 report, typically in mils.
angular cloud width,H+5 minutes,optical equipment,NBC 1 report
Understanding the ALARA Principle for Radiation Safety
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Apply the ALARA principle (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) to all actions involving potential radiation exposure.
2. Minimize exposure time, distance from the source, and shielding when possible.
3. Avoid unnecessary...
Procedure:

1. Apply the ALARA principle (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) to all actions involving potential radiation exposure.
2. Minimize exposure time, distance from the source, and shielding when possible.
3. Avoid unnecessary exposure to radiation even if it is a small dose, unless there is a direct benefit to doing so.
4. Use protective measures such as PPE, sheltering, and monitoring equipment to reduce risk.
5. Follow guidelines provided by public health authorities or emergency responders for safe actions in radiation zones.

Important Notes:
• The ALARA principle should guide all decisions related to radiation exposure.
• This includes both the general public and emergency responders.
• For more information, visit the CDC website on the ALARA principle.
alara principle, radiation safety, minimize exposure, nuclear detonation, public health guidelines

Radiation Shielding (15) -

Constructing Earth-Shielded Fighting Positions for Nuclear Radiation Protection
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Select a location with as much natural earth cover as possible between the soldier and the potential nuclear burst.
2. Dig fighting positions or bunkers to maximize earth shielding. The more earth cover, the better the...
Procedure:

1. Select a location with as much natural earth cover as possible between the soldier and the potential nuclear burst.
2. Dig fighting positions or bunkers to maximize earth shielding. The more earth cover, the better the protection against radiation.
3. Use flat earth cover for underground shelters rather than aboveground structures, as it provides greater line-of-sight thickness for shielding (see Figure 4-5).
4. Add layers of sandbags filled with sand or compacted clay to further reduce radiation exposure:
- Each layer reduces transmitted radiation by a factor of two.
- For example, three layers of sand-or clay-filled sandbags provide a protection factor of 64 (38 cGy) for a hypothetical 2,400 cGy dose.
5. Use denser materials like sand or compacted clay instead of soil for better radiation shielding:
- Sand or clay-filled sandbags can give up to 66% more protection than the same thickness of soil.
6. Avoid cracks between sandbags, as they may allow radiation leakage.
7. Cover sandbags with a small amount of earth and/or sod (see Figure 4-6) to:
- Prevent exposure to thermal radiation, which can cause sandbags to burn or spill their contents.
- Enhance camouflage and provide additional protection from conventional fragmentation.
8. Use materials that stop neutron radiation, such as:
- Damp earth or concrete (12 inches of concrete or 24 inches of damp earth reduce neutron radiation exposure by a factor of 10).
- Wet sandbags (reduce neutron radiation by a factor of two for every 4-inch layer).
- Containers of water, fuel, or oil.
9. Ensure all-around shielding, as radiation scatters in all directions and must be blocked from all sides.
earth-shielded positions,nuclear radiation protection,sandbags,shielding materials,damp earth,concrete,wet sandbags,neutron shielding
Establish Deep-Covered Protective Positions for Nuclear Survivability
Critical Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Procedure:

1. Identify a suitable location for establishing protective positions within a suspected target area.
2. Dig or construct deep-covered positions to minimize the danger from blast and thermal radiation.
3. **Ensure complete...
Procedure:

1. Identify a suitable location for establishing protective positions within a suspected target area.
2. Dig or construct deep-covered positions to minimize the danger from blast and thermal radiation.
3. Ensure complete coverage of hands, face, and neck by wearing full uniforms at all times.
4. Avoid radioactive fallout areas whenever possible. If unavoidable, remain in deep-covered protective positions.
5. Use buttoned-up armor vehicles for limited protection from nuclear radiation.
6. Remove antennae from equipment to reduce the effects of EMP (Electromagnetic Pulse) and TREE (Transient Radiation Effects on Electronics).
7. Place critical electrical equipment into protective positions to minimize damage from EMP and TREE.

Notes:
• Deep-covered positions should be as far underground as possible for maximum protection.
• Protective gear must be worn consistently in nuclear threat environments.
nuclear, radiation, protective positions, survivability, EMP, TREE
Immediate Protective Shelter in Nuclear Conditions
Critical Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
Objective: Provide immediate protection from nuclear radiation.

Steps:
1. Find protective shelter immediately! This is the most critical action to reduce radiation exposure.
2. If possible, gather all survival equipment (time...
Objective: Provide immediate protection from nuclear radiation.

Steps:
1. Find protective shelter immediately! This is the most critical action to reduce radiation exposure.
2. If possible, gather all survival equipment (time permitting).
3. Avoid detection and capture by remaining hidden.
4. Seek existing shelter that may be improved:
- Look for caves, tunnels, or other natural shelters.
5. If no shelter is available, dig a trench or foxhole as follows:
- Dig the trench deep enough to provide protection (at least 3 feet deep).
- Enlarge the trench for comfort.
- Cover the trench with available materials such as branches, leaves, or debris.
6. Use Figure IX-1 and Figure IX-2 for visual guidance on shelter construction.

Important Notes:
• Radiation protection depends on time of exposure, distance from the source, and shielding.
• Avoid unnecessary movement to reduce radiation exposure.
nuclear radiation, shelter construction, immediate action, foxhole, trench digging
Radiation Shielding Efficiencies and Shelter Prioritization
Critical Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
Objective: Understand the effectiveness of different materials in shielding from radiation and prioritize shelter options.

Shielding Material Thickness (inches):
• Iron/Steel: 0.7 inches
• Brick: 2.0 inches
• Concrete: 2.2...
Objective: Understand the effectiveness of different materials in shielding from radiation and prioritize shelter options.

Shielding Material Thickness (inches):
• Iron/Steel: 0.7 inches
• Brick: 2.0 inches
• Concrete: 2.2 inches
• Earth: 3.3 inches
• Cinder Block: 5.3 inches
• Ice: 6.8 inches
• Wood (Soft): 8.8 inches
• Snow: 20.3 inches

Shelter Prioritization:
1. Cave or tunnel covered with 3 or more feet of earth.
2. Storm/storage cellars.
3. Culverts.
4. Basements.
5. Abandoned stone/mud buildings.
6. Foxhole 4 feet deep (remove topsoil within 2 feet radius of foxhole lip).

Important Notes:
• One thickness of any material reduces the received radiation dose by 1/2.
• Additional layers further reduce the dose by half per layer.
• Shelter survival: Keep contaminated materials out of shelter.
• In good weather, bury contaminated clothing outside the shelter (recover later).
• In bad weather, shake or beat wet clothing with branches. Rinse and/or shake wet clothing but do not wring it out.
radiation shielding, material efficiency, shelter prioritization, foxhole construction
Mitigate External Radiation Exposure Through Shelter
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Reduce radiation dose by using adequate shelter.

Procedure:
1. Seek shelter in a building with thick walls, multiple layers of construction, and minimal windows.
2. Stay inside the shelter for as long as possible to avoid...
Objective: Reduce radiation dose by using adequate shelter.

Procedure:
1. Seek shelter in a building with thick walls, multiple layers of construction, and minimal windows.
2. Stay inside the shelter for as long as possible to avoid exposure to external radiation.
3. Ensure that all occupants are protected from direct exposure to radioactive particles outside the shelter.
4. Use additional shielding materials (e.g., lead-lined barriers) if available to further reduce radiation dose.

Notes:
• Adequate shelter reduces radiation dose by a factor of 10 or more.
• Shelter is especially critical in the Downwind Recovery Zone (DRZ).
shelter,radiation exposure,nuclear detonation,DRZ,shielding
Calculating Radiation Protection Factors Using Earth and Sandbag Layers
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Understand the baseline protection factor for an open fighting position (protection factor of 8, reducing radiation to about 12.5% of the original dose).
2. Use Table 4-1 to determine the protection factor based on...
Procedure:

1. Understand the baseline protection factor for an open fighting position (protection factor of 8, reducing radiation to about 12.5% of the original dose).
2. Use Table 4-1 to determine the protection factor based on increasing amounts of earth shielding:
- For example, 12 inches of earth provides a protection factor of 24 (reducing a hypothetical free-in-air dose of 2,400 cGy to 100 cGy).
3. Use Table 4-2 to determine the protection factor based on layers of sandbags filled with sand or compacted clay:
- For example, three layers (12 inches) provide a protection factor of 64 (reducing a hypothetical free-in-air dose of 2,400 cGy to 38 cGy).
4. Apply the rule of thumb that each additional 6-inch thickness of overhead earth cover reduces scattered radiation by a factor of two.
5. Use the same rule for sandbags, where each layer (filled with sand or compacted clay) also reduces transmitted radiation by a factor of two.
protection factors,earth shielding,sandbag layers,radiation reduction,factor calculations
Construct Field-Expedient Overhead Cover
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Purpose: Reduce exposure to thermal and initial nuclear radiation, fallout, and provide protection from blast waves and collapse.

Materials Required:
• U-shaped metal pickets
• Timbers
• Fabrics
• Sandbags or earth
• Ammunition boxes...
Purpose: Reduce exposure to thermal and initial nuclear radiation, fallout, and provide protection from blast waves and collapse.

Materials Required:
• U-shaped metal pickets
• Timbers
• Fabrics
• Sandbags or earth
• Ammunition boxes (filled with earth)

Procedure:
1. Choose dense covering materials to maximize radiation shielding.
2. Construct the cover in depth, ensuring it is thick enough to absorb blast energy and radiation.
3. Provide strong supports for the structure to prevent collapse under pressure from a blast wave.
4. Cover as much of the opening as possible to minimize exposure through gaps or weak points.

Notes:
• Avoid using poorly constructed cover that may not withstand the force of a blast.
• Ensure all sides and openings are adequately covered, including any gaps in vehicles used for overhead cover.
overhead cover, radiation shielding, nuclear blast, fallout protection, field-expedient construction
Constructing Snow and Ice Shelters for Nuclear Fallout Protection
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Assess the terrain: Identify natural features such as hills, valleys, or depressions that can be used to enhance protection from nuclear fallout.
2. Use snow and ice for shielding: Construct shelters using loose snow or...
Procedure:

1. Assess the terrain: Identify natural features such as hills, valleys, or depressions that can be used to enhance protection from nuclear fallout.
2. Use snow and ice for shielding: Construct shelters using loose snow or hard-packed snow to reduce radiation exposure. Snow provides some level of protection even though it is less effective than earth.
3. Snow thickness requirements:
- Loose snow: Use approximately 60 centimeters (24 inches) of loose snow over contaminated areas to reduce the dose rate by about half.
- Hard-packed snow: Use at least 30 centimeters (12 inches) of hard-packed snow for similar radiation reduction benefits.
4. Combine with natural terrain features: Maximize protection by integrating shelters into natural formations such as hills or rock outcrops to increase shielding from fallout.
5. Avoid reliance on tents: Tents are not effective in reducing radiation hazards and should not be used as the primary form of protection against nuclear fallout.
snow, ice, nuclear fallout, radiation shielding, shelter construction
Determine Inadequate Shelter for Fallout Radiation Protection
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Identify structures that provide limited or no protection:
- Single-story wood frame houses without basements offer minimal shielding and are not considered adequate shelters.
- Vehicles (cars, trucks) lack dense...
Procedure:

1. Identify structures that provide limited or no protection:
- Single-story wood frame houses without basements offer minimal shielding and are not considered adequate shelters.
- Vehicles (cars, trucks) lack dense shielding materials and should not be used as shelters.

2. Understand the limitations of inadequate shelter options:
- These structures do not reduce radiation doses by a factor of 10 or more.
- They are at higher risk for exposure to penetrating radiation from fallout.

3. Avoid reliance on materials with minimal shielding properties:
- Wood, drywall, and thin sheet metal provide only limited protection unless multiple layers are used in combination.

4. Assess the need for alternative shelter options:
- If only inadequate shelter is available, consider moving to a more protected location or using additional shielding materials (e.g., adding layers of drywall) to improve protection.
inadequate shelter, radiation exposure, fallout, vehicle shelter, wood frame house
Identify Adequate Shelter for Fallout Radiation Protection
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Assess the structure’s protection factor, which is the degree to which radiation dose is reduced:
- Basements, centers of large multi-story buildings, parking garages, and tunnels are examples of adequate shelters that can...
Procedure:

1. Assess the structure’s protection factor, which is the degree to which radiation dose is reduced:
- Basements, centers of large multi-story buildings, parking garages, and tunnels are examples of adequate shelters that can reduce radiation doses by a factor of 10 or more.
- These structures provide sufficient shielding due to dense building materials such as concrete, brick, stone, and earth.

2. Avoid inadequate shelter options:
- Vehicles (cars, trucks) are not adequate shelters because they lack dense shielding material.
- Single-story wood frame houses without basements offer limited shielding and should not be considered safe shelters.

3. Consider alternative materials for shielding:
- If a structure lacks dense materials, multiple layers of minimal shielding (e.g., drywall) can still provide adequate protection if placed between the individual and radiation sources such as fallout on roofs.

4. Structural integrity is important but not absolute:
- Buildings with minor damage can still be used as shelters as long as they are structurally sound.
- Airtightness is not required for protection against fallout radiation.

5. Increase distance from fallout sources:
- Move to the center of a structure or increase distance from deposited fallout on roofs to reduce exposure.
shelter, radiation protection, fallout, shielding materials, adequate shelter, nuclear detonation
Use a Vehicle as Expedient Overhead Cover
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Purpose: Provide rapid overhead cover in a nuclear or blast scenario using available vehicles.

Materials Required:
• Any vehicle (preferably armored)
• Sandbags (optional)

Procedure:
1. Drive the vehicle over the top of a foxhole to...
Purpose: Provide rapid overhead cover in a nuclear or blast scenario using available vehicles.

Materials Required:
• Any vehicle (preferably armored)
• Sandbags (optional)

Procedure:
1. Drive the vehicle over the top of a foxhole to create immediate overhead cover.
2. Use a heavy armored vehicle for better protection than a wheeled vehicle.
3. If possible, remain inside an armored vehicle for maximum safety.
4. Cover any openings in the vehicle (such as between treads, road wheels, and tires) with sandbags to reduce radiation exposure.

Notes:
• Vehicles are not effective neutron shields; additional measures may be needed.
• The blast wave may displace the vehicle or cause a foxhole collapse, so ensure the structure is stable.
vehicle cover, expedient shelter, nuclear blast, foxhole protection, radiation shielding
Calculate Inside Dose Using Transmission Factor
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Calculate the inside dose (ID) using the outside dose (OD) and transmission factor (TF).

Procedure:
1. Identify the outside dose or dose rate (OD) measured externally.
2. Determine the transmission factor (TF) for the...
Objective: Calculate the inside dose (ID) using the outside dose (OD) and transmission factor (TF).

Procedure:
1. Identify the outside dose or dose rate (OD) measured externally.
2. Determine the transmission factor (TF) for the specific shelter or vehicle being used. TFs are precalculated in Table 6-1 of FM 3-3-1 and depend on the shielding properties of the enclosure.
3. Use the formula:
ID = OD × TF
4. Example:
- If the outside dose is 90 cGyph and the transmission factor for an M113 armored personnel carrier is 0.3, then:
ID = 90 × 0.3 = 27 cGyph
5. For dose rates, use the same formula:
- If outside dose rate is 100 cGyph and TF is 0.3, then inside dose rate is:
ID = 100 × 0.3 = 30 cGyph

Notes:
• Transmission factors are always less than one.
• TFs are based on gamma radiation from Cobalt-60, not fallout. Adjustments may be needed for actual fallout conditions.
• For vehicles with AN/VDR2 installed, the attenuation factor (inverse of TF) is already programmed and can be used directly.
transmission factor, inside dose, outside dose, radiation shielding, M113, nuclear contamination
Determine Protection Factor (PF) for Radiation Shielding
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Calculate the protection factor (PF) to assess the shielding effectiveness of a vehicle or shelter.

Procedure:
1. Use the formula:
PF = OD / ID, where:
- OD is the outside dose or dose rate
- ID is the...
Objective: Calculate the protection factor (PF) to assess the shielding effectiveness of a vehicle or shelter.

Procedure:
1. Use the formula:
PF = OD / ID, where:
- OD is the outside dose or dose rate
- ID is the inside dose or dose rate
2. Alternatively, use the relationship between PF and TF:
PF = 1 / TF
3. For example, if a vehicle has a transmission factor (TF) of 0.3, then:
PF = 1 / 0.3 ≈ 3.33
4. The PF indicates how much the radiation is reduced inside the enclosure compared to outside.

Notes:
• Protection factors are used by commanders and operations personnel to evaluate shielding capabilities of vehicles and shelters.
• For vehicles with AN/VDR2 installed, the attenuation factor (inverse of TF) is already programmed and can be used directly for PF calculations.
protection factor, transmission factor, radiation shielding, vehicle protection, nuclear contamination
Radiation Protection Factors of Sand-or-clay-filled Sandbags
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Radiation Protection Factors of Sand-or-clay-filled Sandbags:

|Soldier in:|Radiation Protection Factor|
|---|---|
|Open|None|
|Open foxhole, 4 feet deep|8|
|Same with 1 layer (4 inches)|16|
|Same with 2 layers (8 inches)|32|
|Same with 3 layers...
Radiation Protection Factors of Sand-or-clay-filled Sandbags:

|Soldier in:|Radiation Protection Factor|
|---|---|
|Open|None|
|Open foxhole, 4 feet deep|8|
|Same with 1 layer (4 inches)|16|
|Same with 2 layers (8 inches)|32|
|Same with 3 layers (12 inches)|64|

Notes:
• These protection factors indicate how much radiation shielding is provided by sandbags filled with sand or clay.
• The more layers, the greater the protection factor.
• This information is useful for creating temporary shelters and protective barriers in fallout situations.
sandbag shielding,nuclear protection,radiation factors
Understanding Shielding Properties of Common Materials for Radiation Protection
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Shielding Properties of Common Materials:

|Material|Half-value layer thickness (cm)|
|---|---|
|Steel|2|
|Concrete|6|
|Earth|8|
|Water|12|
|Wood|22|

Notes:
• The half-value layer is the thickness of a material required to reduce the...
Shielding Properties of Common Materials:

|Material|Half-value layer thickness (cm)|
|---|---|
|Steel|2|
|Concrete|6|
|Earth|8|
|Water|12|
|Wood|22|

Notes:
• The half-value layer is the thickness of a material required to reduce the incident dose or dose rate by 50%.
• These values are essential for determining how much shielding is needed in fallout shelters and other protective structures.
radiation shielding,material properties,nuclear protection

Emp Mitigation (15) -

Mitigate EMP Effects Through Cable and Ground Configuration
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Reduce the risk of electromagnetic pulse (EMP) damage by properly configuring cables and grounding systems.

### Key Considerations for Cables and Grounds
• Avoid loops that function as magnetic dipole antennas. Loops may not be...
Objective: Reduce the risk of electromagnetic pulse (EMP) damage by properly configuring cables and grounding systems.

### Key Considerations for Cables and Grounds
• Avoid loops that function as magnetic dipole antennas. Loops may not be obvious immediately; different types of equipment and connectors may be involved.
• Use short, straight cable runs whenever possible. The length of a cable has a major impact on the amount of EMP energy it picks up and transmits to equipment:
- A 5-foot cable run might pick up an electrical current of 5 amps, which is enough to operationally upset some electronic equipment but not cause functional damage.
- A 1-mile cable run in the same EMP field could pick up as much as 50,000 amps.
• Use short straight cable runs with low impedance grounds; and if possible, cluster cable runs to minimize exposure.

### Cable Placement and Shielding
• If cables are strung in the air, they can pick up more EMP energy. Shallow underground burial does not provide significant protection; deep burial (10 feet or more) is not worth the construction effort.
• Use balanced, shielded, twisted-pair cable in preference to coaxial; and use coaxial in preference to unshielded cable:
- An unshielded cable could pick up as much as 100,000 volts in a 250-foot run.
- A coaxial cable would only pick up about 1/30th of this energy, roughly 2,000 to 3,000 volts.
- A balanced, shielded twisted-pair cable would do even better, picking up only about 500 to 1,000 volts.

### Grounding Practices
• If grounds are required for troop safety, provide them according to the specifications in technical manuals. Also use grounds that offer the best protection against EMP.
• Use insulating material, such as rubber mats, in communication vans, shelters, etc., where possible.

### Grounding Schemes
• Whenever possible, use common grounds. In multiple cabinet systems (communications vans), ground each cabinet within the shelter and have only one common ground reaching outside the vehicle to the earth.
• Employ grounding schemes that provide the best protection against EMP:
- The preferred grounding schemes for EMP mitigation are the single-point star or crow’s foot ground and the tree configuration.
- The tree grounding scheme is likely to be the most practical for many configurations of equipment.
- Since loops are to be avoided, use the tree configuration whenever equipment needs to be linked by connectors that might couple EMP energy.
EMP mitigation,cable runs,grounding schemes,shielded cables,insulating material,tree grounding
Mitigate EMP Effects on C3I Equipment
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Reduce the vulnerability of Command, Control, Communications, and Intelligence (C3I) equipment to EMP effects.

### Dispersed Operations for Survivability
• Use dispersed operations, including the use of remotes, to increase...
Objective: Reduce the vulnerability of Command, Control, Communications, and Intelligence (C3I) equipment to EMP effects.

### Dispersed Operations for Survivability
• Use dispersed operations, including the use of remotes, to increase survivability by reducing the unit’s single-point signatures and increasing the number of targets an enemy must find and engage.
• When operating in this mode, units depend more on electronic devices to maintain command and control through communications. This increases vulnerability if communications are interrupted or destroyed by EMP.

### EMP Effects on C3I Equipment
• Long lines between positions that can function as unintended antennas increase the unit’s vulnerability to EMP.
• If electronic communications are interrupted or destroyed by EMP, the unit may not be able to accomplish its missions.

### Mitigation Strategies for C3I Systems
• Make regular backups of computer memories to ensure that essential data is not lost in case of EMP-induced operational upset or damage. Store these backups in a separate location, ideally an EMP-shielded van, shelter, or vehicle.
• There are realistic tactical trade-offs for which no pat answers can be offered. However, some guidelines can be applied to specific METT-T situations (Mission, Enemy, Terrain, Troops, and Time).
C3I equipment,EMP effects,dispersed operations,data backup,EMP-shielded storage
Avoid Adding Grounding Points in Enclosures or Shelters
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Prevent the formation of grounding loops that can be damaged by EMP.

Procedure:
1. Do not add grounding points within an enclosure or shelter, as this may create unintended loops.
2. Ensure all grounding is done according to...
Objective: Prevent the formation of grounding loops that can be damaged by EMP.

Procedure:
1. Do not add grounding points within an enclosure or shelter, as this may create unintended loops.
2. Ensure all grounding is done according to approved technical specifications.
3. Verify that no additional grounding connections are made unless explicitly required by system design.
4. Train personnel on the risks of improper grounding and the importance of following established standards.
grounding loops, EMP mitigation, enclosure grounding
Develop an EMP Mitigation Plan for Tactical Units
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Understand the mission requirements and the types of electronic equipment in use.
2. Conduct a risk assessment to identify vulnerabilities related to EMP effects on critical systems.
3. **Identify both intentional and...
Procedure:

1. Understand the mission requirements and the types of electronic equipment in use.
2. Conduct a risk assessment to identify vulnerabilities related to EMP effects on critical systems.
3. Identify both intentional and unintentional antennas within the unit’s area of operations.
4. Implement shielding measures for sensitive electronics, such as using Faraday cages or conductive enclosures.
5. Remove or relocate unintended antennas that are in close proximity to electronic equipment where possible.
6. Train personnel on EMP effects and mitigation techniques.
7. Ensure all plans align with higher headquarters directives and standard operating procedures.
8. Review and update the plan regularly, especially after changes in equipment, mission, or environmental conditions.

Important Notes:
• Each unit must develop its own EMP mitigation plan based on its specific needs and capabilities.
• Mitigation plans should be consistent with assigned missions and integrated into overall operational planning.
emp mitigation plan, tactical units, nuclear contamination
Ensure Proper Cable Shield Identification and Maintenance
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Maintain the integrity of cable shielding to prevent EMP damage.

Procedure:
1. Identify all cable shields used in the system by operating and maintenance personnel.
2. Check, repair, or replace worn/damaged shields,...
Objective: Maintain the integrity of cable shielding to prevent EMP damage.

Procedure:
1. Identify all cable shields used in the system by operating and maintenance personnel.
2. Check, repair, or replace worn/damaged shields, ensuring they make good contact with connector shells.
3. Replace loose or damaged connections, such as gaskets and finger stocks.
4. Ensure that all shielded cables are properly grounded according to technical manuals.
cable shielding, EMP mitigation, maintenance procedures
Identify Unintended Antennas for EMP Mitigation
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Survey the area for any metal conducting materials that could act as unintended antennas.
- Examples include rail lines, pipes, power lines, vehicle bodies, and concertina wire.
2. Look for objects that would not be...
Procedure:

1. Survey the area for any metal conducting materials that could act as unintended antennas.
- Examples include rail lines, pipes, power lines, vehicle bodies, and concertina wire.
2. Look for objects that would not be touched during a lightning storm if placed vertically in the air (e.g., long wires, grounding rods, spools of metal cable).
3. Document all identified unintended antennas, as they may act as collectors for EMP energy.
4. Assess their proximity to critical electronic equipment and systems.
5. Mark or label these objects clearly so that mitigation efforts can be applied effectively.

Important Notes:
• This rule of thumb is specific to identifying unintended antennas through which EMP energy might couple to electronic/electrical systems.
• EMP is not the same as lightning, and devices that provide protection against lightning generally do not automatically provide protection against EMP.
unintended antennas, emp mitigation, identification, nuclear contamination
Inspect and Repair Shielding Damage
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Maintain the integrity of EMP shielding through regular inspection and repair.

Procedure:
1. Check for holes or cracks in shielding components.
2. Repair any damage to restore clean metal-to-metal contact.
3. Ensure that...
Objective: Maintain the integrity of EMP shielding through regular inspection and repair.

Procedure:
1. Check for holes or cracks in shielding components.
2. Repair any damage to restore clean metal-to-metal contact.
3. Ensure that all repairs are conducted with materials compatible with existing shielding systems.
4. Verify that no gaps or obstructions exist after repairs.
shielding repair, EMP mitigation, maintenance procedures
Maintain Availability of Technical Manuals and Documents
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Ensure that all required technical manuals and documents are available for reference.

Procedure:
1. Keep copies of all required technical manuals and other pertinent documents on hand at all times.
2. Store these documents in...
Objective: Ensure that all required technical manuals and documents are available for reference.

Procedure:
1. Keep copies of all required technical manuals and other pertinent documents on hand at all times.
2. Store these documents in a secure, accessible location within the unit or facility.
3. Ensure that personnel have access to these materials during maintenance and training activities.
4. Regularly audit document availability and update as needed.
technical manuals, EMP mitigation, document availability
Maintain Filters According to Technical Manuals
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Ensure that filters used in EMP-hardened systems are properly maintained.

Procedure:
1. Ensure all filters are maintained according to appropriate technical manuals.
2. Regularly inspect filters for damage or degradation.
3....
Objective: Ensure that filters used in EMP-hardened systems are properly maintained.

Procedure:
