- Most HEMP occurs in the frequency range between 100 kilohertz and 10 megahertz.
5. Impact on Communication Systems:
- Communication systems operating within these frequency ranges are most likely to be damaged by EMP energy.
- Microwave and other high-frequency systems are less likely to be impaired due to their antenna design.
6. No Blast or Radiation Effects:
- Field units may not experience blast, thermal, or radiation effects from the nuclear weapon.
7. EMP vs. Lightning Differences:
- EMP pulses rise much more rapidly (a few billionths of a second) compared to lightning (millionths of a second).
- EMP field strengths can reach 50,000 volts per meter, while lightning may be in the range of a few thousand volts per meter.
- HEMP pulses are shorter in duration—usually less than a thousandth of a second, compared to lightning pulses that last hundreds of milliseconds.
- Lightning occurs at lower frequencies and is not concentrated in the same frequency bands used by tactical communications systems.
high-altitude emp, heme, electromagnetic pulse, nuclear explosion, communication systems, lightning comparison
Understand SBEMP Coupling with Cables and Systems
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
SBEMP Coupling Effects:

• Coupling with Cables: SBEMP fields can couple (weld together) buried or above-ground cables outside the immediate vicinity of a nuclear detonation. This coupling may result in these cables remaining intact but...
SBEMP Coupling Effects:

• Coupling with Cables: SBEMP fields can couple (weld together) buried or above-ground cables outside the immediate vicinity of a nuclear detonation. This coupling may result in these cables remaining intact but transmitting tremendous surges of energy to connected systems down the line.

Implications for Tactical Units:
• Units located at distances where they are not affected by blast, thermal, or radiation effects can still be damaged by SBEMP through coupled cable systems.
• This damage may occur over long distances and could affect critical electronic equipment far from the point of detonation.
sbemp,coupling,cables,equipment_damage
Protect Against EMP Damage
Standard Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Minimize damage from an electromagnetic pulse (EMP) caused by a nuclear detonation.

Steps:
1. Battery-operated equipment: Most will not be damaged, but some may lose functionality. To restore functionality, power cycle...
Objective: Minimize damage from an electromagnetic pulse (EMP) caused by a nuclear detonation.

Steps:
1. Battery-operated equipment: Most will not be damaged, but some may lose functionality. To restore functionality, power cycle the device by turning it off and then on again.
2. Equipment plugged into wall outlets without surge protection: This equipment is at risk of damage due to power surges. Use surge protectors or unplug devices during an EMP event to prevent damage.
3. Commercial AM/FM radios: These will continue to receive signals from transmitters outside the affected area, making them a reliable source of communication after an EMP event.
EMP, surge protector, battery-operated equipment, power cycle, radio communication
Protect Against EMP Effects on Equipment
Standard Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Action: Ensure battery-operated equipment is functional by power cycling (turning off and then on again) if it loses functionality. Details:
• Most battery-operated equipment will not be damaged, but some may lose functionality.
• Equipment...
Action: Ensure battery-operated equipment is functional by power cycling (turning off and then on again) if it loses functionality. Details:
• Most battery-operated equipment will not be damaged, but some may lose functionality.
• Equipment plugged into wall outlets without a surge protector may be damaged due to power surges.
• Commercial band AM/FM radios will continue to receive signals from transmitters outside the area.
EMP,equipment,functionality,surge protector,radios

Communications (9) -

Prepare Pre-Scripted Messages for Immediate Dissemination
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Prepare and use pre-scripted messages to ensure rapid and effective communication of safety instructions following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Develop pre-scripted, vetted, and federally approved messages that provide...
Objective: Prepare and use pre-scripted messages to ensure rapid and effective communication of safety instructions following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Develop pre-scripted, vetted, and federally approved messages that provide clear guidance on protective actions (e.g., get inside and stay inside, self-decontaminate).
2. Ensure these messages are reviewed by all relevant federal response agencies for accuracy and consistency.
3. Store these messages in a readily accessible format for immediate use during an emergency.
4. Train public affairs staff to practice using the pre-scripted messages so they can be disseminated quickly when needed.
5. Use FEMA’s *Improvised Nuclear Device Response and Recovery: Communicating in the Immediate Aftermath (June 2013)* as a reference for anticipated questions and scientifically accurate safety messaging.

Importance: Pre-scripted messages increase communication effectiveness during critical minutes following an event, ensuring public safety and reducing confusion.
pre-scripted messages,nuclear detonation,safety instructions,communication
Consult State and Local Emergency Communications Committees for EAS Resiliency
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Action: Planners and responders should consult State Emergency Communications Committees (SECCs) and Local Area Emergency Communications Committees.

Reasoning: These committees are responsible for maintaining FCC-mandated Emergency Alert...
Action: Planners and responders should consult State Emergency Communications Committees (SECCs) and Local Area Emergency Communications Committees.

Reasoning: These committees are responsible for maintaining FCC-mandated Emergency Alert System (EAS) plans. Their involvement is critical to ensuring the resiliency of EAS during a nuclear detonation.

Implementation Details:
• Coordinate with SECCs and Local Area Emergency Communications Committees to review current EAS plans.
• Ensure that weekly test messages are being sent through IPAWS for EAS distribution every week to verify alerting software functionality.
• Verify that public alerting authorities can originate required weekly test (RWT) messages through IPAWS.
EAS resiliency,SECC,emergency alert system,nuclear detonation
Ensure Radiation Expertise Availability for Messaging Support
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Ensure radiation expertise is available to support message development during a nuclear detonation response.

Procedure:
1. Identify radiation experts within the jurisdiction who can provide technical input on radiation risks,...
Objective: Ensure radiation expertise is available to support message development during a nuclear detonation response.

Procedure:
1. Identify radiation experts within the jurisdiction who can provide technical input on radiation risks, protective actions, and radiation dose explanations.
2. Address any gaps in radiation expertise by connecting with external experts or training local personnel.
3. Coordinate with these experts to assist communications staff during planning and response operations.
4. Use expert input to develop accurate, consistent messaging that addresses public concerns about radiation exposure.

Importance: Radiation expertise is critical for ensuring effective communication of protective actions and reducing public anxiety.
radiation,expertise,messaging,nuclear detonation,communication
Include Battery-Powered AM/FM Radios in Nuclear Detonation Response Plans

Action: Include battery-powered AM/FM radios in nuclear detonation response plans.

Reasoning: Radio broadcasts may be the most effective means to reach people closest to and directly downwind from a nuclear explosion. This is especially...
Action: Include battery-powered AM/FM radios in nuclear detonation response plans.

Reasoning: Radio broadcasts may be the most effective means to reach people closest to and directly downwind from a nuclear explosion. This is especially important if the electrical grid and communications infrastructure are damaged or destroyed by the detonation.

Implementation Details:
• Ensure radios are available in cars, trucks, homes, schools, and public buildings.
• Radios should be included as part of emergency preparedness kits for individuals and families.
• Consider including instructions on how to use these radios during an emergency.
battery-powered radio,nuclear detonation,response plan,emergency communication
Integrate ROSS Program into Emergency Communications Plans

Objective: Integrate the Radiation Operations Support Specialist (ROSS) program into emergency communications plans to address radiation-related messaging needs.

Procedure:
1. Coordinate with the ROSS program by emailing...
Objective: Integrate the Radiation Operations Support Specialist (ROSS) program into emergency communications plans to address radiation-related messaging needs.

Procedure:

2. Ensure that ROSS is included in planning and response operations for nuclear detonation scenarios.
3. Utilize ROSS-trained personnel to provide technical support, review information, and ensure message consistency across jurisdictions.
4. Incorporate ROSS into plans to fill identified gaps in radiation expertise or communication capabilities.

Importance: The ROSS program provides critical support for developing accurate and consistent messaging during nuclear detonation response operations.

Understand and Utilize Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) for Public Notification
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Action: Understand the use of Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) as a tool for public notification during nuclear detonation scenarios.

Reasoning: WEAs are emergency messages that can be broadcast from cell towers to any WEA-enabled mobile...
Action: Understand the use of Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) as a tool for public notification during nuclear detonation scenarios.

Reasoning: WEAs are emergency messages that can be broadcast from cell towers to any WEA-enabled mobile device in a locally targeted area. They are critical for reaching individuals quickly and effectively, especially when other communication systems may be compromised.

Implementation Details:
• Ensure that alerting authorities have an MOU with FEMA and use IPAWS-compliant CAP AOTs to send WEAs.
• Verify that WEA-enabled devices are widely available in the population for effective coverage.
• Consider how individuals with disabilities, access needs, or functional needs can receive WEAs through compatible devices and content.
WEA,wireless emergency alert,nuclear detonation,public notification
Use Redundant Communication Outlets for Message Dissemination

Objective: Use multiple communication channels to ensure message dissemination even if infrastructure is severely damaged following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Identify and prepare redundant communication outlets (e.g., radio,...
Objective: Use multiple communication channels to ensure message dissemination even if infrastructure is severely damaged following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Identify and prepare redundant communication outlets (e.g., radio, television, social media, community alert systems) for disseminating safety instructions.
2. Ensure these outlets are integrated into emergency response plans and tested regularly.
3. Use multiple channels simultaneously to maximize the reach of critical messages such as 'get inside and stay inside' or 'self-decontaminate'.
4. Coordinate with local, state, and federal agencies to ensure message consistency across all communication platforms.

Importance: Redundant communication outlets are essential for reaching affected populations when traditional infrastructure is compromised.

Request PEP Station Information from IPAWS PMO for Urban Area Planning

Implementation Details:
• Contact the IPAWS PMO directly to request this information.
• Use the data provided to incorporate PEP station capabilities into urban area nuclear detonation response plans.
• Ensure that all relevant stakeholders have access to this information for coordination purposes.

Understand Communication Infrastructure Impact Following a Nuclear Detonation
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Understand the impact of nuclear detonations on communication infrastructure to prepare for response operations.

Procedure:
1. Study how nuclear detonations can damage or disrupt communication systems (e.g., cell towers,...
Objective: Understand the impact of nuclear detonations on communication infrastructure to prepare for response operations.

Procedure:
1. Study how nuclear detonations can damage or disrupt communication systems (e.g., cell towers, landlines, internet services).
2. Recognize that recovery of communication capabilities may take days to months following a nuclear event.
3. Incorporate this understanding into pre-incident preparedness plans and response strategies.
4. Use Chapter 7 of the *Planning Guidance for Response to a Nuclear Detonation* as a reference for detailed information on communications capabilities post-detonation.

Importance: Understanding infrastructure impacts is essential for planning effective communication strategies during and after a nuclear detonation.

Radiation Effects (8) -

Assessing Radiation Dose Levels and Their Effects on Personnel
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Objective: Understand the effects of whole-body irradiation based on dose levels.

Procedure:
1. Determine the Median Lethal Dose (LD50/60): The LD50/60 is approximately 450 cGy, meaning that 50% of exposed individuals will die within 60...
Objective: Understand the effects of whole-body irradiation based on dose levels.

Procedure:
1. Determine the Median Lethal Dose (LD50/60): The LD50/60 is approximately 450 cGy, meaning that 50% of exposed individuals will die within 60 days without medical intervention.
2. Identify Other Key Dose Levels:
- LD5 (Lowest Lethal Dose): Approximately 200-300 cGy (free in air), where virtually no one dies.
- LD95 (Highest Lethal Dose): Approximately 600-700 cGy (free in air), where virtually everyone dies.
3. Understand Battlefield Impacts:
- For nuclear yields of 5-10 KT or less, initial nuclear radiation is the dominant casualty producer.
- A dose of 30 Gy will cause immediate performance degradation and render personnel combat ineffective within several hours, though death may not occur for 5-6 days if no other injuries are present.
- Doses below 150 cGy will keep personnel combat effective.
- Doses between 150 cGy and 830 cGy will cause performance degradation, with some individuals eventually dying without medical care.
4. Recognize Lethal Dose Range: A dose of 530-830 cGy is considered lethal without treatment but not immediately incapacitating.
- Personnel exposed to this range may perform non-demanding tasks for about 6 days before relapsing into a degraded state for approximately 4 weeks.
- Death typically occurs around 6 weeks post-exposure if no medical care is provided.
5. Account for Shielding Effects: Partial body shielding can reduce the behavioral effects of radiation exposure.
radiation dose, LD50/60, battlefield effects, acute radiation sickness, medical intervention
Identifying and Managing Acute Radiation Sickness Syndromes
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Objective: Recognize the three characteristic syndromes of acute radiation sickness and understand their clinical patterns.

Procedure:
1. Hematopoietic Syndrome (Low to Midlethal Dose):
- Occurs at doses in the low to midlethal range...
Objective: Recognize the three characteristic syndromes of acute radiation sickness and understand their clinical patterns.

Procedure:
1. Hematopoietic Syndrome (Low to Midlethal Dose):
- Occurs at doses in the low to midlethal range (approximately 200-530 cGy).
- Characterized by depression of bone-marrow function, leading to pancytopenia.
- With medical treatment, deaths are minimal.
2. Gastrointestinal Syndrome (High Dose):
- Occurs at doses of 800 cGy or more measured in air.
- Characterized by severe fluid losses, hemorrhage, and diarrhea after a short latent period of several days to a week.
- Prognosis is very serious due to non-recoverable bone marrow damage.
3. Neurovascular Syndrome (Supralethal Dose):
- Occurs at extremely high doses that are rarely seen on the battlefield due to immediate lethality from heat and blast effects.
- Exceptions may occur in aircrews exposed to prompt nuclear radiation or personnel protected against heat and blast.
- Latent period is very short, ranging from several hours to 1-3 days.
4. Clinical Course of Radiation Sickness:
- The clinical pattern follows three phases:
- Prodromal Phase: Occurs within the first few hours post-exposure and includes symptoms like nausea, vomiting, and malaise.
- Latent Phase: Follows recovery from prodromal symptoms; duration varies with dose (longest for hematopoietic syndrome, shorter for gastrointestinal and neurovascular syndromes).
- Manifest Phase: Presents with clinical symptoms of the affected organ system (marrow, intestine, or neurovascular system).
acute radiation sickness, hematopoietic syndrome, gastrointestinal syndrome, neurovascular syndrome, clinical phases
Recognizing Early Symptoms of Radiation Exposure
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Objective: Identify early symptoms that may indicate radiation exposure and assess their severity.

Procedure:
1. Nausea and Vomiting:
- Occur with increasing frequency as the radiation dose exceeds 100-200 cGy.
- Onset may be...
Objective: Identify early symptoms that may indicate radiation exposure and assess their severity.

Procedure:
1. Nausea and Vomiting:
- Occur with increasing frequency as the radiation dose exceeds 100-200 cGy.
- Onset may be delayed up to 6-12 hours post-exposure but usually subside within the first day.
- Vomiting within the first 2 hours is associated with a severe radiation dose.
- Vomiting within the first hour, especially if accompanied by explosive diarrhea, indicates a fatal dose.
2. Hyperthermia (Fever and Chills):
- Occurs in individuals who have received potentially lethal radiation doses within the first few hours post-exposure.
- Fever and chills within the first day post-exposure are associated with severe and life-threatening radiation doses.
- Hyperthermia may also occur at lower but still serious doses (200 cGy or more).
- Note: Individuals wearing a chemical ensemble will typically be hyperthermic, making this symptom less useful for diagnosis.
3. Erythema (Skin Redness):
- Develops within the first day post-exposure in individuals who received whole-body doses of 1000-2000 cGy or more.
- May also occur with comparable local body region doses, but erythema is restricted to the affected area.
- Less frequently seen at lower but still potentially fatal doses (200 cGy or more).
4. Hypotension (Low Blood Pressure):
- A noticeable decline in systemic blood pressure has been recorded in victims who received a lethal whole-body radiation dose.
- In individuals receiving several hundred cGy, a drop in systemic blood pressure of more than 10% may be observed.
- Severe hypotension after irradiation is associated with a poor prognosis.
5. Neurologic Dysfunction:
- Almost all persons who demonstrate signs of central nervous system damage within the first hour post-exposure have received a lethal dose.
- Symptoms include mental confusion, convulsions, and coma.
- Intractable hypotension will accompany these symptoms.
- Despite vascular support, patients with neurologic dysfunction typically succumb within 48 hours.
early symptoms, radiation exposure, nausea vomiting, hyperthermia, erythema, hypotension, neurologic dysfunction
Understanding Immediate Transient Ineffectiveness (IT)
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Immediate Transient Ineffectiveness (IT) occurs when personnel become ineffective for any task within 3 minutes of exposure and remain so for approximately 7 minutes.

• Personnel recover to greater than 75 percent of their pre-exposure...
Immediate Transient Ineffectiveness (IT) occurs when personnel become ineffective for any task within 3 minutes of exposure and remain so for approximately 7 minutes.

• Personnel recover to greater than 75 percent of their pre-exposure performance levels after about 10 minutes.
• They remain at this level for about 30 minutes.
• Performance degrades for around 5 hours for undemanding tasks or 2 hours for demanding tasks.
• Radiation sickness becomes severe enough to render personnel ineffective until death, which usually occurs in about 6 weeks.
immediate transient ineffectiveness,radiation recovery,nuclear exposure
Understanding Latent Lethality (LL) Effects
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Latent Lethality (LL) is the physiological response from a dose of 650 cGy (RADs). Follow these effects:

• For physically undemanding tasks:
- Performance degrades about 3 hours after exposure.
- Remains degraded for approximately 2...
Latent Lethality (LL) is the physiological response from a dose of 650 cGy (RADs). Follow these effects:

• For physically undemanding tasks:
- Performance degrades about 3 hours after exposure.
- Remains degraded for approximately 2 days.
- Personnel recover combat effectiveness for 6 days or so.
- Relapse into degraded performance for 4 weeks after exposure.
- Radiation sickness becomes severe enough to render personnel ineffective until death, which usually occurs in about 6 weeks.

• For physically demanding tasks:
- Performance degrades about 2 hours after exposure.
- Remains degraded for approximately 3 weeks.
- Radiation sickness becomes severe enough to render personnel ineffective until death, which usually occurs in about 6 weeks.
latent lethality,radiation effects,performance degradation,nuclear exposure
Understanding Lethal Dosage (LD 50/30)
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Lethal dosage (LD 50/30) is defined as the dose that will prove to be lethal to 50 percent of the exposed population within 30 days.

• This metric helps estimate the severity of radiation exposure and its impact on personnel.
• Lethal dosage...
Lethal dosage (LD 50/30) is defined as the dose that will prove to be lethal to 50 percent of the exposed population within 30 days.

• This metric helps estimate the severity of radiation exposure and its impact on personnel.
• Lethal dosage varies from subject to subject and depends on the physical demands of the task to be performed.
lethal dosage,LD 50/30,radiation exposure,variation in effects
Understand Residual Nuclear Radiation Sources and Effects
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Residual nuclear radiation is emitted after one minute post-detonation and includes fallout, neutron-induced gamma activity (NIGA), and rainout. Fallout comes from three sources:

1. Unused fissionable material
2. Fission products
3....
Residual nuclear radiation is emitted after one minute post-detonation and includes fallout, neutron-induced gamma activity (NIGA), and rainout. Fallout comes from three sources:

1. Unused fissionable material
2. Fission products
3. Neutron-induced activity

These sources emit alpha, beta, and gamma radiation.

### Gamma Radiation Hazards
• Gamma radiation is the most significant threat due to its range and penetrating power.
• It behaves similarly to initial gamma radiation in terms of attenuation and scattering.
• Human biological response to residual radiation is essentially the same as to initial radiation.

### Unused Fissionable Material
• Not all fuel from a nuclear weapon is used; this wasted fuel becomes vaporized during detonation.
• As the fireball cools, it condenses into solid particles that fall out and emit primarily alpha radiation.
• Alpha radiation:
- Travels only up to 4 centimeters in open air
- Cannot penetrate one to two sheets of ordinary paper or human skin
- Is an internal hazard (inhalation) but not tactically significant due to delayed effects

### Fission Products
• Formed from the splitting of large atoms during fission, resulting in elements like mercury, tin, arsenic, iron, and lead.
• These form as gas, rise with the fireball, and condense into solid particles that fall out.
• Fission products are primarily beta and gamma emitters:
- Beta radiation:
- Travels up to 20 feet in open air
- Can penetrate 1/16 inch of aluminum and the first few layers of skin
- Causes skin burns similar to sunburn or internal damage if inhaled
- Not tactically significant due to delayed effects
- Gamma radiation:
- Travels at the speed of light (186,000 miles per second) and is not a particle or dust
- Penetrates materials but does not make them radioactive
- Is the primary tactical concern

### Detection of Gamma Radiation
• Human senses cannot detect gamma radiation.
• Requires specialized instruments like a RADIAC meter, which measures dose rates from fallout, NIGA, or both.
residual radiation,fallout,alpha,beta,gamma,radiation effects,nuclear contamination,RADIAC meter,neutron-induced gamma activity
Understanding the Effects of Nuclear Clouds on Fallout Patterns
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Nuclear clouds play a critical role in determining fallout patterns and radiation effects. The size of the nuclear cloud helps estimate the yield of the explosion, which is essential for predicting:

• The extent of contamination.
• Where...
Nuclear clouds play a critical role in determining fallout patterns and radiation effects. The size of the nuclear cloud helps estimate the yield of the explosion, which is essential for predicting:

• The extent of contamination.
• Where fallout will fall on the battlefield.
• The duration of tactically significant radiation.

Understanding the characteristics of the nuclear cloud, such as its presence or absence, can help determine if the burst was a surface burst (which produces significant fallout). This information is vital for tactical planning and unit protection.
nuclear cloud,fallout patterns,yield estimation,radiation effects,tactical planning

Radiation Units (8) -

Understand Absorbed Dose Definitions
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Understanding Absorbed Dose:

• Absorbed dose (D) is the amount of energy deposited in a given mass of absorbing material.
• It can be measured for any type of radiation and any energy level.
• In the SI system, it is measured in **Gray...
Understanding Absorbed Dose:

• Absorbed dose (D) is the amount of energy deposited in a given mass of absorbing material.
• It can be measured for any type of radiation and any energy level.
• In the SI system, it is measured in Gray (Gy) where:
- 1 Gy = 1 J/kg.
• In the traditional system, it is measured in rads where:
- 100 rads = 1 Gy
- 1 rad = 1 cGy.
• Dose refers to the total amount of energy absorbed and may be from single or multiple exposures, short or long in duration.
• This document uses cGy (centiGray) as the unit.
absorbed dose, gray, rad, cgy
Understand Activity Level of Radioactive Materials
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Understanding Activity Level:

• The activity level of a radioactive material is the number of atoms that decay per second.
• In the SI system, it is measured in becquerels (Bq) where:
- **1 Bq = 1 nuclear transformation per...
Understanding Activity Level:

• The activity level of a radioactive material is the number of atoms that decay per second.
• In the SI system, it is measured in becquerels (Bq) where:
- 1 Bq = 1 nuclear transformation per second.
• In the traditional system, it is measured in curies (Ci) where:
- 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq.
activity level, becquerel, curie
Understand Concentration and Isotope Units for Radioactive Materials
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Understanding Concentration and Isotope Units:

• The exposure rate from a radioactive material is directly related to the quantity of the material present.
• Quantity units depend on the medium in which the material is found:
- Air:...
Understanding Concentration and Isotope Units:

• The exposure rate from a radioactive material is directly related to the quantity of the material present.
• Quantity units depend on the medium in which the material is found:
- Air: Measured in microcuries per cubic meter (µCi/m³) or becquerels per cubic meter (Bq/m³).
- Ground: Measured in microcuries per square meter (µCi/m²) or becquerels per square meter (Bq/m²).
• Field measurements are often expressed in counts per minute (CPM) or counts per second (CPS), but these must be converted to definitive units like µCi/m² or Bq/m² for meaningful comparison.
concentration, isotope units, exposure rate, cpm cps
Understand Conversion Factors Between Radiation Units
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Understanding Conversion Factors:

• A common conversion factor for radiation protection purposes is:
- 1 R = 1 rad = 1 rem.
• This is a conservative estimate, as the actual conversion is:
- 1 R = 0.96 rem in tissue.
• This...
Understanding Conversion Factors:

• A common conversion factor for radiation protection purposes is:
- 1 R = 1 rad = 1 rem.
• This is a conservative estimate, as the actual conversion is:
- 1 R = 0.96 rem in tissue.
• This method applies only to photons with energy less than 3 MeV.
• For particulate radiation, other methods must be used.
conversion factor, roentgen to rem, rad to rem
Understand Dose Equivalent and Quality Factor
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Understanding Dose Equivalent:

• Dose equivalent (H) is a measure of the biological damage caused by radiation, taking into account both energy deposited and the type of radiation.
• It is defined as: **Energy deposited per unit mass ×...
Understanding Dose Equivalent:

• Dose equivalent (H) is a measure of the biological damage caused by radiation, taking into account both energy deposited and the type of radiation.
• It is defined as: Energy deposited per unit mass × quality factor.
• In the SI system, it is measured in Sieverts (Sv).
• In the traditional system, it is measured in rem where:
- 1 Sv = 100 rem.
• The quality factor accounts for different biological effects of radiation types. For example:
- Alpha radiation causes more damage than gamma radiation at the same dose.
• Refer to 10 CFR 20 Table 1004(b)2 for quality factors used by the NRC.
dose equivalent, sieverts, rem, quality factor
Understand Dose Rate and Units
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Understanding Dose Rate:

• Dose rate is the amount of radiation dose per unit time.
• This document uses cGy/hr (centiGray per hour) as the standard unit for dose rate.
dose rate, cgy/hr
Understand Radiation Exposure Definitions
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Understanding Radiation Exposure:

• Exposure has two meanings:
- Dictionary definition: The state of being exposed.
- Scientific definition: The sum of the charges of one sign produced in 1 kg of air by photon irradiation.
• In the SI...
Understanding Radiation Exposure:

• Exposure has two meanings:
- Dictionary definition: The state of being exposed.
- Scientific definition: The sum of the charges of one sign produced in 1 kg of air by photon irradiation.
• In the SI system, exposure is measured in Coulomb per kilogram (C/kg).
• In the traditional system, it is measured in roentgen (R) where:
- 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg.
exposure, radiation units, coulomb per kg, roentgen
Understanding Nuclear Radiation Units
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
### Understanding Nuclear Radiation Units

Overview: When discussing nuclear radiation exposure, scientists use specific units to measure the amount of radiation and its effects on living tissue.

Key Terms and Definitions:
• **Roentgen...
### Understanding Nuclear Radiation Units

Overview: When discussing nuclear radiation exposure, scientists use specific units to measure the amount of radiation and its effects on living tissue.

Key Terms and Definitions:
• Roentgen (R): A unit used to measure the amount of gamma radiation present in an environment. It is not a direct measurement of biological damage but rather the intensity of the radiation field.
• Rad (R): A unit that measures the amount of radiation absorbed by the body or a radiation meter. One rad equals 0.01 gray, which is the SI unit for absorbed dose.
• Rem (R): A unit that measures the biological damage caused by absorbed radiation. It takes into account the type of radiation and the sensitivity of the tissue being exposed.

Conversion Notes:
• For humans exposed to gamma rays from fallout, the three units are approximately equal. In civil defense contexts, they are often treated as interchangeable for simplicity.
• The conversion between these units depends on factors such as the energy of the radiation, the type of living tissue exposed, and the species involved. However, this complexity is simplified in practical scenarios by using the initial 'R' to denote all three units when a more precise meaning is not necessary.

Notes:
• Understanding these units helps in assessing the risks associated with nuclear radiation exposure.
• In emergency situations, it is important to use approximate values for quick decision-making.
roentgen,rad,rem,radiation units,nuclear exposure

Threat Assessment (7) -

Analyze Nuclear Threat Status for Friendly Forces
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Once the threat status estimate has been assessed, the chemical staff must analyze the protection level required for friendly forces.
2. This is accomplished by modifying the MOPP analysis process contained in FM 3-4.
3. Key...
Procedure:

1. Once the threat status estimate has been assessed, the chemical staff must analyze the protection level required for friendly forces.
2. This is accomplished by modifying the MOPP analysis process contained in FM 3-4.
3. Key factors to consider include:
- Mission and commander’s intent for friendly forces.
- Environment, including movement of surface-to-surface missiles to launch sites.
- Soldier factors, such as capabilities and level of training of friendly forces.
4. The chemical staff, in conjunction with the S2, must analyze all information received to formulate the threat status.
5. Consider the following additional factors:
- Availability of nuclear defense equipment and decon assets (information may be obtained from the S2 or G5).
- Location and availability of additional building materials to harden shelters.
- Location of civilian chemical manufacturing and storage facilities, which may be targeted or damaged in a nuclear attack, potentially increasing contamination hazards.
- Availability of civilian contracted labor and water transport for decon operations.
- For urban areas, location of car washes (which may be used as alternative decon stations).
- Obtain data on local fire hydrants (location, type of connections, etc.), which may be used to provide water for decon operations.
6. A threat status number can represent a combined NBC status or individual categories (e.g., CB status of three and nuclear status of zero). This provides the commander with guidance for deployment and operational purposes.
7. The ground commander must be capable of upgrading the NBC defense posture quickly, as the threat status may change rapidly due to factors such as the opposing force obtaining nuclear weapons from a sponsoring nation.
nuclear threat assessment, MOPP analysis, NBC defense posture, chemical staff, decontamination assets
Understand the Evolution of Nuclear Threat Scenarios in Planning Guidance
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Emergency planners must understand the evolution of nuclear threat scenarios as described in updated planning guidance to ensure effective response strategies.

The 2021 Planning Guidance for Response to a Nuclear Detonation reflects an expanded...
Emergency planners must understand the evolution of nuclear threat scenarios as described in updated planning guidance to ensure effective response strategies.

The 2021 Planning Guidance for Response to a Nuclear Detonation reflects an expanded range of threat scenarios compared to previous editions, including:

1. Nation-State Threats: These are threats from other countries or nation-states, which are generally more sophisticated due to the larger size, greater organization, and better funding of such entities.
2. Expanded Range of Explosive Yields: The guidance now addresses a broader range of nuclear detonation yields, including those much larger than previously considered (e.g., 10 kiloton [kT] or smaller-yield detonations in the 2010 edition).
3. Delivery Methods: This edition considers nuclear devices delivered by ballistic missile or aircraft that can deliver detonations elevated above the surface.
4. Impact of Low-Altitude Air Bursts: Detonations at low altitude can increase the scale of blast and thermal damage but may also significantly reduce local fallout impacts.
5. Focus on Surface and Low-Altitude Detonations: Urban emergency planners should focus on surface and low-altitude detonations, as these have the greatest effect on an urban environment.
nation-state threats, nuclear yield range, ballistic missile delivery, aircraft delivery, low-altitude air burst, surface detonation, fallout impact, urban emergency planning
Use the Threat Status Matrix for Nuclear Attack Assessment
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Use the Threat Status Matrix (Figure 1-1) to assess the threat status of nuclear, biological, and chemical attacks.
2. Place an “X” in the appropriate block on the matrix based on observed indicators.
3. Add up each...
Procedure:

1. Use the Threat Status Matrix (Figure 1-1) to assess the threat status of nuclear, biological, and chemical attacks.
2. Place an “X” in the appropriate block on the matrix based on observed indicators.
3. Add up each column’s total number of “X”s.
4. The column with the most X’s indicates the threat status serial number that corresponds to enemy force intent.
5. If an overall threat status cannot be determined due to an informational shortfall, reallocate or position collection assets to gain the needed information.
Threat Status Matrix, nuclear attack assessment, collection assets
Assessing NBC Threat Information Using IPB Process
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Gather Intelligence: Obtain assessment information from higher headquarters regarding potential NBC threats.
2. Evaluate Threat Characteristics: Analyze the enemy's organization, composition, tactical doctrine, weapons,...
Procedure:

1. Gather Intelligence: Obtain assessment information from higher headquarters regarding potential NBC threats.
2. Evaluate Threat Characteristics: Analyze the enemy's organization, composition, tactical doctrine, weapons, equipment, and battlefield functional systems.
3. Assess Terrain and Weather: Evaluate terrain features and weather conditions within the area of operation (AO) that could influence NBC threat employment.
4. Determine Time Periods of Interest: Identify specific times or phases during the battle set by the commander for assessment.
5. Threat Integration: Integrate all collected information with enemy doctrine to determine how the enemy will employ NBC hazards in the AO.
6. Develop Templates: Create doctrine, situation, event, and decision support templates used by the S2 in briefing the commander.
7. Apply Doctrine Template: Use the doctrine template to estimate when, where, why, and how the enemy may use NBC hazards within the AO.
8. Analyze Historical and Anticipated Threat Actions: Apply the situation template to assess past or anticipated threat NBC actions based on current battle developments.
9. Predict Future Events: Examine forecasted events using the events template to identify possible NBC interdictions that could impact unit operations.
10. Estimate Casualties and Threat Impact: Estimate potential chemical casualties if agents are employed at specific times, and assess how future battlefield events may affect units' missions.
11. Create Decision Support Templates: Based on enemy COA, develop decision support templates to provide the commander with possible countermeasures.
12. Provide NBC Updates: Update the commander and staff regularly on NBC situations based on time periods of interest set by the commander.

Details:
• Provide detailed information on enemy NBC capability based on available weapon systems during each period of interest.
• Explain how the enemy would likely employ chemicals.
• Identify areas of likely employment based on threat doctrine.
• Analyze terrain and weather in the AO for each period of interest.
• Provide MOPP guidance tailored to each time period.
• Update predicted fallout data templates as conditions change.
• Suggest alternative actions the commander can take prior to critical periods to minimize unit degradation.
• Continuously monitor S2 message and radio traffic for NBC-related intelligence.

Notes:
• Regular updates are essential due to rapid changes in the situation.
• Prior planning based on chemical officer input helps commanders make informed decisions.
NBC threat assessment, IPB process, MOPP analysis, enemy doctrine, terrain analysis, weather analysis, chemical casualties, fallout data templates
Conduct Nuclear Threat Assessment
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Assess the potential for nuclear threats in the operational environment.

Procedure:
1. Analyze Intelligence Preparation of the Battlefield (IPB): Use IPB to identify potential nuclear-capable adversaries and their...
Objective: Assess the potential for nuclear threats in the operational environment.

Procedure:
1. Analyze Intelligence Preparation of the Battlefield (IPB): Use IPB to identify potential nuclear-capable adversaries and their capabilities.
2. Assess Adversary Capabilities: Determine if opposing forces possess nuclear weapons, chemical, or biological weapons.
3. Evaluate Nuclear Proliferation Trends: Consider global trends in nuclear proliferation and the likelihood of nuclear attacks in the region.
4. Identify Potential Targets: Assess whether friendly units are likely targets based on mission objectives and adversary capabilities.
5. Assess Impact of Nuclear Weapons: Evaluate how nuclear weapons may be used by adversaries and their potential effects on personnel, equipment, and operations.
6. Determine Risk Levels: Weigh the risks associated with different courses of action to ensure mission accomplishment while minimizing exposure to nuclear threats.

Notes:
• This assessment should be conducted continuously as part of operational planning.
• Consider both deliberate and accidental nuclear hazards (e.g., damaged nuclear power plants).
nuclear threat assessment, IPB, adversary capabilities, nuclear proliferation, risk analysis
Prepare for NBC Weapon Threats in Military Operations
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Overview:

Medical personnel must be prepared to provide medical treatment, site hazard surveys, and medical hazard assessments in the event of the use of NBC (Nuclear, Biological, Chemical) weapons.

Procedures:
1. **Understand NBC Weapon...
Overview:

Medical personnel must be prepared to provide medical treatment, site hazard surveys, and medical hazard assessments in the event of the use of NBC (Nuclear, Biological, Chemical) weapons.

Procedures:
1. Understand NBC Weapon Threats:
- Nuclear weapons can range from megaton bombs capable of destroying cities to smaller devices that could be used by terrorists for strategic or tactical purposes.
- Radiological hazards include the use of radiation dispersal devices, destruction of local nuclear reactors, and improperly dumped radioactive waste.
- Biological warfare agents may include engineered microorganisms, toxins, or simple food contaminants. Health care personnel must monitor for unusual disease patterns that could indicate a biological attack.
- Chemical warfare agents are relatively easy to produce and can be used for immediate casualties or terrain denial (e.g., blister agents).
- Industrial chemicals and toxic industrial materials (TIMs) may also pose threats, either through accidents or intentional dispersion by adversaries.

2. Be Aware of Potential Adversaries:
- Russia, China, North Korea, Iraq, and Iran are potential adversaries that could use NBC weapons for tactical advantage or as a terrorist weapon.
- Terrorists may attempt to acquire small nuclear devices or radiological materials to hold cities or states at ransom.

3. Prepare for Escalation:
- The employment of nuclear weapons in stability and support operations is unlikely, but commanders must be prepared for their use.
- The use of NBC weapons could rapidly escalate a conflict into a major war.

4. Monitor for Indicators of Attack:
- For biological warfare, look for unusual disease outbreaks or increases in infectious diseases not typically found in the area of operations.
- For chemical and radiological threats, be alert to symptoms such as burns, respiratory distress, or radiation exposure signs (e.g., nausea, vomiting, skin changes).

5. Implement Proactive NBC Defense Measures:
- Use passive measures such as decontamination procedures, protective gear, and medical preparedness to reduce the threat of NBC weapons.
- Conduct regular training for medical personnel on how to treat casualties, conduct site hazard surveys, and perform medical hazard assessments in NBC environments.
NBC weapons, nuclear threats, biological warfare, chemical agents, radiological hazards, military preparedness
Determine Nuclear Threat Status (Serial 1 - Low)
Standard Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Assess whether the opposing force:
- Has an offensive nuclear capability.
- Has received training in defense and employment techniques.
- There is no indication of the use of nuclear weapons in the immediate future.
-...
Procedure:

1. Assess whether the opposing force:
- Has an offensive nuclear capability.
- Has received training in defense and employment techniques.
- There is no indication of the use of nuclear weapons in the immediate future.
- This may be based on:
- Whether nuclear munitions are dispersed or deployed.
- The stated objectives and intent of opposing forces.

2. Under this status:
- All personnel must carry their personal defense equipment.
- Nuclear defense equipment stockpiles should be identified and made readily available for deployment to the operational area if the threat status increases.
- NBC reconnaissance systems deploy to the operational area of interest to provide continuous monitoring capability of the opposing force.

3. Chemical personnel continue to focus on the same areas as under Serial 0:
- Chemical or biological operations.
- Smoke and herbicide use.
- Flame field expedients (FFE).
- Monitoring threat communication channels for nuclear threat indicators.
nuclear threat status, serial 1, low, personal defense equipment, NBC reconnaissance systems, chemical personnel, IPB process

Collective Protection (7) -

Implement Collective Protection for Shelters and Rooms
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
To achieve collective protection in shelters or rooms:

1. Ensure softwalled shelters (e.g., tents) have at least 0.5 inches of water overpressure.
2. Ensure tight concrete shelters or well-sealed rooms have at least **0.2 inches of...
To achieve collective protection in shelters or rooms:

1. Ensure softwalled shelters (e.g., tents) have at least 0.5 inches of water overpressure.
2. Ensure tight concrete shelters or well-sealed rooms have at least 0.2 inches of water overpressure.
3. Calculate the required air volume flow using these formulas:
- For 0.2 inches of overpressure: Airflow = 0.0367 × Room Volume (in cubic feet)
- For 0.5 inches of overpressure: Airflow = 0.07 × Room Volume (in cubic feet)
4. Use the M20 blower unit to provide 200 cubic feet per minute of airflow.
5. Seal all cracks, windows, ducts, false ceilings, electric outlets, and other openings with plastic sheets and 100 mph tape to create an airtight environment.
collective protection,overpressure,M20 blower unit,sealing
Operate Collective Protection Equipment Hawk M10
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Inspect the equipment for any damage or wear before use.
2. Ensure all components are properly assembled and secured in place according to the operator's manual.
3. Turn on the system and allow it to initialize as per...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the equipment for any damage or wear before use.
2. Ensure all components are properly assembled and secured in place according to the operator's manual.
3. Turn on the system and allow it to initialize as per the instructions provided in TM 3-4240-284-20&P.
4. Monitor the system continuously for any signs of malfunction or chemical agent detection.
5. If a chemical agent is detected, activate the decontamination protocols immediately and evacuate personnel if necessary.
6. After use, clean and store the equipment properly to maintain its functionality.

Notes:
• Always wear appropriate PPE when operating collective protection equipment.
• Follow all safety protocols outlined in TM 3-4240-284-20&P.
collective protection equipment, Hawk M10, chemical agent detection
Implement Collective Protection Procedures in Cold Weather Environments
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure for Implementing Collective Protection in Cold Weather Environments:

1. Ensure Heated Shelters Are Available:
- Heated shelters are essential to maintain combat effectiveness during cold weather operations.
- Without heated...
Procedure for Implementing Collective Protection in Cold Weather Environments:

1. Ensure Heated Shelters Are Available:
- Heated shelters are essential to maintain combat effectiveness during cold weather operations.
- Without heated shelters, the risk of chemical hazards increases significantly.

2. Use Reliable Collective-Protection Systems:
- Most collective-protection systems have been proven reliable and durable in cold environments.
- Monitor for pressure fluctuations caused by high winds when using these systems.

3. Address Indirect Vapor Absorption Issues:
- In cold environments, indirect vapor absorption is the greatest challenge during entry and exit from shelters.
- Ensure detection capability within the shelter to identify contamination.

4. Immediate Masking Upon Detection of Agent:
- If an agent is detected inside a shelter, soldiers must immediately mask.

5. Identify Contaminated Soldier and Purge Shelter:
- Monitor soldiers inside the shelter to determine who brought in the contamination.
- The identified soldier should exit the shelter immediately.
- The shelter must be purged as soon as possible after the contaminated soldier exits.

6. Resume Entry/Exit Procedures After Negative Detection:
- If follow-on detection proves negative, soldiers may resume normal entry and exit procedures.
cold weather,nbc protection,chemical hazards,heated shelters,pressure fluctuations,vapor absorption,detection capability,purging shelter
Implementing Collective Protection Systems
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Collective protection is required to provide a safe environment for soldiers to carry out tactical functions such as medical care, command, control, and communications without being restricted by wearing NBC protective...
Collective protection is required to provide a safe environment for soldiers to carry out tactical functions such as medical care, command, control, and communications without being restricted by wearing NBC protective clothing.

Collective-protection equipment (CPE) is integrated into some weapon systems to increase their effectiveness in an NBC environment. Planning for collective protection should be an integral part of plans for the AirLand Battlefield.

Commanders must examine and accurately plan for the additional manpower and logistics needed to operate in a collective-protection mode. Collective protection does not replace MOPP gear; it only allows the commander to reduce MOPP levels while in a contaminated environment.

Collective protection can provide relief from MOPP4 for eating, rest, and hygiene. Understanding entry/exit procedures will greatly impact the effective use of collective-protection systems.
collective protection, CPE, MOPP gear, NBC environment, entry/exit procedures
Understand the High-Mobility Shelter
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
A high-mobility shelter is a type of collective protection that can be removed with relative ease.
high mobility shelter, collective protection
Understand the Hybrid Collective Protection System
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Hybrid collective protection is a combination of overpressure and ventilated-facepiece system.
hybrid collective protection, overpressure system
Understand the Limited Mobile Shelter
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
A limited mobile shelter is a type of collective protection that is not easily moved.
limited mobile shelter, stationary protection

Radioactive Material Handling (7) -

Handle and Maintain Chemical Agent Monitor (CAM) with Nickel-63 Source
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Purpose: Ensure safe handling of the Chemical Agent Monitor (CAM) to prevent exposure to its radioactive source.

Procedure:
1. Identify that the CAM contains a 10 mCi Nickel-63 source plated on a brass foil cylinder inside a Teflon housing,...
Purpose: Ensure safe handling of the Chemical Agent Monitor (CAM) to prevent exposure to its radioactive source.

Procedure:
1. Identify that the CAM contains a 10 mCi Nickel-63 source plated on a brass foil cylinder inside a Teflon housing, which is installed in an aluminum alloy cylinder.
2. Handle the device with care to avoid damage to the source or its housing.
3. If any damage occurs to the source or housing, report it immediately to the RSO or Safety Officer for proper handling.
4. Ensure that all personnel who handle this equipment are trained on radiation safety procedures and emergency response protocols.

Notes:
• The Nickel-63 source is considered a “special form” source and should be handled with appropriate precautions.
• Refer to the equipment chapter for additional details.
Chemical Agent Monitor (CAM), Nickel-63 source, handling procedures
Handle and Maintain Density and Moisture Tester (MC-1) with Americium-241/Be Source
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Purpose: Ensure safe handling of the MC-1 density and moisture tester to prevent exposure to its radioactive sources.

Procedure:
1. Identify that the MC-1 contains a 50 mCi mixture of Americium-241 and Beryllium (Am-Be) in the base, which...
Purpose: Ensure safe handling of the MC-1 density and moisture tester to prevent exposure to its radioactive sources.

Procedure:
1. Identify that the MC-1 contains a 50 mCi mixture of Americium-241 and Beryllium (Am-Be) in the base, which emits neutrons when activated.
2. The device also has a 10 mCi Cesium-137 source located in a probe tip used to determine soil density at construction sites.
3. Handle the device with care to avoid damage to the sources or their housing.
4. If any damage occurs to the sources or housing, report it immediately to the RSO or Safety Officer for proper handling.
5. Ensure that all personnel who handle this equipment are trained on radiation safety procedures and emergency response protocols.

Notes:
• The Am-Be source is a neutron emitter and should be handled with appropriate precautions.
• The Cesium-137 source is double-encapsulated in stainless steel and classified as a “special form” source.
• TACOM is the NRC license holder for the MC-1.
Density and Moisture Tester (MC-1), Americium-241/Be source, Cesium-137 source
Handle and Maintain M8A1 Chemical Alarm with Americium-241 Source
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Purpose: Ensure safe handling of the M8A1 chemical alarm to prevent exposure to its radioactive source.

Procedure:
1. Identify that the M8A1 chemical alarm contains a component (M43A1) with a 250 µCi Americium-241 source in a cell...
Purpose: Ensure safe handling of the M8A1 chemical alarm to prevent exposure to its radioactive source.

Procedure:
1. Identify that the M8A1 chemical alarm contains a component (M43A1) with a 250 µCi Americium-241 source in a cell module.
2. The source is contained within a foil disc made of Americium-241 oxide in a gold matrix, enclosed between a gold-palladium alloy face and a silver backing.
3. Handle the device with care to avoid damage to the source or its housing.
4. If any damage occurs to the source or housing, report it immediately to the RSO or Safety Officer for proper handling.
5. Ensure that all personnel who handle this equipment are trained on radiation safety procedures and emergency response protocols.

Notes:
• The Americium-241 source is considered a “special form” source and should be handled with appropriate precautions.
• Refer to the equipment chapter for additional details.
M8A1 chemical alarm, Americium-241 source, handling procedures
Handle and Maintain Thermal Optics with Thorium-232 Coating
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Purpose: Ensure safe handling of thermal optics containing thorium-232 to prevent inhalation or ingestion of particles.

Procedure:
1. Identify thermal optics components that contain a multi-layered infrared anti-reflective coating with...
Purpose: Ensure safe handling of thermal optics containing thorium-232 to prevent inhalation or ingestion of particles.

Procedure:
1. Identify thermal optics components that contain a multi-layered infrared anti-reflective coating with thorium tetrafluoride (ThF4) as part of the coating.
2. Handle these components with care to avoid chipping, scratching, or breaking the coated lenses.
3. If any damage occurs to the lens or coating, immediately notify the Safety Officer and take steps to prevent inhalation or ingestion of thorium particles.
4. Provide prompt first aid for anyone who sustains a cut from broken or chipped lenses, including cleansing the wound thoroughly to minimize particle entry into the body.
5. Wear rubber gloves when handling thermal optics components to protect against contaminated dust.
6. Wash hands with mild soap and water after handling any thermal optics components to prevent contamination.

Notes:
• Thorium tetrafluoride is a radioactive compound that can be hazardous if inhaled or ingested.
• All personnel should be trained on proper handling and first aid procedures for thorium-coated lenses.
thermal optics, thorium-232, handling procedures, first aid
Identify and Handle Dials and Gauges with Radium-226
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Purpose: Identify and safely handle dials and gauges that may contain Radium-226 to prevent exposure.

Procedure:
1. Recognize that Radium-226 was used on the faces and pointers of dials and gauges in tactical and combat vehicles, though...
Purpose: Identify and safely handle dials and gauges that may contain Radium-226 to prevent exposure.

Procedure:
1. Recognize that Radium-226 was used on the faces and pointers of dials and gauges in tactical and combat vehicles, though these items are no longer in DOD storage depots since 1969.
2. Use a radiac meter to check for radioactive sources in suspected devices (note: fielded radiac instruments cannot detect tritium).
3. Handle any device that may contain Radium-226 with care, as it can emit Radon-222 gas and cause external exposure or contamination.
4. If any damage occurs to the dial or gauge, report it immediately to the RSO or Safety Officer for proper handling.
5. Ensure that all personnel who handle these devices are trained on radiation safety procedures and emergency response protocols.

Notes:
• Radium-226 is a long-lived alpha emitter with potential hazards from its progeny (Radon-222 gas).
• Foreign vehicles may also contain Radium-226 or other radioactive sources.
dials and gauges, Radium-226, Radon-222 gas
Identify Radioactive Sources in Army Commodities
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Purpose: Identify radioactive sources in Army commodities for proper handling and safety.

Procedure:
1. Refer to the following documents:
- USACHPPM’s TG 238
- ACALA’s _Radioactive Material Handling Safety_
2. Use TB 43-0116,...
Purpose: Identify radioactive sources in Army commodities for proper handling and safety.

Procedure:
1. Refer to the following documents:
- USACHPPM’s TG 238
- ACALA’s _Radioactive Material Handling Safety_
2. Use TB 43-0116, *Identification of Radioactive Items in the Army*, for detailed information on specific items, including NSNs, isotope types, and activity levels.
3. Consult AST-1500Z-100-93, *Identification Guide for Radioactive Sources in Foreign Materiel*, for physical data on radioactive sources found in foreign materials.
4. The inventory control point (ICP) number listed in TB 43-0116 typically indicates the license holder for that commodity.
5. For foreign material documents, review descriptions, locations, and details of radioactive sources within items.

Notes:
• This procedure is for identification purposes only; hazards associated with specific isotopes are covered later in the chapter.
• Ensure all personnel handling these materials are trained in radiation safety.
radioactive sources, identification, army commodities, TB 43-0116, AST-1500Z-100-93
Identify and Handle Check Sources Used by the Military
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Purpose: Identify and safely handle check sources used in military applications to prevent exposure.

Procedure:
1. Recognize that the following isotopes have been used as check sources: Cobalt-60, Krypton-85, Strontium-90, Barium-133,...
Purpose: Identify and safely handle check sources used in military applications to prevent exposure.

Procedure:
1. Recognize that the following isotopes have been used as check sources: Cobalt-60, Krypton-85, Strontium-90, Barium-133, Cesium-137, Lead-210, Radium-226, Thorium-232, Uranium-238, and Plutonium-239.
2. These sources are typically found in sealed form within radiation detection instrumentation or calibration labs.
3. Handle these sources with care to avoid damage or exposure.
4. If any damage occurs to the source or its housing, report it immediately to the RSO or Safety Officer for proper handling.
5. Ensure that all personnel who handle these sources are trained on radiation safety procedures and emergency response protocols.

Notes:
• Sealed sources represent an external exposure hazard only if physically destroyed.
• The AN/PDR-77 with a beta/gamma probe is typically used to detect sealed sources.
check sources, military applications, radiation safety

Radiation Contamination (7) -

Create a Radiological Contamination Overlay
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
To create a radiological contamination overlay, ensure the following minimum information is included:

• Map designation and orientation data.
• Nuclear burst and GZ identification.
• H-hour (the time of the nuclear burst).
• **Reference...
To create a radiological contamination overlay, ensure the following minimum information is included:

• Map designation and orientation data.
• Nuclear burst and GZ identification.
• H-hour (the time of the nuclear burst).
• Reference time.
• Decay rate/soil type.
• Time-of preparation and validity time.
• Source of contamination (fallout or neutron-induced).

Additional information that is desirable but not essential includes:

• Time-of-completion lines for fallout.
• Dose at reference time for key crossings or probable stay times.

The preferred method for transmitting a radiological contamination overlay is by facsimile channels of electrical communications, as it is fast and accurate. Facsimile devices are typically available at battalion and higher headquarters.

Three devices are used to transmit contamination overlays to lateral and higher commands. If liaison or messenger service is not available, the information can be sent as an NBC 5 nuclear report.
radiological contamination overlay,map designation,H-hour,decay rate,soil type,NBC 5 nuclear report,facsimile transmission
Format NBC 5 Nuclear Report for Transmission
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
When formatting an NBC 5 nuclear report for transmission:

• Use line item Xray always for avoidance purposes.
• If the contamination comes from a single burst, normalize dose rates to H + 1.
• If there have been several detonations at...
When formatting an NBC 5 nuclear report for transmission:

• Use line item Xray always for avoidance purposes.
• If the contamination comes from a single burst, normalize dose rates to H + 1.
• If there have been several detonations at different times and no single H + 1 is possible, report dose rates for a specific time using line item Oscar instead of line item Tango.

• It is not necessary or even desirable to report all four line items (Uniform, Victor, Whiskey, and Xray) on the NBC 5 nuclear report. Four lines are provided for flexibility.

• Coordinates should be listed sequentially around the contour. A contour line that completes a plot is represented by repeating the first coordinate.
• Incomplete contours are sent in sequence with the first and last coordinate different, indicating the break in the contour.

• Report the decay rate on line Romeo.
• Users of the NBC 5 nuclear report are not confined to predetermined lines. Any line described in Chapter 2 may be added.
NBC 5 nuclear report,line item Xray,H + 1,decay rate,contour formatting
Prepare Dose-Rate Contours for Radiological Overlays
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
When preparing dose-rate contours for radiological contamination overlays, ensure the following:

• Contour lines should be drawn based on detailed survey data.
• Key dose rates should be included if possible.
• For **new fallout (H-hour to...
When preparing dose-rate contours for radiological contamination overlays, ensure the following:

• Contour lines should be drawn based on detailed survey data.
• Key dose rates should be included if possible.
• For new fallout (H-hour to H + 48 hours), use a threshold of 20 cGyph at H + 1 for short-term (24-hour) occupancy, and 10 cGyph at H + 1 if longer occupancy is expected.
• For old fallout (H + 48 hours), use a threshold of 1 cGyph at the time of preparation.
• For neutron-induced contamination, use a threshold of 2 cGyph at H + 1 hour.

The shapes of dose-rate contours drawn based on a relatively brief series of readings and coordinates can vary significantly, so accuracy is essential.
dose rate contour,map overlay,new fallout,old fallout,neutron-induced contamination,H-hour
Transmit Radiological Contamination Overlays via NBC 5 Nuclear Report
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
When facsimile channels of electrical communications are not available, convert the radiological contamination overlay into a series of readings and coordinates for transmission as an NBC 5 nuclear report.

• Transmit the data from the...
When facsimile channels of electrical communications are not available, convert the radiological contamination overlay into a series of readings and coordinates for transmission as an NBC 5 nuclear report.

• Transmit the data from the division NBCC to:
- The three brigades.
- Division artillery.
- Armored cavalry squadron.
- Aviation, signal, and engineer battalions.
- Chemical company.
- MP company.
- Support command and attached units.
- Any other units designated by the commander.

This method has a disadvantage: it requires the addressee to replot data from the NBC 5 nuclear report and draw dose-rate contours, which is time-consuming. Staff planners must consider that the shapes of dose-rate contours drawn based on a relatively brief series of readings and coordinates can vary significantly.
NBC 5 nuclear report,facsimile transmission,contamination overlay,messenger service
Contamination Limits for Equipment and Protective Clothing
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. NATO Proposed Guidance - 7-Day Mission: Use the following contamination limits based on category:
- Category 1A (.05-.5 cGy): High-tox alpha emitters = 5 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 50 Bq/cm², Beta...
Procedure:

1. NATO Proposed Guidance - 7-Day Mission: Use the following contamination limits based on category:
- Category 1A (.05-.5 cGy): High-tox alpha emitters = 5 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 50 Bq/cm², Beta only = 10 Bq/cm².
- Category 1B (0.5-5 cGy): High-tox alpha emitters = 50 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 500 Bq/cm², Beta only = 10 Bq/cm².
- Category 1C (5-10 cGy): High-tox alpha emitters = 100 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 1000 Bq/cm², Beta only = 10 Bq/cm².
- Category 1D (10-25 cGy): High-tox alpha emitters = 250 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 2500 Bq/cm², Beta only = 10 Bq/cm².
- Category 1E (25-70 cGy): High-tox alpha emitters = 700 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 7000 Bq/cm², Beta only = 10 Bq/cm².

2. NATO Proposed Guidance - 3-Month Mission: Use the following contamination limits based on category:
- Category 1A (.05-.5 cGy): High-tox alpha emitters = 0.5 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 5 Bq/cm², Beta only = 10 Bq/cm².
- Category 1B (0.5-5 cGy): High-tox alpha emitters = 5 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 50 Bq/cm², Beta only = 10 Bq/cm².
- Category 1C (5-10 cGy): High-tox alpha emitters = 10 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 100 Bq/cm², Beta only = 10 Bq/cm².
- Category 1D (10-25 cGy): High-tox alpha emitters = 25 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 250 Bq/cm², Beta only = 10 Bq/cm².
- Category 1E (25-70 cGy): High-tox alpha emitters = 70 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 700 Bq/cm², Beta only = 10 Bq/cm².

3. US Standards: Use the following contamination limits:
- Reg Guide 8.21 (Unrestricted Area): High-tox alpha emitters = 0.0037 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 0.037 Bq/cm².
- Reg Guide 8.21 (Restricted Area): High-tox alpha emitters = 3.7 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 37 Bq/cm².
- Reg Guide 8.23: Equipment contamination limits for high-tox alpha emitters = 0.0333 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 0.17 Bq/cm².
- NRC & ANSI: High-tox alpha emitters = 0.0033 Bq/cm², Beta and low-tox alpha emitters = 0.17 Bq/cm².

4. Notes:
- Radiation contamination is assumed to be removable.
- Handling of contaminated equipment without protective clothing is allowed only in Category 1A.
- If the type of alpha-emitting radionuclide cannot be determined, use the high-tox alpha emitter column for limits.
contamination limits,equipment decontamination,nuclear standards,radiation safety
Determine Contamination Class for Radionuclides
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Identify the type of radionuclide present:
- High-tox Alpha Emitters: These include all alpha emitters except enriched, natural, and depleted uranium, which are classified as low-tox.
- **Beta and Low-tox Alpha...
Procedure:

1. Identify the type of radionuclide present:
- High-tox Alpha Emitters: These include all alpha emitters except enriched, natural, and depleted uranium, which are classified as low-tox.
- Beta and Low-tox Alpha Emitters: Includes normal beta emitters and low-tox alpha emitters (such as enriched, natural, and depleted uranium).

2. If it is not possible to distinguish between high-tox and low-tox alpha emitters, use the high-tox alpha emitter class values for contamination limits.

3. Use the appropriate contamination limit table based on the identified radionuclide type and mission duration (7-day or 3-month).

4. Refer to the following sources for additional guidance:
- NATO Working Group 2 on Low Level Radiation in Military Operations
- Reg Guide 8.21
- Reg Guide 8.23
contamination limits,radionuclide classification,nuclear contamination,high-tox alpha,beta emitters
Prepare a Wind Data Message for Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
### Preparation of a Wind Data Message

#### Step-by-step Procedure:

Step 1. Obtain the following data from Figure E-3 or DA Form 1971-3-R (Effective Downwind Message Worksheet):
• Cloud-top height
• Cloud-bottom height
• Two-thirds stem...
### Preparation of a Wind Data Message

#### Step-by-step Procedure:

Step 1. Obtain the following data from Figure E-3 or DA Form 1971-3-R (Effective Downwind Message Worksheet):
• Cloud-top height
• Cloud-bottom height
• Two-thirds stem height
For each of these yields: 2 KT, 5 KT, 30 KT, 100 KT, 300 KT, 1 MT, and 3 MT.

Step 2. Place a sheet of overlay paper over the wind vector plot:
• Mark a GN reference line and GZ (Grid Zero).
• Use Appendix D for guidance on preparing wind vector plots.
• Mark cloud-top height, cloud-bottom height, and two-thirds stem height for the 2-KT yield using values obtained in Step 1.
• Draw radial lines from GZ through these three points.

Note: All military significant fallout is contained between the two-thirds stem and cloud top height. Ensure that all wind vectors from where two-thirds stem is plotted up to cloud top height fall between these two radial lines. If not, adjust the radial line(s) accordingly. A moved radial line retains the same nomenclature as the original.

Step 3. Determine effective wind speed:
1. Measure the distance along the cloud-bottom radial line from GZ to its intersection with the wind vector plot at the cloud-bottom height point.
2. Divide this distance by the time of fall from the cloud bottom (Figure 3-17), or multiply by the reciprocal as shown on the EDM worksheet.

Note: If effective wind speed for one or more yield groups is less than 8 kmph, use the nomogram in Figure E-6 (Appendix E) to determine Zone I downwind distance:
• Enter the nomogram with an effective wind speed of 8 kmph on the left-hand scale.
• Use the highest yield for each yield group on the right-hand scale.
• Read the downwind distance for Zone I on the center scale.

Step 4. Determine effective downwind direction:
1. Use a protractor to bisect the angle formed by the cloud-top height radial line and the two-thirds stem height radial line.
2. Measure the azimuth of the bisector in degrees from GN (Grid North). This is the effective downwind direction (Figure 3-18).

Step 5. Measure the angle between the cloud-top and two-thirds stem:
• If the angle exceeds 40 degrees, round it to the next highest even number if it's an odd number.
• Record this value in the expanded angle column on the worksheet for the appropriate yield group.

Step 6. Repeat Steps 2 through 5 for all remaining yield groups:
• Use a separate sheet of overlay paper for each yield group.

Step 7. Complete the EDM (Effective Downwind Message) portion of the worksheet based on the data and calculations.

### Additional Notes:
• 3-digit rule: Winds less than 8 kmph; digits represent Zone I distance.
• 6-digit rule: Normal message format.
• 9-digit rule: Expanded radial lines to a given number of degrees.

Example: A completed worksheet and an effective downwind message for normal winds are shown in Figure 3-19. Special cases are depicted in Figure 3-21, page 3-18.
nuclear contamination, wind data message, effective downwind direction, cloud-top height, two-thirds stem height, fallout prediction, military nuclear procedures

Radiological Monitoring (7) -

Conduct Environmental Sampling During Radiological Emergencies
Critical Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Sampling is the process of collecting specimens from various materials (e.g., air, water, soil, vegetation, milk, or animal feed) at field locations to assess contamination levels.

Key points:
• This procedure is crucial for identifying and...
Sampling is the process of collecting specimens from various materials (e.g., air, water, soil, vegetation, milk, or animal feed) at field locations to assess contamination levels.

Key points:
• This procedure is crucial for identifying and quantifying radioactive contaminants in the environment.
• Sampling should be conducted systematically and with appropriate equipment to ensure accurate results.
• Collected samples must be properly labeled, stored, and transported to a laboratory for analysis.
environmental sampling,radiation monitoring,contamination assessment
Conduct Continuous Radiological Monitoring
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Continuously surveil for radiation in the unit area or position to detect changes in radiological hazards.

Procedure:
1. Initiate continuous monitoring under the following conditions:
- When a nuclear detonation is...
Objective: Continuously surveil for radiation in the unit area or position to detect changes in radiological hazards.

Procedure:
1. Initiate continuous monitoring under the following conditions:
- When a nuclear detonation is observed, heard, or reported in their area of operations.
- When an NBC 3 nuclear report is received and the unit is in the predicted area of contamination.
- When a dose rate of 1 cGyph is recorded during periodic monitoring.
- When ordered by the unit commander.

2. Use equipment: Use either the IM174-series Radiacmeter or the AN/VDR-2 radiacmeter for readings.
3. Frequency of readings: Frequency depends on current situation and unit SOPs.
4. Return to periodic monitoring: Units return to periodic monitoring when the dose rate (unshielded) falls below 1 cGyph or when ordered to do so by the unit commander.
continuous monitoring,radiological survey,NBC reports,IM174-series Radiacmeter,AN/VDR-2,nuclear contamination
Conduct Periodic Radiological Monitoring
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Determine the presence and intensity of residual radiation hazards in the unit area.

Procedure:
1. Initiate periodic monitoring under the following conditions:
- After the first use of nuclear weapons in theater.
- When...
Objective: Determine the presence and intensity of residual radiation hazards in the unit area.

Procedure:
1. Initiate periodic monitoring under the following conditions:
- After the first use of nuclear weapons in theater.
- When a unit is out of contact with higher headquarters.
- When ordered by higher headquarters.
- When the unit stops continuous monitoring.

2. Use equipment: Use either the IM174-series Radiacmeter or the AN/VDR-2 radiacmeter for readings.
3. Frequency of readings: Take a reading at least once each hour. Unit SOPs may require more frequent readings and should provide detailed information on monitoring procedures.
4. Number of Radiacmeters required: When a unit has more than one Radiacmeter, only one is required for periodic monitoring.
5. Action if contamination detected: If contamination is detected during periodic monitoring, report it immediately using NBC 3 nuclear reports and take appropriate protective measures.
periodic monitoring,radiological survey,NBC reports,IM174-series Radiacmeter,AN/VDR-2,nuclear contamination
