1. Shield sensitive electronics with Faraday cages. A Faraday cage is an enclosure made of conductive material that blocks external static and non-static electric fields. It can be constructed using materials such as copper mesh, aluminum foil, or thick steel.
2. Store critical electronic devices in metal containers. Any large metal container (e.g., a microwave oven, a car trunk lined with metal, or a sealed metal box) can act as a makeshift Faraday cage if it is properly grounded and has no gaps or openings.
3. Avoid using unshielded electronics during an EMP event. Devices such as CB radios, amateur radio equipment, microcomputers, telephones, and electric blasting caps are particularly vulnerable to EMP damage.
4. Keep backup communication systems in a shielded location. If your retreat group relies on electronic communication, ensure that backup systems (e.g., ham radios or shortwave transmitters) are stored in a Faraday cage or other protected area.
5. Ground all shielding materials properly. Proper grounding is essential to ensure that the Faraday cage effectively diverts the EMP energy away from the enclosed electronics.

Notes:
• The range of EMP damage can be extremely large, as demonstrated by high-altitude nuclear tests in the Pacific, which knocked out communications and power systems over 750 miles away.
• A strong EMP can induce damaging currents in any large metal object, such as cars, power lines, or antennas.
• If you are planning to rely on electronic devices for survival, it is crucial to take steps to protect them from an EMP event.
EMP,Faraday cage,electronics protection,nuclear radiation,shielding

Electronic Equipment Protection (2) -

Mitigate Functional Damage to Electronic Equipment After an EMP Event
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Assess the damage: Inspect all affected electronic equipment for visible signs of functional damage such as burned components or melted circuits.

2. Replace fuses and reset circuit breakers: If fuses are blown or circuit...
Procedure:

1. Assess the damage: Inspect all affected electronic equipment for visible signs of functional damage such as burned components or melted circuits.

2. Replace fuses and reset circuit breakers: If fuses are blown or circuit breakers have tripped, replace them with appropriate replacements and reset the breakers to restore power.

3. Check internal wiring: Ensure that no internal wiring has been damaged by inspecting connections and testing continuity where possible.

4. Use postattack mitigation procedures: Follow established protocols for restoring functionality to electronic systems after an EMP event, which may include recalibrating devices or replacing components.

5. Test equipment: After repairs are made, test the equipment thoroughly to ensure it is functioning correctly and safely.
EMP mitigation,equipment repair,functional damage,postattack procedures
Mitigate Operational Upset in Electronic Equipment After an EMP Event
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Identify equipment with digital computers: Focus on systems that use digital computers, as they are particularly vulnerable to operational upset caused by EMP.

2. Reset memory and data: If data has been erased or...
Procedure:

1. Identify equipment with digital computers: Focus on systems that use digital computers, as they are particularly vulnerable to operational upset caused by EMP.

2. Reset memory and data: If data has been erased or corrupted from computer memories, attempt to reset the system or reload the data from backups if available.

3. Reboot affected devices: Reboot any electronic systems that have experienced an erroneous signal or malfunction due to operational upset.

4. Verify signal integrity: Ensure that signals sent by equipment are correct and not distorted by rechecking communication links or recalibrating sensors.

5. Implement standing operating procedures (SOPs): Follow SOPs designed to mitigate secondary effects of EMP, such as incorrect information from digital instruments or weapon guidance system malfunctions.
operational upset,digital systems,SOPs,EMP effects

Mopps (2) -

Implement MOPP Ready Posture
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Soldiers carry their protective masks with their LCE (Load Carrying Equipment).
2. The soldier's MOPP gear is labeled and stored no further back than the BSA (Body Substance Absorbent) and is ready to be brought forward to the...
Procedure:

1. Soldiers carry their protective masks with their LCE (Load Carrying Equipment).
2. The soldier's MOPP gear is labeled and stored no further back than the BSA (Body Substance Absorbent) and is ready to be brought forward to the soldier when needed.
3. Pushing it forward should not exceed two hours.
4. Units in MOPP Ready are highly vulnerable to persistent agent attacks and will automatically upgrade to MOPP Zero when they determine, or are notified, that chemical weapons have been used or that the threat for use of chemical weapons has risen.
5. When a unit is at MOPP Ready soldiers will have field-expedient items identified for use.

Notes:
• See Standard MOPP Gear and Field-Expedient Items in Chapter 3 for further discussion.
MOPP Ready, protective posture, NBC protection
Implement Mask Only Command
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. The mask is worn.
2. The Mask Only command is given under these situations:
- When RCAs (Radiological Contamination Areas) are being employed and no chemical/biological threat exists.
- In a downwind vapor hazard of a...
Procedure:

1. The mask is worn.
2. The Mask Only command is given under these situations:
- When RCAs (Radiological Contamination Areas) are being employed and no chemical/biological threat exists.
- In a downwind vapor hazard of a nonpersistent chemical agent.
3. MASK ONLY is not an appropriate command when blister or persistent nerve agents are present.
Mask Only, protective posture, NBC protection

Mopp Levels And Protection (2) -

Assess NBC Threat to Determine Appropriate MOPP Level
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure for Assessing NBC Threat and Selecting MOPP Level:

1. Conduct a thorough assessment of the NBC threat environment.
2. Consider factors that contribute to performance degradation, such as heat stress, psychological strain, and exposure...
Procedure for Assessing NBC Threat and Selecting MOPP Level:

1. Conduct a thorough assessment of the NBC threat environment.
2. Consider factors that contribute to performance degradation, such as heat stress, psychological strain, and exposure risks.
3. Evaluate the trade-off between encapsulation (MOPP4) and its physiological and psychological impacts on soldiers.
4. Balance the need for protection against the risk of performance degradation due to increased heat stress, discomfort, and reduced mobility.
5. Select an appropriate MOPP level based on the assessment, ensuring it minimizes both exposure risks and performance issues.

Important Considerations:
• Lower MOPP levels reduce encapsulation stress but increase exposure risk.
• Exposure to low-level agents can still cause performance degradation.
• Leaders must be trained in MOPP analysis procedures to make informed decisions.
• Informed planning and preparation are essential for minimizing casualties and performance issues.
NBC threat,MOPP level,heat stress,performance degradation,encapsulation
Mitigate Physiological Stress from MOPP Wear
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure for Mitigating Physiological Stress During MOPP Operations:

1. Monitor environmental conditions and work intensity to assess the risk of heat stress.
2. Ensure soldiers wear appropriate layers (e.g., protective overgarment, gloves,...
Procedure for Mitigating Physiological Stress During MOPP Operations:

1. Monitor environmental conditions and work intensity to assess the risk of heat stress.
2. Ensure soldiers wear appropriate layers (e.g., protective overgarment, gloves, and overboots) as required by the selected MOPP level.
3. Provide adequate hydration and nutrition to counteract hunger, thirst, and discomfort during prolonged MOPP operations.
4. Implement regular rest periods to reduce heat stress and fatigue.
5. Monitor soldiers for signs of heat casualties (e.g., elevated body temperature, dizziness, nausea) and take immediate corrective action if necessary.

Important Considerations:
• Additional layers increase the risk of heat stress even at moderate temperatures.
• Prolonged MOPP wear can lead to significant performance degradation due to discomfort and fatigue.
• Leaders must be vigilant in managing both physical and psychological factors that impact soldier performance.
heat stress,MOPP wear,hydration,nutrition,performance degradation

Moppprotection (2) -

Analyze Environmental Conditions for MOPP Level Selection
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Determine the time of day and environmental conditions:
- Is the mission taking place during daylight or at night?
- What are the temperature gradients (stable, unstable) in the area? Stable gradients occur between late...
Procedure:

1. Determine the time of day and environmental conditions:
- Is the mission taking place during daylight or at night?
- What are the temperature gradients (stable, unstable) in the area? Stable gradients occur between late evening and early morning, while unstable conditions happen during the heat of the day.

2. Assess the impact of environmental conditions on agent behavior:
- During stable temperature gradients at night, chemical agents tend to linger close to the ground and move horizontally with the wind.
- During unstable conditions in daylight, agents rise rapidly, reducing their time on target and casualty-producing capabilities.

3. Evaluate WBGT index for heat load:
- The WBGT index is a measure of environmental heat stress.
- At night or during shade, the WBGT index is generally lower than during daylight conditions.

4. Use WBGT data to determine appropriate MOPP levels:
- Lower WBGT values at night allow for higher MOPP levels without significantly increasing the risk of heat casualties.
- Higher WBGT values during the day may require a balance between protection and performance degradation due to increased heat load.

5. Consider the impact of MOPP on soldier performance:
- Increased MOPP levels can lead to decreased physical and mental performance, especially in high ambient temperatures.
- Commanders must weigh the risk of chemical agent exposure against potential performance issues and heat casualties.

6. Use tables for work/rest cycles based on environmental conditions:
- During daylight operations, use Table 2-1 (work intensity) and Table 2-2 (guidance for work/rest cycles).
- For night or shade operations, use Table 2-7 (guidance for work/rest cycles).

7. Assess time constraints and mission duration:
- If the mission requires continuous work with few or no rest breaks, refer to Table 2-4 (daylight) or Table 2-9 (night or shade) for guidance on expected heat casualty risks.

8. Use graphical data to estimate operational limits:
- Figures 2-2 and 2-3 provide estimates of how long a unit can operate under varying work loads and temperatures without exceeding minimal (5%) heat casualties.
- For example, in Figure 2-2, soldiers working at moderate intensity in BDUs with WBGT at 85°F may experience heat casualties after about 90 minutes of continuous work.
- In Figure 2-3, the same conditions in MOPP4 reduce operational time to approximately 45 minutes before exceeding a 5% casualty risk and 60 minutes for a 20% risk.
wbgt index,heat load,mopp level selection,work intensity,environmental conditions,continuous work,performance degradation
Assess Mission Factors for NBC Protection Planning
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Determine the mission type and importance:
- Is the mission offensive or defensive?
- How critical is the mission to overall objectives?
- What are the potential consequences of mission failure due to...
Procedure:

1. Determine the mission type and importance:
- Is the mission offensive or defensive?
- How critical is the mission to overall objectives?
- What are the potential consequences of mission failure due to chemical/biological agents?

2. Evaluate likelihood of NBC agent employment:
- What is the probability that chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear agents will be used by the enemy?
- Are there any intelligence indicators suggesting imminent use of NBC weapons?

3. Identify likely agents and targets:
- Based on threat analysis, what types of agents are most likely to be used (e.g., nerve agents, blister agents)?
- What are the likely target areas or units that could be exposed to these agents?

4. Assess warning time for agent employment:
- How much advance notice is expected before an NBC attack occurs?
- Is there a high likelihood of surprise attacks with little or no warning time?

5. Evaluate available protection and shelter:
- What protective equipment (e.g., MOPP gear, masks) is available to the unit?
- Are there shelters, cover areas, or NBC decontamination facilities accessible for use in case of exposure?

6. Assess physical and mental demands of the mission:
- How physically demanding is the work required by the mission (e.g., marching, digging, carrying heavy loads)?
- How mentally taxing will the mission be on soldiers (e.g., prolonged stress, high decision-making pressure)?

7. Determine time constraints for mission completion:
- How quickly must the mission be accomplished?
- Are there deadlines or time-sensitive objectives that could impact NBC protection planning?

8. Estimate expected duration of the mission:
- What is the anticipated length of time soldiers will be engaged in the mission (e.g., hours, days)?

9. Consider follow-on missions and recovery needs:
- What is the likely next phase or follow-on mission after the current one?
- Will there be opportunities for rest, recovery, or re-equipment after completing the mission?

10. Assess availability of water and food supplies:
- Are sufficient quantities of clean drinking water and nourishing food available to sustain soldiers during the mission?
- How will these resources be managed in case of NBC contamination or exposure?

Considerations:
• The answers to these questions will influence the selection of appropriate MOPP (Mission-Oriented Protective Posture) levels.
• Environmental factors such as time of day, temperature gradients, and WBGT (Wet Bulb Globe Temperature) index must also be considered in NBC protection planning.
mission factors,nbc protection,mopp level selection,wbgt index,heat casualties,work intensity,environmental conditions

Vehicle Protection (2) -

Maximize NBC Protection Inside Armored Vehicles
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Get as low as possible inside the armored vehicle, especially for crew members in elevated positions such as the tank commander, gunner, and loader. This reduces radiation exposure by a factor of four.
2. **Keep all hatches...
Procedure:

1. Get as low as possible inside the armored vehicle, especially for crew members in elevated positions such as the tank commander, gunner, and loader. This reduces radiation exposure by a factor of four.
2. Keep all hatches shut to prevent unnecessary exposure to explosion effects and subsequent entry of fallout particles or scattered gamma radiation. Close any other openings, such as the main gun breech.
3. Wear appropriate head protection, such as a combat vehicle crew (CVC) helmet or kevlar helmet with a secured chin strap to help prevent head injuries from the blast wave.
4. Secure all loose equipment inside the vehicle, including tools, weapons, and helmets, to prevent them from becoming projectiles during the blast and causing injury or death.
5. Dig in armored vehicles (hull defilade) or place them in trenches or cuts in roadways for additional protection. This can reduce gamma radiation exposure by up to a factor of two.
6. Use sandbags as overhead shielding on top of the tank turret or armored vehicle hull. Each layer of sandbags reduces gamma radiation by a factor of two. Wetting the sandbags enhances neutron radiation shielding and protects them from thermal damage.
armored vehicle,nuclear,blast,sandbags,protection
Optimize Vehicle Orientation for Nuclear Radiation Exposure
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Consider rear-on orientation of armored vehicles during potential nuclear radiation exposure. This places the mass of the vehicle's engine between the radiation source and the crew, reducing potential radiation exposure to...
Procedure:

1. Consider rear-on orientation of armored vehicles during potential nuclear radiation exposure. This places the mass of the vehicle's engine between the radiation source and the crew, reducing potential radiation exposure to half that of a head-on or broadside exposure.
2. At distances above the median lethal dose to the crew in a rear-on orientation, significant damage to the tank is not expected, making this position preferable for minimizing both injury and vehicle damage.
armored vehicle,nuclear,orientation,radiation

Shelter Operations (2) -

Limiting Entry and Exit to Prevent Contamination
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

To limit contamination entry during an attack:

1. Restrict entry and exit. No personnel or equipment should enter or exit the shelter unless absolutely mission essential.
2. Monitor interior air when entry is required. Use...
Procedure:

To limit contamination entry during an attack:

1. Restrict entry and exit. No personnel or equipment should enter or exit the shelter unless absolutely mission essential.
2. Monitor interior air when entry is required. Use detector/monitoring equipment to determine if contaminants have been introduced into the shelter.
3. Minimize entry and exit requirements. The commander may choose to locate outside the shelter and let the staff operate inside to reduce the need for frequent entry and exit.
4. Appoint a liaison officer. A liaison officer, appointed by the commander, coordinates between the commander, staff, and troops to streamline communication and reduce time spent on entry/exit procedures.

Additional Considerations:
• Direct coordination with troops and staff consumes significant time during entry and exit procedures.
• When using a collective-protection system for relief from MOPP gear, ensure coordination of arrangements such as MOPP-gear resupply and security.
• Shelters used for soldier relief require many entry and exit cycles. Soldiers must continuously monitor shelter operations to ensure the system functions properly and that no contaminants have entered.
entry exit,contamination prevention,monitoring,detector equipment
Shelter Attendant Actions During an Attack
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

During an attack, follow these steps:

1. Don mask and alert shelter occupants. Ensure all individuals inside the shelter are aware of the attack and take appropriate protective measures.
2. Aid in securing air lock doors....
Procedure:

During an attack, follow these steps:

1. Don mask and alert shelter occupants. Ensure all individuals inside the shelter are aware of the attack and take appropriate protective measures.
2. Aid in securing air lock doors. Help ensure that air lock doors remain closed to prevent contamination from entering or exiting the shelter.
3. Prevent unauthorized personnel from entering the shelter. Only authorized personnel should be allowed inside during an attack unless absolutely mission essential.
4. Test periodically for contamination. Use detector/monitoring equipment to check for any signs of contamination inside the shelter at regular intervals.

Additional Considerations:
• If entry into the shelter from a contaminated environment is mission essential, internal monitoring becomes critical to prevent hazardous contamination levels inside.
• Vapor absorbed into clothing can also introduce contamination. To minimize this risk:
- Monitor the shelter interior every 15 minutes using detector/monitoring equipment.
- If hazardous levels of agent are detected inside, suspend shelter operations and have soldiers assume the appropriate MOPP level before evacuating the shelter.
• If mission dictates, the commander may elect to have soldiers don their protective masks to continue operating inside the shelter. This may lengthen the time required to purge the shelter and restore safe conditions.
• When detector/monitoring equipment no longer indicates contamination inside, proceed with unmasking procedures and resume shelter operations.
attack,contamination,shelter operations,MOPP level,detector monitoring equipment

Collective Protection Systems (2) -

TOE Collective-Protection Systems During an Attack
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

When using TOE collective-protection assets during an attack:

1. Decide based on mission requirements. The commander decides whether to use TOE collective-protection assets if more effective command and control, less performance...
Procedure:

When using TOE collective-protection assets during an attack:

1. Decide based on mission requirements. The commander decides whether to use TOE collective-protection assets if more effective command and control, less performance degradation, or relief from MOPP gear is mission essential.
2. No special set-up procedures are needed if anticipated in advance. If the decision is made in anticipation of an attack, no special set-up procedures are necessary.
3. Avoid transferring liquid contamination to shelter interior. When erecting shelters after an attack, soldiers must carefully avoid transferring liquid contamination into the shelter.
4. Purge interior air as specified in TM for the system. Follow technical manuals to ensure proper purging of the shelter's interior air.
5. Test continuously with detector/monitoring equipment. Continue testing until readings are negative.
6. Maintain mask-only posture if operations begin before purge is complete. Soldiers must maintain a mask-only posture until they receive the all-clear signal.

Additional Considerations:
• If operations begin before the purge is complete, soldiers must remain in mask-only posture until receiving the all-clear signal.
TOE systems,purge air,detector monitoring equipment,MOPP gear
Weapon-Systems Collective Protection During an Attack
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

Soldiers in weapon systems with collective protection should:

1. Anticipate the attack. Be at the same MOPP level as other soldiers or be protected by having the collective-protection system functioning.
2. **Maintain...
Procedure:

Soldiers in weapon systems with collective protection should:

1. Anticipate the attack. Be at the same MOPP level as other soldiers or be protected by having the collective-protection system functioning.
2. Maintain appropriate MOPP levels. Those protected by the collective-protection system may continue at reduced MOPP levels shown in Table 6-5.
3. Activate collective-protection systems if not already active. If an attack occurs and a soldier is not protected, activate the collective-protection system and assume MOPP4.
4. Remain at MOPP4 until shelter interior is purged. Required purge times vary with the interior shelter volume and airflow; check specific technical manuals for each system.
5. Unmask as outlined in STP 21-24-SMCT. When the required purge time passes, proceed with unmasking procedures.
6. Resume modified MOPP levels after all-clear signal. Soldiers may resume the modified MOPP level shown in Table 6-5 once the all-clear signal is given.

Additional Considerations:
• During an attack, restrict entry and exit to only mission essential personnel or equipment.
• When an entry must be made, monitor interior air using detector/monitoring equipment to determine if contaminants have been introduced.
• If a chemical agent is detected during testing, assume MOPP3 or MOPP4.
• Purge the air until tests are negative and then perform unmasking procedures.
• After the all-clear signal, soldiers may resume modified MOPP levels.
weapon systems,MOPP level,purge time,detector monitoring equipment

Biological Protection (2) -

Mitigating Risks from Biological Agents in Urban Environments
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Use urban structures for immediate protection: Buildings and other urban structures can offer some initial protection from direct biological agent spray.
2. Be aware of increased persistence: The stable environment inside...
Procedure:

1. Use urban structures for immediate protection: Buildings and other urban structures can offer some initial protection from direct biological agent spray.
2. Be aware of increased persistence: The stable environment inside buildings may increase the longevity of live biological agents.
3. Adopt chemical agent precautions: Apply the same protective measures used for chemical agents to mitigate risks from biological toxins.
4. Maintain personal hygiene: Soldiers must maintain strict personal hygiene, especially in covert operations where water and food supplies are targeted.
5. Ensure safe water consumption: Drink only confirmed safe water sources; never assume hydrant water is safe.
6. Enforce sanitary measures: Commanders must establish and consistently enforce sanitary and personal hygiene protocols, including immunizations.
biological,agent,urban,hygiene,sanitation,immunization,toxin,water,supply
Prevent Biological Agent Exposure in Jungle Environments
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Recognize that jungles provide ideal conditions for the use of live biological agents and toxins due to warm temperatures, high humidity, and protection from sunlight.
2. Implement strict field sanitation procedures, including...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that jungles provide ideal conditions for the use of live biological agents and toxins due to warm temperatures, high humidity, and protection from sunlight.
2. Implement strict field sanitation procedures, including vector and rodent control, to help prevent naturally occurring diseases.
3. Wear protective gear such as masks and long sleeves to cover exposed skin and reduce contact with potential biological agents or toxins.
4. Be vigilant for signs of disease outbreaks among personnel and take immediate action if symptoms are observed.
jungle,biological agents,field sanitation,vector control,toxins

Entry Procedures (2) -

Entry Into Shelter or Van Procedures
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Title: Entry Into Shelter or Van Procedures

Describe procedures for entering. Give sufficient detail to avoid confusion. Standardize these procedures, or at least make them compatible with and similar to those for larger shelters. Your SOP...
Title: Entry Into Shelter or Van Procedures

Describe procedures for entering. Give sufficient detail to avoid confusion. Standardize these procedures, or at least make them compatible with and similar to those for larger shelters. Your SOP could include the following procedures:

• If liquid contamination is detected on soldiers, follow specific entry procedures as outlined in the SOP.
shelter entry,van entry,liquid contamination
Personnel Entry Procedures
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Title: Personnel Entry Procedures

Cover entry procedures to ensure the least risk of contamination to soldiers and equipment inside and the least interference with tactical operations. Each entry involves a trade-off. Consider this question for...
Title: Personnel Entry Procedures

Cover entry procedures to ensure the least risk of contamination to soldiers and equipment inside and the least interference with tactical operations. Each entry involves a trade-off. Consider this question for each person: Does the time that may have to be spent in masks and the corresponding degradation of combat mission performance justify entry of this person?
entry procedures,contamination risk,tactical operations

Emergency Operations (2) -

Shelter or Van Emergency Operations - Malfunctioning of Gas-Particulate Filter Unit
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. If the change-filter light comes on, occupants must put on masks immediately.
2. An assigned occupant determines if the filter is operating by following procedures in the organizational maintenance manual.
3. If...
Procedure:

1. If the change-filter light comes on, occupants must put on masks immediately.
2. An assigned occupant determines if the filter is operating by following procedures in the organizational maintenance manual.
3. If filters need to be replaced, occupants must work in the appropriate MOPP gear until:
- Filters are replaced,
- Air is purged, and
- Detectors indicate masks can be removed.
filter malfunction, MOPP gear, change-filter light
Shelter or Van Emergency Operations - Sudden Loss of Positive Pressure
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. If an alert is given and the shelter or van is prepared for NBC operations, the alarm system alerts occupants to any of several types of equipment failure.
2. Sudden loss of positive pressure in the shelter:
- Lights and...
Procedure:

1. If an alert is given and the shelter or van is prepared for NBC operations, the alarm system alerts occupants to any of several types of equipment failure.
2. Sudden loss of positive pressure in the shelter:
- Lights and horns on the CCM signal an alarm.
- Occupants must put on masks immediately.
3. An assigned occupant confirms this signal by checking:
- CCM power,
- Lights, and
- Horn.
4. If alarms are confirmed and compartment positive pressure is lost, occupants must remain masked while the assigned operator pursues the problem.
5. The operator uses the organizational maintenance manual to locate the problem, and if possible, repair it.
6. In either case (problem not found or cannot be repaired), occupants must dress at the appropriate MOPP level.
positive pressure loss, CCM alarm, MOPP level

Decontamination Equipment (2) -

Using the M11 Decon Apparatus in Cold Weather
Standard Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Functionality range: The M11 decon apparatus is designed to dispense DS2 at temperatures as low as -15°F (-26.1°C).
2. Performance limitations: DS2 becomes thicker as the temperature decreases, which may affect its flow...
Procedure:

1. Functionality range: The M11 decon apparatus is designed to dispense DS2 at temperatures as low as -15°F (-26.1°C).
2. Performance limitations: DS2 becomes thicker as the temperature decreases, which may affect its flow and effectiveness.
3. Nitrogen cylinder usage:
- Normally, one nitrogen cylinder is used to charge the M11 apparatus.
- In cold weather conditions where pressure is inadequate with a single cylinder, use a second nitrogen cylinder.
4. Limitation: Never use more than two nitrogen cylinders for the M11 apparatus.
M11 decon apparatus, DS2, nitrogen cylinder, cold weather, pressure
Using the M17 Lightweight Decon System and M12A1 Decon Apparatus in Cold Weather
Standard Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Cold weather limitations: Both the M17 lightweight decon system and the M12A1 decon apparatus face similar cold-weather challenges as other water-based systems.
2. Common issues:
- Engine "cold-soaking" problems may...
Procedure:

1. Cold weather limitations: Both the M17 lightweight decon system and the M12A1 decon apparatus face similar cold-weather challenges as other water-based systems.
2. Common issues:
- Engine "cold-soaking" problems may cause starting difficulties and brittle metal parts.
- Internal pumps and lines can crack or break when water freezes within these systems.
3. Preventative measures:
- Follow shutdown procedures outlined in TMs 3-4230228-10 and 3-4230-209-20&P to avoid freeze-up problems.
- Ensure all components are properly drained or protected from freezing.
M17 decon system, M12A1 apparatus, cold weather, freeze-up prevention, shutdown procedures

Training Evaluation (2) -

Brief Soldier on C/B Hazard Encounter During Evaluation
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Steps:

1. Brief the Soldier that there will be an encounter with simulated C/B agents or unknown toxic industrial chemical agents.
2. Inform them of the possibility of a C/B alarm being given during the exercise.
3. Emphasize the need to...
Steps:

1. Brief the Soldier that there will be an encounter with simulated C/B agents or unknown toxic industrial chemical agents.
2. Inform them of the possibility of a C/B alarm being given during the exercise.
3. Emphasize the need to recognize hazards, protect themselves, and warn other Soldiers by giving the appropriate alarm.

Details:
• The Soldier should be prepared for realistic scenarios that test their response capabilities.
• Clear communication is essential to ensure understanding of the task.
brief soldier,C/B hazard,alarm simulation,response training
Evaluate C/B Response During Field Exercise
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Steps:

1. Evaluate the C/B response during a field exercise when simulated C/B hazards or toxic industrial chemical contamination can be presented.
2. Select an appropriate site with adequate cover and ensure that all Soldiers have their...
Steps:

1. Evaluate the C/B response during a field exercise when simulated C/B hazards or toxic industrial chemical contamination can be presented.
2. Select an appropriate site with adequate cover and ensure that all Soldiers have their assigned protective masks.

Details:
• The evaluation should simulate real-world conditions to test preparedness.
• Ensure that the environment is safe for training purposes.
field exercise,C/B evaluation,training simulation,protective mask

Fallout Behavior (2) -

Assess Fallout Particle Behavior and Deposition After a Nuclear Detonation
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Understand how fallout particles behave and where they deposit after a nuclear detonation.

### Fallout Particle Behavior
• Larger Particles:
- Fall closer to the detonation site within the first few hours.
- Are more likely...
Objective: Understand how fallout particles behave and where they deposit after a nuclear detonation.

### Fallout Particle Behavior
• Larger Particles:
- Fall closer to the detonation site within the first few hours.
- Are more likely to be visible as they descend.
• Smaller Particles:
- Stay in the atmosphere for longer, potentially for days or weeks.
- May travel farther from the detonation site due to wind and weather conditions.

### Fallout Deposition Patterns
• The most dangerous concentrations of fallout particles deposit during the first few hours after a nuclear detonation.
• These particles are often visible as they fall and may be the size of fine sand or table salt.
• Weather conditions significantly influence how far and where fallout settles.

### Monitoring Considerations
• Fallout is most hazardous in the immediate aftermath of a detonation.
• Continuous monitoring for radiation levels is essential during this period to identify areas with high concentrations of fallout.
fallout behavior, nuclear detonation, radiation monitoring, particle size, deposition patterns
Understand the Nature and Behavior of Fallout Particles
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
### Understanding Fallout Particles

After a near-surface nuclear detonation, fallout is created from fission products, which are radioactive materials formed when uranium or plutonium nuclei split apart. These fission products:

• Are...
### Understanding Fallout Particles

After a near-surface nuclear detonation, fallout is created from fission products, which are radioactive materials formed when uranium or plutonium nuclei split apart. These fission products:

• Are vaporized in the fireball and retained within the resulting nuclear cloud.
• The extreme heat of the fireball causes the nuclear cloud to rise rapidly, often several miles into the atmosphere.
• For near-surface detonations, the cratering and strong updraft below the cloud can incorporate thousands of tons of dirt and debris into the cloud.

### Characteristics of Fallout Particles

Fallout particles vary in size:

1. Large particles (e.g., pebbles):
- Fall closer to the detonation site within the first few hours after the explosion.
- Are more easily visible and detectable due to their larger size.

2. Small particles (e.g., fine sand or table salt):
- Stay in the atmosphere for much longer, potentially for days or weeks following an event.
- Contribute to the most dangerous concentrations of fallout during the first few hours after detonation.

### Immediate Fallout Behavior
• Immediately dangerous fallout will descend back to earth within the first few minutes to hours after a nuclear detonation and can be readily visible as it comes down.
• The smaller particles are more hazardous due to their ability to remain airborne for extended periods, increasing exposure risk over time.
fallout particles, nuclear detonation, fission products, radiation behavior, fallout characteristics

Responder Actions (2) -

Focus of Early Life-Saving Operations in the MDZ
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
- The MDZ should be the focus of early life-saving operations. Response activities should focus on evacuation of endangered populations and medical triage of the injured.
MDZ, life-saving operations, evacuation, medical triage
Responder Actions in the MDZ with DRZ Overlap
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
- Manage fires remotely, if possible, recommend sheltering if safe to do so, and enable public egress to escape life-threatening conditions.

• Minimize outdoor responder activities. Monitor radiological conditions and operate outside the DRZ when...
- Manage fires remotely, if possible, recommend sheltering if safe to do so, and enable public egress to escape life-threatening conditions.

• Minimize outdoor responder activities. Monitor radiological conditions and operate outside the DRZ when possible. Only conduct short, focused, and critical activities, to avoid unnecessary exposure.

• Access to the DRZ will increase over time as radiation levels decay. Responders in this zone should have high range radiation monitoring equipment that alerts them to high exposure rates and excessive dose.
MDZ, DRZ, remote fire management, radiation monitoring

Radiation Response (2) -

Establish and Operate an RTR 1 Site in a Nuclear Detonation Scenario
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure for Establishing and Operating an RTR 1 Site:

1. Identify the location: Set up the RTR 1 site within or near areas of both physical damage and radiation (MDZs or LDZs), where fallout may overlay physically damaged sites.
2....
Procedure for Establishing and Operating an RTR 1 Site:

1. Identify the location: Set up the RTR 1 site within or near areas of both physical damage and radiation (MDZs or LDZs), where fallout may overlay physically damaged sites.
2. Ensure responder safety:
- Use radiation monitoring equipment to measure local radiation levels.
- Enforce predetermined exposure limits for responders to minimize radiation risk.
3. Triage and stabilize patients:
- Provide initial treatment for casualties, including:
- Stabilizing traumatic injuries (e.g., fractures, lacerations, bleeding).
- Covering burns with appropriate materials.
- Performing triage assessments for trauma and radiation victims.
4. Route casualties to appropriate care:
- Direct stabilized patients toward medical care centers (MCs), acute care centers (ACs), or other designated facilities based on the severity of their injuries.
5. Coordinate with local EMS and volunteers:
- Only operate RTR 1 sites after radiation levels have subsided sufficiently to protect responders.
6. Document all activities:
- Keep records of patient intake, treatment provided, and routing decisions for follow-up and resource allocation.
RTR1,nuclear detonation,radiation triage,trauma stabilization,fallout zones
Responder Actions in Hot Zone (HZ) During Nuclear Detonation
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Minimize radiation exposure and support emergency response activities safely.

Steps:
1. Monitor radiation levels using appropriate equipment to assess the extent of contamination.
2. Limit time spent outdoors to reduce...
Objective: Minimize radiation exposure and support emergency response activities safely.

Steps:
1. Monitor radiation levels using appropriate equipment to assess the extent of contamination.
2. Limit time spent outdoors to reduce personal radiation exposure.
3. Wear PPE (Personal Protective Equipment) that is appropriate for all hazards present, including non-radiological threats such as fire or chemical exposure.
4. Support more heavily impacted zones (LDZ, MDZ) if possible and do not delay local emergency response activities.
5. Use radiation detection equipment to verify conditions when determining whether to enter or exit the HZ or DRZ (Downwind Radiation Zone).
responder actions,nuclear detonation,radiation monitoring,PPE,emergency response

Public Health (2) -

Encourage Early Actions to Reduce External Contamination
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Encourage the public to take immediate steps to reduce external contamination in the early hours and days following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Inform individuals that thorough washing or complete removal of external...
Objective: Encourage the public to take immediate steps to reduce external contamination in the early hours and days following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Inform individuals that thorough washing or complete removal of external contamination may not be immediately practical but should still be encouraged.
2. Advise people to dust off contaminated surfaces on their skin, clothing, and shoes as often as possible until they can wash or change clothes.
3. Provide guidance on how to perform basic decontamination using water and soap in the absence of specialized equipment.
4. Encourage family members, companions, or caregivers to assist individuals who may be unable to perform self-decontamination independently.
5. Emphasize that even partial reduction of external contamination can significantly reduce radiation exposure over time.

Notes:
• External contamination is a major source of radiation exposure in the immediate aftermath of a nuclear detonation.
• Public cooperation and understanding are critical for effective decontamination efforts.
external contamination, public health, radiation exposure, decontamination, nuclear detonation
Provide Clear Instructions for Self-Decontamination
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Encourage the public to perform self-decontamination in the early hours and days following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Emergency management officials must provide simple, clear instructions using language appropriate for...
Objective: Encourage the public to perform self-decontamination in the early hours and days following a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Emergency management officials must provide simple, clear instructions using language appropriate for the affected community.
2. Use terms such as washing, changing clothes, or removing shoes instead of decontamination to make instructions more accessible and less threatening.
3. Emphasize the importance of dusting off contaminated surfaces on skin, clothing, and shoes as frequently as possible until a full wash or change of clothes is possible.
4. Encourage individuals to remove contaminated clothing and shoes immediately if feasible.
5. Use all available communication channels (e.g., radio, social media, community loudspeakers) to disseminate instructions.
6. Provide guidance on how to perform basic washing techniques using water and soap, even in limited resource situations.

Notes:
• Communication infrastructure may be damaged or unavailable after a nuclear detonation.
• Instructions must be culturally appropriate and easy to understand for all members of the affected population.
self-decontamination, public health, communication, nuclear detonation, washing instructions

Radiation Registry Planning (2) -

Establish a Radiation Registry
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Plan to establish a radiation registry as soon as possible.

Key Factors to Consider When Planning for a Radiation Registry:

1. Engage Stakeholders:
- Bring key stakeholders together before an emergency occurs.
-...
Objective: Plan to establish a radiation registry as soon as possible.

Key Factors to Consider When Planning for a Radiation Registry:

1. Engage Stakeholders:
- Bring key stakeholders together before an emergency occurs.
- Discuss scientific and sociopolitical challenges related to radiation registries.
- Identify disagreements among stakeholders.
- Expected stakeholders include anyone with a mission, interest, influence, or expectations related to the registry.

2. Define the Purpose of the Registry:
- The purpose defines who should be enrolled in the registry.
- Determines methods for reaching out to individuals.
- Specifies what data must be collected.
- Identifies consent, authorization, and legal requirements that govern the registry.
- Outlines resources needed (personnel, funding, etc.).

Potential Purposes of a Radiation Registry:
- Medical monitoring for those exhibiting clinical symptoms related to Acute Radiation Syndrome (ARS).
- Health monitoring for those affected (exposure, contamination, mental health).
- Access to healthcare for those affected.
- Research on radiation health effects.
- Social recognition of the tragedy and its impact on the population.
- Outreach to those affected with updates on scientific and medical developments or relevant programs/policies.
- Financial compensation for victims.

3. Identify Stakeholders and Define Roles and Responsibilities:
- The roles and responsibilities for establishing long-term health monitoring systems following a nuclear or radiological incident are not well defined.
- Agreement on roles and responsibilities prior to an incident helps avoid confusion, duplicative activities, and competition for scarce resources.

Federal Government (ATSDR) Involvement Options:
- ATSDR develops a framework with stakeholder input, but implementation is the responsibility of state or local health authorities.
- ATSDR creates a radiation registry template and transfers it to state or local authorities for implementation and operation.
- ATSDR implements and operates a centralized registry with stakeholder input.

State or Local Health Authorities Roles:
- Perform data collection for the registry.
- Transfer collected data to ATSDR, which is responsible for operating a centralized registry and reporting adverse outcomes.

Note: Planners should identify local and state capabilities to determine the most likely approach for their jurisdiction.
radiation registry, stakeholder engagement, health monitoring, ATSDR, nuclear incident planning
Identify Stakeholders and Define Roles in Radiation Registry Planning
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Identify stakeholders and define their roles and responsibilities to ensure a coordinated response.

Key Considerations:
• Stakeholder Agreement: Prior agreement on roles and responsibilities among stakeholders is essential...
Objective: Identify stakeholders and define their roles and responsibilities to ensure a coordinated response.

Key Considerations:
• Stakeholder Agreement: Prior agreement on roles and responsibilities among stakeholders is essential for creating an agreed-upon framework that minimizes confusion, duplication, or conflicting activities.
• Expected Stakeholders: Include anyone with a mission, interest, influence, or expectations related to the radiation registry. This may include public health officials, federal agencies (e.g., ATSDR), local governments, healthcare providers, and affected communities.
• Federal Involvement Options:
- ATSDR develops a framework for setting up a radiation registry with stakeholder input, but implementation is handled by state or local authorities.
- ATSDR creates a template and transfers it to state/local authorities for implementation.
- ATSDR implements and operates a centralized registry.
• State/Local Involvement: State or local health authorities may collect data for the registry and transfer it to ATSDR, which is responsible for operating a centralized registry and reporting adverse outcomes.
stakeholder roles, radiation registry planning, ATSDR, state/local involvement

Interjurisdictional Coordination (2) -

Coordinate Nuclear Detonation Plans with Neighboring Jurisdictions
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Ensure that neighboring jurisdictions are prepared to support your jurisdiction during a nuclear detonation response.

Steps:
1. Review and update existing agreements with neighboring jurisdictions to ensure they include...
Objective: Ensure that neighboring jurisdictions are prepared to support your jurisdiction during a nuclear detonation response.

Steps:
1. Review and update existing agreements with neighboring jurisdictions to ensure they include provisions for communications and public information support in the event of a nuclear detonation.
2. Coordinate planning efforts with neighboring jurisdictions to align response strategies, resource allocation, and communication protocols.
3. Identify key points of contact within each jurisdiction who can be reached during an emergency.
4. Incorporate federal resources such as FEMA’s Radiological Operations Support Specialist (ROSS) program into your plans for technical communications support.
5. Plan for the dissemination of time-critical safety messages through a multijurisdictional or regional approach to ensure rapid and effective communication with affected communities.
neighboring jurisdictions, coordination plans, nuclear detonation response, ROSS program
Establish Interjurisdictional Relationships and Memoranda of Understanding (MOUs)
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Build strong interjurisdictional relationships to ensure effective coordination during a nuclear detonation response.

Steps:
1. Identify neighboring jurisdictions, including towns, cities, counties, state agencies, and federal...
Objective: Build strong interjurisdictional relationships to ensure effective coordination during a nuclear detonation response.

Steps:
1. Identify neighboring jurisdictions, including towns, cities, counties, state agencies, and federal organizations that may be involved in the response.
2. Cultivate and maintain relationships with these entities through regular communication and collaboration.
3. Develop Memoranda of Understanding (MOUs) with neighboring jurisdictions to outline roles, responsibilities, and support mechanisms during a nuclear detonation response.
4. Coordinate nuclear detonation plans with neighboring jurisdictions to ensure consistency in response strategies.
5. Exercise joint emergency response plans with these jurisdictions to test coordination and identify areas for improvement.
6. Designate trusted agents within your jurisdiction who can serve as liaisons with other jurisdictions during an incident.
7. Share information about local news acquisition preferences (e.g., preferred media outlets, communication platforms) so that neighboring jurisdictions can effectively disseminate messages in the event of a nuclear detonation.
interjurisdictional coordination, MOUs, nuclear detonation response, emergency planning

Radiation Sickness (2) -

Recognize and Manage Gastrointestinal Subsyndrome (GI-ARS)
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Identify symptoms of GI-ARS and implement appropriate management strategies.

Procedure:
1. Symptoms Recognition: Look for the following signs:
- Nausea and vomiting
- Diarrhea
- Dehydration
- Electrolyte...
Objective: Identify symptoms of GI-ARS and implement appropriate management strategies.

Procedure:
1. Symptoms Recognition: Look for the following signs:
- Nausea and vomiting
- Diarrhea
- Dehydration
- Electrolyte imbalances
- Gastrointestinal bleeding
- Systemic infections
2. Dose Threshold: GI-ARS typically manifests at whole body radiation doses exceeding 6 Gy (600 rad).
3. Factors Affecting Onset and Severity: Consider the following variables:
- Total dose received
- Dose rate
- Host factors (e.g., age, health status)
4. Management Strategies: Implement the following interventions:
- Administer countermeasures for nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea
- Treat infections with appropriate antibiotics
- Replace fluids and electrolytes to address dehydration
- Provide blood replacement if necessary
5. Note on Cytokines: Cytokine treatments used for H-ARS may indirectly improve GI-ARS outcomes by improving overall patient condition.
6. Hospitalization Considerations: Patients with GI-ARS are likely already hospitalized due to concurrent H-ARS, so treatment plans should be coordinated accordingly.

Notes: Symptoms of GI-ARS can overlap with other conditions and are not specific to radiation exposure alone.
GI-ARS symptoms, radiation dose, treatment strategies, dehydration, electrolyte imbalance
Understand LD50/60 for Acute Radiation Syndrome (ARS)
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Understand the concept of LD50/60 and its relevance to ARS survival rates.

Procedure:
1. Define LD50/60: The LD50/60 is the radiation dose that will kill 50% of a population within 60 days without treatment.
2. **Untreated...
Objective: Understand the concept of LD50/60 and its relevance to ARS survival rates.

Procedure:
1. Define LD50/60: The LD50/60 is the radiation dose that will kill 50% of a population within 60 days without treatment.
2. Untreated Survival Curve: Without medical intervention, the LD50/60 for humans is approximately 450 cGy (450 rad) as shown in Figure 47.
3. Effect of Treatment: With currently available treatments and countermeasures, survival rates increase significantly, shifting the lethality curve to the right (i.e., higher doses are required to achieve the same mortality rate).
4. Use for Planning: Use this information to estimate casualty numbers and plan medical resources accordingly.

Notes: This data is derived from _Multiservice Tactics Techniques and Procedures for Treatment of Nuclear and Radiological Casualties, 2014._
LD50/60, ARS, radiation dose, survival rate, nuclear detonation

Health Surveillance (2) -

Implement ERHMS System for Emergency Responder Health Monitoring
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Overview: The ERHMS (Emergency Responder Health Monitoring and Surveillance) system is a framework that supports the health monitoring of emergency responders during all phases of an incident: pre-deployment, deployment, and...
Overview: The ERHMS (Emergency Responder Health Monitoring and Surveillance) system is a framework that supports the health monitoring of emergency responders during all phases of an incident: pre-deployment, deployment, and post-deployment.

Procedure:
1. Access the ERHMS system at [www.cdc.gov/niosh/erhms](http://www.cdc.gov/niosh/erhms).
2. Pre-Deployment Phase:
- Roster and credential emergency response and recovery workers.
- Conduct health screening for all responders to identify pre-existing conditions or risks.
- Provide health and safety training specific to the incident type (e.g., nuclear, CBRN).
- Implement data management systems that ensure information security and privacy.
3. Deployment Phase:
- Perform on-site in-processing of responders before beginning operations.
- Conduct ongoing health monitoring and surveillance during response operations.
- Integrate exposure assessment with responder activity documentation and control measures.
- Communicate exposure and health data to relevant stakeholders in real-time during the emergency response.
4. Post-Deployment Phase:
- Perform out-processing assessments of responders after their deployment ends.
- Track the long-term health and functional status of responders post-event.
- Conduct lessons-learned and after-action assessments to improve future responder health monitoring practices.
erhms, emergency responder health, nuclear detonation response, health surveillance, pre-deployment, deployment, post-deployment
Implement Epi CASE Toolkit for CBRN Exposure Assessment
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Overview: The Epi CASE (Contact Assessment Symptom Exposure) toolkit is designed for rapid assessment of individuals affected by, exposed to, or potentially exposed to CBRN (Chemical, Biological, Radiological, and Nuclear) agents during...
Overview: The Epi CASE (Contact Assessment Symptom Exposure) toolkit is designed for rapid assessment of individuals affected by, exposed to, or potentially exposed to CBRN (Chemical, Biological, Radiological, and Nuclear) agents during incidents. It aids in the development of health registries and provides data for epidemiologic follow-up.

Procedure:
1. Access the Epi CASE toolkit at [www.atsdr.cdc.gov/epitoolkit/index.html](http://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/epitoolkit/index.html).
2. Use the toolkit to collect data from affected individuals, including:
- Contact information
- Exposure details (type of agent, time, and location)
- Symptoms reported
3. Analyze collected data using the toolkit’s built-in functions to generate descriptive statistics.
4. Use the data for epidemiologic follow-up, including:
- Health studies
- Community assessments
- Health registries
5. Consider the time and resource commitment required for health registry development before initiating a large-scale registry.
6. Ensure that all data collected is securely stored and managed in accordance with public health standards.
epi case, cbrn exposure assessment, health registry, epidemiologic follow-up, nuclear detonation response

Construction Preparation (2) -

Implementing Precautionary Measures Against Nuclear Weapons
Critical Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. Take advantage of natural terrain, such as hills or other elevation features, to reduce structural damage from nuclear explosions.
2. Use glass-substitute material in construction to minimize damage and hazards from blast...
Procedure:

1. Take advantage of natural terrain, such as hills or other elevation features, to reduce structural damage from nuclear explosions.
2. Use glass-substitute material in construction to minimize damage and hazards from blast effects.
3. Follow minimum-spacing requirements for buildings and structures to reduce the risk of collapse or damage during a nuclear explosion.
4. In some cases, it may be advisable to dig in or partially bury certain structures for added protection.
5. Ensure an adequate water supply is available for emergency use and firefighting purposes.
6. Use noninflammable exterior finishes on buildings where possible to reduce fire risks from blast effects.
7. Add additional bracing at structural joints to improve the resilience of buildings against blast forces.
nuclear protection, construction safety, fire prevention, blast resistance
Mitigating Nuclear Weapon Effects on Construction
Critical Source: US Army Frame Structures
Procedure:

1. Understand that changes in construction standards due to the potential use of nuclear weapons are governed by Department of the Army policy, typically outlined in TO (Technical Order) or other directives.
2. Consider the...
Procedure:

1. Understand that changes in construction standards due to the potential use of nuclear weapons are governed by Department of the Army policy, typically outlined in TO (Technical Order) or other directives.
2. Consider the following general factors when constructing buildings in areas where nuclear weapons may be used:
- Damage from blast and shock
- Fire caused by thermal radiation or indirect blast effects
- Vulnerability of wooden, brick, and light steel frame buildings to blast damage
- Severe structural damage can occur at distances ranging from 1300 meters for a 2 kt bomb to 8000 meters for a 500 kt bomb in open terrain
- Underground shock may severely affect water, sewer, and gas mains, but electric mains are less affected
nuclear weapons, construction safety, blast effects, radiation

Post-Disaster Recovery (2) -

Restart Life After 3 Weeks Post-Nuclear Disaster
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Action: Begin rebuilding your life after three weeks post-nuclear disaster.

Priority Tasks: Focus on food production and water filtration as top priorities.
post-nuclear recovery, food production, water filtration
Conserve Gasoline and Petroleum Products After a Nuclear Disaster
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Action: Save gasoline and other petroleum products for essential use.

Note: Most vehicles may not operate without mechanical adjustments due to the effects of an EMP.
fuel conservation, nuclear disaster

Incident Response Planning (2) -

Understand the Nature of a Nuclear Detonation Incident
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Understanding the nature of a nuclear detonation incident is critical for effective emergency response planning.

A nuclear detonation in an American city would be one of the most catastrophic incidents the United States has ever experienced....
Understanding the nature of a nuclear detonation incident is critical for effective emergency response planning.

A nuclear detonation in an American city would be one of the most catastrophic incidents the United States has ever experienced. The following are key considerations:

1. Catastrophic Impact: A nuclear detonation can cause massive loss of life, injuries, and widespread destruction due to blast effects, thermal radiation, initial nuclear radiation, and fallout.
2. Unique Challenges for Responders: Emergency responders must be prepared to address the unique challenges associated with a nuclear incident, including radiation exposure, mass casualties, and complex recovery operations.
3. Multi-Hazard Planning Applicability: While the fallout hazard is unique, most aspects of multi-hazard or all-hazard planning and response are applicable to nuclear detonation response and planning.
4. Leverage Existing Capabilities: Planners and responders bring a wealth of experience and expertise that can be leveraged for effective nuclear detonation response.
5. Focus on First 24–72 Hours: Early actions during the first 24 to 72 hours after a detonation are critical in saving lives and mitigating injuries.
6. Preparedness Benefits Other Disasters: Preparing for nuclear detonations can also better equip communities for other natural and man-made hazards, such as fire-spread, hurricanes, earthquakes, and radiological incidents.
nuclear detonation, emergency response planning, multi-hazard planning, radiation exposure, blast effects, thermal radiation, fallout hazard, first 24-72 hours
Identify Primary Audiences for Nuclear Detonation Response Planning
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Emergency response planners and their leadership at all levels must be identified as primary audiences for nuclear detonation response planning.

The following groups are the primary audiences for this planning guidance:

• **Federal, State,...
Emergency response planners and their leadership at all levels must be identified as primary audiences for nuclear detonation response planning.

The following groups are the primary audiences for this planning guidance:

• Federal, State, Local, Tribal, and Territorial (FSLTT) Emergency Response Planners: At all levels of government.
• Emergency Managers: Responsible for overall emergency management coordination.
• Law Enforcement Authority Planners: Involved in security and public safety during the response.
• Fire Response Planners: Critical for fire suppression, search and rescue, and damage assessment.
• Emergency Medical Service (EMS) Planners: Essential for medical triage, treatment, and evacuation of casualties.
• Hazardous Material (HAZMAT) Response Planners: Required to manage radiation exposure and contaminated materials.
• Utility Service and Public Works Emergency Planners: Involved in restoring critical infrastructure such as power, water, and communication systems.
• Transportation Planners: Necessary for managing evacuation routes, traffic control, and movement of resources.
• Public Health Planners: Responsible for monitoring health impacts and implementing public health interventions.
• Medical Provider Planners (e.g., Hospitals): Critical for treating casualties and managing medical surge capacity.
• Mass Care Providers (e.g., American Red Cross): Essential for providing shelter, food, water, and other basic needs to affected populations.
• Public Information Officers (PIOs): Responsible for communication with the public, media, and stakeholders during the incident.

Additional Target Audiences:
• Local and regional private sector industries capable of providing logistical support for immediate response—either by voluntary actions or by requisition of resources.
• Other emergency planners, planning organizations, and professional organizations that represent disciplines conducting emergency response activities.
emergency response planners, public health planners, medical provider planners, transportation planners, hazmat response planners, utility service planners, law enforcement planners, fire response planners, mass care providers, public information officers

Radiation Management (2) -

Manage LDZ Outside of DRZ
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Action: Manage fires, clear routes, and advise shelter in the LDZ outside of the DRZ (exposure rate less than 10 R/h). Details:
• Evacuation is not required to mitigate the radiological hazard but may be warranted due to unsafe shelter...
Action: Manage fires, clear routes, and advise shelter in the LDZ outside of the DRZ (exposure rate less than 10 R/h). Details:
• Evacuation is not required to mitigate the radiological hazard but may be warranted due to unsafe shelter conditions (weather, fire, medical emergency, smoke, etc.).
• Responders should maintain evacuation corridors and treat the injured.
• If elevated radiation levels (HZ) occur, keep people moving out of the contaminated area.
• Identify casualty collection points and Radiation Triage, Treatment, and Transport (RTR 1) sites.
LDZ,DRZ,radiation management,RTR sites,evacuation corridors
Radiation Exposure Management in Hot Zone (HZ)
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Keep radiation exposure as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA) to minimize long-term health risks.

Steps:
1. Sheltering is recommended, even at long distances downwind, and should be followed by a delayed evacuation if...
Objective: Keep radiation exposure as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA) to minimize long-term health risks.

Steps:
1. Sheltering is recommended, even at long distances downwind, and should be followed by a delayed evacuation if necessary.
2. Seek adequate shelter based on guidance from Chapter 3 of the planning document.
3. Use radiation monitoring equipment in the HZ to determine when responders are nearing or entering the DRZ.
4. Be aware that the HZ overlaps with SDZ, MDZ, and LDZ, and actions should be driven by damage zone hazards and priorities in overlapping areas.
5. Prioritize medical emergencies over radiological concerns in the HZ. Lifesaving operations should not be delayed for radiation exposure/contamination concerns.
ALARA,radiation exposure management,nuclear detonation,sheltering,medical emergencies

Communications Planning (2) -

Implement Pre-Incident Communications Planning for Nuclear Detonations
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Pre-incident communications planning is essential to increase the public’s perception of control and familiarity with nuclear detonation scenarios.

Emergency planners, public information officers (PIOs), community leaders, and emergency and first...
Pre-incident communications planning is essential to increase the public’s perception of control and familiarity with nuclear detonation scenarios.

Emergency planners, public information officers (PIOs), community leaders, and emergency and first response personnel are responsible for effectively communicating why preparation for a nuclear detonation scenario is essential to surviving it.

Without pre-incident knowledge, key messaging, and preparedness steps, people may follow the instinct to run from danger, potentially exposing themselves to fatal doses of radiation that could be avoided by sheltering.

Planners can perform two key preparation activities:
1. Plan and execute nuclear detonation preparedness education campaigns related to other hazards.

Note: A picture was intentionally omitted in the source text.
pre-incident planning, nuclear detonation education, public preparedness, communication strategy
Coordinate Communication Efforts for Nuclear Detonation Response
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Federal, state, and local resources must coordinate to send timely and accurate safety messages.

Public affairs staff play a critical role in addressing fear and grief while accurately describing protective actions. Planners should inform public...
Federal, state, and local resources must coordinate to send timely and accurate safety messages.

Public affairs staff play a critical role in addressing fear and grief while accurately describing protective actions. Planners should inform public affairs staff that they do not have to create all communications strategy and messaging themselves.

Radiation communications experts across the country can share best practices and case studies to help fill gaps in nuclear and radiological emergency preparedness and response communications.

References:
• FEMA’s _Improvised Nuclear Device Response and Recovery: Communicating in the Immediate Aftermath:_ [www.fema.gov/sites/default/files/documents/fema_improvised-nuclear-device_communicating aftermath_june 2013.pdf](www.fema.gov/sites/default/files/documents/fema_improvised-nuclear-device_communicating%20aftermath_june%202013.pdf)
• _Exploring Medical and Public Health Preparedness for a Nuclear Incident,_ Chapter 5: Implications of Communication, Education, and Information Challenges: [doi.org/10.17226/25372](doi.org/10.17226/25372)
communication, coordination, nuclear detonation, public affairs, emergency messaging

Public Alerting (2) -

Presidential to Public Alert - Pre-Detonation Scenario
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
### PRESIDENTIAL TO PUBLIC ALERT―PRE-DETONATION SCENARIO

Warning of an attack would come via a NAWAS announcement from the FEMA Operations Center (FOC)/FEMA Alternate Operations Center (FAOC). State-level issuance of WEA would be based on...
### PRESIDENTIAL TO PUBLIC ALERT―PRE-DETONATION SCENARIO

Warning of an attack would come via a NAWAS announcement from the FEMA Operations Center (FOC)/FEMA Alternate Operations Center (FAOC). State-level issuance of WEA would be based on receiving a NAWAS alert from the FOC/FAOC. This section describes how NAWAS operates pre-detonation and how IPAWS and NAWAS operate post-detonation.

The _Response FIOP_ outlines how a national-level EAS alert activated by the president reaches the public through the channels of FEMA-affiliated broadcast stations, SiriusXM satellite radio, and National Public Radio; that is, a President to Public Alert (FEMA, 2016b).

### NAWAS Operation in Pre-Detonation Scenario

• NAWAS delivers alerts via a continuous private line telephone network.
• In a pre-detonation scenario, the NAWAS Manual specifies that:

> "The national-level EAS is activated by an order from the President to the White House Communications Agency (WHCA) duty officer or the President’s Communications Officer (PCO) through the FOC or FAOC. The FOC/FAOC authenticates the request and establishes the PEP conference. At the request of the President, FEMA distributes Presidential Level messages to the PEP stations." (LLNL, 2019)

### Figure Reference

• Figure 43: Delivery of Presidential Alert to the Public (derived from _Response FIOP_ )
presidential alert,nuclear detonation,FEMA,NAWAS,EAS,PEP conference
Public Alerting and Notification in Operational Planning
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
### Public Alerting, Warning and Notification in Operational Planning

A planning approach shared by many is to use multiple systems and platforms to ensure messages are clear and that the source and information content of all AWN public...
### Public Alerting, Warning and Notification in Operational Planning

A planning approach shared by many is to use multiple systems and platforms to ensure messages are clear and that the source and information content of all AWN public messaging can be trusted. Some of the most relevant guidance for pre- and post-detonation planning is the _Response FIOP_. It and the closely related _NRIA_ include very important guidance for the federal interagency and SLTT planners.

Ensuring that nuclear detonation response plans are informed by this proven guidance will contribute substantially to public trust in AWN received during such catastrophic emergency. Additional federal guidance specific to AWN from the Community Lifelines’ Communications Component, and its important relationship to ESF-15, is also discussed.

### Refer To

• _Response Federal Interagency Operational Plan_ describes how federal agencies coordinate activities to respond to emergency incidents: [www.fema.gov/sites/default/files/documents/fema_response fiop.pdf](http://www.fema.gov/sites/default/files/documents/fema_response%20fiop.pdf).
public alerting,nuclear detonation,FEMA,Response FIOP,NAWAS

Animal Handling (2) -

Coordinate with Veterinary Professionals for Contaminated Animals
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Action: Coordinate with veterinary professionals to ensure contaminated animals are treated appropriately.

Details:
• Incident management officials must coordinate with veterinary professionals to provide appropriate care and treatment for...
Action: Coordinate with veterinary professionals to ensure contaminated animals are treated appropriately.

Details:
• Incident management officials must coordinate with veterinary professionals to provide appropriate care and treatment for contaminated animals.
• This includes ensuring that veterinary services are available to clients whose animals may have been contaminated or received harmful levels of radiation exposure.
veterinary coordination, contaminated animals, radiation treatment
Prevent Re-contamination of Animals and Owners
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Action: Prevent animals from re-contaminating themselves or spreading contamination to people.

Details:
• Animals may re-contaminate themselves and bring contamination inside homes or shelters. At CRCs or public shelters, animal spaces are...
Action: Prevent animals from re-contaminating themselves or spreading contamination to people.

Details:
• Animals may re-contaminate themselves and bring contamination inside homes or shelters. At CRCs or public shelters, animal spaces are usually restricted.
• For people sheltering at home, communications should address placing pets in cages or on a leash if there is risk of recontamination after washing.
• Animals cross-contaminating owners, especially children who pet them, present a health risk.
• Communications should also target veterinary professionals to ensure they provide appropriate advice and services to clients whose animals may have been contaminated or received harmful levels of radiation exposure.
re-contamination prevention, animal containment, radiation risk, child safety

Radiation Protection Volunteers (2) -

Engage with the Radiation Response Volunteer Corps (RRVC)
Standard Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Participate in a program designed to support states during nuclear emergencies by providing trained radiological health professionals.

Steps:
1. Access information about the Radiation Response Volunteer Corps (RRVC) through...
Objective: Participate in a program designed to support states during nuclear emergencies by providing trained radiological health professionals.

Steps:
1. Access information about the Radiation Response Volunteer Corps (RRVC) through the CRCPD website at [www.crcpd.org/page/RRVC](https://www.crcpd.org/page/RRVC).
2. Review eligibility requirements and training opportunities provided by the RRVC program.
3. Register as a volunteer with the RRVC to be included in state-level emergency response planning.
4. Complete any required training or drills organized by the RRVC or local radiation control programs.
5. Maintain readiness for deployment during nuclear emergencies, including population monitoring or environmental sampling operations.
rrvc, radiation response volunteer corps, nuclear emergency response
Utilize ESAR-VHP for Credentialing Radiological Health Professionals
Standard Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Recruit and register radiological health professionals (e.g., health physicists, radiation protection technologists) for response to nuclear emergencies.

Steps:
1. Access the ESAR-VHP system through the Public Health...
Objective: Recruit and register radiological health professionals (e.g., health physicists, radiation protection technologists) for response to nuclear emergencies.

Steps:
1. Access the ESAR-VHP system through the Public Health Emergency (PHE) website at [www.phe.gov/esarvhp/Pages/about.aspx](https://www.phe.gov/esarvhp/Pages/about.aspx).
2. Register as a radiological health professional using the ESAR-VHP credentialing and deployment system.
3. Complete any required training or documentation to be eligible for deployment during nuclear emergencies.
4. Maintain active status in the ESAR-VHP system to ensure availability for emergency response operations.
5. Coordinate with local radiation control programs or emergency management agencies to participate in drills or exercises.
esar-vhp, credentialing, radiological health professionals, nuclear emergency response

Radiation Protection Operations (2) -

Deploy Radiological Operations Support Specialists (ROSS)
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Provide radiation protection expertise during nuclear detonations or radiological incidents.

Steps:
1. Identify jurisdictions in need of Radiological Operations Support Specialists (ROSS), particularly those impacted by a...
Objective: Provide radiation protection expertise during nuclear detonations or radiological incidents.

Steps:
