1. Identify jurisdictions in need of Radiological Operations Support Specialists (ROSS), particularly those impacted by a major incident involving radiation release.
2. Request ROSS support from unaffected jurisdictions through mutual aid agreements.
3. Deploy trained ROSS personnel to assist with:
- Interpreting radiological data and dose projections
- Providing actionable guidance for decision-makers
- Managing environmental sampling plans that meet data quality objectives
4. Use RadResponder software to manage environmental data and provide situational awareness during incidents.
5. Coordinate with local Radiation Control Programs and emergency preparedness agencies to ensure seamless integration into incident command structures.
ross, radiological operations support specialists, nuclear detonation response
Implement Environmental Sampling Plans for Radiological Incidents
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Develop and manage environmental sampling plans that meet data quality objectives during nuclear or radiological incidents.

Steps:
1. Identify the type of incident (e.g., nuclear detonation, RDD release) to determine appropriate...
Objective: Develop and manage environmental sampling plans that meet data quality objectives during nuclear or radiological incidents.

Steps:
1. Identify the type of incident (e.g., nuclear detonation, RDD release) to determine appropriate sampling strategies.
2. Collaborate with local Radiation Control Programs and emergency management agencies to establish data quality objectives.
3. Design a sampling plan that includes:
- Sampling locations
- Frequency of sampling
- Types of samples collected (e.g., air, water, soil)
4. Use RadResponder software to track and manage environmental data collection efforts.
5. Ensure all samples are handled in accordance with safety protocols and transported to appropriate laboratories for analysis.
environmental sampling plan, data quality objectives, rad responder software

Radiation Monitoring Tools (2) -

Implement Rapid Response Registry (RRR) Survey Instrument
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Purpose: Collect critical data on individuals exposed to chemical, biological, or radiological materials during a disaster.

Steps:
1. Access the [RRR survey instrument](www.atsdr.cdc.gov/rapidresponse/#tools).
2. Distribute the two-page...
Purpose: Collect critical data on individuals exposed to chemical, biological, or radiological materials during a disaster.

Steps:
1. Access the [RRR survey instrument](www.atsdr.cdc.gov/rapidresponse/#tools).
2. Distribute the two-page form either on paper or electronically.
3. Collect the following information:
- Demographics: Name, age, sex, home address, status, place of employment
- Health Information: Current health status and medical history
- Exposure Information: Type and duration of exposure to hazardous materials
- Exposure-Related Health Effects: Symptoms or conditions related to exposure
- Immediate Health Needs: Access to medical care, medications, or other urgent needs
- Health Insurance Details: Policy number and provider information
4. For mass casualty incidents, collect only the following four fields:
- Name
- Sex
- Address
- Contact Information (telephone and email)
5. Use collected data to support real-time needs assessment, medical intervention planning, and follow-up services.

Notes:
• The RRR helps identify victims, displaced individuals, and those in need of health education or assistance.
• It can be implemented quickly for large-scale incidents.
Rapid Response Registry, RRR survey, exposure tracking, health data collection
Set Up CRC eTool for Radiation Monitoring
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Purpose: Prepare the CRC eTool for data collection, analysis, and visualization during a nuclear emergency.

Steps:
1. Access the [CRC eTool website](www.cdc.gov/nceh/radiation/emergencies/crcetool.htm).
2. Download and install the tool...
Purpose: Prepare the CRC eTool for data collection, analysis, and visualization during a nuclear emergency.

Steps:
1. Access the [CRC eTool website](www.cdc.gov/nceh/radiation/emergencies/crcetool.htm).
2. Download and install the tool using the Epi Info™ platform.
3. Ensure compatibility with local networks, including laptops, tablets, and cellphones.
4. Train personnel on how to use the CRC eTool for data collection, analysis, and visualization.
5. Verify that IT support is available for setup and troubleshooting.

Notes:
• The tool collects demographic data, radiation contamination measurements, exposure assessments, and health outcomes.
• It improves efficiency in data processing during emergencies.
CRC eTool, radiation monitoring, Epi Info, data collection, nuclear emergency

Equipment Checklist (2) -

Prepare NBC CBRN Emergency Kit
Critical Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Objective: Assemble a complete NBC (Nuclear, Biological, Chemical, Radiological) emergency kit for protection during CBRN threats.

Procedure:
1. Gas Mask Selection: Choose a gas mask that meets CBRN standards and includes features such...
Objective: Assemble a complete NBC (Nuclear, Biological, Chemical, Radiological) emergency kit for protection during CBRN threats.

Procedure:
1. Gas Mask Selection: Choose a gas mask that meets CBRN standards and includes features such as a hydration system, speech diaphragm, and compatibility with filters. The MIRA Safety CM-6M is recommended.
2. Filter Compatibility: Use the NBC 77 SOF Gas Mask Filter, which has a 20-year shelf life and filters all known CBRN agents. Ensure it is compatible with your gas mask (e.g., NATO 40mm threaded).
3. Storage Pouch: Store the gas mask and filters in a protective pouch like the BLACKHAWK! Omega Elite Gas Mask Pouch to prevent damage.
4. Protective Clothing: Include a HAZ-SUIT from MIRA Safety for full-body protection against CBRN agents.
5. Boots and Gloves: Add Honeywell Rubber Butyl Boots and Honeywell Mil Spec Butyl Gloves for hand and foot protection.
6. Radiation Detection: Include a Pocket Geiger Counter to detect radiation levels in the environment.
7. Thyroid Protection: Pack Thyrosafe Potassium Iodide Tablets (20 count) to protect against radioactive iodine exposure.
8. Transportation: Store the NBC kit separately from your primary bug-out bag, and if using a vehicle, keep it in the car for quick access during an emergency.

Notes:
• If you live within 50 miles of a nuclear power plant or are at risk of CBRN threats, this kit is essential.
• Ensure all equipment is regularly inspected and replaced as needed (e.g., filters have a shelf life).
NBC CBRN, gas mask, filter, protective clothing, radiation detection, potassium iodide, emergency kit
Hydration System Integration with Gas Mask
High Priority Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Objective: Ensure hydration is possible while wearing a gas mask during an NBC CBRN emergency.

Procedure:
1. Select a Compatible Mask: Choose a gas mask that includes a pre-installed hydration system, such as the MIRA Safety CM-6M.
2....
Objective: Ensure hydration is possible while wearing a gas mask during an NBC CBRN emergency.

Procedure:
1. Select a Compatible Mask: Choose a gas mask that includes a pre-installed hydration system, such as the MIRA Safety CM-6M.
2. Attach Hydration System: If your mask does not have a built-in hydration system, attach a compatible drinking tube and reservoir to the mask's intake valve or side ports.
3. Secure Connections: Ensure all connections are tight and leak-proof to prevent water from escaping during use.
4. Test System: Before an emergency, test the hydration system by filling it with water and checking for leaks or blockages.
5. Use During Emergency: In a CBRN event, use the hydration system without removing your mask to avoid exposure to contaminants.

Notes:
• A hydration system is critical in scenarios where you may not be able to remove your gas mask frequently.
• Regularly inspect and maintain the hydration system to ensure it functions properly during an emergency.
hydration system, gas mask, NBC CBRN, water access

Protection Equipment (1) -

Verify Protective Mask Appropriateness for NBC Agents
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Ensure that the protective mask being used is appropriate for the situation.

• Military protective masks may not protect against toxic industrial chemicals.
• Civilian protective masks used in industrial hygiene and hazardous waste operations...
Ensure that the protective mask being used is appropriate for the situation.

• Military protective masks may not protect against toxic industrial chemicals.
• Civilian protective masks used in industrial hygiene and hazardous waste operations may not protect against military NBC agents.
• Consult USACHPPM (U.S. Army Center for Health Promotion and Preventive Medicine) or SBCCOM (Soldier and Biological Chemical Command) to assist in selecting the proper mask.
protective mask,NBC agents,USACHPPM,SBCCOM

Nuclear Effects Prediction (1) -

Determine Casualty Prediction Radii for Nuclear Detonation Effects
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
### Procedure: Determine Casualty Prediction Radii for Nuclear Detonation Effects

Use the following tables to estimate casualty prediction radii based on nuclear weapon yield:

#### Table 2-A: Radii of Effects in Kilometers versus Weapons...
### Procedure: Determine Casualty Prediction Radii for Nuclear Detonation Effects

Use the following tables to estimate casualty prediction radii based on nuclear weapon yield:

#### Table 2-A: Radii of Effects in Kilometers versus Weapons Yield

|Effect|1 KT|20 KT|100 KT|1 MT|10 MT|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|Nuclear Radiation (1,000 cGy)|0.71|1.3|1.6|2.3|3.7|
|Blast (50% incidence of translation with subsequent impact with a non-yielding surface)|0.28|1.0|1.4|3.8|11.7|
|Thermal (50% incidence of 2nd-degree burns to bare skin, 10 km visibility)|0.77|1.8|3.2|4.8|14.5|

#### Table 2-B: Ranges for Probabilities of Injuries from Flying Debris

|YIELD (KT)|Range for given probability of serious injury in km|||
||1% Probability of serious injury|50% Probability of serious injury|99% Probability of serious injury|
|---|---|---|---|
|1|0.28|0.22|0.17|
|10|0.73|0.57|0.44|
|20|0.98|0.76|0.58|
|50|1.4|1.1|0.84|
|100|1.9|1.5|1.1|
|200|2.5|1.9|1.5|
|500|3.6|2.7|2.1|
|1000|4.8|3.6|2.7|

#### Table 2-C: Ranges for Translational Injuries for Different Yield Weapons

|YIELD (KT)|Range in km for given probability of blunt injuries & fractures|||Range in km for given probability of fatal injuries||
||>1%|50%|99%|>1%|50%|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|1|0.38|0.27|0.19|0.27|0.19|
|10|1.0|0.75|0.53|0.75|0.53|
|20|1.3|0.99|0.71|0.99|0.71|
|50|1.9|1.4|1.0|1.4|1.0|
|100|2.5|1.9|1.4|1.9|1.4|
|200|3.2|2.5|1.9|2.5|1.9|
|500|4.6|3.6|2.7|3.6|2.7|
|1000|5.9|4.8|3.6|4.8|3.6|

Note: Data accounts for ground friction and considers only prone personnel.

#### Table 2-D: Probability of Radiation or Thermal Burns

|YIELD OF WEAPON|1 KT|10 KT|100 KT|1 MT|10 MT|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|Range (km) for production of second-degree burns on exposed skin|0.78|2.1|4.8|9.1|14.5|
|Duration of thermal pulse in seconds|0.12|0.32|0.9|2.4|6.4|

Reference: Tables 2-1, 2-3, 4-IV, 4-V, and 2-7 from FM 8-10-7 and FM 8-9 (Part I).
nuclear detonation,casualty prediction,blast radius,thermal burns,nuclear radiation

Shelter Management (1) -

Implementing Shelter Management Functions in Fallout Shelters
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Steps to Implement Shelter Management Functions:

1. Develop a system: Create an effective system for managing the shelter, especially if it is used by medical units.
2. Separate responsibilities:
- Assign shelter management functions...
Steps to Implement Shelter Management Functions:

1. Develop a system: Create an effective system for managing the shelter, especially if it is used by medical units.
2. Separate responsibilities:
- Assign shelter management functions separately from patient care duties.
3. Ensure essential functions:
- Radiological monitoring of personnel and areas.
- Provide safe food and water supplies.
- Monitor water storage facilities to prevent leakage and contamination.
- Arrange sleeping accommodations for all individuals.
- Control fire hazards within the shelter area.
- Enforce health and sanitation rules strictly.
- Implement a waste disposal plan.
4. Prepare in advance: Develop shelter management plans before occupying shelters, ensuring that all personnel are familiar with them.

Notes:
• Effective management is critical to maintaining order and safety during fallout situations.
• All personnel must be trained on their roles within the shelter system.
shelter management,nuclear fallout,emergency planning

Radiation Decay Estimation (1) -

Using the 7:10 Rule for Residual Radiation Decay Estimation
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
The 7:10 Rule for Residual Radiation Decay:

|t – Time after detonation (hr)|Dose rate at time t divided by the dose rate at 1 hour|
|---|---|
|1|-|
|7|0.1|
|49|0.01|
|343|0.001|

Notes:
• This rule assumes a single nuclear detonation.
• It...
The 7:10 Rule for Residual Radiation Decay:

|t – Time after detonation (hr)|Dose rate at time t divided by the dose rate at 1 hour|
|---|---|
|1|-|
|7|0.1|
|49|0.01|
|343|0.001|

Notes:
• This rule assumes a single nuclear detonation.
• It is used to estimate the residual radiation dose rate at any given time after a nuclear explosion.
• The dose rate decreases by a factor of 10 every 7 hours.
radiation decay,7:10 rule,nuclear fallout

Determining Radii of Vulnerability for Equipment in Nuclear Detonations
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Radii of Vulnerability for Equipment (distance in meters):

|Yield (KT)|Moderate Damage|Moderate Damage|Moderate Damage|Moderate Damage|Severe Damage|Severe Damage|Severe Damage|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
||Wheeled...
Radii of Vulnerability for Equipment (distance in meters):

|Yield (KT)|Moderate Damage|Moderate Damage|Moderate Damage|Moderate Damage|Severe Damage|Severe Damage|Severe Damage|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
||Wheeled vehicles|Tanks|Towed|Supply|Randomly Parked Helicopters|Cargo & Transport|Light Observation|
||Exposed|Shielded|Arty|Depot|Cargo & Transport|Light Observation|
|0.1|200|150|100|100|100|400|500|
|1|400|350|300|250|250|700|1,100|
|3|600|500|500|400|450|1,000|1,600|
|6|700|600|600|500|500|1,200|1,900|
|10|800|700|700|600|600|1,500|2,500|
|20|1,000|900|900|800|800|1,900|3,400|
|30|1,200|1,100|1,000|900|950|2,200|3,700|
|50|1,700|1,500|1,200|1,200|1,400|2,700|4,500|
|100|2,200|1,900|1,300|1,300|1,700|3,200|5,700|
|200|2,600|2,000|1,500|1,500|1,900|3,700|6,200|
|300|3,000|2,100|1,600|1,600|2,000|3,800|7,100|

Notes:
• These radii are distances at which moderate or severe damage is expected for different types of equipment.
• The same notes apply as in the previous table regarding the use of FM 1.1-31-2(S) for accurate calculations.
equipment vulnerability,nuclear detonation,moderate damage

Nuclear Burst Types (1) -

Understand Types of Nuclear Bursts and Their Effects
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Types of Bursts and Their Effects:

A. Airburst:
• Detonated in air at an altitude below 30 km but high enough that the fireball does not contact the surface.
• Causes burns to exposed skin over many square kilometers and eye injuries over a...
Types of Bursts and Their Effects:

A. Airburst:
• Detonated in air at an altitude below 30 km but high enough that the fireball does not contact the surface.
• Causes burns to exposed skin over many square kilometers and eye injuries over a larger area.
• Initial radiation is a significant hazard with smaller weapons, but fallout can be ignored.
• Fission products are dispersed globally unless local rainfall causes localized fallout.
• A small area of neutron-induced activity may be hazardous near ground zero.

B. Surface Burst:
• Detonated on or slightly above the surface so that the fireball touches the land or water.
• Blast, thermal radiation, and initial nuclear radiation effects are less extensive than airbursts except at ground zero where destruction is concentrated.
• Local fallout can be a hazard over a larger downwind area than blast and thermal radiation.

C. Subsurface Burst:
• Detonated beneath the surface of land or water.
• Causes cratering similar to surface bursts.
• If the burst does not penetrate the surface, only ground or water shock is present.
• If it penetrates, blast, thermal, and initial radiation effects are less than in a surface burst but still significant.
• Local fallout will be very heavy if penetration occurs.

D. High Altitude Burst:
• Detonated above 30 km where initial soft x-rays dissipate energy as heat in a large volume of air molecules.
• Fireball is larger and expands rapidly, causing significant ionization of the upper atmosphere and disruption in communications.
• Generates an intense electromagnetic pulse (EMP) that can degrade or destroy electronic equipment.
• No known biological effects from EMP, but indirect effects may occur due to failure of critical medical equipment.

E. Enhanced Radiation (ER) Weapons:
• Neutrons and x-rays constitute a substantial portion of the total energy released.
• Energy distribution: 30% blast; 20% thermal radiation; 45% initial radiation; 5% residual radiation.
• A 3-kiloton ER weapon produces nuclear radiation equivalent to a 10-kiloton fission weapon and blast/thermal effects of a 1-kiloton fission device.
airburst, surface burst, subsurface burst, high altitude burst, enhanced radiation weapon, nuclear effects

Nuclear Blast Estimation (1) -

Estimate Nuclear Yield Using Illumination Time
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Determine the illumination time:
- Close your eyes and start counting when the nuclear burst occurs.
- Stop counting when the intense light begins to fade.
- This is the illumination time in seconds.

2. **Use Figure...
Procedure:

1. Determine the illumination time:
- Close your eyes and start counting when the nuclear burst occurs.
- Stop counting when the intense light begins to fade.
- This is the illumination time in seconds.

2. Use Figure 2-D (Yield Estimates vs. Illumination Time):
- Locate the illumination time on the horizontal axis of the graph.
- Find the corresponding yield estimate (in kilotons, kT) on the vertical axis.
- This provides an approximate yield estimation with an accuracy within a factor-of-ten.

Important Notes:
• Do not look directly at the fireball. This can cause permanent eye damage.
• If you are in a foxhole, you may look at the floor of the foxhole to observe the fading light.
• The counting procedure is similar to flash-to-bang time estimation.

Reference:
• Figure 2-D: Yield Estimates (KTs) for Illumination Times (seconds)
• Table 3-1 from FM 3-3-1
nuclear yield, illumination time, nuclear burst estimation, radiation exposure, nuclear blast

Radiological Dispersal Weapons (1) -

Identify Potential Sources of Radiological Dispersal Weapons (RDWs)
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Recognize common sources of radioactive material that could be used to create RDWs:
- Hospital radiation therapy equipment containing Cobalt-60 or Cesium-137.
- Nuclear power fuel rods with Uranium-235 or Plutonium.
-...
Procedure:

1. Recognize common sources of radioactive material that could be used to create RDWs:
- Hospital radiation therapy equipment containing Cobalt-60 or Cesium-137.
- Nuclear power fuel rods with Uranium-235 or Plutonium.
- University and laboratory facilities storing radioactive materials such as Cobalt-60, Cesium-137, Iridium-192, or Radium-226.
- Radiography and gauging equipment using Cobalt-60, Cesium-137, Iridium-192, or Radium-226.

2. Be aware of historical examples:
- The Iraqis and Russian separatists in Chechnya have demonstrated practical knowledge of RDWs.

3. Understand future trends: As more countries pursue nuclear power (and weapons) programs, the availability of material to make RDWs will increase.
radiological dispersal weapons,RDWs,radioactive material sources,Cobalt-60,Cesium-137,Uranium-235,Plutonium,Iridium-192,Radium-226

Radiation Safety Procedures (1) -

Report Incidents Involving Radioactive Sources
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Purpose: Ensure proper reporting of incidents involving radioactive sources to maintain safety and compliance.

Procedure:
1. Any incident involving radioactive sources must be reported immediately to the unit’s Radiation Safety Officer...
Purpose: Ensure proper reporting of incidents involving radioactive sources to maintain safety and compliance.

Procedure:
1. Any incident involving radioactive sources must be reported immediately to the unit’s Radiation Safety Officer (RSO).
2. The RSO should inform the license manager about the incident as soon as possible.
3. Document all details of the incident, including time, location, type of source involved, and any injuries or damage caused.
4. Follow up with the appropriate authorities to ensure proper handling and investigation of the incident.

Notes:
• Prompt reporting is essential to prevent further exposure and ensure compliance with safety regulations.
• All personnel should be trained on how to report such incidents.
incident reporting, radiation safety officer, radioactive sources

Radioactive Waste Disposal (1) -

Dispose of Low-Level Radioactive Material in the US Army
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Purpose: Ensure safe and legal disposal of low-level radioactive material in accordance with federal and state regulations.

Procedure:
1. Contact the Industrial Operations Command (IOC) for information on the proper disposal of unwanted,...
Purpose: Ensure safe and legal disposal of low-level radioactive material in accordance with federal and state regulations.

Procedure:
1. Contact the Industrial Operations Command (IOC) for information on the proper disposal of unwanted, low-level radioactive materials.
2. The IOC’s Radioactive Waste Disposal office (AMSMC-RW) is responsible for managing this process.
3. AMSMC-RW provides guidance to all US Army “generators” of unwanted radioactive material.
4. Generators include DS maintenance shops and depots, among others.
5. Ensure that the post RSO or Safety Officer is made aware of any radioactive waste being generated or stored.
6. Follow all procedures outlined by AMSMC-RW to ensure compliance with regulations for transport and burial of radioactive material.

Notes:
• Proper disposal prevents environmental contamination and ensures legal compliance.
• All personnel involved in handling radioactive materials should be trained on proper disposal protocols.
disposal, low-level radioactive material, AMSMC-RW, IOC

Tritium Handling (1) -

Handle and Maintain Fire Control Devices with Tritium Sources
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Purpose: Ensure safe handling and maintenance of fire control devices containing tritium sources to prevent exposure and damage.

Procedure:
1. Identify fire control devices that contain tritium, such as collimators and aiming lights on...
Purpose: Ensure safe handling and maintenance of fire control devices containing tritium sources to prevent exposure and damage.

Procedure:
1. Identify fire control devices that contain tritium, such as collimators and aiming lights on howitzers, mortars, and tanks.
2. Ensure all equipment with tritium sources is clearly labeled with warning signs visible from several meters away.
3. Do not attempt to purge or repair any device containing a tritium source if there is suspicion that the Pyrex tube has been broken or is leaking.
4. If a fire control device fails to illuminate, it may indicate damage to the tritium source and should be reported immediately to the RSO or Safety Officer.
5. Avoid using high-pressure purging kits on devices with tritium sources, as this can cause breakage of the Pyrex tube and release of radioactive material.
6. If any damage is suspected, do not attempt maintenance; instead, report it for proper handling by trained personnel.

Notes:
• Tritium is a low-energy beta emitter that cannot penetrate intact Pyrex tubes but becomes hazardous if the tube breaks.
• Accidents involving tritium sources have resulted in exposure levels exceeding 1.5 cGy.
fire control devices, tritium sources, Pyrex tube, maintenance procedures

Depleted Uranium Handling (1) -

Identify and Handle Depleted Uranium (DU) Munitions
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Purpose: Identify DU munitions and ensure safe handling to prevent exposure and contamination.

Procedure:
1. Recognize that DU is used in armor-piercing projectiles, such as those fired by M1A1 tanks, Bradley Fighting vehicles, AV-8B...
Purpose: Identify DU munitions and ensure safe handling to prevent exposure and contamination.

Procedure:
1. Recognize that DU is used in armor-piercing projectiles, such as those fired by M1A1 tanks, Bradley Fighting vehicles, AV-8B Harriers, and A-10 aircraft.
2. Identify DU-containing items:
- M1A1 and M1A2 tanks have DU packets molded into turret armor (indicated by a “U” at the end of the serial number).
- DU munitions are typically black with white markings on the projectile end, though corrosion may alter this appearance.
3. Ensure that all DU munitions are only used on approved ranges licensed by the NRC or host nations.
4. Avoid handling DU munitions without proper training and protective equipment.
5. If exposed to DU fragments or dust, report immediately to the RSO or Safety Officer for decontamination procedures.

Notes:
• DU is not used in training exercises.
• Peacetime firings are prohibited except on approved ranges.
DU munitions, identification, armor, handling procedures

Biological Hazards (1) -

Biological Hazard Prediction and NBC Reports
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Biological Hazard Prediction and NBC Reports

A. General: Downwind hazard prediction for biological agents is very similar to procedures for chemical agents. The resulting prediction provides a general, minimal estimate of danger zones before...
Biological Hazard Prediction and NBC Reports

A. General: Downwind hazard prediction for biological agents is very similar to procedures for chemical agents. The resulting prediction provides a general, minimal estimate of danger zones before actual agent has been identified, and before sampling is done to further identify areas affected.

B. Warning Reports: The NBC Center (NBCC) will issue an NBC 3 chemical / biological report to alert units in the immediate downwind hazard area. Each unit evaluates the report and distributes it to subordinate units that might be affected. This warning will be adequate for the first 1-5 hours (depending on wind speed), and will relate to about half of Zone I of a simplified biological hazard prediction. (Zone I is the area for which more than 20-30% casualties are predicted. Casualty prediction in Zone II is 20-30%, decreasing to 2-3%.). Units in Zones I and II should receive NBCC biological reports for adequate warning.

C. Hazard Prediction: All attacks in daytime and all toxin attacks are presumed to have a period of 8 hours in which the agent cloud retains its effectiveness. For nighttime attacks, the cloud effectiveness may be longer, and should be calculated by NBCC. Further effects of time and weather conditions on the persistence of biological agents are discussed in FM 8-9, FM 3-3, Chap. 4, and FM 3-6, Appendix B, (Draft, 1997).

D. The Maximum Downwind Hazard (MDWHD) for a biological attack may be calculated by multiplying the windspeed (in km per hr) times four, times the duration of effective agent cloud. For example, for a windspeed of 5 km/hr, and an effective cloud duration of 8 hours, the MDWHD = 4 × 5 × 8 = 160 km. Hazard area predictions become less reliable as distance and time from the point of attack increase.
biological hazard, NBC report, MDWHD calculation, downwind prediction

Biological Contamination (1) -

Biological Contamination of Food and Water
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Biological Contamination of Food and Water

Toxins and microorganisms will probably be the primary form of contamination of food and water. It is unlikely that a biological agent will materially affect the appearance, taste or smell enough for...
Biological Contamination of Food and Water

Toxins and microorganisms will probably be the primary form of contamination of food and water. It is unlikely that a biological agent will materially affect the appearance, taste or smell enough for the change to be apparent. The normal packaging and packing of food provides protection against most biological agents. Decontamination procedures are only necessary for spore-forming agents such as anthrax. If the agent is unknown, decontamination is advised. Operational rations are packaged in metal containers or aluminum laminated plastics that can withstand boiling and are resistant to arthropod penetration, thus making them highly resistant to biological agents. Food stored in freezers, refrigerators, and in refrigerated trucks or rail cars will be safe if the containers remain sealed until the outer surfaces are decontaminated. The use of unpackaged items should be restricted. Use only operational rations. Unprotected food in the open and close to the source will become contaminated. The inspection and monitoring of food and water is absolutely essential for rapid identification. Samples of suspect food must be sent to the supporting laboratory (in theater) and will be confirmed by CONUS laboratory evaluation. No field expedient method exists for the identification of food suspected of being contaminated with a biological agent.
food contamination, water contamination, decontamination procedures, operational rations

Medical Support (1) -

Medical Support Considerations for Biological Attacks
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Medical Support Considerations for Biological Attacks

A. General: The medical management of casualties resulting from the use of BW agents is a problem of managing large numbers of individuals with infectious disease or exposure to toxins....
Medical Support Considerations for Biological Attacks

A. General: The medical management of casualties resulting from the use of BW agents is a problem of managing large numbers of individuals with infectious disease or exposure to toxins. Medical units will require augmentation to decontaminate incoming casualties. The fact that the source of the exposure may have been artificially created by deliberate, hostile means will not change the basic principles of treatment. For instance, in the event of a biological attack, the most important factor in providing operationally relevant information and adequate medical management will be the rapid establishment of an accurate, specific identification of the agent. In contrast to naturally occurring epidemics in which the disease incidence increases over a period of weeks or months, an artificially induced epidemic will peak in a few hours or days. Since a biological attack may be silent or nearly so, the first indication of a problem may well be the appearance of a wave of casualties in which medical personnel are unable to differentiate natural disease epidemics from covert enemy attacks. Onset of illness following exposure to toxic agents may range from minutes to as long as 3 weeks. Some potential BW agents are transmissible among humans, so spread after the initial attack may become an important planning consideration. An increasing casualty load is anticipated with relatively few initial casualties and a greater number over successive hours or days until a peak is reached. An exception to this aspect would be an attack with a biological toxin that might create an immediate and dramatic mass casualty situation.

B. Preventive Medicine Principles: In a BW environment, preventive medicine (PM) services will be in great demand. PM personnel must assist the commander in determining the health hazards associated with BW contamination, such as safe food and water sources, and in determining when to use prophylaxis, immunization, and other preventive measures associated with BW warfare. The tendency of individuals in an emergency situation to become careless regarding food and water sanitation, general hygiene, and other common disease control measures could be a significant cause of secondary spread of disease. The problems of maintaining safer water and food conditions will differ for military personnel deployed throughout the operations area and for the civilian.

C. Impact on Medical Facilities: BW agents are capable of producing mass casualties. In the first few hours after a BW attack, medical facilities can be swamped with casualties who may require lengthy hospitalization. At the same time the patient load is increasing, the factors combine to complicate Health Service Support (HSS) operations. Operations in MOPP gear reduce individual and collective efficiency at a time when manpower requirements increase. Patient decontamination requires manpower, and will reduce the number of personnel available to treat casualties. Heat stress in MOPP will require more frequent rest breaks, further reducing care capability. Establishing and maintaining a facility with collective protection support and continuously monitoring the air inside the shelter for contaminants calls for additional personnel. These procedures decrease the ability to treat patients as efficiently and effectively.

D. Impact on HSS: Immediately obtaining very large amounts of the appropriate treatment (if any) is critical. This requires informing the chain of command before absolute confirmation of a BW attack can be made. The number of soldiers believing they are ill will likely be much larger than those actually exposed. Commanders, soldiers, host nation leaders and populations and national leaders will immediately ask for more information than will be available.

E. Oxygen production and resupply: Production of liquid and gaseous oxygen normally will not occur in an NBC contaminated environment. Although newer production plants have been designed for potential operation in such a state, generally production will be stopped until air quality improves. Product transfer operations (gaseous and liquid) will also be curtailed unless medical and flight line requirements demand such. Supply and medical units must develop plans to resupply critical gases and cryogenic liquids in the contaminated area from primary and alternate sources. These sources include production and storage capabilities organic to the unit from other services, from host nation support, and from commercial contracts.

F. Special Medical Augmentation for Operations in an NBC Contaminated Environment: Decontamination operations are extremely resource intensive. It is unrealistic to expect that medical personnel will be able to manage both medical treatment and decontamination of patients. For this reason, plans must address how decontamination will be accomplished. Augmentation to provide decontamination support must also be addressed in planning.
biological attack, medical management, HSS impact, oxygen resupply

Logistics Planning (1) -

Logistics Planning for BW Attacks
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Logistics Planning for BW Attacks

A. General: Actions to be taken by field units at the platoon, company, and battalion levels are given, in the form of checklists, for a number of possible BW scenarios in FM 3-3, Appendix B. See also Figure...
Logistics Planning for BW Attacks

A. General: Actions to be taken by field units at the platoon, company, and battalion levels are given, in the form of checklists, for a number of possible BW scenarios in FM 3-3, Appendix B. See also Figure 4-A and Table 4-A in this chapter.

B. Logistics Planning: A BW attack would result in an increased use in the supplies (Table 4-D).

Table 4-D: Supply Increase during a BW Attack
|Laboratory Supplies (e.g. sample collection containers, diagnostic test, shipping supplies)|Sample Transport Assets (boxes, ice, vehicles, personnel)|
|---|---|
|NBC respirator, suits, gloves, and boots|Casualty wraps for patients (to protect against exposure)|
|Filter blower units to provide overpressure protection|Air filters to seal off building air entrance mechanisms|
|Decontamination Supplies (e.g. water, extra clothing, disinfectants)|General Medical Supplies (e.g. IV fluids & supplies)|
|Α100mile/hr≅tape for sealing windows/doors|Collective protection units with airlocks|
|Rodent control supplies (check with PM units)|Field sanitation supplies (check with PM units)|
|Vaccines|Antibiotics|
|Liquid and Gaseous Oxygen|Evacuation Assets|
|Patient Respirators|Water|
|Extra Personnel||
BW attack logistics, supply increase, decontamination supplies

Protection Measures (1) -

Individual and Collective Protection in Biologically Contaminated Environments
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Individual and Collective Protection in Biologically Contaminated Environments

A. Individual Protection: The NBC respirator, suit, and gloves will provide protection against most biological agent attacks delivered by the aerosol route....
Individual and Collective Protection in Biologically Contaminated Environments

A. Individual Protection: The NBC respirator, suit, and gloves will provide protection against most biological agent attacks delivered by the aerosol route. Currently, fielded respirators equipped with standard NBC filter canisters will protect the respiratory system against particles greater than 1-1.5 micron in size. While the MOPP suits employed against chemical agents will also protect against biological agents, even standard uniform clothing of good quality affords reasonable protection against dermal exposure to biological agents. Casualties unable to wear MOPP should be in casualty wraps designed to protect the patient against chemical or biological agent exposure. The addition of a filter blower unit, if available, provides air overpressure to enhance protection and cooling. Individual Protective Equipment is discussed more fully in FM 3-4. Most BW agents are only inhalation, water, or a food hazard. While the BW agent may not contaminate the environment, the potential for repeated attacks may require maintaining an increased protective posture. It is important that overprotection (MOPP 4) not be maintained longer than necessary. Overprotection will greatly reduce operational effectiveness.

B. Collective Protection: A dedicated hardened or unhardened shelter, equipped with an air filtration unit providing overpressure, can offer collective protection for personnel in biologically contaminated environments. An airlock to the unit ensures that no contamination will be brought into the shelter. Casualties and contaminated personnel must be decontaminated prior to entering the unit. In the absence of a dedicated structure, enhanced protection can be afforded within most buildings by sealing cracks and entry ports, and providing air filtration within existing ventilation systems. Due to the requirement to continue operations in a contaminated environment, most medical treatment will likely take place in a collective protection unit. These units provide the most effective method for protecting patients and ensuring continued medical capability in a contaminated environment. Patients whose illness is thought to be the result of a biological attack, or those who are thought to have a contagious infectious disease, will necessarily be cared for using barrier nursing techniques while inside a collective protection system. Collective protective systems are further discussed in FM 3-4, Chapter 6.
NBC respirator, MOPP suits, collective protection, decontamination

Sanitation Measures (1) -

Sanitation in Biological Operations Environments
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Sanitation in Biological Operations Environments

The importance of effective hygiene and sanitation in a biological operations environment cannot be over-emphasized. Everyone is responsible to ensure that standards of hygiene are maintained...
Sanitation in Biological Operations Environments

The importance of effective hygiene and sanitation in a biological operations environment cannot be over-emphasized. Everyone is responsible to ensure that standards of hygiene are maintained even in the most difficult circumstances. Personal hygiene measures such as: frequent and adequate washing with soap and water, regular changes with laundered clothing, use of liberally disinfected toilets and field latrines (as opposed to cat-scratch methods), and hand-washing after latrine use should be mandatory and emphasized by commanders. Since mechanical means or natural vectors may spread biological agents, effective control of rodents and arthropods is also a sanitation priority.
hygiene standards, rodent control, field latrines, sanitation priorities

Radiofrequency Hazards (1) -

Recognize and Identify Radiofrequency Hazards
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
### Recognition and Identification of Radiofrequency Hazards

Introduction:
Use of Radiofrequency (RF) sources in the military is widespread and not necessarily associated with weapons systems. RF radiation is used for communication, target...
### Recognition and Identification of Radiofrequency Hazards

Introduction:
Use of Radiofrequency (RF) sources in the military is widespread and not necessarily associated with weapons systems. RF radiation is used for communication, target detection, navigation, surveillance, imaging, electronic countermeasures, therapeutic medical diathermy, medical monitoring, industrial heating, and food preparation. Although the military is experimenting with high-power sources as potential weapons, the main applications continue to be communications, information gathering, and electronic countermeasures.

Threat:
Radiofrequency radiation has direct (thermal), indirect biological effects (athermal or nonthermal) and potential psychological effects associated with exposure or suspected exposure to individuals.

#### A. Direct Biological Effects
• (1) Thermal: The absorption of energy is the key mechanism by which RF radiation affects living cells directly. Deposition of RF energy into the body increases its thermal load. If the temperature increase is large enough, thermal overload of the body will result. The body’s thermoregulatory system normally responds to this thermal load by transfer of energy to the surrounding environment through convection, evaporation of body water (sweating), heavier breathing, and radiation (primarily IR). If the thermal load is too great for the body to overcome, injury to the body may occur. These injuries are dependent on the frequency of the RF energy absorbed.
- At higher frequencies (>3 GHz), the skin and eyes (cataracts - clouding of the eye lens) are most susceptible.
- At lower frequencies, internal organs are more susceptible due to the penetration depth of the radiofrequency waves.

• (2) RF Shock and Burns: For systems operating at less than 100 MHz, a shock and/or burn potential is also present if an individual is very close to an energized antenna or makes physical contact with the metallic portions of the antenna. The threshold for RF current perception is a function of the frequency, surface area of the contact point, and individual sensitivity.
- If the intensity of the current density is minimal, only a shock may be perceived.
- If higher, an actual RF burn may result.

#### B. Nonthermal Effects
Nonthermal effects have been defined as physiological changes in which the core body temperature is not elevated but a quantity of energy is absorbed sufficient to activate receptors and cause a physiological response. Nonthermal effects included immune and endocrine effects supposedly stimulated by exposure to low intensity RF. Some nonthermal effects reported in the literature have included carcinogenesis, reproductive problems, and increased permeability in the blood-brain barrier and behavioral changes. No conclusive evidence substantiates these reported effects from low-level RF radiation.

#### C. Psychological Effects
Due to a lack of understanding of nonionizing radiation and RF radiation in particular, many individuals also have a fear of working near radiating sources and also erroneously suspect that certain physical ailments have been a result of RF exposure. Headaches, anxiety, nausea, dizziness, fatigue, and sunburn, etc., have frequently been blamed on perceived exposure to RF radiation. Most often these symptoms have actually resulted from dehydration, sleep deprivation, poor ventilation, and high temperature environments.

Recognition:
• If the temperature rise of the body is large enough, due to deposition of energy from the Radiofrequency radiation, thermal overload of the body will result.
• Exposure to radiation levels that are significantly greater than permissible exposure limits will be felt as heat to the body.
- At high frequencies (>3 GHz), skin erythema may occur.
- At lower frequencies less than 3 GHz, nausea may occur as well as internal discomfort.
• Although lenticular cataracts are possible at higher frequencies, the possibility is extremely remote, as an individual would feel extreme heat on his/her face before a cataract could actually form.
• For RF shock, a tingling or startle reaction would be noticeable.
• For an RF burn, a 3rd degree burn to the skin could result from direct contact to an antenna such as a dipole or monopole antenna (element type antenna). Unlike an electrical burn, which would be somewhat contiguous on the skin, an RF burn is concentrated in a very small area of the skin and penetrates deeply such as on a fingertip.
radiofrequency,rf,hazards,thermal effects,nonthermal effects,psychological effects,burns,shock,cataracts

Identification (1) -

Identify NBC Warning Signs
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Chemical warning signs have yellow backgrounds with red lettering.
2. Biological warning signs have blue backgrounds with red lettering.
3. Radiological warning signs have white backgrounds with...
Procedure:

1. Chemical warning signs have yellow backgrounds with red lettering.
2. Biological warning signs have blue backgrounds with red lettering.
3. Radiological warning signs have white backgrounds with black lettering.
4. Chemical minefield signs have red backgrounds with yellow lettering and a stripe.
5. Refer to Appendix C of FM 3-7 for information on Warsaw Pact Markers.
NBC signs, chemical warning signs, biological warning signs, radiological warning signs, minefield signs

Chemical Protection Overgarments (1) -

Use and Maintain Chemical Overgarment-84 (OG-84)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Inspect the overgarment: Ensure it is a two-piece set consisting of one coat and one pair of trousers in camouflage color (woodland or desert).
2. Check for damage: Look for tears, holes, or other defects that may...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the overgarment: Ensure it is a two-piece set consisting of one coat and one pair of trousers in camouflage color (woodland or desert).
2. Check for damage: Look for tears, holes, or other defects that may compromise its protective qualities.
3. Store properly: When not in use, store the overgarment in its vapor-barrier bag to maintain its integrity and extend its service life.
4. Wear when exposed: The OG-84 provides protection against chemical agent vapors, liquid droplets, biological agents, toxins, and radioactive alpha contamination.
5. Use for minimum duration: When removed from its sealed vapor bag, it provides a minimum of 30 days of protection if not exposed to chemical agents. If exposed, it provides at least 24 hours of protection against liquid or vapor chemical agents.
OG-84 overgarment, chemical protection, biological protection, radioactive contamination

Protection Systems (1) -

Deploying the Portable Collective Protection System (PCPS)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Objective: Deploy the PCPS to provide protection from chemical, biological, and radiological agents in a contaminated environment.

Materials Required:
• Protective shelter (tent and fly)
• Support kit
• Hermetically sealed filter canister
-...
Objective: Deploy the PCPS to provide protection from chemical, biological, and radiological agents in a contaminated environment.

Materials Required:
• Protective shelter (tent and fly)
• Support kit
• Hermetically sealed filter canister
• Airlock system

Procedure:
1. Select a Deployment Site: Choose a flat, stable area away from potential contamination sources.
2. Assemble the Shelter Structure:
- Unpack the tent and fly made of saranaex composite material.
- Set up the aluminum support structure to stabilize the tent.
3. Install the Filter Canister:
- Attach the hermetically sealed filter canister to the shelter's air intake system.
4. Deploy the Airlock System:
- Ensure the airlock is properly connected and functional for decontaminating personnel entering the shelter.
5. Activate Overpressure System:
- Turn on the overpressure mechanism to ensure positive pressure within the shelter, preventing agent penetration from outside.
6. Verify Functionality:
- Check that all systems (filter canister, airlock, and overpressure) are operational.
7. Use as Command or Relief Facility:
- The PCPS can accommodate up to 14 people at a time for command and control operations or rest and relief in contaminated environments.

Notes:
• Ensure the shelter is fully sealed before activation.
• Regularly inspect filter canisters for damage or blockage.
• Personnel entering must use the airlock system to prevent contamination.
PCPS deployment, chemical protection, biological protection, radiological protection, overpressure system, airlock decontamination

Chemical Biological Incident Response (1) -

Contact CBIRF for Chemical/Biological Incident Response
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Public Affairs Contact: Call 910-451-8118 for public affairs or 910-451-9093 for operations.
2. The Marine CBIRF is a strategic organization: manned, trained and equipped to counter the growing chemical/biological terrorist...
Procedure:

1. Public Affairs Contact: Call 910-451-8118 for public affairs or 910-451-9093 for operations.
2. The Marine CBIRF is a strategic organization: manned, trained and equipped to counter the growing chemical/biological terrorist threat. This response force will respond to chemical or biological incidents worldwide, when directed by the National Command Authority, to assist local civilian and military agencies in order to assist the on-scene commander in providing initial post incident consequence management.
3. The CBIRF deploys to incident locations by the most expeditious means possible, where they will:
- Coordinate initial relief efforts
- Provide security and area isolation at the affected site
- Conduct detection, identification and decontamination
- Provide expert medical advice and assistance to local medical authorities
- Provide service support assistance as required.
4. The CBIRF’s home base is Camp Lejeune, N.C.
CBIRF, chemical incident response, biological incident response, military emergency response

Hazardous Material Response (1) -

Contact Tech Escort for Hazardous Material Response
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

2. The United States Army Technical Escort Unit provides...
Procedure:

1. Public Affairs Contact: Call the United States Army Chemical Biological Defense Command at (410) 671-4345; EPA at (214) 665-6783; or EPA Headquarters at (202) 260-5589.
2. The United States Army Technical Escort Unit provides the Department of Defense and other federal agencies with a unique, immediate response capability for:
- Escorting hazardous materials
- Rendering-safe hazardous materials
- Disposing of hazardous materials
- Sampling hazardous materials
- Verifying hazardous materials
- Mitigating hazards from hazardous materials
- Identifying weaponized and nonweaponized chemical, biological and hazardous material.
Tech Escort, hazardous material response, chemical disposal, biological identification

4. JNACC is the central agency for:

- 20 personnel
- Expandable ISO shelter
- Alpha/beta counting system
- Liquid scintillation
- Multichannel analyzer
6. RADCOM is under CECOM, which is under AMC.
RADCON, radiological incident response, Army nuclear support, Broken Arrow follow-on

Purpose: The Industrial Operations Command (IOC) is responsible for the safe disposal of unwanted, low-level radioactive material in the US Army. Reference: WRAIR’s Web Site: www.wrair.army.mil

Telephone Number:
• Commercial: (202) 782-7580

Mission: The WRAIR’s goal is to prevent illnesses and injuries, enhance human performance, and improve survivability on the battlefield. It has produced many safe and effective drugs and vaccines, medical devices, diagnostic tests, and other much needed medical products.

Additional Information:
• US Army Medical Research Detachment of WRAIR is located at Brooks Air Force Base, San Antonio, TX. The detachment can provide expertise in the medical effects, symptoms and treatment of both laser and radiofrequency injuries.

Regulatory Authorities (1) -

Access Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) Resources
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Visit the NRC website at [www.nrc.gov](http://www.nrc.gov) for information on nuclear safety and regulation.
2. Contact the Nuclear Regulatory Commission directly by phone at 301-415-5385 for inquiries related to nuclear...
Procedure:

1. Visit the NRC website at [www.nrc.gov](http://www.nrc.gov) for information on nuclear safety and regulation.
2. Contact the Nuclear Regulatory Commission directly by phone at 301-415-5385 for inquiries related to nuclear energy, radiation safety, or emergency response protocols.
3. Use the official address: Nuclear Regulatory Commission, 11555 Rockville Pike, Rockville, MD 20852-2738 for written correspondence.

Purpose: To access regulatory and safety information related to nuclear energy and radiation from a federal oversight agency.
nrc, nuclear safety, regulatory information, radiation control

Military Protection (1) -

Understand SBCCOM (Soldier and Biological Chemical Command)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
SBCCOM stands for Soldier and Biological Chemical Command, which is responsible for managing biological and chemical threats to military personnel and providing protective measures against such hazards.
sbccom,biological chemical command,military protection,hazard management

Chemical Operations (1) -

Chemical and Nuclear Operations in Cold Weather Conditions
Critical Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. The principles of employment of chemical agents and defense against them in northern latitudes are the same as for temperate zones.
2. Application of these principles during low-temperature operations (below -18°C) must be based...
Procedure:

1. The principles of employment of chemical agents and defense against them in northern latitudes are the same as for temperate zones.
2. Application of these principles during low-temperature operations (below -18°C) must be based on a thorough understanding of the peculiar characteristics of the area of operation, the organization and tactics of opposing forces, and the technical limitations of chemical agents.
3. Low temperatures adversely affect the casualty-producing characteristics of most toxic chemical agents, as they reduce the ability to vaporize or convert them into aerosol forms.
4. The lower the temperature, the more difficult it is to vaporize a given chemical agent, which may limit its effectiveness in causing casualties through respiratory exposure.
5. Delays in taking protective measures due to cold weather can result in prolonged exposure to chemical agents compared to what might be expected in temperate climates.
6. Nerve, blood, and blister agents can produce casualties if ingested through contaminated food or water.
7. Blister agents are generally non-effective at low temperatures due to their high freezing point.
8. However, chemical agents on frozen ground can become a greater hazard when the ground warms up—potentially even months after initial contamination.
cold weather,chemical agents,nuclear operations,toxic exposure,blister agent

Cold Weather Nuclear Operations (1) -

Consider Terrain Effects on Nuclear Operations in Northern Areas
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Assess the limited road network in northern areas and ensure good cross-country mobility is available to mitigate the effects of nuclear radiation on operations.
2. Recognize that unfrozen muskeg and tundra provide an average...
Procedure:

1. Assess the limited road network in northern areas and ensure good cross-country mobility is available to mitigate the effects of nuclear radiation on operations.
2. Recognize that unfrozen muskeg and tundra provide an average blast-reflecting surface, but when frozen or covered with ice or packed snow, they become effective blast-reflecting surfaces.
3. Understand that newly fallen, loose snow provides a poor reflecting surface and reduces blast effects.

Details:
• Terrain significantly affects the effectiveness of nuclear operations in northern areas.
• Frozen terrain enhances blast reflection, while loose snow diminishes it.
nuclear operations,northern areas,muskeg,tundra,blast reflection

Tactical Operations (1) -

Assess Mission Options in Nuclear Contamination Areas
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
When faced with radioactive contamination, the commander must evaluate three mission options:

1. First Option: Complete the mission in a clean area
- Determine if the mission can be accomplished without entering contaminated zones.
- This...
When faced with radioactive contamination, the commander must evaluate three mission options:

1. First Option: Complete the mission in a clean area
- Determine if the mission can be accomplished without entering contaminated zones.
- This option minimizes risk to personnel and equipment.

2. Second Option: Complete the mission in a contaminated area with increased resources
- Use more soldiers or higher risk levels to accomplish the task faster.
- This may increase exposure but reduces time spent in contamination.

3. Third Option: Complete the mission at the same pace with the same number of soldiers, but delay initiation
- Allow for natural decay of radioactive materials over time before starting the mission.
- This option requires patience and careful planning to ensure safety.
mission options,nuclear contamination,tactical decisions,radioactive areas

Enemy Threat Assessment (1) -

Evaluate Enemy Use of Nuclear Weapons
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
To anticipate enemy actions involving nuclear weapons, commanders must consider:

• Likely scenarios for nuclear use
- Expect nuclear weapons to be used during attacks to create gaps in defensive lines or destroy advancing forces.
- Identify...
To anticipate enemy actions involving nuclear weapons, commanders must consider:

• Likely scenarios for nuclear use
- Expect nuclear weapons to be used during attacks to create gaps in defensive lines or destroy advancing forces.
- Identify potential target areas where the enemy is likely to deploy nuclear devices.

• Impact on mission planning
- Adjust tactics based on expected enemy behavior and nuclear capabilities.
- Prepare for both immediate and long-term effects of nuclear weapons.
enemy tactics,nuclear weapon use,threat assessment

Terrain Analysis (1) -

Analyze Terrain Effects on Nuclear Weapons
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Terrain significantly influences the effects of nuclear weapons:

• Hills and elevated areas
- Limit the area affected by initial blast and thermal effects.
- Disrupt the normal dispersion of fallout, potentially creating localized...
Terrain significantly influences the effects of nuclear weapons:

• Hills and elevated areas
- Limit the area affected by initial blast and thermal effects.
- Disrupt the normal dispersion of fallout, potentially creating localized hotspots.

• Valleys and low areas
- Provide protection from initial nuclear effects (blast and heat).
- May accumulate residual radiation, leading to prolonged contamination risks.

• Consideration in planning
- Use terrain features strategically to minimize exposure during nuclear events.
- Be aware of potential fallout accumulation in low-lying areas.
terrain effects,nuclear weapons,fallout dispersion

Troop Preparedness (1) -

Assess Troop Readiness in a Nuclear Environment
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
The physical and mental condition of troops is critical during nuclear contamination:

• Physical fitness
- Ensure soldiers are physically capable of performing tasks under increased stress and fatigue.
- Monitor for signs of radiation...
The physical and mental condition of troops is critical during nuclear contamination:

• Physical fitness
- Ensure soldiers are physically capable of performing tasks under increased stress and fatigue.
- Monitor for signs of radiation exposure or illness.

• Mental preparedness
- Train personnel to remain calm and focused in high-stress, contaminated environments.
- Encourage resilience and adaptability during prolonged operations.

• Impact on performance
- Poor physical condition can increase vulnerability to radiation effects.
- Mental fatigue may reduce decision-making abilities and response times.
troop readiness,nuclear environment,physical fitness

Time Management (1) -

Plan for Time Constraints in a Nuclear Environment
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Nuclear contamination significantly affects mission timing:

• Increased time requirements
- Reconnaissance and decontamination operations take longer due to added procedures.
- Adjust timelines to account for delays caused by...
Nuclear contamination significantly affects mission timing:

• Increased time requirements
- Reconnaissance and decontamination operations take longer due to added procedures.
- Adjust timelines to account for delays caused by nuclear-related tasks.

• Timing of attacks
- Anticipate that chemical, biological, or nuclear attacks may occur during night hours (early morning or evening).
- Plan for increased vulnerability during these times and adjust patrol schedules accordingly.

• Impact on personnel
- Dazzle and flash blindness from nuclear weapons can severely affect pilots and crewmen.
- Allow additional recovery time for affected personnel before resuming operations.
mission timing,nuclear effects,time constraints

Training Programs (1) -

Implement Training for Nuclear Contamination Scenarios
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Training is essential to ensure soldiers can perform in a nuclear environment:

• Importance of training
- Well-trained troops are more likely to survive and complete missions in contaminated areas.
- Poorly trained personnel may not...
Training is essential to ensure soldiers can perform in a nuclear environment:

• Importance of training
- Well-trained troops are more likely to survive and complete missions in contaminated areas.
- Poorly trained personnel may not recognize the signs of a nuclear attack or know how to respond effectively.

• Training objectives
- Teach soldiers how to identify nuclear threats, assess contamination levels, and take protective measures.
- Prepare troops for increased task durations and modified procedures in contaminated environments.

• Adaptability
- Train personnel to adjust their work rate or procedures based on the situation.
- Encourage flexibility and problem-solving skills during nuclear-related operations.
nuclear training,contaminated battlefield,soldier preparedness

Hazard Identification (1) -

Identify and Locate Nuclear Hazards on the Battlefield
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Identify potential nuclear hazards to avoid or mitigate their effects.

Procedure:
1. Use Intelligence Sources: Gather information from intelligence reports, reconnaissance, and surveillance to identify potential nuclear...
Objective: Identify potential nuclear hazards to avoid or mitigate their effects.

Procedure:
1. Use Intelligence Sources: Gather information from intelligence reports, reconnaissance, and surveillance to identify potential nuclear threats (e.g., enemy weapons, damaged nuclear facilities).
2. Map Hazard Locations: Plot the locations of identified nuclear hazards on the battlefield using maps or digital tools.
3. Assess Impact Areas: Determine the blast radius, radiation zones, and contamination areas associated with each hazard.
4. Avoid High-Risk Zones: Plan operations to avoid or minimize exposure to high-risk nuclear hazard areas unless absolutely necessary for mission accomplishment.
5. Implement Protective Measures: If unavoidable, apply protective procedures (e.g., sheltering, NBC equipment) to reduce the risk of contamination and injury.

Notes:
• This process is critical for reducing casualties and preserving operational capability.
• Consider both deliberate and accidental nuclear hazards (e.g., damaged nuclear power plants).
nuclear hazard identification, battlefield mapping, radiation zones, contamination avoidance, NBC procedures

Battlefield Assessment (1) -

Conduct Nuclear Vulnerability Assessment for Battlefield Risk Analysis
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Identify potential nuclear targets based on threat, terrain, and area of operations (AO). Potential targets include:
- Key terrain features
- Choke points
- Command and control facilities
- Counterattack routes
-...
Procedure:

1. Identify potential nuclear targets based on threat, terrain, and area of operations (AO). Potential targets include:
- Key terrain features
- Choke points
- Command and control facilities
- Counterattack routes
- Mobility corridors
- Troop concentrations
- Rear area assembly points

2. Analyze the following information from the S2:
- Time of interest
- Threat probable courses of action and intent
- Named areas of interest (NAIs) and target areas of interest (TAIs)
- Summary of enemy activity, including nuclear attacks, movement of nuclear equipment or material, presence and level of training of threat forces, and indicators of enemy nuclear warfare comments (e.g., queuing up weather radar)

3. Gather the following specific information:
- Direction and speed of winds between 0 and 30,000 meters above the surface
- How weather conditions may affect fallout patterns
- Terrain characteristics
- Transportation assets available for shipment of nuclear munitions (railways, airfields, road networks)
- Availability and location of industrial assets capable of producing or weaponizing nuclear material
- Availability and location of nuclear weapons, delivery systems, and stockpiles

4. Obtain information from the fire support officer (FSO) on casualty percentages from friendly and threat conventional munitions:
- Casualty percentages based on target size
- Casualty percentages based on weapon systems

5. Compile general information from various sources such as news bulletins, spot reports, and intelligence summaries (INSUMs). This includes:
- Availability of nuclear defense equipment to enemy forces
- Amount of overhead cover or collective protection shelters/systems available
- If enemy forces seek overhead cover or move into collective protection shelters, it may indicate intent to use nuclear weapons
- Enemy’s stated national policy on the use of nuclear weapons (no-use, first-use, limited-use only-for-retaliation-in-kind)
- Whether the enemy considers flame or smoke as NBC agents
- Leadership willingness to use nuclear weapons on their own territory or expose populations to hazards from nuclear munitions
- Reports indicating presence of foreign advisors working with enemy forces
- International trade agreements or shipments of equipment (e.g., fuzes, weapons-grade uranium)

6. Use the compiled information to formulate a nuclear threat status and integrate it into various staff estimates.

Note: This assessment is an important part of battlefield risk analysis and helps the chemical staff advisor participate in the battlefield assessment process.
nuclear vulnerability, battlefield risk analysis, threat status, nuclear targets, NBC agents, fallout patterns, chemical staff advisor

Battlefield Planning (1) -

Integrate Chemical Staff into the IPB Process for Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Ensure that the chemical officer/NCO is directly involved in the Intelligence Preparation of the Battlefield (IPB) process, as it is essential to address nuclear warfare during all phases of the battle.

2. In coordination...
Procedure:

1. Ensure that the chemical officer/NCO is directly involved in the Intelligence Preparation of the Battlefield (IPB) process, as it is essential to address nuclear warfare during all phases of the battle.

2. In coordination with the S2/3, the chemical staff must:
- Generate template(s) of potential nuclear targets or areas of contamination
- Designate templated areas that influence the scheme of maneuver as named areas of interest (NAIs)
- Include NAIs in the collection plan and identify indicators for detection or denial of enemy activities
- Include designated NAIs in the reconnaissance and surveillance (R&S) plan, and assign responsibility for confirming or denying the template

3. Using the IPB process, the chemical officer/NCO must:
- Provide the commander updates on the nuclear situation based on time periods of interest
- Provide detailed information on enemy nuclear capabilities, including:
- Type of units and weapons available in the AO/AI during a selected period
- How the enemy would employ nuclear weapons to support their battle plan
- Areas of likely employment based on threat employment doctrine
- Detailed analysis of terrain and weather in the unit’s AO during each period of interest, and how they could impact nuclear weapons
- Templates of predicted fallout data updated as conditions change
- Alternative actions the commander can initiate prior to the phase timeline to minimize degradation of forces
- Continuous monitoring of intelligence messages and radio traffic for any nuclear-related information relevant to the unit’s mission

4. Ensure that chemical staff input is included in the decision support template (DST), which must visually present nuclear concerns to the commander.

5. The DST becomes an operational document and is briefed to the commander by the S3 after being developed under the direction of the S2.

6. If the chemical staff is an active participant in the IPB process, they must work within that process in developing the DST and R&S plan, ensuring their input serves the commander effectively as special staff warfare experts.
IPB process, chemical staff, nuclear contamination, DST, R&S plan, named areas of interest, fallout data

Intelligence Coordination (1) -

Coordinate with Intelligence Officer for Nuclear Target Analysis
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. The chemical staff advisor works with the S2 (intelligence officer) to analyze and identify nuclear targets based on threat, terrain, and AO.

2. Use the following criteria to determine potential nuclear targets:
- Key...
Procedure:

1. The chemical staff advisor works with the S2 (intelligence officer) to analyze and identify nuclear targets based on threat, terrain, and AO.

2. Use the following criteria to determine potential nuclear targets:
- Key terrain features
- Choke points
- Command and control facilities
- Counterattack routes
- Mobility corridors
- Troop concentrations
- Rear area assembly points

3. Ensure that all identified nuclear targets are integrated into the IPB process for effective battlefield planning.

4. Coordinate with the S2 to obtain the following information:
- Time of interest
- Threat probable courses of action and intent
- Named areas of interest (NAIs) and target areas of interest (TAIs)
- Summary of enemy activity, including nuclear attacks, movement of nuclear equipment or material, presence and level of training of threat forces, and indicators of enemy nuclear warfare comments (e.g., queuing up weather radar)

5. Analyze the following specific information:
- Direction and speed of winds between 0 and 30,000 meters above the surface
- How weather conditions may affect fallout patterns
- Terrain characteristics
- Transportation assets available for shipment of nuclear munitions (railways, airfields, road networks)
- Availability and location of industrial assets capable of producing or weaponizing nuclear material
- Availability and location of nuclear weapons, delivery systems, and stockpiles

6. Ensure that the chemical staff’s input is included in all relevant staff estimates for effective battlefield assessment.
chemical staff advisor, S2 coordination, nuclear target analysis, IPB process, fallout patterns, terrain analysis

Equipment Deployment (1) -

Deploy Nuclear Defense Equipment Based on Threat Status
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. If the initial threat estimate indicates no nuclear threat (Serial 0):
- Do not deploy radiac equipment or dosimeters to units.
- Radiac equipment may be pre-palletized and stored at division support areas or brigade...
Procedure:

1. If the initial threat estimate indicates no nuclear threat (Serial 0):
- Do not deploy radiac equipment or dosimeters to units.
- Radiac equipment may be pre-palletized and stored at division support areas or brigade support areas for rapid deployment if needed.

2. If the threat status changes to Serial 1 (low) or higher:
- Deploy nuclear defense equipment forward to the division support area or brigade support area as required.
- Radiac equipment may be deployed by aircraft or other transportation systems depending on availability.

3. Deployment decisions must consider:
- Availability of aircraft or other transportation systems.
- The need for forces not to carry radiac equipment in field packs or ruck sacks during low threat conditions.
radiac equipment deployment, nuclear defense assets, threat status change, pre-palletized equipment, aircraft deployment

Preparation And Deployment (1) -

Prepare for Nuclear Defense and Decontamination Operations
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. US forces should deploy with nuclear defense equipment in the unit load, depending on the nuclear threat to the airfield or port at which they land.
2. Soldiers must ensure that radiac (radiation) equipment is serviceable prior to...
Procedure:

1. US forces should deploy with nuclear defense equipment in the unit load, depending on the nuclear threat to the airfield or port at which they land.
2. Soldiers must ensure that radiac (radiation) equipment is serviceable prior to deployment.
3. Decontamination and nuclear recon assets should be task organized and moved forward.
4. Contingency stocks of nuclear defense equipment may be moved forward to the battalion trains.
5. EDMs (Emergency Detection Measures) are initiated.
6. Place collective protection systems into a state of readiness, including those in combat vehicles.
7. Ensure that decontamination operations have access to water sources such as fire hydrants and car washes in urban areas.
8. Prepare for potential contamination hazards from civilian chemical manufacturing and storage facilities (e.g., leaks from storage containers may produce areas of contamination).
radiac equipment, decontamination assets, EDMs, collective protection systems, nuclear defense equipment deployment

Casualty Assessment (1) -

Assessing Casualty and Damage from Nuclear Effects
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
When assessing casualties or damage, consider the following:

1. Blast effects are a primary cause of immediate damage.
2. Nuclear radiation effects contribute to long-term casualties and health impacts.
3. Thermal casualty data are...
When assessing casualties or damage, consider the following:

1. Blast effects are a primary cause of immediate damage.
2. Nuclear radiation effects contribute to long-term casualties and health impacts.
3. Thermal casualty data are included in the effects tables for planning purposes.
4. The combined coverage of blast, thermal, and nuclear radiation effects is listed in the tables.

> Note: These assessments should be used by commanders to understand potential risks from a nuclear detonation and make informed decisions about unit positioning and protection.
casualty assessment,nuclear effects,blast damage,radiation casualties

Target Vulnerability (1) -

Unit Concentration and Target Vulnerability
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
In an active nuclear environment:

1. The more concentrated a unit is, the more lucrative it becomes as a target.
2. If the unit itself is not the target, but falls within the fallout pattern, unit monitors will be capable of providing...
In an active nuclear environment:

1. The more concentrated a unit is, the more lucrative it becomes as a target.
2. If the unit itself is not the target, but falls within the fallout pattern, unit monitors will be capable of providing essential information regarding the hazard to the commander.
3. Use this information for nuclear hazard prediction (covered in detail in Chapter 3).
unit concentration,target vulnerability,nuclear fallout,monitoring systems

Structural Damage Assessment (1) -

Define Structural Damage Levels for Buildings
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Understand the degree of structural damage to buildings.

Structural Damage Definitions:
• Severe Damage (Sev): A degree of structural damage that precludes further use of a structure for its intended purpose without...
Objective: Understand the degree of structural damage to buildings.

Structural Damage Definitions:
• Severe Damage (Sev): A degree of structural damage that precludes further use of a structure for its intended purpose without essentially complete reconstruction. Generally, collapse is implied.
• Moderate Damage (Mod): A degree of structural damage to principal load-carrying members and walls that precludes effective use until major repairs are made.
• Light Damage (Lit): A degree of damage resulting in broken windows, slight damage to roofing and siding, blowing down of light interior partitions, and slight cracking of curtain walls. Minor repairs can restore functionality.

Use Case: This classification helps assess the impact on infrastructure after a nuclear detonation.
building damage,nuclear effects,structure assessment

Vehicle Damage Assessment (1) -

Define Vehicle Damage Levels for Military Equipment
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Understand the degree of damage to military vehicles.

Vehicle Damage Definitions:
• Severe Damage (Sev): Damaged, nonfunctional, very difficult to repair. At least one subsystem is nonfunctional and not repairable.
-...
Objective: Understand the degree of damage to military vehicles.

Vehicle Damage Definitions:
