• Severe Damage (Sev): Damaged, nonfunctional, very difficult to repair. At least one subsystem is nonfunctional and not repairable.
• Moderate Damage (Mod): Damaged, nonfunctional, but repairable with special tools, skills, and parts. At least half of all subsystems are not functional, but are repairable.

Use Case: This classification helps assess the impact on military equipment after a nuclear detonation.
military vehicle damage,nuclear effects,equipment assessment

Preparation And Planning (1) -

Plan Ahead for Nuclear Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Plan Ahead for Nuclear Contamination

• Tasks take longer to perform in a nuclear environment.
• Use FM 3-4 tables to estimate how long it takes to accomplish missions in a nuclear environment.
• Commanders must carefully think out courses of...
Plan Ahead for Nuclear Contamination

• Tasks take longer to perform in a nuclear environment.
• Use FM 3-4 tables to estimate how long it takes to accomplish missions in a nuclear environment.
• Commanders must carefully think out courses of action and allow for the additional time requirement, commonly referred to as wargaming.
• A bad decision could cause the unit to become needlessly contaminated or suffer casualties.
• Use the nuclear threat status for planning and stocking nuclear defense equipment.
• Units must prepare to continue the mission after a nuclear attack.
• Following an enemy nuclear strike, commanders must quickly assess the damage and reconstitute lost or weakened units.
plan ahead,nuclear contamination,wargaming,threat status,stocking equipment

Avoidance And Security (1) -

Implement OPSEC Measures to Avoid Detection
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Implement OPSEC Measures to Avoid Detection

• Avoid detection is the best way to prevent nuclear attacks.
• Employ good OPSEC measures, including:
- Camouflage
- Light discipline
- Signal security
• Both active and passive...
Implement OPSEC Measures to Avoid Detection

• Avoid detection is the best way to prevent nuclear attacks.
• Employ good OPSEC measures, including:
- Camouflage
- Light discipline
- Signal security
• Both active and passive measures must be used to prevent the enemy from gaining target information.
• Use defensive electronic warfare, such as:
- Electronic countermeasures (ECM)
- Electronic counter-countermeasures (ECCM)
• These reduce the chances of identification and location in a nuclear environment.
• If detection and/or identification is suspected, commanders must consider displacing.
opsec,camouflage,light discipline,signal security,displacement

Early Warning And Monitoring (1) -

Provide Early Warning of Nuclear Attacks
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Provide Early Warning of Nuclear Attacks

• If the unit is unable to avoid a nuclear attack, early warning of battlefield hazards is very important.
• The NBCWRS (Nuclear, Biological, and Chemical Warning and Reporting System) notifies units...
Provide Early Warning of Nuclear Attacks

• If the unit is unable to avoid a nuclear attack, early warning of battlefield hazards is very important.
• The NBCWRS (Nuclear, Biological, and Chemical Warning and Reporting System) notifies units that:
- Adjacent units have been attacked
- A downwind hazard is present
• When no NBCWRS warning is received, periodic monitoring is essential.
• Troops must be able to identify nuclear attacks and take appropriate actions.
• NBC recon teams using the NBC Reconnaissance System (NBCRS) alert moving units before they enter contaminated areas.
nbcrs,nbcwrs,early warning,monitoring,hazard identification

Leadership And Psychological Resilience (1) -

Maintain Discipline and Confidence in the Unit
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Maintain Discipline and Confidence in the Unit

• The unit must maintain discipline and confidence in its ability to survive and operate if it is to overcome the shock of a nuclear attack and continue the mission.
• Commanders must be able to...
Maintain Discipline and Confidence in the Unit

• The unit must maintain discipline and confidence in its ability to survive and operate if it is to overcome the shock of a nuclear attack and continue the mission.
• Commanders must be able to rely on their troops not to give up hope or lose the sense of duty.
• Plan ahead and use these plans during unit training.
• Use information from FM 3-4 to assist in developing unit plans.
discipline,confidence,training,unit planning,fm 3-4

Shelter And Protection (1) -

Seek Natural and Artificial Protection from Nuclear Effects
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Seek Natural and Artificial Protection from Nuclear Effects

• Natural terrain may provide shelter from the effects of nuclear weapons.
- Ditches, ravines, and natural depressions reduce initial nuclear effects.
• Foxholes with...
Seek Natural and Artificial Protection from Nuclear Effects

• Natural terrain may provide shelter from the effects of nuclear weapons.
- Ditches, ravines, and natural depressions reduce initial nuclear effects.
• Foxholes with solid overhead cover and shelters offer good protection against nuclear weapons.
• Any overhead cover such as tents, tarpaulins, and ponchos provide at least some protection from fallout.
• Use NBC protective covers (NBC-PCs) to protect equipment whenever possible.
natural terrain,foxholes,overhead cover,nbc pcs,equipment protection

Equipment Protection And Storage (1) -

Cover Supplies and Equipment to Prevent Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Cover Supplies and Equipment to Prevent Contamination

• Store supplies and equipment under cover to prevent contamination.
• Buildings offer excellent protection from fallout.
• Field expedient methods are abundant, including:
- **NBC...
Cover Supplies and Equipment to Prevent Contamination

• Store supplies and equipment under cover to prevent contamination.
• Buildings offer excellent protection from fallout.
• Field expedient methods are abundant, including:
- NBC protective covers (NBC-PCs)
- Tarpaulins
- Pallets
- Packing materials
- Dunnage
- Plastic sheets, bags, and rolls
• Field expedient covers, especially canvas and cardboard, provide protection from fallout for a short period.
• Contamination seeps through all such covers; however, NBC-PCs provide protection for up to 24 hours.
• Units must replace covers as soon as possible after heavy contamination.
• Although these covers may provide protection against fallout, a contact hazard will remain until the dust on the ground and on the protective cover has decayed.
cover supplies,equipment protection,nbc pcs,tarpaulins,field expedient

Transportation (1) -

Transport Contaminated Material Safely
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Notify the MCC:
- Notify the Medical Control Center (MCC) of contaminated vehicles or contaminated routes.
2. Minimize transport vehicles used:
- Use as few transport vehicles as possible when moving contaminated...
Procedure:

1. Notify the MCC:
- Notify the Medical Control Center (MCC) of contaminated vehicles or contaminated routes.
2. Minimize transport vehicles used:
- Use as few transport vehicles as possible when moving contaminated material.
3. Use a single route:
- Use one route, especially around congested areas, and monitor the route periodically for contamination.
4. Cover contaminated material:
- Cover the material to prevent contamination from being blown onto the road (weigh the risk of ground contamination with the additional burden of decontamination/disposing of potentially contaminated covering material).
5. Warn personnel downwind:
- Warn personnel downwind if a vapor hazard is present.
6. Protect transport crews:
- Ensure transport crews wear protective masks to reduce the hazard from airborne radioactive particles.
7. Bury contaminated waste:
- Bury contaminated material or waste, as burial is effective for all types of contamination.
8. Mark and avoid burial sites:
- Mark and avoid the area where contaminated waste is buried.
9. Submit NBC 5 report:
- Submit an NBC 5 nuclear report outlining the contaminated waste burial site through the NBC Control Center (NBCC) to assign a line item Alpha (strike serial number).
transport contaminated material, notify MCC, cover material, protect transport crews, bury waste, NBC 5 report

Warning Systems (1) -

Use the NBC Warning and Reporting System (NBCWRS)
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Utilize the NBCWRS:
- The NBCWRS is the primary means of warning units of an actual or predicted nuclear hazard.
2. Determine protective measures:
- Use the NBCWRS to determine required protective measures and plan...
Procedure:

1. Utilize the NBCWRS:
- The NBCWRS is the primary means of warning units of an actual or predicted nuclear hazard.
2. Determine protective measures:
- Use the NBCWRS to determine required protective measures and plan operations based on the type of hazard present.
3. Adjust plans if possible:
- If the mission allows, affected units should alter plans to avoid the hazard.
4. Upgrade protective measures:
- If avoiding the hazard is not possible, upgrade protective measures and occupy or cross the hazard area.
5. Use as battlefield intelligence:
- Use the NBCWRS as battlefield intelligence to inform operational decisions.
NBCWRS, nuclear hazard warning, protective measures, battlefield intelligence, avoid hazards

Radiological Defense (1) -

Understanding Standard NBC Reports for Radiological Defense
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Standard NBC Reports are used by military units to exchange critical information during nuclear contamination scenarios. These reports follow standardized formats defined in ATP 45/STANAG 2103 and the US Message Text Format (USMTF). The United...
Standard NBC Reports are used by military units to exchange critical information during nuclear contamination scenarios. These reports follow standardized formats defined in ATP 45/STANAG 2103 and the US Message Text Format (USMTF). The United States, along with NATO allies such as Britain, Canada, and Australia, use these same message formats for improved accuracy, comprehension, interoperability, speed of dissemination, and submission.

There are five standard NBC reports:

1. NBC 1 – Initial report containing basic data compiled at the unit level.
2. NBC 2 – Report used to pass evaluated data.
3. NBC 3 – Report used for immediate warning of predicted contamination and hazard areas.
4. NBC 4 – Report used for passing monitoring and survey results.
5. NBC 5 – Report used for passing information on areas of actual contamination.

The reports use standard formats to shorten the message being passed. The warning and reporting system is based on a code letter system, with each letter having a specific meaning described in Table 2-1 (not included here). All units must be completely familiar with the NBC 1 report format and its information. This report must be prepared quickly and accurately and sent to the next higher headquarters.
NBC reports, radiological defense, nuclear contamination, military communication

Radiation Contamination Reporting (1) -

Prepare an NBC 5 Report for Nuclear Contamination Areas
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Gather contamination plot data: Use a contamination plot to extract grid coordinates for the contaminated area.
2. Determine necessary line items: Include only relevant line items such as:
- Delta (date/time group):...
Procedure:

1. Gather contamination plot data: Use a contamination plot to extract grid coordinates for the contaminated area.
2. Determine necessary line items: Include only relevant line items such as:
- Delta (date/time group): Time of the report.
- Foxtrot (location): Coordinates of the contaminated area.
- Tango (H+ 1 date-time group): Estimated time for the next update.
- Oscar (reference time): Reference point for timing calculations.
- Romeo (dose rate): Radiation dose rates in cGyph (coulombs per gray per hour).
- Uniform (1,000 cGyph): Dose rate at 1,000 cGyph level.
- Victor (300 cGyph): Dose rate at 300 cGyph level.
- Whiskey (100 cGyph): Dose rate at 100 cGyph level.
- X-Ray (20 cGyph): Dose rate at 20 cGyph level.
3. Exclude redundant data: If the unit has already received information via other NBC reports, such as an NBC 3 report showing GZ location (Foxtrot), this data does not need to be repeated on the NBC 5.
4. Report neutron-induced contamination separately: Areas of neutron-induced contamination must be reported using an overlay rather than the NBC 5.
5. Include decay rate information: The NBC 5 is also used to transmit the decay rate of fallout (e.g., n = 1.2). If a different decay rate is determined, it should be included in the report.
6. Report closure of decontamination sites: Include coordinates for the site and sump in the NBC 5 to notify other units of contamination areas.
7. Transmit via FM radio nets: The NBC 5 report may need to be sent on FM radio nets, which requires lengthy transmission time due to the series of grid coordinates.
8. Recipient must plot coordinates: Recipients are required to plot each coordinate and redraw the contamination plot based on the received data.
NBC5, nuclear report, radiation contamination, grid coordinates, dose rate, decay rate, decontamination site closure

Radiation Contamination Management (1) -

Manage the NBC Warning and Reporting System (NBCWRS)
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Collect nuclear information: Gather all relevant data from monitoring, survey, and reconnaissance operations.
2. Report feedback to NBCC: Provide feedback on contamination areas through NBC 4 reports to update the NBC...
Procedure:

1. Collect nuclear information: Gather all relevant data from monitoring, survey, and reconnaissance operations.
2. Report feedback to NBCC: Provide feedback on contamination areas through NBC 4 reports to update the NBC Control Center (NBCC) with accurate location details.
3. Use automated systems: Transmit initial monitoring and recon reports via ANBACIS (Automated NBC Information System), if available, for faster processing by the NBCC.
4. Evaluate nuclear information: Convert collected data into usable battlefield intelligence to support tactical operations and decision-making.
5. Avoid communication overload: Ensure that the volume of reported information does not disrupt communications or tactical operations by managing the flow of NBCWRS data effectively.
6. Predict hazard areas with NBC 1 reports: Use NBC 1 reports to predict potential contamination zones, which are then refined using feedback from units on the ground (NBC 3 and NBC 4 reports).
NBCWRS, nuclear information management, ANBACIS, NBC1 report, NBC3 report, NBC4 report, contamination prediction

Information Transmission (1) -

Transmitting Nuclear Information to the NBCC
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedures used to transmit nuclear information to and from the NBC Control Center (NBCC) are an important part of the NBC information system. The flow of information is typically through the chain of command: from company to battalion to...
Procedures used to transmit nuclear information to and from the NBC Control Center (NBCC) are an important part of the NBC information system. The flow of information is typically through the chain of command: from company to battalion to brigade to division. However, there are exceptions:

• The NBCC may request specific information, such as survey data. In these cases, the unit conducting the survey may report directly back to the division. This is especially true for aerial surveys.

• The monitoring unit must also send an information copy back to the parent unit for command and control (C2) and for recording of radiation exposure if necessary.

• Designated observers should send reports simultaneously to both the NBCC and their parent organization.

In cases where units are attached or OPCON and have no direct contact with a parent unit, the headquarters to which they are OPCON passes nuclear information. For example:

• Units that operate independently (such as Military Police or Engineers) operating in an area will report through the headquarters controlling that area of operation, normally a brigade tactical operations center (TOC).

The method of transmitting information depends on the tactical situation and mission of the unit, as specified in FSOP/OPLAN/OPORD and unit SOP. At brigade and higher headquarters, NBC reports are usually passed on the intelligence net rather than the command net. At battalion level and lower, there is generally only one FM net available for command communications. Therefore, NBC reports should be formatted ahead of time and be as short and concise as possible. In this case, wire communications are best.

Support units use admin-log nets but must also inform the brigade TOC or division TOC when operating in that unit’s area of operations. Again, wire communications are excellent if available.

There are numerous methods to communicate nuclear information, including ANBACIS, which accesses information from the maneuver control system (MCS). The NBCC should evaluate all possible methods and select those that best suit the purpose. This information should be contained in the unit SOP or current operations order.
nuclear, information transmission, NBCC, NBC reports, command chain, wire communications, ANBACIS

Command Structure (1) -

Responsibility for Initiating and Coordinating Nuclear Attack Warnings
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. The executing commander is responsible for initiating the warning.
2. Commanders authorized to release nuclear attacks must coordinate with adjacent or other commands that may be affected by the attack.
3. Attack notification will...
Procedure:

1. The executing commander is responsible for initiating the warning.
2. Commanders authorized to release nuclear attacks must coordinate with adjacent or other commands that may be affected by the attack.
3. Attack notification will normally be transmitted from the division NBCC (Nuclear, Biological, and Chemical Contamination Control Center) or from DIVARTY (Division Artillery).
4. This provides adjacent commands enough time to warn personnel, take protective measures, and prepare to exploit the weapons' effects.
executing commander,nuclear attack coordination,strikwarn transmission

Detonation Response (1) -

Measure Flash-to-Bang Time for Nuclear Detonation
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Flash-to-Bang Time

• At the instant of the blue-white flash, cover eyes, hit the ground, and start counting slowly: 1,000 and 1, 1,000 and 2, 1,000 and 3, etc., until the arrival of the shock wave or bang.
• Make a mental note of the count on...
Flash-to-Bang Time

• At the instant of the blue-white flash, cover eyes, hit the ground, and start counting slowly: 1,000 and 1, 1,000 and 2, 1,000 and 3, etc., until the arrival of the shock wave or bang.
• Make a mental note of the count on which the shock wave arrives (for example, 1,000 and 4).
• If the observer has a watch and can note the exact time in seconds, use the watch to record the flash-to-bang time.
• This data is entered as line item Juliet on the NBC 1 nuclear report.
• Remain where you are until debris has stopped falling.
• Note that there will be two shock waves—one blowing in one direction and the other blowing a few minutes later in the opposite direction.
• If the bang is not heard in five minutes (a count of 1,000 and 300), continue with other measurements.
flash-to-bang time, nuclear detonation, shock wave, NBC report

Detonation Analysis (1) -

Determine Type of Burst and Time of Attack
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Type of Burst and Time of Attack

• After the second shock wave has passed, uncover your eyes and read the watch to the nearest minute.
• This data is entered as line item Delta of the NBC 1 nuclear report.
• Observe the developing cloud to...
Type of Burst and Time of Attack

• After the second shock wave has passed, uncover your eyes and read the watch to the nearest minute.
• This data is entered as line item Delta of the NBC 1 nuclear report.
• Observe the developing cloud to determine the type of burst:
- If the cloud is lighter in color than the stem or if the stem is ragged or broken (does not solidly connect with the cloud), record “air” as line item Hotel of the NBC 1 nuclear report.
- If the stem is thick and dark, and it connects with the cloud, record “surface” as line item Hotel.
- When the cloud does not match any mental image for air or surface, record “unknown” as line item Hotel.
• “Unknown” also may be recorded whenever the attack occurs at night.
• A subsurface burst is recorded as “surface,” only if the detonation ruptures the surface.
• This data is recorded on line item Hotel of the NBC 1 nuclear report.
type of burst, airburst, surface burst, time of attack, NBC report

Location Estimation (1) -

Determine Distance Between GZ and Observer Using Azimuths
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
To determine the distance between ground zero (GZ) and the observer using azimuths, follow these steps:

1. Collect azimuth data: Obtain azimuth information from multiple observers who have reported their observations of the nuclear burst.
2....
To determine the distance between ground zero (GZ) and the observer using azimuths, follow these steps:

1. Collect azimuth data: Obtain azimuth information from multiple observers who have reported their observations of the nuclear burst.
2. Plot the azimuths on a map or chart: Mark each observer's position and draw lines representing the direction (azimuth) they observed the burst from GZ.
3. Find the intersection point: The point where these lines intersect is an approximate location for GZ.
4. Measure distance between GZ and observer: Use the plotted map or chart to measure the distance in kilometers between the estimated GZ location and each observer's position.

Note: This method provides a more accurate estimate of distance than flash-to-bang time, which is considered unreliable for yield estimation at NBCC level.
azimuths,GZ location,distance measurement,map plotting

Nuclear Contamination Plotting (1) -

Plotting NBC 3 Nuclear Report with Line Item Yankee
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure for plotting an NBC 3 nuclear report when line item Yankee is present:

Step 1. Identify the map scale to be used. Obtain a sheet of overlay paper or other transparent material. Mark the GZ (Ground Zero) location and GN (Grid...
Procedure for plotting an NBC 3 nuclear report when line item Yankee is present:

Step 1. Identify the map scale to be used. Obtain a sheet of overlay paper or other transparent material. Mark the GZ (Ground Zero) location and GN (Grid North).

Step 2. Examine line item Yankee. Starting at the GZ location, draw the left radial line and then the right radial line measured from GN.

Step 3. From line item Zulu, determine the downwind distance of Zone I. Starting from GZ, draw an arc between the radial lines with a radius equal to the distance of Zone I. Label this area Zone I. Draw a second arc between the radial lines at twice the radius as the downwind distance of Zone II. Label this area Zone II.

Step 4. From line item Zulu, determine the size of the cloud radius. Using GZ as the center, draw a circle with a radius equal to the stabilized cloud radius.

Step 5. Draw tangent lines from the outer edge of the cloud radius to the points of intersection of the radial lines with the Zone I arc.

Step 6. From line item Zulu, determine the effective wind speed. Beginning at GZ, draw as many dashed time-of-arrival arcs between the radial and tangent lines as will fit inside the prediction. Label the dashed arcs as hours after the burst: H + 1, H + 2, and so on. H + 1 is the closest arc to GZ. If a time-of-arrival arc coincides with a Zone I or Zone II arc, extend the zone boundary with a dashed line.

Step 7. Add marginal information to the plot. This should be all known information about the attack.

Step 8. Orient the prediction to the map and evaluate the hazard.
NBC 3 report plotting, nuclear contamination map, Zone I and II, radial lines, cloud radius, time-of-arrival arcs

Radiological Surveillance (1) -

Conducting Nuclear Contamination Surveys
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Overview: Nuclear surveys are conducted to determine the extent and intensity of contamination, providing detailed information for future operations.

### When to Conduct a Survey
• A survey is only conducted when the intensity of...
Overview: Nuclear surveys are conducted to determine the extent and intensity of contamination, providing detailed information for future operations.

### When to Conduct a Survey
• A survey is only conducted when the intensity of contamination must be known.
• If no operations are planned in the area, a survey is not required. However, if there is a remote chance that this may change, a survey should be conducted at the earliest opportunity.
• Surveys are rarely conducted in the Covering Force Area (CFA) or forward of the Forward Line of Troops (FLOT), unless knowledge is imperative and loss of the survey team is acceptable.

### Survey Planning
• Surveys require time, coordination, and diversion of men and equipment from primary missions. They are not conducted by units unless directed by the NBC Control Center (NBCC).
• The NBCC will initiate surveys or request that a subordinate unit be directed to conduct a survey.

### Survey Team Composition
• A survey is performed by a group comprised of:
- A defense team: Plans and directs the survey, screens and transmits data to the authority that ordered the survey.
- One or more survey teams: Organized within the company/troop/battery NBC team. Each survey team consists of a monitor and necessary support and security personnel.
• Only the minimum number of personnel are exposed to radiation during the survey.

### Types of Surveys
There are two types of surveys: aerial and ground.

#### Aerial Surveys
• Conducted for large areas.
• Advantages:
- Faster and more flexible than ground surveys.
- Expose personnel to lower radiation doses.
- Require fewer personnel and equipment.
• Disadvantages:
- Aircraft may not always be available.

#### Ground Surveys
• Conducted under unit control using unit equipment.
• Advantages:
- Can be done in any type of weather.
- Can be conducted when aircraft cannot fly.
- More accurate than aerial surveys.
• Disadvantages:
- Require more personnel and equipment.

### Survey Execution
• The defense team briefs the survey teams and controls their movements during the operation.
• Surveys are not conducted by units unless directed by the NBCC.

### Resurvey Procedures
• Calculations based on initial survey data are approximations. Resurveying is essential for accuracy, especially as contamination decays over time.
• The frequency and detail of resurvey will be determined by the reliability of the initial survey.
• Resurveys are planned and conducted in the same way as the initial survey.
nuclear contamination, radiological survey, NBC team, aerial survey, ground survey, resurvey, radiation exposure, NBCC

Radiological Survey Planning (1) -

Determine Contamination Type for Radiological Survey Planning
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure for Determining Contamination Type to Guide Survey Planning:

1. Identify the type of contamination present in the affected area:
- Induced contamination: This occurs from a nuclear burst where fallout did not occur, or it is...
Procedure for Determining Contamination Type to Guide Survey Planning:

1. Identify the type of contamination present in the affected area:
- Induced contamination: This occurs from a nuclear burst where fallout did not occur, or it is localized around an obvious ground zero area.
- Fallout (known weapon): Contamination that can be related to a specific nuclear burst.
- Fallout (unknown weapon): Contamination identifiable as fallout but cannot be linked to a particular nuclear burst.
- Contamination (unknown source): Induced or fallout contamination that cannot be traced back to a known source.
- Combination and multiples of the above: A mix of any of the above types.

2. Based on the identified contamination type, determine the appropriate survey plan:
- Establish the level of detail required for the survey.
- Decide on the method of control (decentralized or centralized).
- Choose the type of survey (ground or aerial).
- Select the technique to be used (route, point, course leg, or preselected dose rate).

3. The survey plan may include a combination of methods and techniques:
- A typical example is combining an aerial survey with a ground survey, each covering different zones within the contaminated area.
- All available techniques (route, point, course leg, or preselected dose rate) may be employed depending on the situation.
contamination type, radiological survey planning, aerial survey, ground survey, fallout, induced contamination

Radiological Data Recording (1) -

Record Radiological Data Using DA Form 1971-1-R for Aerial Surveys
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Use DA Form 1971-1-R to record data obtained from aerial surveys using the route or course leg technique.
2. Ensure all block headings are filled out accurately and completely, as they are self-explanatory.
3. If any...
Procedure:

1. Use DA Form 1971-1-R to record data obtained from aerial surveys using the route or course leg technique.
2. Ensure all block headings are filled out accurately and completely, as they are self-explanatory.
3. If any headings are not applicable to the current situation, line them through by the monitor.
4. Use the DO NOT USE* blocks for entry of the AGCF (Average Gamma Count Factor) and normalized readings by the control team.
5. The Remarks block should be used by the monitor to provide any additional information that may be valuable to the unit NBC defense team.
6. The Remarks block is also used by the defense team to enter:
- Time of nuclear burst
- Computations of AGCF
- Normalizing factor
- Overall correction factor
7. Ensure all data collected during aerial surveys using the course leg technique are recorded on DA Form 1971-1-R.
8. After completion of the survey, deliver the completed radiological data sheet to the defense team via:
- Physical drop
- Radio
- Telephone from the nearest landing zone
9. The NBCC (Nuclear, Biological, and Chemical Control Center) must specify in the survey briefing how the data is to be delivered.
DA Form 1971-1-R, aerial surveys, course leg technique, AGCF, normalized readings, NBC defense team, data delivery, NBCC briefing

Contaminated Materiel Handling (1) -

Marking Contaminated Materiel
Standard Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Special Marking Procedures for Contaminated Materiel:
- Mark contaminated materiel to prevent accidental contamination of personnel.
- Ensure markers are visible from any angle.

2. Disposal and Holding Areas:
-...
Procedure:

1. Special Marking Procedures for Contaminated Materiel:
- Mark contaminated materiel to prevent accidental contamination of personnel.
- Ensure markers are visible from any angle.

2. Disposal and Holding Areas:
- If contaminated materiel can be left in place to weather, this may be the best solution.
- If collected in a holding area, mark and monitor for residual hazards.
- Vapor hazards are additive; avoid placing multiple contaminated items near each other (e.g., in maintenance holding areas).

3. Vehicle and Equipment Marking:
- Contaminated vehicles and equipment must be marked or identified to prevent injury from hidden contamination.
- Attach a marker to the outside of the vehicle for visibility.
contaminated materiel, nuclear handling, radiological marking, equipment safety

Radiological Marking (1) -

Radiological Contamination Marking Procedures
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Placement of Radiological Signs:
- Unit recon teams place radiological contamination signs on principal routes of entry into the contaminated area where the dose rate is 1 cGyph at 1 meter above the ground.
- Do not...
Procedure:

1. Placement of Radiological Signs:
- Unit recon teams place radiological contamination signs on principal routes of entry into the contaminated area where the dose rate is 1 cGyph at 1 meter above the ground.
- Do not place signs in the interior portion of the area; they should only be placed on the perimeter.

2. Sign Maintenance:
- Move signs periodically as contamination decays to ensure accuracy and effectiveness.

3. Commander's Discretion:
- If marking a contaminated area would result in a military advantage being lost, the commander may order that the area not be marked.
- In such cases, unit defense team personnel should be placed at principal entry points if the tactical situation permits.

4. Traffic Control and Safety Measures:
- Traffic control personnel are positioned to inform personnel entering contaminated areas about the extent of the hazard.
- Defense team personnel should not be too close to the contaminated area and must wear a tactical dosimeter.
- They should not receive a dose greater than 1 cGy per day.
radiological marking, nuclear contamination signs, tactical dosimeter, dose rate monitoring

Battlefield Operations (1) -

Apply Avoidance Principles for Nuclear Contamination on the Battlefield
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
To apply avoidance principles for nuclear contamination on the battlefield, follow these steps:

1. Use the checklist provided in Appendix G as a guide for tactical operations.
2. Integrate the concepts from this chapter into all aspects of...
To apply avoidance principles for nuclear contamination on the battlefield, follow these steps:

1. Use the checklist provided in Appendix G as a guide for tactical operations.
2. Integrate the concepts from this chapter into all aspects of military planning and execution.
3. Ensure that all personnel are trained to recognize and respond to potential nuclear contamination scenarios.
4. Maintain situational awareness of NBC (Nuclear, Biological, Chemical) threats at all times.
5. Coordinate with other units and command structures to ensure a unified response to nuclear contamination hazards.
avoidance principles,nuclear contamination,battlefield operations,appendix g,tactical operations

Radiation Dosimetry (1) -

Using a Keller Nomogram to Calculate Time (t) from RI and Rt for Soil Type IV
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Select the appropriate nomogram based on the given soil type (in this case, Soil Type IV).
2. Align the hairline with the following points:
- The 200 cGyph point on the R1 scale
- The 70 cGyph point on the Rt...
Procedure:

1. Select the appropriate nomogram based on the given soil type (in this case, Soil Type IV).
2. Align the hairline with the following points:
- The 200 cGyph point on the R1 scale
- The 70 cGyph point on the Rt scale
3. Read the time (t) at the point where the hairline intersects the Time (middle) scale.
4. Record the result, which in this example is H + 11 hours.

Notes:
• Ensure that the hairline intersects both the vertical line and the interpolated value (tick mark) as closely as possible for accuracy.
• This procedure assumes known R1 and Rt values and calculates the time at which the dose rate will decrease to a specified level (in this case, from 200 cGyph to 70 cGyph).
Keller nomogram,time,Rt,RI,dose rate,nuclear contamination,soil type iv

Electronics Protection (1) -

Understand Effects of EMP on Electronic Equipment
Standard Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Nuclear Burst Effects on Electronics:

• Electromagnetic Pulse (EMP): A nuclear weapons effect that can destroy or cause serious damage to electrical and electronic equipment through current surges.
• **Transient-Radiation Effects on...
Nuclear Burst Effects on Electronics:

• Electromagnetic Pulse (EMP): A nuclear weapons effect that can destroy or cause serious damage to electrical and electronic equipment through current surges.
• Transient-Radiation Effects on Electronics (TREE): Similar effects to EMP, but caused by gamma and neutron radiation. Gamma radiation causes temporary ionization of components, which may lead to permanent damage in other parts of the equipment. Fast neutrons cause permanent damage by dislodging atoms in crystals.
• Shielding: Shielding that reduces gamma and neutron radiation will reduce the effects of TREE. However, the damage caused by these effects may or may not be shielded out.
• EMP Characteristics: Unlike lightning, EMP does not produce a flash in the sky or a loud noise. Devices that protect equipment against lightning do not necessarily provide protection against EMP.
EMP, TREE, electronic shielding, nuclear effects on electronics

Personnel Safety (1) -

Avoid Direct Injury from EMP Through Personnel Safety Measures
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Instruct personnel to avoid contact with metallic collectors: Ensure all troops are trained not to touch any metallic objects such as cables, railroad lines, or other conductive materials during an EMP event.

2. **Provide...
Procedure:

1. Instruct personnel to avoid contact with metallic collectors: Ensure all troops are trained not to touch any metallic objects such as cables, railroad lines, or other conductive materials during an EMP event.

2. Provide safety training: Conduct regular training sessions on the risks of direct injury from EMP and how to avoid them.

3. Implement physical barriers: Where possible, install protective barriers around exposed metallic collectors to prevent accidental contact by personnel.

4. Monitor equipment for secondary effects: Watch for signs that damaged electronic systems may catch fire or cause other hazards due to relays being switched incorrectly.

5. Use SOPs for secondary effect mitigation: Follow established procedures to address any secondary effects of EMP, such as incorrect information from digital instruments.
EMP safety,personnel training,metallic collectors,SOPs

Equipment Vulnerability Assessment (1) -

Assess Vulnerability of Electronic Equipment to EMP
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Procedure:

1. Identify equipment with digital computers: Systems that use digital computers are particularly susceptible to both functional damage and operational upset.

2. Evaluate transistor technology: Equipment using...
Procedure:

1. Identify equipment with digital computers: Systems that use digital computers are particularly susceptible to both functional damage and operational upset.

2. Evaluate transistor technology: Equipment using state-of-the-art, low-power transistors and semiconductors is more vulnerable than those using older vacuum tube technologies.

3. Assess signal strength: Equipment designed to receive stronger signals (such as radio receivers) is less susceptible to EMP-induced damage compared to equipment with weaker signal reception capabilities.

4. Evaluate collector size: Larger collectors increase the vulnerability of equipment to EMP effects, so prioritize protection for systems with large antenna arrays or other conductive surfaces.

5. Consider frequency range: Unhardened radios operating at frequencies of 100 megahertz or below (such as high-frequency systems) are more susceptible than those operating above 100 MHz (e.g., super high frequency and microwave equipment).
EMP vulnerability,equipment assessment,frequency range,collector size

Power Management (1) -

Mitigate Power Source Vulnerability During EMP Events
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Reduce vulnerability of power sources to electromagnetic pulse (EMP) effects.

Procedure Steps:
1. Use military power-generating equipment instead of commercial power sources when possible, as they are more resistant to EMP...
Objective: Reduce vulnerability of power sources to electromagnetic pulse (EMP) effects.

Procedure Steps:
1. Use military power-generating equipment instead of commercial power sources when possible, as they are more resistant to EMP effects.
2. Disconnect and shut off automatic control systems and other components that may be vulnerable to EMP effects if consistent with the mission.
3. Avoid reliance on civilian power grids, as long lines used in these systems can pick up significant amounts of EMP energy and damage connected military equipment.
4. Evaluate trade-offs before using commercial power sources after an attack:
- Consider availability and backup potential for combat support units.
- Recognize the risk that a subsequent EMP attack may damage critical equipment.
5. Use selective interconnection of systems on a mission-specific basis to minimize risk while maintaining operational capability.
EMP, power sources, vulnerability mitigation, military equipment, civilian grid

Post Attack Recovery (1) -

Postattack Recovery and Continued Operations
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Ensure continued tactical operations and recovery efforts after an EMP attack.

Procedure Steps:
1. Establish priorities before the attack using standing operating procedures to guide postattack recovery and reconstitution...
Objective: Ensure continued tactical operations and recovery efforts after an EMP attack.

Procedure Steps:
1. Establish priorities before the attack using standing operating procedures to guide postattack recovery and reconstitution operations.
2. Maintain focus on mission-essential tasks during confusion following an EMP event.
3. Integrate EMP mitigation with other tactical considerations, ensuring that recovery efforts do not compromise ongoing defense posture.
4. Ensure commanders exercise oversight to ensure priorities are followed and executed effectively.
EMP recovery, postattack operations, tactical continuity, mission priorities

Command Recovery (1) -

Reestablish Command Functions After EMP Event
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Prioritize the reestablishment of command functions to ensure effective operations after an EMP event.

Procedure Steps:
1. Give high priority to restoring command and control systems, as they are essential for effective...
Objective: Prioritize the reestablishment of command functions to ensure effective operations after an EMP event.

Procedure Steps:
1. Give high priority to restoring command and control systems, as they are essential for effective military operations.
2. Implement mitigation techniques that support the restoration of communication, coordination, and decision-making capabilities.
3. Ensure that command functions are restored before other systems, as this is a prerequisite for successful recovery and reconstitution.
EMP, command restoration, command functions, mitigation techniques

Damage Assessment (1) -

Conduct Rapid Damage Assessment After EMP Event
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Quickly assess the damage to electronic equipment following an EMP event.

Procedure Steps:
1. Immediately conduct a rapid assessment of damage to predesignated high-priority equipment after an EMP event.
2. **Evaluate the...
Objective: Quickly assess the damage to electronic equipment following an EMP event.

Procedure Steps:
1. Immediately conduct a rapid assessment of damage to predesignated high-priority equipment after an EMP event.
2. Evaluate the condition of electronic systems, determining if they are functionally damaged, operationally upset, or unaffected.
3. Consider factors such as shielding and effectiveness of EMP mitigation posture in assessing overall impact on equipment.
4. Use this assessment to guide recovery efforts and prioritize repairs based on mission requirements.
EMP damage, rapid assessment, equipment evaluation, mitigation posture

Equipment Repair (1) -

Implement Quick-Fix Repairs on Critical Equipment After EMP Event
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Objective: Restore functionality of critical electronic systems through quick-fix repairs after an EMP event.

Procedure Steps:
1. Train personnel in procedures for quick fixes, such as resetting circuit breakers or replacing fuses, that...
Objective: Restore functionality of critical electronic systems through quick-fix repairs after an EMP event.

Procedure Steps:
1. Train personnel in procedures for quick fixes, such as resetting circuit breakers or replacing fuses, that can restore basic functionality to damaged equipment.
2. Ensure troops have access to necessary tools and parts, including fuses, on hand at all times.
3. Focus repairs on critical systems that are essential for mission execution and survival.
4. Perform quick fixes as soon as possible after an EMP event to minimize downtime and maintain operational readiness.
EMP repair, quick fixes, critical systems, tool availability

Retaliation (1) -

Retaliation in Response to NBC Attacks
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Defensive measures alone are not an effective response to enemy NBC attacks. The US retains the right to retaliate in response to enemy NBC attack. Depending upon the situation, this retaliation may take the form of nuclear, conventional, or...
Defensive measures alone are not an effective response to enemy NBC attacks. The US retains the right to retaliate in response to enemy NBC attack. Depending upon the situation, this retaliation may take the form of nuclear, conventional, or political options. There is no requirement that the US response be strictly proportional to an enemy attack in kind; US offensive biological and chemical operations are prohibited. See FM 3-100/FMFM 11-2, Chemical Operations for a general discussion of NBC retaliation.
NBC attack, retaliation, nuclear, conventional, political options

Serviceability Testing (1) -

Serviceability Test for GVO/BVO Overshoes
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure for Testing the Serviceability of GVO/BVO Overshoes:

1. **Visual Inspection:
- Inspect the GVO/BVO regularly for signs of deterioration such as cracks, tears, or punctures.
2. **Water Test (Alternative Method):
- Fill the...
Procedure for Testing the Serviceability of GVO/BVO Overshoes:

1. **Visual Inspection:
- Inspect the GVO/BVO regularly for signs of deterioration such as cracks, tears, or punctures.
2. **Water Test (Alternative Method):
- Fill the overshoe with water and look for leaks.
3. **Air Submersion Test (Preferred Method):
- Fill the overshoe with air and submerge it in water.
4. **Proper Technique:
- Hold the base of the overshoe with both hands (using thumb and index fingers), with the overshoe fingers hanging downward.
- Rotate the overshoe several times to ensure an air pocket is formed below the palm.
5. **Examine for Leaks:
- Squeeze the inflated overshoe and examine for escaping air.
- Submerge in water. If bubbles escape, the overshoe is unserviceable.
GVO serviceability test, BVO inspection, air submersion test, water leak detection

Sample Collection (1) -

Using the M34 Soil Sampling Kit to Collect Samples
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Gather Equipment: Retrieve the M34 soil sampling kit, which includes a carrier, plastic scoop, 2 extraction fluid bottles, 2 soil extraction bags, 16 individually wrapped ampules, 6 soil collection bags, 10 plastic-covered...
Procedure:

1. Gather Equipment: Retrieve the M34 soil sampling kit, which includes a carrier, plastic scoop, 2 extraction fluid bottles, 2 soil extraction bags, 16 individually wrapped ampules, 6 soil collection bags, 10 plastic-covered wire ties, M8 paper, 3 radiation hazard tags, and 8 shipping tags with envelopes.
2. Select Sampling Location: Choose an area where soil or surface matter needs to be sampled for laboratory analysis (e.g., contaminated surfaces, munition fragments, material fragments, small objects, dead animals).
3. Collect Soil Sample:
- Use the plastic scoop to gather a representative sample of soil or surface matter.
- Place the collected sample into one of the soil collection bags.
4. Prepare for Transport: Secure the sample in the carrier using wire ties and label it with appropriate shipping tags and radiation hazard tags if necessary.
5. Transport Sample: The carrier is used as a shipping container to transmit samples to the laboratories for analysis.
6. Maintain Kit: Follow instructions in TM 3-6665-2608-10 for care and maintenance of the kit.
M34 soil sampling kit, sample collection, NBC personnel, laboratory analysis, contamination detection

Mopp Gear Sustainment (1) -

Implement MOPP Gear Sustainment Procedures
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
MOPP Gear Sustainment

To ensure effective NBC operations, commanders must apply the sustainment imperatives of AirLand Battle doctrine: anticipation, integration, continuity, responsiveness, and improvisation. These principles guide the...
MOPP Gear Sustainment

To ensure effective NBC operations, commanders must apply the sustainment imperatives of AirLand Battle doctrine: anticipation, integration, continuity, responsiveness, and improvisation. These principles guide the planning and delivery of needed items.

### Contingency MOPP Gear Sets
• Units maintain two sets of contingency MOPP gear: the NBC 'A' bag and 'B' bag of individual chemical equipment (ICE) packs.
• The 'A' bag is carried by soldiers as part of their initial deployment gear if there is a likelihood of an NBC threat.
• The 'B' bag is provided if needed, based on operational requirements and planning using the sustainment imperatives.

### Forward Deployed Units
• Forward deployed units (e.g., armored cavalry regiments) may require both sets of MOPP gear to be immediately available depending on the threat level.
• The 'A' bag is carried by the soldier as part of their individual field gear.
• The 'B' bag can be stored in troop or squadron trains, based on unit policy and threat assessment.

### Low NBC Threat Situations
• In low NBC threat situations (e.g., Operations Urgent Fury and Just Cause), units may deploy at MOPP zero.
• Unit sets of MOPP gear, including 'A' bags, are palletized for immediate follow-on resupply if the NBC threat situation changes.

### Resupply via Palletization
• Resupply of the second set of MOPP gear can be accomplished by palletizing individual protective equipment (IPE).
• This method is applicable to both light and heavy units.
• The intent of palletizing is to create a 'push package' that can be broken down at an arrival airfield or unit trains site for immediate issue to company-level units or further movement forward to units in the field.

### Pallet Configuration
• Pallet configurations depend on the type of unit and its mission requirements.
• In light infantry divisions, pallets must be configured to fit on aircraft designated for resupplying the units.
• In heavy divisions, pallets are configured to fit on prime movers designated to haul the pallets.

### Example Palletized Configuration (Light Infantry Unit)
1. One pallet with the unit's 'A' bags and related IPE equipment: M256-series kits, M8 and 9 paper, M258A1/M291 kits, DS2, and M4 winterization kit.
2. One pallet with the unit's 'B' bags and related equipment.
3. Another pallet containing other unit NBC equipment.
4. One other pallet containing STB by itself (DS2 and STB cannot be transported together for safety reasons).

### Configuration Determination
• Configurations will vary based on a unit’s general deployment plan (GDP) or contingency mission, as well as the likelihood of an NBC threat in their area of operation (AO).
• All configurations are integrated and executed through logistics channels.
• Items are moved based on timelines dictated by the operation plan (OPLAN) and expected events during the operation.
NBC,MOPP gear,sustainment,palletization,contingency gear,logistics,undergarment,chemical protection

Chemical Protection Equipment (1) -

Understand and Use Chemical Protective Undergarment (CPU)
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Chemical Protective Undergarment (CPU)

### Description
• The CPU is a lightweight two-piece undergarment made of nonwoven fabric with activated charcoal.
• It consists of a jacket with a zippered front and velcro wrist attachments, and...
Chemical Protective Undergarment (CPU)

### Description
• The CPU is a lightweight two-piece undergarment made of nonwoven fabric with activated charcoal.
• It consists of a jacket with a zippered front and velcro wrist attachments, and trousers with an elastic waistband for a snug fit to the body.
• The total weight of the undergarment is 2 pounds, 11 ounces.

### Issue Policy
• The CPU is primarily issued to:
- Special Forces units.
- Combat vehicle crews.

### Purpose and Use
• The CPU provides basic chemical protection by adsorbing vapors and particulates.
• It is worn as part of the MOPP gear system under other protective clothing layers for additional protection in NBC environments.
CPU,chemical protective undergarment,activated charcoal,NBC protection,MOPP gear

Soldier Preparation (1) -

Assess Soldier Factors for NBC Operations
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Soldier Factors Checklist:

• Hydration and Nutrition: Are soldiers well hydrated and nourished?
• Rest: Are soldiers well rested?
• Heat Acclimatization: Are soldiers heat acclimatized?
• Physical Fitness and Training: Are...
Soldier Factors Checklist:

• Hydration and Nutrition: Are soldiers well hydrated and nourished?
• Rest: Are soldiers well rested?
• Heat Acclimatization: Are soldiers heat acclimatized?
• Physical Fitness and Training: Are soldiers physically fit and well trained?
• Health Status: Are soldiers healthy?

Importance of Hydration:
Maintaining full hydration is the most important factor influencing work performance for soldiers wearing MOPP (Mission Oriented Protective Posture) in warm environments. Dehydration negates the advantages of heat acclimation and high physical fitness, impairs body temperature regulation, reduces mental and physical work performance, and increases susceptibility to heat injuries/illnesses.

Dehydration Impact:
Even a 2 percent loss of body weight due to dehydration degrades performance. Leaders should establish policies for enforced drinking, using guidelines from Tables 2-3 and 2-5. To reduce inadequate drinking in MOPP, especially when masked, leaders can:

• Use flexible masking policies (e.g., MOPP1--25 percent masked).
• Rotate masked and unmasked soldiers frequently.

Performance Assumptions:
The work/rest and maximum continuous work time guidance assumes that all troops are fully hydrated, heat acclimatized, physically fit, and fully rested. If these assumptions do not hold, estimates in the tables must be adjusted:

• Upward Adjustment: Required if soldiers are dehydrated, fatigued, or sleep-deprived (e.g., going without sleep for more than 30 hours impairs thermoregulation and performance).
• Downward Adjustment: May be necessary in cases of overtrained or experienced troops.

Performance Degradation Factors:
• Physical work: Multiply by 1.5.
• Mental work: Multiply by 2.5.

These factors should be considered when estimating time required for tasks under MOPP conditions.
hydration,nutrition,rest,heat acclimation,physical fitness,MOPP,performance degradation

Task Planning (1) -

Adjust Task Time for MOPP Conditions
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Scenario: An engineer unit must complete a river crossing site in two hours under MOPP4 conditions with a WBGT of 75°F.

Normal Task Time:
• Normally takes 90 minutes to complete the task.

Adjustment for Performance Degradation:
-...
Scenario: An engineer unit must complete a river crossing site in two hours under MOPP4 conditions with a WBGT of 75°F.

Normal Task Time:
• Normally takes 90 minutes to complete the task.

Adjustment for Performance Degradation:
• Physical work degradation factor: 1.5.
• Adjusted time = 1.5 × 90 minutes = 135 minutes (2 hours and 15 minutes).

Options to Meet Mission Guidance:
• Schedule additional engineer units to complete the job.
• Augment the unit with additional manpower.
• If liquid contamination hazard is low, adopt a lower MOPP posture to reduce performance degradation factor and allow mission completion within two hours.
MOPP,time adjustment,WBGT,performance degradation

Work Rest Cycle (1) -

Implement Work/Rest Cycles for Non-Contaminated Units
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Scenario: 1st and 2d platoons are not required to operate in MOPP, but the 3d platoon is in contaminated terrain.

Work/Rest Cycle Recommendations:
• For non-contaminated units: A work/rest cycle of 30 minutes work/30 minutes rest can be...
Scenario: 1st and 2d platoons are not required to operate in MOPP, but the 3d platoon is in contaminated terrain.

Work/Rest Cycle Recommendations:
• For non-contaminated units: A work/rest cycle of 30 minutes work/30 minutes rest can be implemented to complete the task with minimal casualties and degradation.

Heat Exhaustion Risk:
• If no rest breaks are taken, heat exhaustion casualties may not recover in the remaining hour even with complete rest and dehydration in the shade unless cooling systems and intravenous fluids are utilized.
work/rest cycle,heat exhaustion,rest breaks

Heat Casualty Response (1) -

Respond to Heat Casualties in MOPP Conditions
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Scenario: A platoon has been working at moderate intensity in MOPP4 for 45 minutes under clear, sunny skies with a WBGT of 70°F. Two cases of heat exhaustion have occurred in the past five minutes; many others have slowed the pace of...
Scenario: A platoon has been working at moderate intensity in MOPP4 for 45 minutes under clear, sunny skies with a WBGT of 70°F. Two cases of heat exhaustion have occurred in the past five minutes; many others have slowed the pace of work.

Heat Casualty Analysis (Figure 2-3):
• At 70°F WBGT, the unit should have been able to work for an additional 15 minutes before casualties were noted.
• One heat casualty is often quickly followed by many others. The mismatch between guidance and reality may be due to:

• Lack of adequate rest.
• Slight dehydration.
• Individual variance.
• Underestimate of work intensity.

Response Actions:
• Call for a rest break immediately.
• If mission success depends on task completion, and a 20 percent casualty rate is deemed acceptable:

1. Use Figure 2-3 to estimate remaining work time.
2. Find the point where the lower (5 percent) limit of the shaded area crosses the 45-minute line.
3. Read up from this point to find an effective WBGT of 85°F.
4. Note that 15 minutes of work may be possible before reaching the 20 percent casualty risk point.
heat exhaustion,MOPP,rest break,casualty response,WBGT

Environmental Monitoring (1) -

Correct WBGT Measurement Using WGT Kit (Botsball)
Reference Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Purpose: To correct the Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT) measurement when using the WGT kit (Botsball).

Procedure:
1. Use the following formula to calculate WBGT:
- WBGT = 0.8 x WGT + 0.2 x dry bulb temperature.
2. Measure the...
Purpose: To correct the Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT) measurement when using the WGT kit (Botsball).

Procedure:
1. Use the following formula to calculate WBGT:
- WBGT = 0.8 x WGT + 0.2 x dry bulb temperature.
2. Measure the dry bulb temperature by:
- Removing the dial thermometer from the Botsball.
- Reading the air temperature after three minutes, ensuring the sensor is shaded from direct sunlight.

Notes:
• This correction procedure was referenced in message SGPS-PSP, 23 May 1990.
• Accurate WBGT measurements are critical for assessing environmental conditions and protecting personnel.
WBGT correction, Botsball, dry bulb temperature, environmental monitoring

Chemical Attack Effects (1) -

Generate Effects Information for NBC Attacks
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Objective: Develop effects information based on time period/munition delivery estimate combinations.

Procedure Steps:
1. For each combination of time period and munition delivery estimates, generate the following:
- **Casualty...
Objective: Develop effects information based on time period/munition delivery estimate combinations.

Procedure Steps:
1. For each combination of time period and munition delivery estimates, generate the following:
- Casualty estimates: Estimate casualties based on factors such as target size, masking times, and agent type.
- Contamination barriers: Identify areas contaminated to a level that prevents crossing (e.g., four 200-meter by 400-meter areas).
- Persistence: Determine how long an area remains hazardous under different conditions (e.g., a particular contaminated area can be crossed in mask and summer uniform in four hours; it may be occupied in summer uniform, possibly without mask, in nine hours).
- Downwind agent effects: Subdivide each time period of interest into phases that correspond to specific chemical downwind hazard effects estimates.

Notes:
• These procedures are not specific to any particular attack or agent type and are designed for use across multiple theaters and levels of command.
• To determine the effect of a specific attack, refer to FM 3-3.
• Effects information helps units assess periods of high or low risk from chemical attacks based on AO (Area of Operations) and weather conditions.
NBC protection, chemical attack effects, casualty estimates, contamination barriers, persistence, downwind agent effects

Casualty Estimation (1) -

Determine Casualty Effects from NBC Attacks
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Objective: Estimate casualties based on agent delivery and environmental conditions.

Procedure Steps:
1. Use Tables 3-1, 3-2, and 3-3 to determine casualty estimates as a function of temperature and agent delivered (in kg/ha).
2. These...
Objective: Estimate casualties based on agent delivery and environmental conditions.

Procedure Steps:
1. Use Tables 3-1, 3-2, and 3-3 to determine casualty estimates as a function of temperature and agent delivered (in kg/ha).
2. These tables are valid for wind speeds less than 20 km/h.
3. Other factors such as air stability category, humidity, variations in wind speed under 20 km/h, and delivery errors have minimal impact on casualty estimates over a given time period of hours compared to specific instances in time.
4. Follow the procedures outlined in Figure 3-2 for determining casualty estimates.

Notes:
• Casualty estimates are based on assumptions such as ten 100-meter by 200-meter targets being attacked, with troops masking within 15 seconds suffering 10 percent casualties.
• These procedures are unclassified and generic in nature.
NBC protection, casualty estimation, chemical attack casualties, kg/ha agent delivery, wind speed effects

Shelter Selection (1) -

Selecting Buildings for Nuclear Hazard Shelter
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Choose buildings that offer the best protection from nuclear hazards:
- Preferred structures: Heavily framed buildings made of steel and reinforced concrete.
- Avoid: Shed-type industrial buildings with light frames...
Procedure:

1. Choose buildings that offer the best protection from nuclear hazards:
- Preferred structures: Heavily framed buildings made of steel and reinforced concrete.
- Avoid: Shed-type industrial buildings with light frames and long beam spans.
- Acceptable alternatives: Well-constructed frame houses, which are stronger than shed-type buildings.
- Exceptional protection: Ammunition storage bunkers, typically large enough to accommodate vehicles and equipment.

2. Consider European rural and urban structures:
- Pre-World War II farmhouses, churches, and municipal buildings provide good blast and radiation protection.
- Look for the following characteristics in these buildings:
- Pre-World War II design: Thick, full-span floor and ceiling beams; heavy roofing tiles; dense, reinforced walls; and a full basement.
- Full basements: Constructed of concrete or stone with an exit directly to the outside as well as through upper floors in case of emergency.
- Thick-walled masonry structures: Walls at least 36 centimeters (greater than 1 foot) thick are indicative of good pre-World War II construction. In areas where stucco finishes conceal features, look for diagonal supports and blockworks when the outside walls are wet.
- Buildings with minimal glass: European windows and doors typically have roll-up or folding shutters that provide additional blast and thermal protection.

3. Utilize shielded buildings:
- Buildings located in the interior of towns, surrounded by other structures, suffer less blast overpressure and structural damage than exposed buildings.
- However, be aware of increased debris, rubble, and fire hazards toward the center of town.
- Commanders should consider using shelters located two or three rows from the edge of town to avoid hindrance to postattack maneuver.
building selection,nuclear shelter,blast protection,radiation protection,pre-World War II buildings

Shelter Positioning (1) -

Optimal Positioning Within Buildings for Radiation and Blast Protection
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Position inside the building: If sufficient time is available to prepare it, positioning within the building can significantly reduce radiation exposure.
2. Aboveground floors: The center of the building offers the...
Procedure:

1. Position inside the building: If sufficient time is available to prepare it, positioning within the building can significantly reduce radiation exposure.
2. Aboveground floors: The center of the building offers the greatest protection from both initial and residual radiation.
3. Belowground levels: Corners of the building provide the greatest protection.
4. Posture matters:
- A prone soldier receives about one-half the dose compared to a standing soldier.
5. Shelter options:
- If an underground shelter is not available, lie in the center of a shelter under a sturdy table (see Figure 4-9).
- Other options include lying inside a fireplace, under a stairway, or in a bathroom where plumbing and close spacing of walls may provide increased structural strength.
radiation positioning,blast positioning,prone posture,underground shelter,aboveground shelter

Shelter Protection (1) -

Increase Tent Protection During Nuclear Emergencies
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Pile dirt and sandbags as far up the sides of the tent as possible to increase protection.
2. Lie on the floor inside the tent for better safety, especially for bed patients.
3. Ensure that all equipment and glassware are...
Procedure:

1. Pile dirt and sandbags as far up the sides of the tent as possible to increase protection.
2. Lie on the floor inside the tent for better safety, especially for bed patients.
3. Ensure that all equipment and glassware are secured within the tent to prevent them from being propelled by the blast wave and causing injury.
4. Avoid loose items such as small instruments, chairs, clipboards, and bottles inside the tent, as they can become dangerous projectiles during a blast.
5. Provide additional support around the center pole of the tent with sandbags to prevent breakage from the blast and ensure safe evacuation after the initial flash.
tent,nuclear,blast,sandbags,protection

Vehicle Sheltering (1) -

Protecting Personnel in Wheeled Vehicles During Nuclear Attacks
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Avoid using wheeled vehicles as shelter. They offer little or no protection from nuclear explosions and are vulnerable to overturning, increasing the risk of injury.
2. **If mission-essential activities must be conducted...
Procedure:

1. Avoid using wheeled vehicles as shelter. They offer little or no protection from nuclear explosions and are vulnerable to overturning, increasing the risk of injury.
2. If mission-essential activities must be conducted inside a vehicle, soldiers should:
- Wear kevlar helmets with chin straps secured to prevent head injuries in case of overturning.
3. Secure all loose equipment inside the vehicle (e.g., weapons, radios, desks, file cabinets, generators) to prevent injury from tipping or sliding during an accident.
4. Tie down, block, and brace equipment to minimize movement during sudden maneuvers or impacts.
5. Plan for and prepare adequate field shelters immediately adjacent to facilities where operations in wheeled vehicles are required.
6. Park the vehicle inside or under a shelter if possible to provide some protection to soldiers inside.
7. Use existing or natural structures such as ammunition bunkers, underpasses, tunnels, and caves for additional protection.
wheeled vehicles,nuclear attack,sheltering,soldier safety,equipment securing

Aircraft Protection (1) -

Protecting Aircraft During Nuclear Attacks
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Use revetments and barricades to protect aircraft from damage by dynamic wind, rocks, sand, other aircraft, or debris.
2. Revetments provide little protection against blast overpressure, but they are useful for...
Procedure:

1. Use revetments and barricades to protect aircraft from damage by dynamic wind, rocks, sand, other aircraft, or debris.
2. Revetments provide little protection against blast overpressure, but they are useful for conventional weapons blast and fragmentation damage.
3. Use overhead cover for aircraft if available to increase protection.
4. Close all doors and windows on the aircraft to protect against damaging overpressure and thermal radiation exposure.
5. Use tiedowns to reduce damage from tumbling of the aircraft, ensuring they do not produce excessive stress on tiedown points.
6. Tape the edges and centers of plexiglass windows to prevent shattering into fragments at low blast overpressure (1.5 pounds per square inch). This reduces fragmentation hazards for cockpit operations.
aircraft,nuclear attack,revetments,tiedowns,window taping

Nuclear Attack Response (1) -

Immediate Actions During a Nuclear Detonation
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

When exposed to a nuclear detonation without warning:
1. Immediately drop facedown on the ground. Use natural cover such as logs, rocks, or depressions in the earth for added protection.
2. Close your eyes to protect them...
Procedure:

When exposed to a nuclear detonation without warning:
1. Immediately drop facedown on the ground. Use natural cover such as logs, rocks, or depressions in the earth for added protection.
2. Close your eyes to protect them from intense light and debris.
3. Protect exposed skin from heat by placing hands and arms under or near the body. Keep the helmet on at all times.
4. Remain facedown until the blast wave passes and debris stops falling.
5. Stay calm, check for injuries, assess damage to weapons and equipment, and prepare to continue the mission.

Additional Precautions in Foxholes:
• Soldiers in foxholes should curl up on one side or lie on their back with knees drawn up to the chest (see Figure 4-11).
• The belly-up position is more vulnerable but offers better protection due to the radiation resistance of arms and legs.
• Store bulky equipment such as packs or radios in adjacent pits if they prevent soldiers from getting low in foxholes. Alternatively, place these items over the face and hands for additional blast and radiation protection.

Additional Precautions in Shelters:
• Lying facedown on the floor of the shelter offers worthwhile protection against blast waves and debris.
• Avoid standing near or facing doors or windows to prevent injury from violent air flow.
• Lying near a wall may increase pressure wave intensity due to reflection, but it is safer than being blown out of the shelter.
• Constructing baffles or turns in shelter entrances can help reduce overpressure buildups and entry of dust and debris (see Figure 4-12).
nuclear attack, blast wave, radiation protection, foxhole position, shelter safety

Biological Attack Response (1) -

Responding to a Biological Attack
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
After a biological attack, take samples using the M34, M256-series, or CBASK devices. Identify casualties by their symptoms and treat them accordingly. Early recognition of symptoms and prompt treatment will increase recovery time and reduce...
After a biological attack, take samples using the M34, M256-series, or CBASK devices. Identify casualties by their symptoms and treat them accordingly. Early recognition of symptoms and prompt treatment will increase recovery time and reduce fatalities.

Isolate soldiers showing symptoms of disease to prevent possible spread if the disease is communicable. Limit the number of personnel providing care to these casualties. Treatment of live biological agent or toxin casualties requires immediate medical assistance.

An indication of a live biological agent attack is a large number of soldiers and civilians with unexplained illness over a short period. Soldiers should decontaminate immediately after a toxin attack using soap and water, the M258A1, or M291 decon kits.

Self-aid and buddy-aid vary depending on the agent. First, mask to prevent inhaling or ingesting agents. Remove agents from exposed skin, observe each other for early symptoms of toxic exposure, and request medical assistance.
biological attack, decontamination, M258A1, M291, M34, CBASK, isolation, symptoms, medical aid

Toxin Symptoms (1) -

Recognizing Toxin Symptoms
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Symptoms associated with some toxins include:

• Dizziness, mental confusion, or double or blurred vision.
• Tingling of skin, numbness, paralysis, or convulsions.
• Formation of rashes or blisters.
• Coughing.
• Fever,...
Symptoms associated with some toxins include:

• Dizziness, mental confusion, or double or blurred vision.
• Tingling of skin, numbness, paralysis, or convulsions.
• Formation of rashes or blisters.
• Coughing.
• Fever, aching muscles, and fatigue. *Difficulty in swallowing.*
• Nausea, vomiting, and/or diarrhea.
• Bleeding from body openings or blood in urine, stool, or sputum (spit).
• Shock.

These symptoms appear in minutes or hours after a toxin attack.
toxins, symptoms, dizziness, numbness, paralysis, convulsions, rash, coughing, fever, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, bleeding, shock

Collective Protection Types (1) -

Types of Collective Protection Equipment (CPE)
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Collective-protection equipment (CPE) provides protection to a group of individuals under NBC conditions that permits relaxation of individual NBC protection.

Under NBC conditions, CPE allows soldiers to function effectively. CPE comes to a...
Collective-protection equipment (CPE) provides protection to a group of individuals under NBC conditions that permits relaxation of individual NBC protection.

Under NBC conditions, CPE allows soldiers to function effectively. CPE comes to a unit as either a component of a piece of equipment or as a TOE asset. This includes equipment such as the M51 shelter and the M20 SCPE.

Used together, these systems enhance a unit's capability to perform its mission in an NBC environment. There are four basic types of CPE: vent ventilated-facepiece, overpressure, hybrid, and total systems (Table 6-1). See Table 6-2 for the advantages and disadvantages of each system.
CPE types, M51 shelter, M20 SCPE, vent ventilated-facepiece, overpressure, hybrid, total systems

Ventilated Facepiece System (1) -

Ventilated-Facepiece System Overview
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Ventilated-facepiece systems supply filtered air to the protective mask canisters (both the M25A1 and M42 masks) of combat vehicle crew members and the M24 and M43 aircrew protective masks. The systems are assigned as GPFUS and...
Ventilated-facepiece systems supply filtered air to the protective mask canisters (both the M25A1 and M42 masks) of combat vehicle crew members and the M24 and M43 aircrew protective masks. The systems are assigned as GPFUS and are rated by their airflow capacity, in cubic feet per minute.

The currently fielded systems are given in Table 6-3. Except for the M13A I GPFU, the components of these systems are similar.

The filtered, pressurized air supplied to individuals extends the MOPP gear's capabilities. It reduces breathing resistance through masks and aids in sweat evaporation. In addition, it can provide warm air to facepieces in cold weather.
ventilated-facepiece system, M25A1 mask, M42 mask, M24 mask, M43 mask, GPFUS, airflow capacity, MOPP gear

Heat Stress Management (1) -

Implement Cooling Systems for Heat Stress Reduction
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Cooling systems are used to reduce heat stress in soldiers operating in extremely hot and/or humid conditions, especially when wearing MOPP gear. There are two basic types of cooling systems:

1. Crew compartment cooling: Provides air...
Cooling systems are used to reduce heat stress in soldiers operating in extremely hot and/or humid conditions, especially when wearing MOPP gear. There are two basic types of cooling systems:

1. Crew compartment cooling: Provides air conditioning to the compartment.
2. Individual cooling: More effective when used while wearing MOPP gear.

The choice of cooling system depends on the vehicle type and primary mission. The next generation of combat vehicles will provide both individual and compartment cooling systems.
cooling, heat stress, MOPP gear, crew compartment, individual cooling

Entry Exit Procedures (1) -

Entry and Exit Procedures for Collective-Protection Systems
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Entry and exit procedures are slow and risky; therefore, the commander must allow only mission-essential soldiers to enter and exit. These procedures transition between individual protection and collective protection. Step-by-step instructions...
Entry and exit procedures are slow and risky; therefore, the commander must allow only mission-essential soldiers to enter and exit. These procedures transition between individual protection and collective protection. Step-by-step instructions ensure a safe transition.

Two possible MOPP gear ensemble combinations are used in examples:

1. Ground-troop IPE: Field protective mask with hood, carrier, helmet with chemical protective cover, individual weapon, armored vest (if worn), and MOPP gear.
2. Combat vehicle and aircrew IPE: Tank or aircraft mask with hood, combat vehicle crewman or aircraft crewman helmet, individual weapon, armored vest (if worn), and MOPP gear.

Each ensemble and type of enclosure has characteristics that dictate different steps. Procedures for a particular combination are a composite of general guidelines for individual and collective protection. Entry and exit procedures in this chapter illustrate the necessity to modify procedures based on their application and system configuration.
