Actual procedures for a specific system should be more specific, as outlined in the system's technical manual and unit SOP (Standard Operating Procedure). Guidelines for an SOP are at Appendix B.
entry exit procedures, MOPP gear, collective protection systems, SOP guidelines, technical manual

Shelter Setup (1) -

Setting Up a Collective-Protection Shelter or Van with an Air Lock
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Select a site for shelter erection that is free of liquid contamination. Soldiers setting up the shelter (either M51 or SCPE) should perform steps 1 through 9 of the entry procedures before entering the selected set-up site or handling...
Select a site for shelter erection that is free of liquid contamination. Soldiers setting up the shelter (either M51 or SCPE) should perform steps 1 through 9 of the entry procedures before entering the selected set-up site or handling unpackaged liners or support-kit components.

• If setting up a shelter where the external agent concentration produces a relative hazard reading of one bar or less on the CAM (indication that no agent is present), entry into the shelter is unlimited.
• Where the external concentration of agent produces a CAM reading of seven bars or more, entries should be discontinued unless they are mission essential.

If liquid contamination is detected or suspected on overgarments:
1. Establish a hot line at least 4 feet from the air lock.
2. Check the floor area between the hot line and the entrance for evidence of liquid contamination using both visual checks and detector/monitoring equipment.
3. If contamination is present, decontaminate this area, cover it with a plastic sheet, poncho, or similar material, or find another area if possible.
4. If possible, remove overgarments in a room or covered area that is separate from the room where the entrance is located and establish the hot line at the doorway between the two rooms.
5. Keep the room with the air lock as clean as possible.
shelter setup, air lock procedures, liquid contamination, hot line establishment, decontamination of area

Equipment Handling (1) -

Equipment Handling for Collective-Protection Shelters or Vans
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Allow no equipment to enter the shelter unless it is known to be free of contamination.

• Pre-position decon kits, alarms, detector kit samplers, and a CAM inside the air lock.
• These components require periodic replenishment,...
Allow no equipment to enter the shelter unless it is known to be free of contamination.

• Pre-position decon kits, alarms, detector kit samplers, and a CAM inside the air lock.
• These components require periodic replenishment, depending on the frequency of entries.
• The CAM will require fresh batteries based on technical manual guidance.
equipment handling, decon kits, CAM battery replacement, air lock pre-positioning

Safety Precautions (1) -

WARNING: Undressing Area Ventilation and Contamination Avoidance
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
WARNING: Ensure the undressing area is well ventilated, and remove contaminated overgarment from the hot line area to avoid buildup of vapor.

WARNING: Do not touch the eye lens area or the canister hose. These are difficult to decontaminate...
WARNING: Ensure the undressing area is well ventilated, and remove contaminated overgarment from the hot line area to avoid buildup of vapor.

WARNING: Do not touch the eye lens area or the canister hose. These are difficult to decontaminate and are potential transfer hazards.
undressing area,ventilation,contamination,eye lens,canister hose,safety precautions

Cold Weather Operations (1) -

Operations in Special Environments: Cold Weather and NBC Protection
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Weather and terrain must be considered for NBC protection. Certain weather conditions influence the use of NBC weapons, and different types of terrain alter their effects. The type of operation also directly affects the need for NBC...
Weather and terrain must be considered for NBC protection. Certain weather conditions influence the use of NBC weapons, and different types of terrain alter their effects. The type of operation also directly affects the need for NBC protection.

Cold Weather Operations:
• Cold weather creates new problems in individual protection. These conditions may exist in the extreme northern United States, Alaska, and northern Europe.
• These conditions alter planning and implementation of individual NBC defensive measures.

Nuclear Effects in Cold Weather:
Blast Effects:
• At subzero temperatures, the radius of damage to material targets can increase by as much as 20 percent. Targets include tanks, IFVs, APCs, artillery, and military vehicles.
• A precursor wave over heat-absorbing surfaces can increase dynamic pressure waves. However, tundra, irregular terrain features, and broken ice caps break up the pressure wave.
• Blast effects interfere with troop movement, may break up covers, cause quick thaws, and lead to avalanches in mountainous areas or disrupt movement on flat lands due to disturbed permafrost.

Thermal Effects:
• Ice and snow have high reflectivity, increasing minimum safe distance by as much as 50 percent for unwarned troops and even warned, exposed troops.
• Reflectivity increases the number of personnel affected by light dazzle, especially at night.
• Pale colors used in cold environments provide an advantage due to low absorption properties, reducing vulnerability to thermal effects.
• Cold temperatures reduce thermal effects on materials. Snow, ice, and frost coverings on combustible material reduce fire risk but may dry out tundra areas, leading to grass fires.

Radiation Effects:
• Limited roadways are further restricted by radiological contamination in cold weather.
• Seasonal high winds in the arctic affect radiological contamination predictions, reducing dose rates at ground zero but extending coverage and creating problems for survey/monitoring teams.
• Hot spots may occur in areas of heavy snow and drifts.

Monitoring Procedures:
• Battery-powered radiac equipment must be kept warm to maintain efficiency in extreme cold.
• Radiological surveys are limited to occupied or used areas by large units. Aerial survey is most practical in extreme cold weather areas.
NBC, cold weather, nuclear effects, radiological contamination, blast effects, thermal effects, radiation monitoring

Biological Agents (1) -

Protecting Against Biological Agents in Arctic Conditions
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Be vigilant for covert delivery methods: Since biological agents are more likely to be delivered by sabotage or covert means in arctic conditions, maintain a high level of situational awareness.
2. **Monitor temperature...
Procedure:

1. Be vigilant for covert delivery methods: Since biological agents are more likely to be delivered by sabotage or covert means in arctic conditions, maintain a high level of situational awareness.
2. Monitor temperature inversions: Be aware that temperature inversions over snowfields can prolong the integrity of aerosolized biological clouds, increasing their threat duration.
3. Maintain hygiene and health standards:
- Ensure adequate food, water, rest, and cleanliness for personnel to reduce vulnerability to disease outbreaks.
- Prevent dehydration, malnutrition, or fatigue that may increase susceptibility to biological agents.
4. Be alert to increased risk of disease spread: In arctic environments, diseases can spread rapidly in warm, crowded conditions. Implement strict hygiene protocols and isolate sick individuals promptly.
5. Prioritize toxin preparedness: Toxins are more stable at low temperatures than live microorganisms, so be prepared for potential toxin-based biological attacks.
biological agents, arctic conditions, toxins, disease prevention, hygiene protocols

Desert Operations (1) -

Plan for Desert Operations Considering Temperature and NBC Threats
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure for Planning Desert Operations:

1. Account for Extreme Daytime Temperatures:
- Desert daytime temperatures can range from 90° F to 125° F (32° C to 52° C).
- These extreme temperatures create an unstable temperature...
Procedure for Planning Desert Operations:

1. Account for Extreme Daytime Temperatures:
- Desert daytime temperatures can range from 90° F to 125° F (32° C to 52° C).
- These extreme temperatures create an unstable temperature gradient, which is not favorable for NBC attacks.

2. Consider Night and Early Morning Attacks:
- The desert cools rapidly at night, creating a stable temperature gradient.
- Plan for the possibility of NBC attacks during night or early morning hours in all operations.

3. Plan for Fortifications in Desert Areas:
- Lack of vegetation and permanent structures (e.g., forests, buildings) necessitates planning for fortifications to provide nuclear defense.
- Use sand combined with sandbags to construct protective barriers against radiation exposure.

4. Monitor for Radiological Contamination from Wind and Sand:
- Blowing winds and sands can spread radiological contamination over large areas.
- Varying terrain may make it difficult to monitor radiological survey data effectively.

5. Be Aware of Biological Agent Effectiveness in Deserts:
- Most aerosolized live biological agents are ineffective due to low humidity and UV radiation from direct sunlight.
- Spore-forming agents remain a threat, especially during covert night attacks.
- Toxins are resistant to desert conditions and can be used similarly to chemical agents.

6. Plan for Chemical Attacks in Desert Environments:
- Most chemical attacks will be spot or on-target due to rapid agent evaporation.
- For example, mustard evaporates rapidly at 90° F (32° C) with light winds, reducing its concentration by over 50% within an hour.

7. Be Aware of Porous Desert Soil and Agent Behavior:
- Desert soil may be highly porous, allowing liquid agents to be absorbed quickly.
- During predawn attacks, the agent can be soaked into the ground and later evaporate with sunrise, creating a downwind hazard.

8. Implement Alarm Systems for Maximum Coverage:
- Place alarm systems strategically to cover as wide an area as possible.

9. Plan Strenuous Activities During Night Hours:
- Conduct strenuous activities at night to reduce heat stress from wearing MOPP gear.

10. Ensure Soldiers Are Masked and Checked Periodically at Night:
- Ensure sleeping soldiers are masked if appropriate.
- Check periodically to ensure mask seals remain intact, as attacks are more likely at night.
- Use the buddy system or have guards check soldiers during rounds.
- The unit SOP must address this requirement.
desert operations,temperature gradient,nbc threats,fortifications,sandbags,radiological contamination,biological agents,chemical attacks,mopp gear

Jungle Operations (1) -

Implement Jungle Operations NBC Defense Procedures
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure for Implementing NBC Defense in Jungle Environments:

1. Maintain Individual Discipline and Conditioning:
- Tropical climates require the highest degree of individual discipline and conditioning to maintain effective NBC defense...
Procedure for Implementing NBC Defense in Jungle Environments:

1. Maintain Individual Discipline and Conditioning:
- Tropical climates require the highest degree of individual discipline and conditioning to maintain effective NBC defense readiness.

2. Account for High Temperature, Humidity, and Rainfall:
- Jungle areas are characterized by high, constant temperatures; heavy rainfall; and very high humidity.
- Little or no wind is common in thick jungle environments.
- The canopy blocks much of the sunlight from reaching the ground.

3. Plan for Decreased Unit Efficiency:
- Commanders must expect a rapid decrease in unit efficiency due to environmental conditions.

4. Prevent Heat Casualties:
- Plan and implement measures to prevent heat casualties among troops.

5. Maintain NBC Defensive Equipment in Usable Condition:
- Special precautions are required to keep NBC defensive equipment functional in jungle environments.
- Rapid mildew, dry rot, and rust can damage equipment if not properly maintained.
jungle operations,nbc defense,equipment maintenance,heat casualties,mildew,dry rot,rust,tropical climate

Mountain Operations (1) -

Prepare for NBC Defense in Mountainous Terrain
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Recognize that mountainous terrain requires a high degree of NBC defense preparedness due to rugged conditions and limited support.
2. Understand that small units must be nearly self-sufficient in NBC protection because of...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that mountainous terrain requires a high degree of NBC defense preparedness due to rugged conditions and limited support.
2. Understand that small units must be nearly self-sufficient in NBC protection because of logistical challenges and reduced mobility.
3. Be aware that nuclear targeting is easier in mountainous areas due to the lack of roads, trails, and slow troop movement.
4. Prepare for improvised shelters made from snow, ice, or rocks as a means of protection in rocky or frozen ground.
5. Recognize that radiological contamination may be erratic due to rapidly changing wind patterns, creating hot spots far from the detonation point.
mountains,NBC defense,radiological contamination,improvised shelters

Urban Operations (1) -

Plan NBC Defense in Urban Terrain Operations
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Understand that urban terrain significantly affects NBC defense planning and mission execution.
2. Familiarize yourself with the unique challenges of urban environments, such as limited mobility, restricted lines of sight, and...
Procedure:

1. Understand that urban terrain significantly affects NBC defense planning and mission execution.
2. Familiarize yourself with the unique challenges of urban environments, such as limited mobility, restricted lines of sight, and potential for contamination to be trapped within buildings or confined spaces.
3. Develop contingency plans for decontamination, evacuation, and sheltering in place based on the specific characteristics of the urban area.
4. Coordinate with local authorities and emergency services to ensure a unified response to NBC threats in urban settings.
urban terrain,NBC defense,decontamination,evacuation,sheltering

Airborne Operations (1) -

Planning Airborne and Air-Assault Operations in NBC Environments
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Avoid massing troops: Do not concentrate large numbers of troops or aircraft near small landing zones (LZs) or drop zones (DZs), as this increases vulnerability to NBC attacks.
2. Contaminated LZ/DZ considerations: If...
Procedure:

1. Avoid massing troops: Do not concentrate large numbers of troops or aircraft near small landing zones (LZs) or drop zones (DZs), as this increases vulnerability to NBC attacks.
2. Contaminated LZ/DZ considerations: If enemy forces may contaminate suitable LZs or DZs with persistent chemical agents, plan for the use of MOPP gear and decontamination procedures.
3. Use multiple LZ/DZs simultaneously: Deploy small elements using several LZs or DZs at once to avoid presenting large targets for nuclear weapons.
4. Avoid massing until final assault: After landing, approach objectives via separate routes and only mass troops at the last possible moment before the assault.
5. Plan for MOPP gear relief: Consider either a quick extraction of forces or a rapid linkup with ground mobile task forces to allow for removal from extended wear of MOPP gear.
airborne,assault,nuclear,chemical,MOPP,gear,decontamination,LZ,DZ

Sop Development (1) -

Develop NBC Portion of a Collective-Protection System SOP
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Title: Develop NBC Portion of a Collective-Protection System SOP

Each unit should modify and expand this guidance to develop its own Standard Operating Procedure (SOP). The SOP must consider the following:

1. Type and location of the...
Title: Develop NBC Portion of a Collective-Protection System SOP

Each unit should modify and expand this guidance to develop its own Standard Operating Procedure (SOP). The SOP must consider the following:

1. Type and location of the shelter or van.
2. Frequency of and requirements for entries and exits.
3. General climate of the area of operations—temperate, arctic, tropic, or desert.
4. Local environmental conditions.
5. Number of nonshelter personnel assigned to the same site as the shelter or van.

The SOP should delineate operational details of a shelter or van equipped for collective protection. For example, details must include NBC-related duties of a guard (where applicable) and entry and exit procedures.
NBC,SOP,shelter,van,collective protection

Combat Integration (1) -

General Guidance for Integration of NBC Operations with Combat Mission Operations
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Title: General Guidance for Integration of NBC Operations with Combat Mission Operations

Provide general guidance for integration of NBC operations with combat mission operations. This could include the following:

• During active chemical...
Title: General Guidance for Integration of NBC Operations with Combat Mission Operations

Provide general guidance for integration of NBC operations with combat mission operations. This could include the following:

• During active chemical operations, the shelter guard checks all soldiers for contamination using a chemical agent detector or detector paper.
• Unless the absence of contamination can be verified, assume soldiers are contaminated and they must perform specific entry procedures.
• Detector paper detects agents only in liquid form (most likely thickened liquid agents). Unthickened agents absorb into overgarment materials and cannot be detected by detector paper. However, agent vapor may desorb from clothing inside shelters or vans and present vapor hazards.
• The CAM can detect desorbing vapors.
• One of the guard's main NBC duties is assisting entering soldiers. He must help them decontaminate and remove contaminated overgarments. Therefore, he must check himself periodically for contamination, especially his gloves.
NBC operations,combat integration,chemical agent detection,CAM

Entry Eligibility (1) -

Preliminary Steps for Deciding Entry Eligibility
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Title: Preliminary Steps for Deciding Entry Eligibility

Indicate steps required to decide if a person should be allowed entry. You could use the following in your SOP:

1. Use a chemical agent detector or detector paper, and check for the...
Title: Preliminary Steps for Deciding Entry Eligibility

Indicate steps required to decide if a person should be allowed entry. You could use the following in your SOP:

1. Use a chemical agent detector or detector paper, and check for the presence of liquid chemical agent on soldiers.
2. Check each soldier's weapon, overgarment, gloves, mask and hood assembly, helmet, and overboots. A buddy or guard assists the soldier when necessary.
3. After the detector check, store contaminated items outside. Use a protective cover (such as plastic or canvas) near the entrance, and ensure items are not in the path of the entrance door.
4. Carefully evaluate the need for grossly contaminated soldiers to enter. Consider possible interior contamination and resulting degradation. Determine if these will be offset by combat mission performance of entering soldiers.
entry eligibility,contamination check,preliminary steps

Chemical Agent Identification (1) -

Identify Field Concentrations of Chemical Agents
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
To positively identify field concentrations of blood, blister, and nerve agents, use an M256, M256A1, or M256A2 detector kit. Almost all biological agents require a laboratory facility for identification. Nuclear radiation is measured by...
To positively identify field concentrations of blood, blister, and nerve agents, use an M256, M256A1, or M256A2 detector kit. Almost all biological agents require a laboratory facility for identification. Nuclear radiation is measured by the unit's radiac instruments.
chemical agent, identification, M256 detector kit, radiac instrument

Radiation Hazard (1) -

Understand Gamma Radiation Hazard
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Gamma radiation is electromagnetic emissions of short wavelength from the nucleus of the atom. The hazard is primarily internal. Gamma rays are the soldier's primary radiation hazard on the battlefield.
gamma radiation, nuclear hazard, internal exposure

Chemical Agent Effects (1) -

Understand Nerve Agent Effects
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
A nerve agent is a lethal chemical that causes paralysis by interfering with the transmission of nerve impulse.
nerve agent, paralysis, neurotoxin

Chemical Agent Characteristics (1) -

Understand Nonpersistent Agent Characteristics
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
A nonpersistent agent is a chemical agent that, when released, dissipates and/or loses its ability to cause casualties after a passage of 10 to 15 minutes.
nonpersistent agent, chemical warfare, short duration

Protective Posture (1) -

Understand the Mask-Only Posture
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
The mask-only protective posture provides some relief from MOPP gear for personnel who must work in a contaminated environment. Personnel must be within protective shelters, some kinds of vans, tanks, or buildings where danger of transfer...
The mask-only protective posture provides some relief from MOPP gear for personnel who must work in a contaminated environment. Personnel must be within protective shelters, some kinds of vans, tanks, or buildings where danger of transfer hazards is minimal. A soldier in mask-only posture can tolerate exposure to vapor hazards but not transfer hazards. Mask-only permits longer work periods, but personnel must assume full MOPP level before exiting their sheltered area.
mask only posture, vapor exposure, transfer hazards

Field Manuals (FMs):
1. FM 3-3, NBC Contamination Avoidance
2. FM 3-4-1, Fixed Site Protection
3. FM 3-5, NBC Decontamination...

Soldier Training Publications (STPs):

2. STP 21-24-SMCT, Soldier's Manual of Common Tasks, Skill Levels 2-4
3. STP 21-II-MQS, Military Qualification Standards II, Manual of Common Tasks.

Technical Manuals (TMs):

2. TM 3-4240-241-12, Operator's and Organizational Maintenance Manual for Filter Unit, Gas-Particulate... ABC-M6Al

4. TM 3-4240-279-10, Operator's Manual: Mask, Chemical-Biological: Field ABC-M17, M17A1, and M17A2

required publications,nbc protection,field manuals,soldier training publications,technical manuals

Contamination Types (1) -

Identify Different Forms of Contamination
Standard Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: Recognize the different forms of contamination to properly assess decontamination needs.

Forms of Contamination:
1. Solids – Radioactive particles, biological spores, or dusty agents that may appear as a fine dust.
2....
Objective: Recognize the different forms of contamination to properly assess decontamination needs.

Forms of Contamination:
1. Solids – Radioactive particles, biological spores, or dusty agents that may appear as a fine dust.
2. Liquids – Liquid droplets that fall like rain. Droplets can range from thick and sticky to the consistency of water.
3. Vapors or Gases – Created by bursting munitions or generators. These clouds are affected by weather and can cover large areas.
4. Aerosols – Fine liquids or solid particles suspended in the air. They behave much like vapors.
contamination forms,nuclear,biological,chemical,vapors,aerosols,solids,liquids

Planning Decontamination (1) -

Plan for Thorough Decontamination Operations
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Objective: Develop a comprehensive plan to conduct thorough-decon operations based on METT-TC factors and resource availability.

Procedure Steps:
1. All echelons must prepare for thorough-decon operations as part of the overall planning...
Objective: Develop a comprehensive plan to conduct thorough-decon operations based on METT-TC factors and resource availability.

Procedure Steps:
1. All echelons must prepare for thorough-decon operations as part of the overall planning process.
2. The chemical staff develops the decon plan based on the commander’s guidance and coordination with higher HQ to determine engineer support availability for site preparation and closure.
3. Coordinate with civil-affairs offices for host-nation support (personnel, equipment, supplies) and environmental requirements.
4. Select possible decon sites throughout the unit’s area of operation (AO) based on:
- Decon type
- Terrain
- Mission requirements
- Threat assessment
- Road network availability
- Water availability
5. Choose linkup points to support each site; a single site may have multiple linkup points.
6. Establish a priority of decon support based on the commander’s intent and operational phases.
7. Develop a priority of work for equipment decontamination, typically ordered by:
- Engineer equipment
- Artillery pieces
- Main battle tanks
- Long-haul vehicles
8. Consider water availability as a limiting factor; a typical vehicle may require up to 500 gallons of water during DED operations.
9. Develop a water resupply plan for thorough-decon operations, which can include:
- Selecting linkup points along routes for bulk water truck support
- Caching water throughout the AO
- Coordinating aircraft movement of water bladders
- Identifying available water sources in the unit’s AO
10. Consider using nonpotable, salt, or brackish water if necessary.
thorough decon planning, DED, DTD, water resupply, nuclear cbrn

Biological Decontamination (1) -

Establish Mortuary Affairs Decontamination Collection Point (MADCP)
Critical Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

To establish and operate the MADCP:

1. Set up the collection point: Mortuary affairs personnel must establish a MADCP in areas affected by NBC events.
2. Treat remains as contaminated: If it is not possible to determine...
Procedure:

To establish and operate the MADCP:

1. Set up the collection point: Mortuary affairs personnel must establish a MADCP in areas affected by NBC events.
2. Treat remains as contaminated: If it is not possible to determine individual contamination status, all remains within the affected area should be treated as if contaminated.
3. Follow theater surgeon directives: If biological agents are confirmed or suspected, all remains will be treated as contaminated based on orders from the theater surgeon or his staff.
4. Coordinate with JMAO: The unified commander must direct the Joint Mortuary Affairs Office (JMAO) to prepare for handling contaminated remains in NBC theaters of operations.
5. Follow joint procedures: Use Joint Publication 4-06 as a guide for joint operations involving contaminated remains.

Notes:
• All remains from an NBC event must be handled with extreme care and decontaminated before processing or transport.
• The MADCP site requires complete decon by chemical decon units after the evacuation mission is completed.
MADCP, contaminated remains, JMAO, NBC event, decon procedure

Environmental Conditions (1) -

Decontaminant Freezing Point Considerations in Cold-Weather Areas
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Managing Decontaminants in Cold and Arctic Weather Conditions:

1. Understand Freezing Points of Decontaminants:
- Water: Freezes at 32°F.
- STB slurry with antiset: Freezes at 0°F.
- DS2: Freezes at -25°F.
- SKD:...
Procedure for Managing Decontaminants in Cold and Arctic Weather Conditions:

1. Understand Freezing Points of Decontaminants:
- Water: Freezes at 32°F.
- STB slurry with antiset: Freezes at 0°F.
- DS2: Freezes at -25°F.
- SKD: Freezes at -50°F.
- IEDK: Freezes at -50°F.

2. Select Appropriate Decontaminants Based on Temperature:
- In cold and arctic environments, choose decontaminants with lower freezing points to ensure effectiveness in low temperatures.

3. Account for Environmental Impact on Chemical Agents:
- Low temperatures increase the persistence of chemical agents (e.g., sarin can remain a transfer hazard for up to 30 days in arctic climates).
- Frozen chemical agents may not present immediate hazards but become dangerous when they warm up.

4. Adjust Decontamination Strategies:
- Low temperatures reduce the effectiveness of decontaminants, so alternative methods or more robust decontaminants should be considered.
cold weather, arctic conditions, freezing points, decontaminant effectiveness, chemical persistence, sarin, temperature impact

Detection Equipment (1) -

Using the M256A1 Detector Kit in Cold Weather Conditions
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Performance limitations at low temperatures: As temperatures approach freezing (32°F/0°C), the M256A1 detector kit may take longer to provide a positive or negative indication.
2. Cold weather handling:
- Carry liquid...
Procedure:

1. Performance limitations at low temperatures: As temperatures approach freezing (32°F/0°C), the M256A1 detector kit may take longer to provide a positive or negative indication.
2. Cold weather handling:
- Carry liquid components of the M256A1 kit in the breast pockets of chemical protective clothing to maintain functionality.
- If necessary, place the test sampler back into its shipping bag and put it on a heated surface to hasten the reaction.
3. Temperature limit: The M256A1 detector kit will not function at temperatures below -25°F (-31.7°C).
M256A1 detector kit, cold weather, temperature limit, liquid components, heated surface

Storage Protection (1) -

Storage of Ammunition and Supplies to Prevent NBC Contamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Storing Ammunition and Supplies:

1. Store all ammunition and other supplies under cover to protect them from NBC contamination.
2. Ensure that storage areas are secure and shielded from potential exposure to chemical, biological,...
Procedure for Storing Ammunition and Supplies:

1. Store all ammunition and other supplies under cover to protect them from NBC contamination.
2. Ensure that storage areas are secure and shielded from potential exposure to chemical, biological, or radiological agents.
3. Use appropriate contamination covers as detailed in FM 3-4 for additional protection.

Important Considerations:
• Proper storage is essential to prevent the degradation of supplies and ensure operational readiness.
• Contamination covers should be used according to guidelines provided in FM 3-4.
ammunition, supplies, storage, NBC contamination, contamination covers

Mountain Decontamination (1) -

Decontamination Operations in Mountainous Terrain
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Conducting Decontamination in Mountains:

1. Assess the terrain and determine if mountain operations require decentralization due to disrupted routes.
2. Ensure that decon operations are supported by additional water-carrying...
Procedure for Conducting Decontamination in Mountains:

1. Assess the terrain and determine if mountain operations require decentralization due to disrupted routes.
2. Ensure that decon operations are supported by additional water-carrying vehicles, as needed.
3. Organize forces to be self-sufficient with decon elements attached initially at the brigade or battalion level.
4. If decon elements operate independently, ensure they have greater hauling capabilities for water and administrative/logistical support from a designated unit.

Important Considerations:
• Mountainous terrain can significantly impact decontamination operations due to limited routes and variable weather conditions.
• Decon platoons should be attached to brigades, while decon squads are attached to battalions.
mountains, decontamination, water-carrying vehicles, self-sufficiency, logistics

Temperature Effects (1) -

Impact of Temperature on Decontamination in Mountainous Areas
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Managing Temperature Effects During Decontamination:

1. Recognize that cool or cold temperatures negatively affect decontamination operations.
2. Understand that temperature drops approximately 3[o] to 5[o] F with every 328-foot...
Procedure for Managing Temperature Effects During Decontamination:

1. Recognize that cool or cold temperatures negatively affect decontamination operations.
2. Understand that temperature drops approximately 3[o] to 5[o] F with every 328-foot gain in elevation, and this drop is more pronounced in drier air.
3. Be aware of the significant temperature differences (40[o] to 50[o] F) between sunny and shaded areas at high altitudes.
4. Schedule daytime operations to avoid low-temperature challenges after sunrise or before sunset.
5. Implement decon procedures similar to those used in cold-weather operations if temperatures are extremely low.

Important Considerations:
• Low temperatures can significantly slow down decontamination processes and require special precautions.
• Adjust operational schedules based on temperature fluctuations to ensure efficiency.
mountains, temperature, cold weather, elevation, decon procedures

Sunlight Effects (1) -

Effect of Sunlight on Biological Agents in Mountainous Areas
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Managing the Effect of Sunlight on Biological Agents:

1. Recognize that sunlight is highly effective at destroying most biological agents.
2. Understand that above the timberline, there is little protection from the sun, which...
Procedure for Managing the Effect of Sunlight on Biological Agents:

1. Recognize that sunlight is highly effective at destroying most biological agents.
2. Understand that above the timberline, there is little protection from the sun, which reduces the effectiveness of a biological attack.
3. Use this knowledge to plan decontamination operations and assess risks in high-altitude areas.

Important Considerations:
• Sunlight can significantly reduce the threat posed by biological agents in exposed areas.
• Decontamination efforts may be less intensive in regions with prolonged sunlight exposure.
sunlight, biological agents, timberline, decontamination, high altitude

Equipment Decontamination Procedures (1) -

Remove the LCE (Local Chemical Equipment)
Standard Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Step 1 - Remove the LCE:
1. Unfasten or unbutton all connectors or tie straps of the LCE.
2. Place the equipment in a plastic bag.
3. Place the plastic bag in the designated storage area for later decontamination.
LCE removal, chemical equipment, storage procedure

Equipment Management (1) -

Reissue Masks with Components to Soldiers
Standard Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. The unit NBC NCO reissues the masks with their components to the soldiers.
2. The soldiers will reassemble the masks in the postdecon AA (Area Assembly).
3. **The unit NBC NCO provides the soldiers with replacement parts...
Procedure:

1. The unit NBC NCO reissues the masks with their components to the soldiers.
2. The soldiers will reassemble the masks in the postdecon AA (Area Assembly).
3. The unit NBC NCO provides the soldiers with replacement parts for all types of protective masks.
4. The soldiers pick up individual gear and move to the postdecon AA.
mask reissue, protective mask components, postdecon AA, unit NBC NCO, gear pickup

Explosive Survivability (1) -

Fuel-Air Munitions Survivability Considerations
Critical Source: FM 5 103 Survivability
Procedure:

1. Understand the mechanism of fuel-air munitions, which disperse fuel into the atmosphere, forming a fuel-air mixture that is detonated.
2. Identify potential threats from fuel-air munitions, as they create peak pressures of...
Procedure:

1. Understand the mechanism of fuel-air munitions, which disperse fuel into the atmosphere, forming a fuel-air mixture that is detonated.
2. Identify potential threats from fuel-air munitions, as they create peak pressures of up to 300 pounds per square inch (psi) during detonation.
3. Avoid areas where fuel-air munitions may be deployed, due to the large area loading on structures and the risk of ignition of flammable materials.
4. Use covered positions with small apertures for protection from napalm and flamethrowers, as these weapons produce intense heat and noxious gases that can neutralize accessible positions.
5. Utilize deep-supported tunnels or positions to provide protection from fuel-air munitions and other explosives.

Notes:
• Fuel-air munitions are particularly effective in penetrating protective positions due to the high pressure generated during detonation.
• Flamethrowers and napalm can cause respiratory injuries by depleting oxygen levels inside enclosed spaces.
fuel-air munitions, survivability, flamethrowers, napalm, pressure

Waste Disposal (1) -

Dispose of Hazardous Waste Materials After Decontamination
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Dispose of uncontaminated hazardous waste materials:
- Follow federal, state, and local laws.
- Comply with military regulations and publications.
- Adhere to host-nation laws if more restrictive than U.S. laws.
-...
Procedure:

1. Dispose of uncontaminated hazardous waste materials:
- Follow federal, state, and local laws.
- Comply with military regulations and publications.
- Adhere to host-nation laws if more restrictive than U.S. laws.
- Follow local standing operating procedures (SOPs).

2. Place used decontaminating materials in a sealed plastic bag.
3. Label the bag with its contents.
4. Give the bag to your supervisor for proper disposal.

5. Dispose of contaminated hazardous waste materials:
- Inform your supervisor of the status of the contaminated waste.
- Follow all applicable regulations and procedures for handling contaminated materials.
hazardous waste disposal, decontamination materials, chemical waste, CBRN, waste management

Cbnr Protection (1) -

Protect Yourself From Chemical, Biological, Radiological, and Nuclear (CBRN) Injury or Contamination with MOPP Gear
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Assume MOPP3 as instructed.
2. Achieve MOPP4 within 8 minutes, following all steps in sequence.
3. Use MOPP gear to protect yourself from chemical warfare agents, but be aware that it is not designed for industrial...
Procedure:

1. Assume MOPP3 as instructed.
2. Achieve MOPP4 within 8 minutes, following all steps in sequence.
3. Use MOPP gear to protect yourself from chemical warfare agents, but be aware that it is not designed for industrial chemical hazards.

Conditions:
• You are given mission-oriented protective posture (MOPP) gear and one of the following situations:
- Ordered to achieve MOPP4.
- Learn a chemical attack is about to happen.
- Must enter an area where chemical agents have been used.
- Recognize a chemical hazard.
- Attacked with chemical agents without warning.

Note:
• If you are equipped with the joint service lightweight integrated suit technology (JSLIST), use task number 031-503-1040 instead of this task. This task was developed for the older battle dress overgarment (BDO).
MOPP gear, CBRN protection, chemical decontamination, MOPP4, nuclear_cbrn

Survival In Contaminated Areas (1) -

Supervise Unit Movement Through a Contaminated Area
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Procedure:

1. Ensure that Soldiers have been trained on the following tasks: 031-503-1001, 031-503-1037, 031-503-1013, and 031-503-1009.

2. Evaluate this task during a field exercise or a normal training session.

3. The contaminated area may...
Procedure:

1. Ensure that Soldiers have been trained on the following tasks: 031-503-1001, 031-503-1037, 031-503-1013, and 031-503-1009.

2. Evaluate this task during a field exercise or a normal training session.

3. The contaminated area may have been marked with the appropriate markers; or a diagram may have been prepared showing:
- The boundary of the contaminated area
- The Soldier's present location
- The desired direction of travel through the contaminated area

4. Gather the necessary equipment and personnel to conduct the movement.

5. Obtain, for training purposes, a fictitious commander's operational exposure guidance (OEG) on the turn-back dose and the turn-back dose rate.

6. Brief the Soldier to supervise the movement of a unit through a CBRN contaminated area by performing the appropriate measures before, during, and after the movement through the area.

7. Provide the Soldier with an illustration showing:
- The boundary of the contaminated area
- The Soldier's present location
- The desired direction of travel through the contaminated area

8. Do as many of the steps as possible for the types of contamination present in the area (e.g., nuclear, chemical, biological).

9. Seek medical aid if required.
contaminated area, CBRN, nuclear, chemical, biological, survival, supervision, movement

Cbnr Preparation (1) -

Prepare a Unit for a Chemical, Biological, and Nuclear (CBRN) Attack
Critical Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Overview: Prepare a unit for a CBRN attack by supervising unit preparation; preparing and protecting materiel from becoming damaged, contaminated, or inoperable, and preventing unit personnel from becoming casualties. This can be performed in...
Overview: Prepare a unit for a CBRN attack by supervising unit preparation; preparing and protecting materiel from becoming damaged, contaminated, or inoperable, and preventing unit personnel from becoming casualties. This can be performed in MOPP4.

Conditions: You are given the commander's guidance, a radio, pioneer tools (ax, shovel, mattock), chemical alarms, chemical-agent detector kits, covering materials (plastic sheets, ponchos, tarpaulins), FM 3-11.4, and Soldiers who have their mission-oriented protective posture (MOPP) gear.

Steps:
1. Receive Commander's Guidance: Understand the commander's guidance for preparing the unit for a CBRN attack.
2. Use Communication Tools: Utilize a radio to coordinate with other units or command posts as needed during the preparation process.
3. Utilize Pioneer Tools: Use pioneer tools (ax, shovel, mattock) to assist in digging trenches or creating barriers if necessary for protection against CBRN threats.
4. Deploy Chemical Alarms and Detectors: Set up chemical alarms and use chemical-agent detector kits to monitor the environment for any signs of chemical agents.
5. Protect Materiel with Covering Materials: Use covering materials (plastic sheets, ponchos, tarpaulins) to protect equipment from contamination or damage during a CBRN attack.
6. Implement MOPP Gear: Ensure all Soldiers are properly equipped with mission-oriented protective posture (MOPP) gear as per FM 3-11.4 guidelines.
7. Supervise Unit Preparation: Oversee the preparation of the unit to ensure that all measures are taken to protect both personnel and materiel from becoming casualties or inoperable due to CBRN threats.

Standards: The standard is not degraded if performed in MOPP4.
CBRN attack preparation, MOPP gear, chemical alarms, chemical-agent detector kits, covering materials, FM 3-11.4, pioneer tools, radio communication

Cbirn Marking Procedures (1) -

Supervise Employment of CBRN Markers for Nuclear Contamination
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
### Supervise Employment of CBRN Markers for Nuclear Contamination

Steps:
1. Ensure that all exposed skin is covered (shirt sleeves down, shirt buttoned up, and nose and mouth covered with a rag or t-shirt) prior to actually marking the...
### Supervise Employment of CBRN Markers for Nuclear Contamination

Steps:
1. Ensure that all exposed skin is covered (shirt sleeves down, shirt buttoned up, and nose and mouth covered with a rag or t-shirt) prior to actually marking the contaminated area.
2. Place markers at locations where a dose rate of 1 centigray per hour (cGyph) or more was measured.
3. Print all information on the front side of the marker so that the word 'atom' is facing toward you in an upright position. Information should include:
- The dose rate in cGyph.
- The date-time group (DTG) (specify local or Zulu) of the detonation. If the DTG is not known, print 'unknown.'
- The DTG (specify local or Zulu) of the reading.
4. Position markers so that they can be easily seen and that the recorded information faces away from the area of contamination.
- Attach markers to objects (such as trees or poles) that will be visible from all probable routes through the contaminated area.
- Ensure that each marker is placed so that the next one can be seen from the last one emplaced.
CBRN markers,nuclear contamination,marking procedures,dose rate,DTG,contaminated area

Nuclear Attack Preparation (1) -

Supervise Unit Preparation for a Nuclear Attack
Critical Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
### Supervise Unit Preparation for a Nuclear Attack

#### Individual Protection

1. Ensure that personnel are sheltered in well-constructed fighting positions with overhead cover, bunkers, and/or armored vehicles.
2. Ensure that personnel cover all...
### Supervise Unit Preparation for a Nuclear Attack

#### Individual Protection

1. Ensure that personnel are sheltered in well-constructed fighting positions with overhead cover, bunkers, and/or armored vehicles.
2. Ensure that personnel cover all exposed skin (roll sleeves down, button collars).
- Note: A handkerchief or similar cloth may be worn over the nose and mouth to prevent inhaling contaminated dust.

#### Position

1. Use terrain effectively to minimize the effects of an attack.
2. Select available shelter that provides the best protection from weapon effects (covered foxholes, field-expedient overhead cover, buildings, tents, and armored vehicles).

#### Materiel

1. Disperse and/or dig in supplies, equipment, and vehicles as much as possible. Disperse and/or dig in explosives, ammunition, and flammables (fuel and oil). Cover them if possible.
2. Ensure that existing cover provides protection and natural shielding for vehicles, supplies, and equipment in the event of a nuclear explosion.
3. Park vans so that their air conditioner intakes are opposite the prevailing wind direction. Turn off air conditioners and cover intakes with nonporous materials (plastic sheets or ponchos).
4. Keep flammable debris to a minimum. Secure small objects to minimize the danger of casualties and damage from flying debris.
5. Seal all food and water in containers and secure them under available cover.
6. Turn off electronic equipment and radios if not required for use.
7. Disconnect and remove power cables, antennas, and unused electronic equipment from power mounts.
8. Place communications and electronics equipment inside bunkers or armored vehicles to enhance protection against an electromagnetic pulse (EMP).

#### Unit

1. Ensure that CBRN equipment operators prepare the equipment for use.
nuclear attack preparation, individual protection, position selection, materiel dispersion, EMP protection

Radiation Preparedness (1) -

Understand the Impact of Nuclear Terrorism vs. All-Out War
High Priority Source: Nuclear War Survival Skills Cresson Kearny
Understanding the Difference Between Nuclear Terrorism and All-Out Nuclear War

• Nuclear terrorism typically involves a small number of nuclear weapons, causing catastrophic damage in localized areas but minimal overall impact on national...
Understanding the Difference Between Nuclear Terrorism and All-Out Nuclear War

• Nuclear terrorism typically involves a small number of nuclear weapons, causing catastrophic damage in localized areas but minimal overall impact on national infrastructure.
• All-out nuclear war, as feared during the Cold War, would involve massive Soviet attacks with far greater fallout and destruction across large regions.
• Panic caused by radiation fears can be more damaging than the attack itself. This was observed historically during World War II when fear of bombing in London led to greater disruption than the bombs themselves.
• In a nuclear terrorist attack, medical aid is likely available for most citizens, allowing them to withstand higher radiation doses and continue daily life activities.
• Key takeaway: Rational understanding and preparedness can prevent unnecessary panic and ensure continuity of essential services.
nuclear terrorism, radiation fear, all-out war, medical aid, panic prevention

Fire Hazard Management (1) -

Assessing and Mitigating Fire Hazards After a Nuclear Detonation
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
### Procedure: Assessing and Mitigating Fire Hazards After a Nuclear Detonation

Overview:
Nuclear detonation-induced fires pose significant risks, especially in the MDZ (Medical Disaster Zone). These fires can be caused by the initial thermal...
### Procedure: Assessing and Mitigating Fire Hazards After a Nuclear Detonation

Overview:
Nuclear detonation-induced fires pose significant risks, especially in the MDZ (Medical Disaster Zone). These fires can be caused by the initial thermal pulse igniting flammable materials or secondary fires from ruptured gas lines and fuel tanks. Fires may spread beyond the MDZ depending on weather and terrain conditions.

Steps to Assess Fire Hazards:
1. Identify fire-prone areas: Focus on the MDZ, where buildings are more likely to remain standing and infrastructure damage (e.g., broken windows, gas lines, fuel tanks) is extensive.
2. Assess secondary fire risks: Look for ruptured gas lines, leaking fuel tanks, or other flammable sources that could ignite after a detonation.
3. Evaluate weather conditions: Wind direction and speed, as well as humidity levels, can significantly influence the spread of fires.
4. Monitor smoke production: Smoke from fires may complicate response efforts and pose additional hazards to survivors and responders.
5. Consider urban design factors: Modern U.S. city construction makes firestorms unlikely; however, residential areas with wooden houses may experience significant fire activity.

Mitigation Strategies:
• Coordinate with local and regional fire departments to develop strategies for containing and mitigating fire spread in post-detonation conditions.
• Ensure that evacuation routes are clear of flammable materials where possible.
• Prepare for rapid response by pre-positioning firefighting resources near high-risk areas.
• Train responders on the unique challenges posed by nuclear detonation-induced fires, including potential co-location with blast damage and infrastructure disruption.
nuclear fire hazards, post-detonation fires, MDZ fire risks, secondary fires, fire mitigation strategies

Eye Injuries (1) -

Respond to Eye Injuries Caused by Nuclear Detonation Flash Blindness
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Recognize symptoms of flash blindness:
- Temporary loss of vision or severe dimming of vision after observing the intense light from a nuclear detonation.
- A darkened after image that may last several minutes.
-...
Procedure:

1. Recognize symptoms of flash blindness:
- Temporary loss of vision or severe dimming of vision after observing the intense light from a nuclear detonation.
- A darkened after image that may last several minutes.
- Disorientation and difficulty navigating due to impaired vision.

2. Provide immediate assistance to visually impaired individuals:
- Ensure they are not left alone in potentially dangerous environments (e.g., roads, open areas).
- Use verbal cues and physical guidance to help them move safely.
- Avoid leading them by the arm; instead, guide them by their elbow or shoulder for better control.

3. Evacuate with assistance:
- If evacuation is necessary, pair visually impaired individuals with sighted companions who can navigate on their behalf.
- Use tactile markers (e.g., raised lines, textured surfaces) to help them identify safe paths and obstacles.

4. Avoid exposure to bright light sources:
- Keep flash-blinded individuals away from direct sunlight or artificial lights that may worsen their condition.
- If possible, provide temporary eye protection such as sunglasses or opaque shields until vision returns.

5. Monitor for signs of permanent eye damage:
- Watch for symptoms like persistent pain, redness, swelling, or changes in vision (e.g., blind spots, loss of visual acuity).
- If retinal burns are suspected, seek medical attention immediately, even if the individual is not near a healthcare facility.

6. Prevent secondary injuries:
- Ensure that visually impaired individuals do not attempt to move independently in hazardous environments (e.g., after a blast or fire). This can lead to traffic accidents, blocked roads, and other life-threatening situations.
flash blindness,nuclear detonation,eye injuries,evacuation,retinal burns

Fallout Assessment (1) -

Assess Fallout and Radiation Hazards Based on Cloud Color
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Observe the color of the cloud cap (if visible):
- A dark brown cloud cap indicates a high likelihood of local fallout from a near-surface detonation.
- A white cloud top suggests less immediate fallout hazard,...
Procedure:

1. Observe the color of the cloud cap (if visible):
- A dark brown cloud cap indicates a high likelihood of local fallout from a near-surface detonation.
- A white cloud top suggests less immediate fallout hazard, possibly due to higher altitude or different detonation conditions.

2. Understand the implications of cloud color:
- Dark brown cloud caps are associated with large particles that fall quickly and create a radiation field on the ground within hours.
- White cloud tops may indicate less immediate fallout, but this does not eliminate the need for monitoring or protective actions.

3. Take appropriate protective measures:
- If a dark brown cloud is observed, immediately seek shelter and avoid exposure to external radiation from fallout particles on the ground.
- If a white cloud is observed, continue to monitor the situation and be prepared for potential fallout based on wind patterns and time elapsed since detonation.

4. Consider the following factors:
- Fallout particle size affects both inhalation risk (smaller particles are more hazardous) and external radiation exposure (larger particles emit external gamma radiation).
- Wind direction and speed can influence fallout distribution, potentially leading to multiple deposition areas.

5. Plan for changing conditions:
- Radiation levels decrease rapidly over time, with the highest dose rates occurring in the first few hours after detonation.
- Sheltering during the first 24 hours is a critical protective measure to reduce exposure.
cloud color,fallout assessment,nuclear detonation,radiation hazards,sheltering,response planning

Planning Zones (1) -

Identify and Understand Planning Zones for Nuclear Detonation Response
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Understanding planning zones is critical to effective response actions following a nuclear detonation.

### Key Planning Zones:
1. Severe Damage Zone (SDZ): Characterized by destroyed infrastructure and high radiation levels.
2. **Moderate...
Understanding planning zones is critical to effective response actions following a nuclear detonation.

### Key Planning Zones:
1. Severe Damage Zone (SDZ): Characterized by destroyed infrastructure and high radiation levels.
2. Moderate Damage Zone (MDZ): Features significant building damage, rubble, downed utility lines, some downed poles, overturned automobiles, fires, and serious injuries.
3. Light Damage Zone (LDZ): Involves broken windows and easily managed injuries.
4. Dangerous Radiation Zone (DRZ): Prolonged outdoor exposure can result in injury or death due to high radiation levels.
5. Hot Zone (HZ): Actions are warranted to reduce radiation exposure and the possibility of long-term health effects.
nuclear detonation, planning zones, SDZ, MDZ, LDZ, DRZ, HZ

Radiation Health Effects (1) -

Understand Radiation Health Effects and Dose Management
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Radiation exposure can lead to both acute and long-term health effects. Here's what you need to know:

### Acute Health Effects:
• High radiation doses can cause immediate health issues, including death.
• These effects manifest quickly...
Radiation exposure can lead to both acute and long-term health effects. Here's what you need to know:

### Acute Health Effects:
• High radiation doses can cause immediate health issues, including death.
• These effects manifest quickly following exposure.

### Long-Term Health Effects:
• Increased risk of cancer over years due to prolonged exposure.

### Radiation Dose Management:
1. Minimize dose levels in the DRZ, where acute effects and lethal doses are more likely.
2. In the HZ, ensure adequate shelter is available to reduce unnecessary radiation exposures.
3. A radiation dose received over a long period of time is less likely to result in health effects than if the same dose were received over a short period of time.

### Additional Information:
• Chapters 2 and 3 provide more information on radiation dose management and protective actions.
• Chapter 4 discusses the effects of various radiation exposures.
radiation health, acute effects, long-term effects, dose management, DRZ, HZ

Communication (1) -

Use of Radios During EMP Events
Standard Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
- Commercial band AM/FM radios will be able to receive signals from transmitters outside the area.
radios, EMP, communication

Public Response (1) -

Public Actions During a Nuclear Detonation
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Seek immediate shelter in a large, dense building. Stay sheltered unless threatened by fire or building collapse. Tune in to local radio to determine radiation hazard. Evacuate if directed or experiencing life-threatening conditions, such as...
Seek immediate shelter in a large, dense building. Stay sheltered unless threatened by fire or building collapse. Tune in to local radio to determine radiation hazard. Evacuate if directed or experiencing life-threatening conditions, such as impending building collapse, fire, or medical emergency.
shelter, radiation hazard, evacuation, nuclear detonation

Firefighting Evacuation Planning (1) -

Address Firefighting Concerns in Nuclear Incident Plans
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Action Item:

• Address firefighting concerns in nuclear incident plans, including a prioritization structure for potentially limited water resources.

• When planning evacuation routes, ensure the routes do not obstruct critical transportation...
Action Item:

• Address firefighting concerns in nuclear incident plans, including a prioritization structure for potentially limited water resources.

• When planning evacuation routes, ensure the routes do not obstruct critical transportation routes or response operations at large.
firefighting,nuclear incident,water resources,evacuation routes

Evacuation Sheltering Protocols (1) -

Implement Evacuation Guidance and Sheltering Protocols Post-Nuclear Detonation
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Evacuation Guidance:

• Sheltering is implicitly short term. Initial sheltering should be followed by staged, facilitated evacuation for those in fallout-impacted areas.

• Optimal shelter stay times can range from a few hours to several days...
Evacuation Guidance:

• Sheltering is implicitly short term. Initial sheltering should be followed by staged, facilitated evacuation for those in fallout-impacted areas.

• Optimal shelter stay times can range from a few hours to several days and depend both on the local fallout dose rate and the radiation dose incurred during evacuation.

• Where possible, individuals should stay in an adequate shelter for the first 24 hours following detonation to prevent exposure to high levels of radiation.

• Evacuations should occur only after appropriate paths have been identified and cleared.

• Fire concerns should be closely monitored as rapidly evolving fires may warrant emergency evacuation of potentially impacted areas.

• Attempting to evacuate excessively large areas at a single time unnecessarily diverts resources from other response needs.

• Be aware that many people may choose to self-evacuate.
sheltering,evacuation protocols,nuclear detonation,fallout-impacted areas,radiation dose rates

Coordination Planning (1) -

Coordinate with RITN Facilities for Nuclear Detonation Response Planning
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Ensure local and regional planners coordinate with RITN facilities when developing nuclear detonation response plans.

Procedure:
1. Identify local and regional planners involved in emergency preparedness.
2. Coordinate with the...
Objective: Ensure local and regional planners coordinate with RITN facilities when developing nuclear detonation response plans.

Procedure:
1. Identify local and regional planners involved in emergency preparedness.
2. Coordinate with the RITN (Radiation Injury Treatment Network), a consortium of U.S. medical specialists with expertise in major emergencies.
3. Develop response plans that incorporate RITN’s capabilities for implementing care during large-scale nuclear detonation incidents.
4. Consider utilizing telemedicine consultations with RITN during emergencies involving large numbers of patients.

Resources:
• Visit [ritn.net/treatment](http://ritn.net/treatment) for more information on RITN services and treatment protocols.
RITN, coordination, nuclear detonation, emergency planning, medical response

Radiation Communication (1) -

Prepare for Public Concerns Regarding Radiation Exposure
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Prepare emergency planners and public information officers (PIOs) in neighboring jurisdictions to address public concerns about radiation exposure following a nuclear detonation.

Steps:
1. Educate PIOs and emergency planners in...
Objective: Prepare emergency planners and public information officers (PIOs) in neighboring jurisdictions to address public concerns about radiation exposure following a nuclear detonation.

Steps:
1. Educate PIOs and emergency planners in neighboring jurisdictions on the potential for detectable radiation levels downwind of a detonation site.
2. Provide training on how to explain radiation risks, protective measures, and monitoring efforts to the public.
3. Ensure that PIOs understand that radiation exposure may vary based on weather and atmospheric conditions and should be prepared to address questions from concerned members of the public.
4. Incorporate federal modeling and monitoring data for radioactive material into communication strategies.
5. Prepare a standardized set of messages and frequently asked questions (FAQs) about radiation risks, detection levels, and protective actions that can be shared with neighboring jurisdictions.
public concerns, radiation exposure, communication planning, nuclear detonation response

Emergency Reporting (1) -

Reporting a Nuclear Detonation via NAWAS
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Identify the detonation:
- Confirm that a nuclear detonation has occurred.
- Determine if it is a trans-attack or local-origin detonation (see note 51).

2. Prepare the flash nuclear detonation report:
- Include...
Procedure:

1. Identify the detonation:
- Confirm that a nuclear detonation has occurred.
- Determine if it is a trans-attack or local-origin detonation (see note 51).

2. Prepare the flash nuclear detonation report:
- Include the area hit and the time of detonation in the initial report.
- If more details are available, include them immediately.
- For trans-attack detonations, mark the report as “transmit over NAWAS only”.

3. Submit the report:
- Trans-attack reports should be sent directly to the FOC/FAOC via NAWAS.
- Local-origin reports must first be sent to the SWP for relay to the FOC/FAOC.

4. Use NAWAS for urgent reporting:
- Flash nuclear detonation reports and other extremely urgent reports should be transmitted through NAWAS.
- Ensure that the location of the detonation is known before transmitting the report.

5. Coordinate with SWPs and FOC/FAOC:
- Maintain communication with SWPs to ensure timely relay of information.
- Coordinate with the FOC/FAOC for further instructions on messaging distribution.
nuclear detonation reporting, NAWAS, FOC/FAOC, SWP, flash nuclear detonation report

Public Communication (1) -

Using AWN for Public Communication in Post-Detonation Scenarios
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Activate AWN messaging:
- Once a nuclear detonation has occurred, the AWN system is used to provide critical information to the public.
- The initial activation of AWN is based on immediate analysis of technical factors...
Procedure:

1. Activate AWN messaging:
- Once a nuclear detonation has occurred, the AWN system is used to provide critical information to the public.
- The initial activation of AWN is based on immediate analysis of technical factors such as fallout wind vectors and forecast plots (see other sections for details).

2. Issue AWN through FOC/FAOC:
- The FOC/FAOC uses NAWAS to issue AWN messages to SWPs.
- Ensure that the message includes accurate information about the detonation, fallout patterns, and safety instructions.

3. Distribute AWN via local channels:
- SWPs relay the AWN messaging to localities or the public using available communications systems.
- Follow state EAS plans and operational AWN distribution channels in local EOCs for message dissemination.

4. Continue messaging during Recovery phase:
- AWN continues to provide critical information throughout the Recovery phase of the response.
- Ensure that messages are updated as new information becomes available (e.g., changes in fallout patterns, sheltering guidance).
AWN messaging, public communication, nuclear detonation response, fallout wind vectors, EAS plan

Geomagnetic Disturbances (1) -

Understand Geomagnetic Disturbances (GMD) and Their Effects
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Understanding Geomagnetic Disturbances (GMD)

1. Cause of GMD:
- Interaction of large numbers of charged particles from the sun, such as a coronal mass ejection (CME), with Earth’s magnetic field.

2. Effects of GMD:
- Creates...
Understanding Geomagnetic Disturbances (GMD)

1. Cause of GMD:
- Interaction of large numbers of charged particles from the sun, such as a coronal mass ejection (CME), with Earth’s magnetic field.

2. Effects of GMD:
- Creates low-frequency electric fields similar to those produced by the slow-rising portion of HEMP.
- These fields can couple to long conductors like power transmission lines, inducing low-frequency electric currents.

3. Duration and Coverage:
- GMD incidents can last for several minutes, hours, or even days.
- Can affect large geographic areas.

4. Primary Threats from GMD:
- Large power transformers are at risk of overheating due to induced currents.
- The magnitude (volts/km) and duration of the disturbance are critical factors in determining the impact on infrastructure.

5. Mitigation Considerations:
- Protection strategies for electrical systems should account for both HEMP and GMD effects, as they share similar mechanisms.

Summary: GMDs result from solar activity and can cause long-lasting low-frequency electric fields that threaten power grids through transformer overheating.
GMD, geomagnetic disturbance, solar activity, power grid, transformers, electromagnetic field

Fallout Patterns (1) -

Understanding Fallout Cloud Patterns from Urban or Underground Nuclear Detonations
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Understanding the variation in fallout cloud patterns is critical for emergency planning and response.

### Key Factors Affecting Fallout Cloud Shape:
• Wind direction and speed change with altitude: This can significantly alter how fallout...
Understanding the variation in fallout cloud patterns is critical for emergency planning and response.

### Key Factors Affecting Fallout Cloud Shape:
• Wind direction and speed change with altitude: This can significantly alter how fallout spreads.
• Urban and underground material incorporation into the fireball: Materials from buildings or underground structures may be drawn into the fireball, altering its shape and behavior.
• Surrounding buildings partially blocking fallout cloud airflow: Urban environments can restrict the movement of fallout clouds.
• Blast waves reflecting off nearby buildings: This can distort the typical mushroom cloud formation.
• Fireball interacting with building surfaces: The interaction between the fireball and surrounding structures may influence the shape and trajectory of the fallout cloud.

### Example: Teapot ESS Test (1955)
• A 1 kT device was detonated 67 feet underground at the Nevada Test Site.
• The resulting fallout cloud had an irregularly shaped pattern, climbing over 2 miles in about 5 minutes.
• Fallout contamination on the ground produced dose rates of more than 10 R/hr approximately 3.5 miles away, 1 hour after detonation.

### Implications for Planning:
• Fallout patterns can vary by tens of roentgens per hour over short distances.
• This variability makes it difficult to establish clear boundaries of the Dose Reduction Zone (DRZ) or Hazard Zone (HZ), especially in cities with a regular grid of streets.
• Simple planning models may produce only cigar-shaped clouds, which do not reflect real-world irregularities.

### Actionable Insight:
Emergency planners must be aware that fallout patterns from urban or underground detonations can be highly irregular. This awareness is critical to ensuring plans are flexible enough to account for unusual plume shapes.
nuclear detonation,fallout cloud,urban environment,underground detonation,mushroom cloud,plume shape,emergency planning,DRZ,HZ,dose rates

Decontamination Infrastructure (1) -

Initiating Decontamination Activities After a Nuclear Detonation
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Overview: Critical infrastructure decontamination should only be initiated when basic information becomes available regarding fallout distribution, current and projected radiation dose rates, and structural integrity of the elements to be...
Overview: Critical infrastructure decontamination should only be initiated when basic information becomes available regarding fallout distribution, current and projected radiation dose rates, and structural integrity of the elements to be decontaminated.

There are several references and tools for critical infrastructure decontamination planning and assessing dose to workers—see Refer To below.
decontamination initiation, fallout distribution, radiation dose rates, structural integrity assessment

Steps:
1. Consult NCRP Report 175, _Decision Making for Late-Phase Recovery from Major Nuclear or Radiological Incidents_, available at:...
Objective: Locate additional resources to support decontamination planning and method selection.

2. Use the RESRAD family of codes for free download from: [www.evs.anl.gov/research areas/highlights/resrad.cfm](https://www.evs.anl.gov/research%20areas/highlights/resrad.cfm) to estimate contamination and decontamination needs.
3. Access the Radiation Decontamination Planning Tool on RadResponder's resource page (requires account creation).
4. Utilize these resources to refine planning, assess risks, and select appropriate decontamination strategies.
