Screening And Decontamination (1) -

Implement Strategies for Screening and Decontaminating People
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Develop and implement effective strategies for screening and decontamination of individuals following a nuclear detonation.

Steps:
1. Establish clear protocols for screening individuals at CRCs, shelters, and ports of entry based...
Objective: Develop and implement effective strategies for screening and decontamination of individuals following a nuclear detonation.

Steps:
1. Establish clear protocols for screening individuals at CRCs, shelters, and ports of entry based on guidance from radiation protection professionals.
2. Ensure that all individuals who may be contaminated (e.g., evacuees, self-evacuating individuals) are provided with decontamination instructions before and after screening.
3. Coordinate with public health planners to ensure consistency in screening procedures across different locations and populations.
4. Use tools and resources available from CDC and other agencies to support these strategies.
screening strategy, decontamination protocol, nuclear emergency

Radiological Emergency (1) -

Recognize and Respond to a Site Area Emergency (SAE)
Critical Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
A Site Area Emergency (SAE) is an emergency level (ECL) that indicates events are in progress or have occurred involving an actual or likely major failure of plant functions needed for the protection of the public, or hostile action that could...
A Site Area Emergency (SAE) is an emergency level (ECL) that indicates events are in progress or have occurred involving an actual or likely major failure of plant functions needed for the protection of the public, or hostile action that could lead to such failures.

Key points:
• An SAE involves potential risks to site personnel and equipment that may affect public safety.
• It does not involve releases expected to exceed EPA PAG exposure levels beyond the site boundary.
• Emergency response plans must include procedures for managing an SAE, including communication with the public and coordination with local authorities.
site area emergency,nuclear plant failure,radiation safety

Nuclear Facility Components (1) -

Define and Understand the Concept of a Spent Fuel Pool in Nuclear Facilities
Critical Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
A spent fuel pool is a structure that provides onsite storage for spent nuclear fuel. These pools are constructed with reinforced concrete several feet thick, with steel liners, and contain water about 40 feet deep.

Key points:
• The...
A spent fuel pool is a structure that provides onsite storage for spent nuclear fuel. These pools are constructed with reinforced concrete several feet thick, with steel liners, and contain water about 40 feet deep.

Key points:
• The primary functions of the spent fuel pool include shielding radiation and cooling the fuel rods.
• They are critical components in nuclear facilities for managing radioactive waste safely.
• In an emergency, these pools must be protected from damage to prevent potential radiological releases.
spent fuel pool,nuclear waste storage,radiation shielding

Radiological Emergency Preparedness (1) -

Implementing the Federal Policy on Use of Potassium Iodide (KI)
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Review the Federal Register, Vol. 67, No. 7, pp.1355-1357, issued by the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) on January 10, 2002.

2. Understand the policy guidelines for the use of Potassium Iodide (KI), including:
...
Procedure:

1. Review the Federal Register, Vol. 67, No. 7, pp.1355-1357, issued by the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) on January 10, 2002.

2. Understand the policy guidelines for the use of Potassium Iodide (KI), including:
- Indications for KI administration during a radiological emergency.
- Dosage recommendations, which vary by age group and exposure scenario.
- Contraindications, such as allergies or pre-existing medical conditions that may prevent use of KI.

3. Ensure compliance with the policy when planning for or responding to nuclear incidents, including:
- Stockpiling KI tablets in accordance with population needs.
- Training personnel on proper administration and distribution protocols.
- Coordinating with public health agencies for mass dispensing operations.

4. Document all actions taken related to the implementation of this policy for accountability and future reference.
Potassium Iodide, KI, radiological emergency, nuclear incident, FEMA policy

Radiological Emergency Assessment (1) -

Assessing Offsite Emergency Preparedness Infrastructure and Capabilities Following a Nuclear Incident
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Refer to FEMA Interim SOG, *Assessment of Offsite Emergency Preparedness Infrastructure and Capabilities Following an Incident in the Vicinity of a U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission Licensed-Nuclear Power Plant*, August 17,...
Procedure:

1. Refer to FEMA Interim SOG, *Assessment of Offsite Emergency Preparedness Infrastructure and Capabilities Following an Incident in the Vicinity of a U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission Licensed-Nuclear Power Plant*, August 17, 2018.

2. Conduct an assessment that includes:
- Evaluating infrastructure readiness, such as transportation systems, shelters, and communication networks.
- Assessing response capabilities of local, state, and federal agencies to ensure they are prepared for a radiological emergency.
- Reviewing training and exercise programs conducted by response teams to identify gaps or areas needing improvement.

3. Document the findings of the assessment and use them to inform planning, resource allocation, and training initiatives.

4. Coordinate with nuclear power plant operators and other stakeholders to ensure a unified approach to emergency preparedness.
nuclear incident assessment, radiological emergency, FEMA SOG

Radiological Emergency Planning (1) -

Implementing the Criteria for Preparation and Evaluation of Radiological Emergency Response Plans
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Refer to NUREG-0654/FEMA-REP-1, Rev. 2, *Criteria for Preparation and Evaluation of Radiological Emergency Response Plans and Preparedness in Support of Nuclear Power Plants*, December 23, 2019.

2. Use the criteria outlined...
Procedure:

1. Refer to NUREG-0654/FEMA-REP-1, Rev. 2, *Criteria for Preparation and Evaluation of Radiological Emergency Response Plans and Preparedness in Support of Nuclear Power Plants*, December 23, 2019.

2. Use the criteria outlined in this document to:
- Develop radiological emergency response plans that align with federal guidelines and best practices.
- Evaluate existing response plans to ensure they are comprehensive, up-to-date, and effective in addressing potential nuclear incidents.
- Ensure coordination between nuclear power plant operators, local jurisdictions, and federal agencies during planning and evaluation processes.

3. Incorporate the criteria into training programs for emergency responders and public health officials involved in radiological emergencies.

4. Document all evaluations and use them to guide improvements in response plans and preparedness efforts.
radiological emergency response plan, FEMA REP, NUREG criteria

Explosion Response (1) -

Respond to a Nuclear Explosion
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
In the event of a nuclear explosion, take immediate action to protect yourself and others.

### Immediate Effects:
• Most deaths occur due to exposure to the blast, which includes intense heat, wind, radiation, light, and physical trauma...
In the event of a nuclear explosion, take immediate action to protect yourself and others.

### Immediate Effects:
• Most deaths occur due to exposure to the blast, which includes intense heat, wind, radiation, light, and physical trauma from collapsing structures or fires.
• The wind caused by the blast can reach speeds of 200 mph or more, extending up to about 12 miles from the epicenter.

### Casualty Statistics:
• Even in the worst-case scenario (a weekday at noon during winter), it is estimated that only about 2% of the population would die immediately from the explosion itself.
• The majority of casualties occur due to radiation sickness and injuries sustained afterward, over days or months.

### Safety Precautions:
• Never look directly at a nuclear explosion. It can cause permanent blindness within 20 miles and may still be visible up to 50 miles away.
• The entire detonation process lasts only a few seconds, but the effects are long-lasting.
• Radiation levels from an explosion can reach as high as 5,000 R. or more, which is extremely dangerous.
nuclear explosion, blast effects, radiation levels, survival tips, nuclear disaster response

Fallout Shelter Preparation (1) -

Prepare a Home Basement as a Fallout Shelter
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Steps to Prepare Your Home Basement as a Fallout Shelter:

1. Understand the limitations: This is a *minimal protection* fallout shelter and *not* a blast shelter.
2. Start with a full basement: If you have a half, daylight, or walkout...
Steps to Prepare Your Home Basement as a Fallout Shelter:

1. Understand the limitations: This is a *minimal protection* fallout shelter and *not* a blast shelter.
2. Start with a full basement: If you have a half, daylight, or walkout basement, additional protection must be added to exposed walls or parts of walls above ground level. A full basement uses the surrounding dirt on all four sides for minimal protection, but the main concern is overhead radiation.
3. Fortify window wells: Fill them with dense materials such as dirt, sand, books, or other heavy objects from either the outside or inside (on window ledges). The glass in windows offers no protection against fallout.
4. Build overhead shielding: Use sturdy structures like tables, workbenches, bookshelves with doors across them, or doors leaned against outer basement walls. Pile dense materials on top of these structures to provide additional overhead protection. This can also be done on the main floor above the part of the basement you are using.
5. Fortify all sides: Ensure that the shelter is protected from radiation on all four sides as well as overhead. The first seven hours after a nuclear blast are critical for minimizing exposure.
6. Allow limited movement: You may need to stretch your legs briefly (emphasis on *minute*), but you should remain in the basement as much as possible during this time.
7. Ensure proper ventilation: Do not seal yourself in so tightly that you cannot breathe. Avoid using duct tape and plastic for sealing, as this can lead to suffocation.
8. Prepare for long-term stay: You may need to remain in your shelter for up to 14 days or longer. Prepare with sanitation measures, water, food, and other necessary supplies. Keep these items easily accessible in one place for quick use during an emergency.
fallout shelter,basement preparation,nuclear emergency,shelter construction,emergency preparedness

Nuclear Preparedness (1) -

Understand the Reality of Nuclear War for Effective Preparedness
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Objective: Gain accurate knowledge about nuclear war to plan effectively.

Why This Matters:
• Misinformation from disarmament spokesmen, science fiction, and Hollywood films can lead to incorrect survival strategies.
• There are legitimate...
Objective: Gain accurate knowledge about nuclear war to plan effectively.

Why This Matters:
• Misinformation from disarmament spokesmen, science fiction, and Hollywood films can lead to incorrect survival strategies.
• There are legitimate survival challenges related to nuclear weapons that may be unfamiliar to most people.
• Inaccurate information will result in flawed preparedness plans.

Steps to Take:
1. Read factual material: Focus on sources like *The Effects of Nuclear Weapons* by Samuel Glasstone and official Defense Civil Preparedness Agency (DCPA) manuals for accurate survival strategies.
2. Be aware of discrepancies: Recognize that some reference books may contain errors, particularly regarding the danger radii and fallout patterns of nuclear weapons.
3. Verify information: Cross-check sources to ensure your understanding is based on reliable data rather than myths or legends.

Warnings/Contraindications:
• Do not rely solely on media portrayals or unverified sources for planning.
• Avoid assuming that all survival challenges are well-known; some may be overlooked by the general public.
nuclear war, survival planning, misinformation, Glasstone, DCPA manuals, fallout patterns

Military Strategy (1) -

Strategic Use of Neutron Bombs in Military Scenarios
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Assess military objective: Determine the need to neutralize armored vehicles or tanks with minimal collateral damage.
2. Deploy neutron bombs: Use a large number (e.g., 2,000) of small neutron bombs (1 kiloton each) to...
Procedure:

1. Assess military objective: Determine the need to neutralize armored vehicles or tanks with minimal collateral damage.
2. Deploy neutron bombs: Use a large number (e.g., 2,000) of small neutron bombs (1 kiloton each) to target enemy forces without causing widespread destruction.
3. Avoid fallout and blast damage: Neutron bombs produce no radioactive fallout and limited blast effects compared to traditional nuclear weapons.

Details:
• Neutron bombs are ideal for defending territories with high population density.
• They are more 'clean' than conventional H-bombs but still pose a significant radiation threat to enemy personnel.
neutron bomb deployment, military strategy, tactical nuclear use, fallout avoidance

Nuclear Explosion Effects (1) -

Understanding Nuclear Air and Surface Bursts
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Understanding the Effects of Nuclear Explosions

When a nuclear weapon is detonated, it releases an immense amount of energy that heats air to tens of millions of degrees. This creates a fireball that radiates intense heat and light, visible...
Understanding the Effects of Nuclear Explosions

When a nuclear weapon is detonated, it releases an immense amount of energy that heats air to tens of millions of degrees. This creates a fireball that radiates intense heat and light, visible from up to 400 miles away at night.

• At the moment of detonation, a 1-megaton bomb produces a fireball about 400 feet in diameter.
• Ten seconds after the explosion, it expands to 5,700 feet across.
• The fireball rises rapidly at 300 feet per second, reaching 25,000 feet and cooling enough to stop emitting light within a minute.

As the superheated gas rises, drag from surrounding air causes the outer edges of the cloud to slow down. This forms a rolling mushroom-shaped cloud, with a stem formed by windblown smoke and dirt.

• The initial color of the mushroom cloud is red or reddish-brown due to high concentrations of oxides of nitrogen (similar to smog).
• As it cools, water vapor condenses into droplets, causing the cloud to turn white.
• A 1-megaton burst creates a cloud that is typically twelve miles high and twelve miles across, while a 10-megaton burst produces a cloud about twenty miles high and fifty miles wide.

This information is crucial for understanding the scale of destruction and planning survival strategies in the aftermath of a nuclear attack.
nuclear explosion, mushroom cloud, fireball, shock wave, overpressure, dynamic pressure, radiation effects

Response Limitations (1) -

Understand Federal Response Limitations in a Nuclear Detonation Scenario
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
- The immediate federal response will involve modeling, coordination, mobilization, public communication (Chapter 6), and alert, warning, and notification (Chapter 7). However, it is unlikely that significant federal response assets will arrive...
- The immediate federal response will involve modeling, coordination, mobilization, public communication (Chapter 6), and alert, warning, and notification (Chapter 7). However, it is unlikely that significant federal response assets will arrive on-scene for 24 hours, and the full extent of federal assets will not be available for several days. Emergency response is principally a local function and, for purposes of this document, no significant federal on-scene response is assumed for 24–72 hours.

• Based on technical analyses and modeling, recommendations are intentionally simplified to maximize their utility in uncertain situations where technical information is limited.
federal response,nuclear detonation,local emergency,response limitations

Purpose: This reference ensures that...
Action: Refer to *Chapter 5: Population Monitoring* in the *Planning Guidance for Response to a Nuclear Detonation, Third Edition* for detailed information about contamination screening procedures.

Purpose: This reference ensures that individuals involved in response efforts have access to accurate and comprehensive guidance on how to monitor and screen populations for radioactive contamination following a nuclear detonation.
contamination screening, population monitoring, nuclear detonation, radiation

Detonation Impacts (1) -

Understand Nuclear Detonation Impact Scenarios
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Purpose: To provide a baseline understanding of the potential impacts of nuclear detonations based on different yield sizes and burst heights.

Key Information:
• This guidance is primarily based on the Department of Homeland Security (DHS)...
Purpose: To provide a baseline understanding of the potential impacts of nuclear detonations based on different yield sizes and burst heights.

Key Information:
• This guidance is primarily based on the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) National Planning Scenario (NPS) #1, which describes a 10 kiloton (kT) yield nuclear detonation at ground level in an urban environment.
• The document also covers:
- Smaller and larger yields
- Detonations that occur above ground

Reference Table:
|Yield|Height Above Ground|
|---|---|
|0.1 kT|Ground burst|
|1.0 kT|Ground burst|
|10 kT|Ground burst|
|100 kT|Ground burst|
|100 kT|Air burst, 1000 ft|
|100 kT|Air burst, 5000 ft|

Note: This information is essential for planning and response efforts to account for a wide range of potential nuclear detonation scenarios.
nuclear detonation, impact scenarios, yield sizes, burst height, planning guidance

Detonation Effects (1) -

Understand the Effects of a Nuclear Detonation
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Purpose: To outline the immediate and residual effects of a nuclear detonation.

Immediate Effects:
1. Intense Light: A blinding flash that can cause temporary or permanent vision loss depending on proximity to the blast.
2. **Thermal...
Purpose: To outline the immediate and residual effects of a nuclear detonation.

Immediate Effects:
1. Intense Light: A blinding flash that can cause temporary or permanent vision loss depending on proximity to the blast.
2. Thermal Pulse: An intense heat wave capable of causing severe burns and igniting fires over large areas.
3. Radiation Pulse (Initial Radiation): High-energy radiation that can cause acute radiation sickness and death in exposed individuals.
4. Blast Wave: A powerful shockwave that can destroy buildings, infrastructure, and cause injuries or fatalities from flying debris and pressure.

Residual Effects:
• Fallout: Radioactive particles dispersed into the atmosphere following the detonation, which can contaminate areas far from the blast site.
• Electromagnetic Pulse (EMP): A burst of electromagnetic energy that can disrupt or damage electronic devices over a wide area.

Note: Understanding these effects is critical for emergency planning and response to mitigate harm and protect populations.
nuclear detonation effects, thermal pulse, radiation, blast wave, fallout, electromagnetic pulse

Blast Effects (1) -

Understanding Nuclear Blast Effects and Overpressure
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Understand the effects of a nuclear blast wave and overpressure.

Overview:
A primary effect of a nuclear detonation is the blast wave, generated by the rapidly expanding fireball. This blast is often measured by the...
Objective: Understand the effects of a nuclear blast wave and overpressure.

Overview:
A primary effect of a nuclear detonation is the blast wave, generated by the rapidly expanding fireball. This blast is often measured by the overpressure it produces, which is the pressure above ambient atmospheric pressure caused by the explosion.

Key Information:
• Near the detonation point, overpressure can reach extremely high levels (thousands of pounds per square inch [psi]).
• The blast wave expands in all directions from the detonation and initially moves faster than the speed of sound.
• Beyond a few miles from the detonation, the blast wave will travel approximately one mile every five seconds.

Actionable Insight:
• Observers who see the flash of the explosion may have a few seconds to take cover, which can help reduce injury from flying debris.
• This time window is critical for minimizing harm and should be used to seek shelter or protective cover immediately.
nuclear blast, overpressure, detonation effects, nuclear explosion, blast wave, protective cover

Fallout Formation (1) -

Understand Fission Products and Fallout Formation in a Nuclear Detonation
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Understand the process of fission product formation and how fallout is created after a nuclear detonation.

### Fission Products
• Definition: Radioactive material produced when uranium or plutonium nuclei split apart during a...
Objective: Understand the process of fission product formation and how fallout is created after a nuclear detonation.

### Fission Products
• Definition: Radioactive material produced when uranium or plutonium nuclei split apart during a nuclear explosion.
• Characteristics:
- Most of the radioactivity in fallout comes from these products.
- They are short-lived, making them most hazardous in the first few hours to days after detonation.

### Fallout Formation Process
1. Fission products are vaporized in the fireball and retained within the nuclear cloud.
2. The extreme heat of the fireball causes the nuclear cloud to rise rapidly, often several miles into the atmosphere.
3. For near-surface detonations:
- Cratering and strong updrafts below the cloud can incorporate thousands of tons of dirt and debris into the cloud.
4. The highly radioactive fission products condense on this dirt and debris, forming fallout particles.
5. Fallout particles vary in size:
- Some are too small to be seen by the naked eye.
- Others can be as large as pebbles.

### Immediate Dangerous Fallout
• Immediately dangerous fallout will descend back to Earth within the first few minutes to hours after detonation.
• This fallout is often visible and may resemble fine sand or table salt in size.
fission products, fallout formation, nuclear detonation, radiation, debris

Weapon Effects (1) -

Understand the Impact of Height of Burst (HOB) on Nuclear Weapon Effects
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Define HOB as the height of the nuclear explosion relative to ground level.
2. Assess how HOB affects various weapon effects:
- Thermal energy output and range.
- Fallout hazard magnitude.
- Blast wave strength...
Procedure:

1. Define HOB as the height of the nuclear explosion relative to ground level.
2. Assess how HOB affects various weapon effects:
- Thermal energy output and range.
- Fallout hazard magnitude.
- Blast wave strength and interaction with the ground.
- EMP severity and range.
3. Use this information in planning for different detonation scenarios.

Notes:
• HOB is a critical factor in determining the type and scale of hazards after a nuclear detonation.
• Understanding HOB helps in tailoring response strategies to specific scenarios.
height of burst,nuclear effects,thermal energy,blast wave

Fallout Hazards (1) -

Understand Near-Surface Burst Effects and Hazards
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Define a near-surface burst as a nuclear explosion at or near the surface, which incorporates ground material into the resulting cloud.
2. Assess the effects of ground material incorporation:
- Reduces thermal output...
Procedure:

1. Define a near-surface burst as a nuclear explosion at or near the surface, which incorporates ground material into the resulting cloud.
2. Assess the effects of ground material incorporation:
- Reduces thermal output compared to low-altitude air bursts with the same yield.
- Increases fallout hazard magnitude due to the formation of fallout particles from combined fission products and ground material.
3. Identify dominant hazards in a near-surface burst scenario:
- Blast effects.
- Local fallout radiation.
- Initial radiation exposure.
4. Plan for high local fallout radiation levels that may occur after such an event.

Notes:
• Near-surface bursts produce significant local fallout and blast hazards.
• Immediate response planning must account for the potential severity of these effects.
near surface burst,fallout hazards,blast effects

Nuclear Detonation Hazards (1) -

Identify Low-Altitude Air Burst Characteristics
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Recognize low-altitude air burst features:
- The fireball does not interact with the ground, so no dirt or debris is incorporated into the cap of the nuclear cloud.
- The mushroom cloud will have a **white or...
Procedure:

1. Recognize low-altitude air burst features:
- The fireball does not interact with the ground, so no dirt or debris is incorporated into the cap of the nuclear cloud.
- The mushroom cloud will have a white or light-colored cap, in contrast to the darker stem of the cloud.
- This type of detonation results in minimal or negligible local fallout due to the lack of ground material mixing with fission products.

2. Understand radiation hazards:
- The dominant hazards are blast, thermal (for higher yields), and initial radiation (for lower yields).
- Fallout may be minimal, but initial radiation exposure during the explosion can still harm people.

3. Use visual observations as a guide:
- A white or light-colored cloud cap compared to the darker stem indicates an air burst with less hazardous fallout conditions.
- A darkened cloud cap suggests a near-surface detonation, which may produce more hazardous local fallout.

4. Confirm with radiation survey measurements:
- Visual observations should be supplemented by radiation survey data to accurately assess the level of radiation hazards in an area.

5. Be aware of long-term atmospheric effects:
- Fission products form microscopic particles that remain in the atmosphere for days to months after a low-altitude air burst.
- Precipitation can cause “rainout”, leading to localized, low-dose rate radiation hot spots downwind.
low-altitude air burst,nuclear cloud characteristics,mushroom cloud,fallout,radiation hazards,initial radiation,thermal damage,blast damage

Emp Effects (1) -

Understand the Effects of Source Region EMP (SREMP) on Electronic Systems
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Recognize that a low-altitude detonation produces SREMP, which differs from high-altitude EMP (HEMP).
2. Identify two major disruptive effects of SREMP:
- Electromagnetic Illumination: This occurs when induced...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that a low-altitude detonation produces SREMP, which differs from high-altitude EMP (HEMP).
2. Identify two major disruptive effects of SREMP:
- Electromagnetic Illumination: This occurs when induced voltage on internal wires and conductors disables or damages electronic equipment.
- Line Coupling: Large voltage/current surges in long power lines and other conductors near the detonation can propagate for significant distances, causing disruption and potential damage a few miles outside the blast area.
3. Assess the impact of SREMP on critical infrastructure, such as power grids, communication systems, and electronic devices.
4. Implement protective measures to shield or harden electronic equipment against EMP effects when in areas at risk from low-altitude detonations.
5. Monitor for disruptions in power lines and communication systems that may indicate SREMP exposure.
6. Follow guidance on EMP effects provided in Appendix 1.1 for more detailed information on SREMP and HEMP differences.

Notes:
• SREMP can cause significant damage to electronic equipment through electromagnetic induction and line coupling.
• Protective measures are essential to mitigate the impact of SREMP on infrastructure.
emp,srem,electronic damage,nuclear effects,disruption

Community Actions (1) -

Additional Actions in the LDZ After a Nuclear Detonation
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Provide additional guidance for individuals and communities within the LDZ after a nuclear detonation.

Steps:
1. Seek adequate shelter: The uninjured and those with minor injuries should seek adequate shelter to...
Objective: Provide additional guidance for individuals and communities within the LDZ after a nuclear detonation.

Steps:
1. Seek adequate shelter: The uninjured and those with minor injuries should seek adequate shelter to protect themselves from radiation exposure.
2. Clear blocked roads: Crashed and abandoned vehicles will block roads, preventing or slowing emergency vehicle access. Clear these obstructions if safe to do so.
3. Promote self-treatment and community-organized first aid: Where safe, encourage self-treatment and community-organized first aid, such as at RTR 1 sites described in Chapter 4 of the guidance document.
4. Direct injured survivors to RTR sites: If injured survivors are mobile, they should be directed to RTR sites for further assistance.
LDZ, RTR site, self-treatment, community first aid

Emergency Worker Safety (1) -

Implement Incident Command System (ICS) for Emergency Worker Safety
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Adhere to the National Incident Management System (NIMS):
- Ensure all incident response organizations follow NIMS standards.
- Use ICS as the emergency response standard at all levels of government.
- Facilitate safe...
Procedure:

1. Adhere to the National Incident Management System (NIMS):
- Ensure all incident response organizations follow NIMS standards.
- Use ICS as the emergency response standard at all levels of government.
- Facilitate safe operations in highly hazardous environments through structured command and control systems.

2. Establish Worker Accountability Practices:
- Track the location and status of all emergency workers during a nuclear incident.
- Maintain real-time communication between incident commanders and field personnel.

3. Enforce Stay Time Limits:
- Limit the amount of time emergency workers spend in areas with high radiation exposure.
- Use dosimeters to monitor cumulative radiation exposure for each worker.

4. Use Predictive Modeling Tools (IMAAC):
- Access IMAAC models provided by FSLTT authorities to estimate fallout deposition and dangerous radiation zones within the first hour after a nuclear detonation.
- Note that initial model outputs are estimates with high uncertainty, but accuracy improves over time.

5. Combine Predictive Modeling with Real-Time Data:
- Use radiation measurements and fallout cloud observations to confirm affected areas and make informed protective action decisions.
- Rely on actual field data rather than predictive models alone for worker protection decisions.
incident command system,ics,nims,radiation exposure,worker safety,predictive modeling,fallout zones

Situational Awareness (1) -

Implement Situational Awareness for Fallout and Hazards
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

• Use a zoned-based approach to define shelter and evacuation zones based on accurate estimates of fallout distribution, building status, and resource availability.

• Continuously incorporate new resources and information as they...
Procedure:

• Use a zoned-based approach to define shelter and evacuation zones based on accurate estimates of fallout distribution, building status, and resource availability.

• Continuously incorporate new resources and information as they become available to refine zone definitions.

• Monitor for evolving hazards such as:
- Fallout deposition
- Fire initiation, spread, and possible coalescence

• Use radiation-monitoring data from local responders to improve situational awareness.

• Utilize plume models (e.g., IMAAC’s products) to project hazardous areas based on available information and parameters. Model accuracy improves as measurements are incorporated.

• Observe visual signs of fallout, such as fine, sandy material falling out of the plume or accumulating on clean surfaces. Note that visible fallout may not be noticeable on rough or dirty surfaces and cannot directly estimate radiation dose rates.
fallout monitoring,plume models,situational awareness,nuclear detonation

Evacuation Planning (1) -

Evacuation Planning and Execution in Fallout Zones
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

• Sheltering is implicitly short term. Initial sheltering should be followed by staged, facilitated evacuation for those in fallout-impacted areas.

• Optimal shelter stay times can range from a few hours to several days and depend...
Procedure:

• Sheltering is implicitly short term. Initial sheltering should be followed by staged, facilitated evacuation for those in fallout-impacted areas.

• Optimal shelter stay times can range from a few hours to several days and depend on:
- Local fallout dose rate
- Radiation dose incurred during evacuation

• Where possible, individuals should stay in an adequate shelter for the first 24 hours following detonation to prevent exposure to high levels of radiation.

• Evacuations should occur only after appropriate paths have been identified and cleared.

• Closely monitor fire concerns as rapidly evolving fires may warrant emergency evacuation of potentially impacted areas.

• Avoid attempting to evacuate excessively large areas at a single time, as this unnecessarily diverts resources from other response needs.

• Be aware that many people may choose to self-evacuate.
evacuation planning,fallout zones,nuclear detonation,sheltering

Emergency Response Planning (1) -

Implement Multi-Jurisdictional Alert and Warning Network (AWN) Planning for Nuclear Detonation Scenarios
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Ensure continuity and survivability of public Alert and Warning Network (AWN) capabilities in the event of a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Understand the Response FIOP: Review and familiarize all planners with the...
Objective: Ensure continuity and survivability of public Alert and Warning Network (AWN) capabilities in the event of a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Understand the Response FIOP: Review and familiarize all planners with the _Response FIOP_ to ensure local, state, and tribal emergency management plans include information that enables preparation for nuclear detonation scenarios.
2. Identify Nation-Level Messages: Use the _Response FIOP_ as a reference to understand the source of nation-level messages and what to expect during a nuclear detonation event.
3. Prepare Pre-Scripted Messages: Develop well-informed, pre-scripted messages that can be quickly deployed in response to a nuclear detonation scenario.
4. Multi-Jurisdictional Planning: Ensure planning for AWN is multi-jurisdictional and includes collaboration between local, regional, and national authorities.
5. Regional Response Considerations: Plan for regional responses to black sky disasters that may affect multiple states and jurisdictions, as this is often the most realistic approach.
6. Black Sky Scenario Awareness: Understand that a "black sky" scenario refers to widespread infrastructure failure caused by catastrophic incidents such as nuclear detonation or grid collapse.
7. Operational Coordination Core Capability Annex: Incorporate the Operational Coordination core capability annex from the _Response FIOP_ into planning, which supports execution of other Response core capabilities and emphasizes AWN as a crosscutter for all Community Lifelines.
8. Survivability of Alert Dissemination Means: Pay careful attention to the survivability of means used to disseminate lifesaving messages immediately following a nuclear detonation or in advance if adequate technical warning systems are employed.
9. Determine Alerting Authority Locations and Capabilities: Urban area planners must develop methodologies for determining where and how many alerting authorities are needed to sustain continuity and survivability of public AWN from nuclear detonation blast effects.
10. Establish Additional IPAWS Alerting Authorities: Consider establishing additional Integrated Public Alert and Warning System (IPAWS) alerting authorities to prevent or strongly mitigate the loss of AWN capability, such as Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) and Emergency Alert System (EAS).
11. Know EOC Locations and Capabilities: Planners must be aware of all Emergency Operations Center (EOC) locations and the capabilities of their AWN tools, including each IPAWS alerting authority.

Notes:
• The _Response FIOP_ emphasizes that timely restoration of communications infrastructure is critical but also highlights the need for advance identification and implementation of measures to sustain operational capabilities of alert origination authorities.
• This planning should be done in conjunction with all relevant stakeholders, including local emergency managers, regional planners, and federal agencies.
nuclear detonation, AWN planning, multi-jurisdictional response, IPAWS, EOC, black sky disaster, alert and warning network, Response FIOP

Radiation Treatment (1) -

Utilize the Radiation Injury Treatment Network (RITN)
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Access medical treatment and training resources from RITN for radiation injuries.

Steps:
1. Identify the need for specialized care in a nuclear detonation scenario involving radiation injury.
2. Contact the Radiation Injury...
Objective: Access medical treatment and training resources from RITN for radiation injuries.

Steps:
1. Identify the need for specialized care in a nuclear detonation scenario involving radiation injury.
2. Contact the Radiation Injury Treatment Network (RITN) through its website at [www.RITN.net](www.RITN.net).
3. Use RITN resources to:
- Access hospitals and medical providers with capabilities in treating radiation injuries
- Obtain training materials for healthcare professionals
- Retrieve adult and pediatric medical treatment recommendations
- Get medical referral assessments for Acute Radiation Syndrome (ARS) patients
4. Ensure that the network is used as a resource for both immediate care and long-term management of radiation injuries.

Details:
• RITN is composed of private sector hospitals and medical providers trained in radiation injury treatment.
• It provides critical support during nuclear detonation scenarios involving radiation exposure.
RITN, Radiation Injury Treatment Network, nuclear detonation, radiation treatment

Vehicle Decontamination (1) -

Handle Fallout-Contaminated Vehicles
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Action: Handle fallout-contaminated vehicles to mitigate the spread of contamination.

Details:
• Fallout-contaminated vehicles can spread contamination outside of the damage and hazard zones.
• Vehicle decontamination can help reduce the...
Action: Handle fallout-contaminated vehicles to mitigate the spread of contamination.

Details:
• Fallout-contaminated vehicles can spread contamination outside of the damage and hazard zones.
• Vehicle decontamination can help reduce the spread of contamination but should not restrict or inhibit evacuations.
• Use of contaminated vehicles (personal or mass transit) for evacuation should not be discouraged in the initial days following a nuclear detonation because cross-contamination issues are a secondary concern.
vehicle decontamination, fallout contamination, evacuation vehicles

Public Information (1) -

Provide Public Information on Simple Decontamination Methods
Standard Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Planners must coordinate with PIOs (Public Information Officers) to prepare messaging that describes simple decontamination methods for the public:

• Rinsing vehicles with soap and water is a simple method that can be used by evacuees to...
Planners must coordinate with PIOs (Public Information Officers) to prepare messaging that describes simple decontamination methods for the public:

• Rinsing vehicles with soap and water is a simple method that can be used by evacuees to minimize the spread of contamination.

> Note: This is particularly important for individuals using unscreened vehicles during evacuation.
public information, decontamination methods, soap and water

Prevention (1) -

Preventive Measures for Nuclear Attack Survival
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Prevention Strategy:

• Relocate to Avoid Risk: Try to relocate to avoid living near a nuclear target such as a big city or military base.

• Avoidance of Risk is the Only Valid Prevention Strategy: There are no other effective...
Prevention Strategy:

• Relocate to Avoid Risk: Try to relocate to avoid living near a nuclear target such as a big city or military base.

• Avoidance of Risk is the Only Valid Prevention Strategy: There are no other effective preventive measures for nuclear attacks beyond relocating away from high-risk areas.
nuclear attack, prevention, relocation, risk avoidance

Equipment (1) -

Nuclear Shelter and Equipment Recommendations
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Equipment Strategy:

• Shelter is the Most Important Item: The most important thing to have is shelter. We recommend *"Nuclear War Survival Skills"* by Cresson H. Kearny (http://www.oism.org/nwss/). This book discusses several low-tech...
Equipment Strategy:

• Shelter is the Most Important Item: The most important thing to have is shelter. We recommend *"Nuclear War Survival Skills"* by Cresson H. Kearny (http://www.oism.org/nwss/). This book discusses several low-tech approaches to nuclear survival.

• Standard Home Equipment Sufficient for Most Situations: Most of the equipment recommended is standard in most homes or readily available.

• Monitoring Equipment Considerations: The decision on the purchase of monitoring equipment is a personal one. If you have a formal shelter and long-term supplies, there may be a role for monitoring equipment. However, if using an expedient shelter, it is logical to stay in your shelter for as long as practical.

• Brief Periods Outside Shelter: If forced to leave the shelter, only do so for brief periods. The value of knowing how much radiation you have received may be limited during these times.
nuclear shelter, equipment, monitoring devices, expedient shelter

Procedures (1) -

Proper Donning and Doffing of PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)
Critical Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Objective: Safely put on (don) and take off (doff) NBC CBRN protective equipment to avoid contamination.

Procedure:
1. Preparation: Ensure all components of your PPE are clean, dry, and in good condition before use.
2. **Donning...
Objective: Safely put on (don) and take off (doff) NBC CBRN protective equipment to avoid contamination.

Procedure:
1. Preparation: Ensure all components of your PPE are clean, dry, and in good condition before use.
2. Donning Steps:
- Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water or hand sanitizer.
- Put on the HAZ-SUIT first, ensuring it is fully sealed around the neck and wrists.
- Don gloves next, making sure they are snug but not tight.
- Insert the gas mask filter into the mask, then put on the mask. Ensure a proper seal by checking for air leaks (e.g., by covering the intake valve and feeling for airflow).
- Secure the straps of the mask to ensure it is tightly fastened around the head and face.
- Put on boots last, ensuring they are securely fastened.
3. Doffing Steps:
- Remove gloves first, taking care not to touch any contaminated surfaces with bare hands.
- Carefully remove the mask by unfastening the straps and lifting it off the face.
- Remove the HAZ-SUIT by pulling it over your head, avoiding contact with the outside of the suit.
- Remove boots last, taking care not to contaminate the inside of the suit.
4. Post-Doffing:
- Wash hands and any exposed skin thoroughly with soap and water or hand sanitizer.
- Decontaminate all PPE components if they were used in a contaminated environment.

Notes:
• Always follow proper donning and doffing procedures to avoid cross-contamination.
• If unsure about the process, refer to official CBRN training materials.
PPE donning, PPE doffing, decontamination, gas mask use, protective gear

Response Procedures (1) -

Responding to a CBRN Attack Scenario
Critical Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Recognize signs of a CBRN incident:
- Look for unusual odors, visible smoke or vapor clouds, unexplained illness among people, or abandoned containers.
- Be aware of suspicious behavior or unattended packages.

2....
Procedure:

1. Recognize signs of a CBRN incident:
- Look for unusual odors, visible smoke or vapor clouds, unexplained illness among people, or abandoned containers.
- Be aware of suspicious behavior or unattended packages.

2. Evacuate the area immediately:
- If you suspect a CBRN attack, leave the location as quickly and safely as possible.
- Avoid touching any objects or materials that may be contaminated.

3. Follow official instructions:
- Listen to emergency broadcasts or alerts from local authorities.
- Do not return to the affected area until it is declared safe by officials.

4. Decontaminate if exposed:
- If you suspect exposure, remove contaminated clothing and wash your skin thoroughly with soap and water.
- Seek medical attention immediately for any signs of illness or injury.

5. Report the incident:
- Contact local emergency services (999 in the UK) to report the suspected CBRN attack.
- Provide as much information as possible, including location, time, and details of what you observed.

6. Stay informed:
- Monitor updates from official sources such as the government or public health agencies.
- Follow guidelines on how to protect yourself and others in case of further incidents.

Warnings:
• Do not attempt to handle or clean up hazardous materials unless trained and equipped to do so.
• Avoid spreading contamination by touching surfaces, objects, or other people.
CBRN attack,response procedures,evacuation,decontamination,emergency services

Awareness And Preparedness (1) -

Understanding CBRN Threats in the UK
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Be aware of potential threats:
- Understand that while large-scale CBRN attacks are unlikely, smaller incidents involving hazardous materials (e.g., illegal drug labs) occur regularly.
- Be informed about past events...
Procedure:

1. Be aware of potential threats:
- Understand that while large-scale CBRN attacks are unlikely, smaller incidents involving hazardous materials (e.g., illegal drug labs) occur regularly.
- Be informed about past events such as the poisoning of Alexander Litvinenko and the Novichok attack on the Skripal family.

2. Recognize the types of CBRN threats:
- Chemical: Involves toxic substances that can cause harm through inhalation, ingestion, or contact with skin.
- Biological: Involves pathogens such as bacteria or viruses that can spread disease.
- Radiological/Nuclear: Involves radioactive materials that can cause radiation sickness or long-term environmental damage.

3. Understand the impact scenarios:
- Small-scale incidents may result in localized casualties and fatalities.
- Large-scale events could lead to widespread contamination, economic disruption, and long-term environmental effects.

4. Stay informed about government efforts:
- The UK government works to detect and monitor CBRN materials at borders and limit access to hazardous substances.
- Stay updated on public health advisories or national security alerts.

5. Prepare for potential exposure:
- Know the signs of chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear exposure (e.g., difficulty breathing, nausea, skin irritation).
- Keep a supply of protective gear such as masks and gloves if possible.

6. Support emergency response efforts:
- Cooperate with authorities during investigations or decontamination operations.
- Report any suspicious activity to the police or relevant agencies.
CBRN threats,UK preparedness,chemical attack,biological threat,radiological incident

Government Measures (1) -

Mitigating CBRN Risks Through Government Measures
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Understand government initiatives:
- The UK government is focused on reducing the vulnerability of the country to CBRN attacks through enhanced detection and monitoring systems.
- These include improved border controls,...
Procedure:

1. Understand government initiatives:
- The UK government is focused on reducing the vulnerability of the country to CBRN attacks through enhanced detection and monitoring systems.
- These include improved border controls, surveillance technologies, and intelligence gathering.

2. Support public health measures:
- Follow guidelines from health authorities regarding food and water safety to prevent contamination-related illnesses.
- Be aware of how consumer behavior may change in the event of a CBRN incident (e.g., increased demand for bottled water or sealed food products).

3. Limit access to hazardous materials:
- The government works to restrict access to dangerous substances and their precursors, such as chemicals used in explosives or biological agents.
- As an individual, avoid purchasing or handling unregulated materials that could be used for malicious purposes.

4. Participate in community preparedness programs:
- Engage with local emergency management agencies to learn about CBRN response plans and drills.
- Encourage others to take part in training sessions on how to respond during a CBRN incident.

5. Stay informed about risk assessments:
- Review the National Risk Register regularly to understand current threats and government strategies for mitigating them.
- Pay attention to updates from national security agencies regarding potential risks or changes in threat levels.

6. Report suspicious activity:
- If you notice anything unusual, such as unattended packages or individuals acting suspiciously near public places, report it immediately to the police or relevant authorities.
CBRN mitigation,government initiatives,detection systems,hazardous materials,public health

Nuclear Miscalculation Preparedness (1) -

Prepare for Nuclear Miscalculation Not Involving the UK or Its Allies
Critical Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Understand the risk: Recognize that nuclear miscalculation refers to a scenario where one state mistakenly interprets another’s intentions and launches a nuclear strike, potentially leading to limited nuclear conflict between...
Procedure:

1. Understand the risk: Recognize that nuclear miscalculation refers to a scenario where one state mistakenly interprets another’s intentions and launches a nuclear strike, potentially leading to limited nuclear conflict between two states not involving the UK.

2. Assess potential impacts: Be aware of the following:
- Casualties and fatalities in the affected region based on weapon yield, location, and local population density.
- Economic disruption affecting UK businesses with ties to the impacted area.
- Need for support to British Nationals residing in the affected region.
- Increased migration from the affected region, placing pressure on infrastructure.
- Potential rise in community tensions involving diaspora communities within the UK.

3. Support diplomatic and non-proliferation efforts: Promote nuclear disarmament and cooperation through adherence to the Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) and other international agreements.

4. Prepare for humanitarian response: Ensure readiness to provide assistance to affected populations, including refugees and British Nationals in need of support.

5. Enhance border preparedness: Prepare UK border staff to manage increased refugee flows and provide necessary assistance.

6. Monitor radiation levels: Maintain a civilian workforce trained to monitor radiation levels in the event of nuclear fallout or contamination.

Notes:
• This scenario does not involve direct UK involvement, but the UK may still be affected indirectly through economic, social, and humanitarian impacts.
nuclear miscalculation, non-proliferation treaty, radiation monitoring, refugee support, humanitarian assistance

Shelter Installation (1) -

Install Integral Protective Entrance (Internal and External Configurations)
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Determine whether an internal or external integral protective entrance is required based on shelter design and available internal space.
2. For internal configurations:
a. Deploy the internal integral protective entrance...
Procedure:

1. Determine whether an internal or external integral protective entrance is required based on shelter design and available internal space.
2. For internal configurations:
a. Deploy the internal integral protective entrance within the shelter.
b. Ensure it remains in its functional configuration without needing to be stowed for transport.
c. Verify that the integral protective entrance and shelter door are provided as a single bolt assembly.
3. For external configurations:
a. Use the external integral protective entrance when internal space cannot be sacrificed.
b. Ensure the self-supporting integral protective entrance is stowed properly for transport.
c. Confirm that it is designed for use with S250 and S280 shelters.
d. Verify that it is provided as a single bolt assembly with the shelter door.
4. Both configurations are currently designed for use with S250 and S280 shelters.

Notes:
• The internal configuration provides improved accessibility, convenience in storage and transport, and reduced setup time. It is less vulnerable on the battlefield since it remains contained within the shelter.
• The external configuration requires additional setup time compared to internal integral entrances but is suitable when internal space cannot be compromised.
integral protective entrance, internal installation, external installation, S250 shelter, S280 shelter, NBC protection

Collective-Protection Systems (1) -

Implement and Plan Logistics for Collective-Protection Systems
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Assess the tactical situation to determine if collective protection is necessary.
2. Consider whether it is possible to avoid or displace from contaminated terrain.
3. If displacement or rotation to clean areas is not feasible,...
Procedure:

1. Assess the tactical situation to determine if collective protection is necessary.
2. Consider whether it is possible to avoid or displace from contaminated terrain.
3. If displacement or rotation to clean areas is not feasible, deploy collective-protection systems.
4. Use flexible systems that allow commanders to balance mission capability with NBC survivability.
5. Plan for the supply of materials needed to support collective-protection systems.
6. Ensure maintenance is scheduled, including filter replacement and system checks.
7. Arrange transportation for all components required to deploy and maintain collective-protection systems.
8. Coordinate with logistics units to ensure continuous operation of these systems in the field.

Notes:
• Rotation of contaminated soldiers to a known clean area is the least costly method for relief from MOPP gear.
• Collective protection should be used when tactical conditions prevent displacement or rotation.
• Proper logistics planning ensures that collective-protection systems remain functional during extended operations.
• Filter replacement is a critical maintenance task that must be included in all plans.
collective protection, NBC survivability, logistics planning, filter replacement, MOPP gear, contaminated terrain

Mopp-Gear Exchange Procedures (1) -

MOPP-Gear Exchange Planning, Buddy-Team, and Triple Buddy-Team Methods
Standard Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Estimate Time Required:
- A MOPP-gear exchange can be conducted by a buddy team, triple buddy team, or an individual (in emergencies).
- Estimate about 60 minutes for a squad or platoon to complete the exchange.

2....
Procedure:

1. Estimate Time Required:
- A MOPP-gear exchange can be conducted by a buddy team, triple buddy team, or an individual (in emergencies).
- Estimate about 60 minutes for a squad or platoon to complete the exchange.

2. Schedule Exchange Timely:
- Individual performance is severely degraded after 6 hours at MOPP4.
- Schedule the MOPP-gear exchange before this time to maintain operational effectiveness.

3. Consider Buddy Team Options:
- Use a buddy team, triple buddy team, or individual (emergency) for the exchange.
- For the buddy-team method, use pairs of soldiers under supervision of their squad/team leader. See Table 3-4 (pages 3-7 through 3-17) for detailed step-by-step procedures.

4. Consider CVCUS/CPU Units:
- For units equipped with the Combat Vehicle Crewman Uniform System (CVCUS)/Chemical Protective Undergarment (CPU), refer to Table 3-5 (pages 3-18 through 3-21) for specific procedures.

5. Use Triple Buddy Teams for Specific Equipment:
- This method is used by soldiers equipped with the M40A1, M42, or M43 mask with the quick-doff hood.
- A third soldier is needed to hold the filter canister and hose to prevent contamination transfer.
- See Table 3-6 (pages 3-22 through 3-27) for detailed step-by-step procedures for this method.
- For units equipped with CVCUS/CPU, refer to Table 3-7 (pages 3-28 through 3-30).

Notes:
• Ensure sufficient space and resources are available for the exchange process.
• Coordinate with unit leadership to ensure timing aligns with operational needs.
• Ensure all personnel are trained in buddy-team and triple buddy-team decontamination methods.
• Coordinate with unit leadership to ensure proper supervision and safety protocols.
MOPP-gear exchange, buddy team, triple buddy team, decontamination, NBC procedures, CVCUS, CPU

Radiation Support (1) -

Access Radiation Emergency Assistance Center/Training Site (REAC/TS)

Objective: Access radiation emergency response and training resources from REAC/TS.

Steps:
1. Identify the need for radiation incident management or medical expertise during a nuclear detonation scenario.
2. Contact the U.S. Department of...
Objective: Access radiation emergency response and training resources from REAC/TS.

Steps:
1. Identify the need for radiation incident management or medical expertise during a nuclear detonation scenario.
2. Contact the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) to access Radiation Emergency Assistance Center/Training Site (REAC/TS) services.
3. Use REAC/TS resources for:
- Emergency response support
- Subject matter expertise on medical management of radiation incidents
- Continuing medical education and outreach exercises
4. Access clinical information, training opportunities, and radiation incident management guidance at the following link: [orise.orau.gov/reacts](orise.orau.gov/reacts)

Details:
• REAC/TS provides critical support for managing radiation injuries.
• It is a key resource in nuclear detonation scenarios involving radiation exposure.
REAC/TS, Radiation Emergency Assistance Center, DOE, nuclear detonation, radiation support

Nuclear Attack Preparedness (1) -

Assessing Unit Vulnerability to Nuclear Attack
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
To assess a unit’s vulnerability to a nuclear attack, follow these steps:

1. Determine the yield of the warhead likely to be used.
2. Evaluate available protection for the unit (e.g., shelters, vehicles, or structures).
3. **Assess...
To assess a unit’s vulnerability to a nuclear attack, follow these steps:

1. Determine the yield of the warhead likely to be used.
2. Evaluate available protection for the unit (e.g., shelters, vehicles, or structures).
3. Assess dispersion of the unit across the target area.
4. Use Tables 1-1 through 1-3 from FM 3-3-1 to estimate damage caused by a nuclear detonation.
5. Consider that these tables assume the worst-case scenario at ground zero (GZ) and uniform dispersion of units.
6. Use this information to help the commander determine unit vulnerability to nuclear attack.

> Note: These tables are simplified and safesided for planning purposes only. For actual vulnerability radii, refer to FM 101-31-2 (S).
nuclear attack,vulnerability analysis,unit dispersion,warhead yield,protection assessment

Mapping Contamination Areas (1) -

Determine and Plot Neutron-Induced Gamma Activity (NIGA) Areas
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
To determine and plot neutron-induced gamma activity areas (NIGA), follow these steps:

Step 1: Use the Keller Nomogram:
• Refer to Figure 7-4, which contains two parts (Part 1 and Part 2).
• Locate the known weapon yield on the left side of...
To determine and plot neutron-induced gamma activity areas (NIGA), follow these steps:

Step 1: Use the Keller Nomogram:
• Refer to Figure 7-4, which contains two parts (Part 1 and Part 2).
• Locate the known weapon yield on the left side of the chart.
• Lay a straight edge (hairline) across the chart so that it aligns with the yield value.

Step 2: Determine Radii:
• Pin down the hairline on the dark black index line.
• Rotate the hairline laterally and read the radii of the NIGA pattern in hundreds of meters at the bottom of the chart.

Step 3: Plot the Area on a Map Overlay:
• Draw a circle around the GZ (Ground Zero) point with the radius determined from the nomogram.
• Label the area and record nuclear burst data and overlay information on the map.
• This area is considered contaminated until actual dose-rate readings indicate otherwise.

Note: The size of the NIGA pattern depends on the yield of the weapon and the height of burst.
keller nomogram,niga areas,neutron-induced gamma activity,map overlay,ground zero

Radiation Fallout Estimation (1) -

Estimate Time of Completion (Tcomp) for Fallout
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
To estimate the time of completion (Tcomp) for fallout at a specific location, follow these steps:

1. Determine the time of arrival (Tarrival) of the radioactive cloud at the location:
- Calculate this by dividing the distance from the...
To estimate the time of completion (Tcomp) for fallout at a specific location, follow these steps:

1. Determine the time of arrival (Tarrival) of the radioactive cloud at the location:
- Calculate this by dividing the distance from the ground zero (GZ) to the given point by the effective wind speed.
2. Calculate Tcomp using the formula:
$ T_{comp} = 1.25 imes T_{arrival} + ext{time required for cloud to pass over} $
3. Determine the time required for the cloud to pass over:
- This is calculated by dividing the cloud diameter (in kilometers) by the effective wind speed (in km/h).
4. Add the two values together to get Tcomp.
5. Convert decimal hours into minutes if necessary:
- Multiply the decimal portion of the hour by 60 to convert it into minutes.

Example:
• Time arrival = H + 2 hours (calculated from distance and wind speed)
• Cloud diameter = 4 km (determined using Figure 5-9)
• Effective wind speed = 20 km/h (from NBC 3 report)
• Tcomp = $1.25 imes 2 + (4 / 20) = 2.7$ hours
• Convert 0.7 hours to minutes: $0.7 imes 60 = 42$ minutes
• Final Tcomp = H + 2 hours and 42 minutes
fallout estimation, nuclear contamination, radiation, Tcomp calculation

Radiation Survey Processing (1) -

Prepare Worksheet Overlay for Aerial Survey Data Plotting
High Priority Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
Before receiving dose-rate information from an aerial survey, complete the following steps:

1. Mark and label checkpoints on the worksheet overlay corresponding to the route or course leg that was traveled by the survey party.
2. **Trace out...
Before receiving dose-rate information from an aerial survey, complete the following steps:

1. Mark and label checkpoints on the worksheet overlay corresponding to the route or course leg that was traveled by the survey party.
2. Trace out the preselected route or course leg on the worksheet overlay to visually represent the path taken during the survey.
3. Ensure all readings, including zero readings, are accounted for in the data processing phase.
4. Confirm that the aircraft flew at a constant ground speed and that readings were taken at equal time intervals.
5. Divide the route or leg into segments based on the number of readings taken:
- The total number of segments will be one less than the number of readings (e.g., 7 readings = 6 segments).
aerial survey, worksheet overlay, radiation data plotting

Radiation Shielding Calculations (1) -

Calculate Initial Dose Rate with No Shielding Using the 2n Table
Critical Source: FM 3 3 1 Nuclear Contamination Avoidance
To calculate the initial dose rate (Ro) using a 2n table:

1. Identify the total thickness of shielding material (X).
2. Determine the half-thickness of the shielding material (X½).
3. **Measure the final dose rate after applying shielding...
To calculate the initial dose rate (Ro) using a 2n table:

1. Identify the total thickness of shielding material (X).
2. Determine the half-thickness of the shielding material (X½).
3. Measure the final dose rate after applying shielding (R).
4. Calculate the number of half-thicknesses (n) using the formula: n = X / X½.
5. Look up the corresponding 2ⁿ value in a 2n table.
6. Use the equation: Ro = R × 2ⁿ to find the initial dose rate.

Example:
• Total shielding thickness (X): 12 cm
• Half-thickness of shielding material (X½): 6.25 cm
• Final dose rate (R): 5 cGyph
• n = X / X½ = 12 / 6.25 = 1.92
• From the table, find that 2ⁿ is approximately 4.0 when n ≈ 2.0
• Ro = R × 2ⁿ = 5 cGyph × 4.0 = 20 cGyph
initial dose rate, 2n table, radiation calculation

Chemical Biological Radiological Nuclear Procedures (1) -

Conduct Unmasking Procedures
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Objective: Conduct unmasking procedures in a tactical situation involving chemical agents.

Conditions: You are given a tactical situation, a group of Soldiers in mission-oriented protective posture (MOPP) 4, an M256-series chemical-agent...
Objective: Conduct unmasking procedures in a tactical situation involving chemical agents.

Conditions: You are given a tactical situation, a group of Soldiers in mission-oriented protective posture (MOPP) 4, an M256-series chemical-agent detector kit, a chemical-agent monitor (CAM), M8 and M9 detector paper, a watch, TM 3-6665-307-10, FM 3-11.4, an area where chemical agents have been used, and one of the following situations:

1. An M256A1 chemical-agent detector kit is available.
2. An M256A1 chemical-agent detector kit is not available.

Standards: Conduct unmasking procedures in sequence with or without using the M256-series detector kit and without incurring casualties.

### Steps to Conduct Unmasking Procedures:

1. Assess the situation: Determine if there are any immediate threats or signs of chemical agent exposure, such as unusual odors, visible signs of contamination, or symptoms among personnel.

2. Ensure MOPP 4 is maintained: All Soldiers must remain in mission-oriented protective posture (MOPP) 4 until unmasking procedures are confirmed safe and complete.

3. Use the M256-series chemical-agent detector kit if available:
- Open the M256A1 chemical-agent detector kit.
- Use the M8 and M9 detector paper to test for the presence of chemical agents in the area.
- If a chemical agent is detected, do not proceed with unmasking until the threat has been neutralized or confirmed safe by the appropriate personnel.

4. If the M256A1 chemical-agent detector kit is not available:
- Use alternative methods to assess for chemical agents, such as visual inspection of the area and observation of symptoms among Soldiers.
- If any signs of contamination are suspected, do not proceed with unmasking until further confirmation can be obtained from other sources or personnel trained in chemical detection.

5. Conduct a sweep of the area: Use the M256A1 chemical-agent detector kit (if available) and/or the chemical-agent monitor (CAM), along with M8 and M9 detector paper, to ensure that no chemical agents are present in the area where unmasking will take place.

6. Confirm safety before unmasking: Only proceed with unmasking if all tests confirm that the area is free of chemical agents and no further threats are detected.

7. Unmask personnel:
- Begin by removing the mask in a controlled manner, following standard operating procedures for unmasking.
- Ensure that all Soldiers follow the same procedure to maintain consistency and safety during the process.

8. Monitor for signs of chemical agent exposure: After unmasking, continue to monitor all personnel for any symptoms or signs of chemical agent exposure, such as difficulty breathing, skin irritation, or eye discomfort.

9. Document the process: Record all steps taken during the unmasking procedure, including the results of any tests conducted and the final determination that the area was safe for unmasking.

10. Maintain readiness: After unmasking, ensure that all personnel remain alert and prepared to re-enter MOPP 4 if necessary due to a change in the tactical situation or new threats detected.
unmasking procedures, chemical agents, MOPP 4, M256-series detector kit, CAM, M8 detector paper, M9 detector paper, safety protocols, nuclear_cbrn

Chemical Biological Radiological Nuclear Protection (1) -

Implement Mission-Oriented Protective Posture (MOPP)
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Procedure:

1. Assess the CBRN threat level based on available information and guidance from FM 3-11.4.
2. Determine the appropriate MOPP level to be assumed by Soldiers, considering the type of hazard (chemical, biological,...
Procedure:

1. Assess the CBRN threat level based on available information and guidance from FM 3-11.4.
2. Determine the appropriate MOPP level to be assumed by Soldiers, considering the type of hazard (chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear) and its potential impact.
3. Direct Soldiers to assume the selected MOPP level, ensuring that all required protective gear is properly donned and secured.
4. Check each Soldier’s equipment for any deficiencies, such as missing items, improper fit, or damage.
5. Ensure proper use of M8 and M9 detector paper to monitor for chemical agents in the environment.
6. Verify that decontamination kits (M291 or M295) are accessible and ready for use if needed.
7. Confirm availability and correct placement of nerve-agent antidote autoinjectors, ensuring they are within reach of all Soldiers.
8. Inspect Soldiers sleeping in MOPP4 to ensure that their gear is intact, properly secured, and not causing any discomfort or risk.
9. Rectify any deficiencies identified during inspections, such as adjusting gear, replacing damaged items, or retraining Soldiers on proper procedures.
10. Maintain situational awareness of the CBRN environment throughout the operation to ensure continued compliance with MOPP requirements.

Warnings/Contraindications:
• Do not assume a higher MOPP level than necessary, as it can reduce mobility and increase heat stress.
• Ensure that all Soldiers are trained in the proper use of CBRN protective gear before deployment.
• If any Soldier shows signs of distress or equipment failure while in MOPP4, immediately provide assistance and reassess the situation.
MOPP,CBRN,decontamination,protective gear,nerve agent antidote,FM 3-11.4,TM 3-6665-307-10

Waste Disposal Procedures (1) -

Dispose of Contaminated Waste
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Dispose of contaminated bandages and coverings by placing them in a contaminated-waste bag.
2. Seal the bag and place it in the contaminated-waste dump.
contaminated waste disposal,waste management,decon waste

Patient Evacuation Procedures (1) -

Transfer Patient to the Hot Line
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Transfer patient to the hot line:
1. Move the patient to the hot line.
2. Two medics from the clean side of the hot line move the patient into the clean treatment area.

Note: Before decontaminating another patient, each decon team...
Transfer patient to the hot line:
1. Move the patient to the hot line.
2. Two medics from the clean side of the hot line move the patient into the clean treatment area.

Note: Before decontaminating another patient, each decon team member drinks about half a quart of water. The exact amount of water consumed is increased or decreased according to the work level and temperature (see Table 8-2, page 8-11).
hot line transfer, patient movement, decon team hydration
